dimensions.summary:
Two distinct "AI" angles must be kept separate, because conflating them is the gimmick trap. (a) ML INSIDE the cache: the learned-caching literature is real and has measured wins, but almost entirely off the hot path or in latency-tolerant domains (CDN, flash, CPU-microarchitecture simulators), NOT in microsecond-budget in-memory KV serving. LRB (NSDI'20, Song et al.) uses a gradient-boosted decision tree (LightGBM-class) to approximate "Relaxed Belady" — predicting which objects fall beyond a Belady boundary — and cuts CDN WAN traffic 4-25% vs a production design, with explicitly engineered lightweight training/inference and bounded memory. LeCaR (HotStorage'18) uses online regret-minimization over just two experts (LRU+LFU) and beats ARC by up to ~18x at very small cache/working-set ratios. CACHEUS (FAST'21) generalizes this to an adaptive mixture of experts (SR-LRU + CR-LFU) chosen per workload primitive (LFU/LRU/scan/churn). Parrot (ICML'20) is a neural (attention-based) imitation-learning approximation of Belady that cuts CPU-cache miss rate ~20% on SPEC and raises web-search hit rate ~61% over LRU — but it is a research simulator, explicitly not hot-path-deployable (NN inference per access). Baleen (FAST'24) applies ML to flash-cache ADMISSION + prefetching using an "episodes" residency model, cutting peak disk-head time ~12% and TCO ~17%.

(b) The hot-path cost problem is the crux for IronCache. The "Can Increasing the Hit Ratio Hurt Cache Throughput?" analysis (ValueTools'24 / Queueing Systems'26, Harchol-Balter et al.) shows that for LRU/SLRU the bottleneck is the per-hit list-relink (delinkT) under lock contention, so chasing hit ratio can REDUCE throughput; FIFO alone monotonically improves throughput with hit ratio. SIEVE (NSDI'24, best paper) and S3-FIFO show that lazy promotion + quick demotion on FIFO-style queues beats LRU on both hit ratio (SIEVE cuts FIFO miss ratio ~21% mean) and thread scalability, with <20 LOC integration. The lesson for a max-throughput-per-core Rust cache: any per-request ML inference is a non-starter; ML belongs OFF the hot path as a background advisor that retunes cheap, deterministic policy knobs (eviction policy choice, sample count, LFU decay, slab/encoding thresholds, per-value compression decisions), with the hot path remaining a branch-free, allocation-free, lock-light O(1) policy. Note Redis itself already rejects "true" anything: defaults are maxmemory-policy=noeviction, sampled approximate LRU/LFU (maxmemory-samples=5, a 5-bit Morris-counter LFU with lfu-log-factor=10, lfu-decay-time=1) — explicitly trading exactness for memory/CPU. IronCache should beat this with W-TinyLFU-style admission (Caffeine: 4-bit count-min sketch, ~8 bytes/entry, near-Belady hit rate) as the non-ML SOTA floor, then use ML only to pick/tune among such cheap policies.

(b') AI-assisted DEVELOPMENT of IronCache is the more immediately credible "AI" story and is the project's stated method. The right framing: LLM/agent pipelines mine prior art (this very task), propose candidate mechanisms, AUTO-GENERATE benchmarks/traces and adversarially verify quantitative claims before they enter design docs, and gate experiments. The discipline must be: every numeric claim pinned to a primary source + version, an adversarial verifier agent that tries to falsify each claim and a benchmark harness that reproduces it on real traces (Wikipedia/MSR/Twitter Cluster/SocialNetwork) before any policy is accepted. This is the safe, non-gimmick AI: it improves the engineering process and the rigor of claims, with humans gating merges, rather than putting a model on the data path.

Net stance: BORROW the off-hot-path "advisor" pattern (LeCaR/CACHEUS-style expert selection, LRB-style background reuse-distance signal) and ADAPT it to retune deterministic knobs; REJECT per-request neural inference (Parrot-class) on the hot path; treat W-TinyLFU + SIEVE/S3-FIFO as the strong non-ML baselines the advisor switches between; and BORROW the LLM-agent verification harness as core project infrastructure, with hard guardrails (deterministic replay, adversarial fact-check, human merge gate).

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis ships with eviction disabled by default; the cache must be explicitly configured to evict.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from the redis.conf source comment line via gh API.

verification.corrected_value: maxmemory-policy noeviction (default)
verification.note: Verified via gh API on redis/redis@unstable (fetched 2026-06-13). redis.conf line 1209 '# maxmemory-policy noeviction' with line 1207 'The default is:'. Line 1153-1185 confirm noeviction returns errors on writes when memory is full. unstable is the 8.x dev line (version.h is the dev placeholder 255.255.255; latest releases are all 8.x: 8.8.0/8.6.4/8.4.4/8.2.7). Confirmed exactly.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis approximates LRU/LFU by sampling N keys per eviction rather than maintaining exact order, trading accuracy for memory/CPU.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from redis.conf source and corroborated by redis.io eviction docs.

verification.corrected_value: maxmemory-samples 5 (default); 10 approaches true LRU
verification.note: Verified via gh API on redis/redis@unstable. redis.conf line 1221 '# maxmemory-samples 5'; surrounding text (lines 1216-1218): 'The default of 5 produces good enough results. 10 Approximates very closely true LRU but costs more CPU. 3 is faster but not very accurate. The maximum value that can be set is 64.' Exact match for both the default and the '10 approaches true LRU' statement.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis LFU uses a probabilistic (Morris) counter with logarithmic increment and time decay, not an exact frequency count.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from redis.conf source defaults.

verification.corrected_value: lfu-log-factor 10, lfu-decay-time 1 (defaults)
verification.note: Verified via gh API on redis/redis@unstable. redis.conf lines 2414-2415 '# lfu-log-factor 10' / '# lfu-decay-time 1'; lines 2375-2411 describe the 8-bit probabilistic logarithmic counter (P = 1/(old_value*lfu_log_factor+1)) with time decay (decay-time in minutes, 0 = never decay). 'Morris counter' is the correct academic name for this probabilistic logarithmic counter; the mechanism described matches exactly. Both defaults confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: LRB uses a gradient-boosted decision tree (LightGBM-class) to approximate Belady by predicting whether an object's next access lies beyond a 'Belady boundary'; deployed in an Apache Traffic Server prototype with modest overhead.
prior_art_claims.value: 4-25% WAN traffic reduction vs a typical production CDN cache across 6 production traces
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Reduction range and GBT/ATS-prototype design from USENIX abstract + repo README; exact per-trace numbers not re-extracted (PDF 403/binary).

verification.corrected_value: 4-25% WAN traffic reduction vs a typical production CDN cache across 6 production traces
verification.note: Verified by downloading the PDF and extracting text. Abstract: 'Our simulation results with 6 production CDN traces show that LRB reduces WAN traffic compared to a typical production CDN cache design by 4-25%, and consistently outperform other state-of-the-art methods. Our evaluation of the LRB prototype shows its overhead is modest.' Model: body Sec. (line ~755-769) 'LRB uses Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM)' - GBM is the gradient-boosted-decision-tree family (webcachesim2/sunnyszy/lrb uses LightGBM specifically), so 'LightGBM-class GBDT' is accurate. Belady boundary and ATS prototype confirmed in abstract. All elements match.

prior_art_claims.claim: LeCaR uses online machine learning via regret minimization to weight two experts (LRU and LFU), reweighting after observed misses; an off-hot-path policy-selection technique.
prior_art_claims.value: >18x better than ARC when cache is small relative to working set; gap grows as cache shrinks
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 18x figure consistently reported in abstract/summaries; specific workloads where it holds are narrow (small-cache regime).

verification.corrected_value: >18x better than ARC when cache is small relative to working set; gap grows as cache shrinks
verification.note: Verified by downloading the HotStorage'18 PDF and extracting text. Abstract verbatim: 'the LeCaR framework outperforms ARC using only two fundamental eviction policies, LRU and LFU, by more than 18x when the cache size is small relative to the size of the working set.' Body (line ~62, 303): 'as much as 18x in hit rate across the 8 production storage I/O workloads from FIU.' Mechanism confirmed: regret-minimization online/reinforcement learning weighting two experts (LRU+LFU), reweighting on misses via cumulative regret. 'Gap grows as cache shrinks' is supported by the paper's central thesis (ARC's 'Achilles heel' when working set exceeds cache). Confidence raised from medium to high.

prior_art_claims.claim: CACHEUS is an adaptive learned policy combining experts (state-of-the-art ARC/LIRS/LFU plus new SR-LRU scan-resistant and CR-LFU churn-resistant experts), classifying workloads into LFU/LRU/scan/churn primitives.
prior_art_claims.value: Most consistently performing across 329 workloads x 6 cache sizes (17,766 experiments)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Experiment counts and expert set quoted directly from USENIX abstract.

prior_art_claims.claim: Parrot trains a neural (attention-based) policy via imitation learning to approximate Belady's optimal eviction from past accesses only; a research simulator, not a deployable hot-path policy due to per-access NN inference.
prior_art_claims.value: ~20% CPU-cache miss-rate reduction over SOTA on 13 SPEC apps; ~61% hit-rate increase over LRU on a web-search benchmark
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 20%/61% figures from abstract/summaries; PDF body did not extract cleanly to confirm architecture wording or deployment caveat verbatim.

verification.corrected_value: ~20% CPU-cache miss-rate reduction over SOTA on 13 SPEC apps; ~61% hit-rate increase over LRU on a web-search benchmark
verification.note: Verified by extracting text from the ICML 2020 PDF (Liu, Hashemi, Swersky, Ranganathan, Ahn). Abstract: 'When evaluated on 13 of the most memory-intensive SPEC applications, PARROT increases cache miss rates by 20% over the current state of the art' (the abstract's wording is a known awkward phrasing meaning a ~20% improvement/reduction relative to SOTA) 'on a large-scale web search benchmark, PARROT increases cache hit rates by 61% over a conventional LRU policy.' Architecture: body uses an LSTM (line 342) with Attention(Q,K) (line 348), so 'attention-based neural' is accurate; trained via imitation learning to approximate Belady's oracle from past accesses only. Released as a Gym environment (research simulator). All elements match; confidence raised to high.

prior_art_claims.claim: Baleen applies ML to flash-cache ADMISSION and prefetching using an 'episodes' cache-residency model (accesses grouped admission-to-eviction by interarrival vs eviction age), optimizing a disk-head-time TCO metric rather than raw hit ratio.
prior_art_claims.value: ~12% reduction in peak disk-head time vs SOTA; Baleen-TCO ~17% TCO reduction; ML-Range prefetch saves ~16% peak disk-head time
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Figures quoted directly from USENIX abstract.

prior_art_claims.version: Caffeine wiki (last edited 2019-02-24); TinyLFU ACM TOS 2017
prior_art_claims.claim: W-TinyLFU couples a small LRU admission window (~1%) with a TinyLFU admission filter backed by a 4-bit Count-Min Sketch with periodic aging; near-Belady hit rates without any ML inference on the hot path.
prior_art_claims.value: 4-bit Count-Min Sketch, ~8 bytes per cache entry; near-optimal hit rate, competitive with ARC/LIRS
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 4-bit sketch and 8 bytes/entry quoted verbatim from Caffeine wiki; the precise 'within 1% of optimal' phrasing varies by source.

verification.corrected_value: 4-bit Count-Min Sketch with periodic aging (reset halves all counters), ~8 bytes per cache entry; near-optimal hit rate competitive with ARC/LIRS. NOTE: Caffeine's admission window is NOT a fixed ~1% - it is adaptively sized via hill-climbing; the fixed ~1% window is from the original academic W-TinyLFU/TinyLFU paper, not the current Caffeine implementation.
verification.note: Core verified against the cited wiki (raw): '4-bit CountMinSketch, growing at 8 bytes per cache entry' and 'Window TinyLfu provides a near optimal hit rate and is competitive with ARC and LIRS.' Periodic aging verified in source (FrequencySketch.java lines 24-25,33,54): 'aging process periodically halves the popularity of all elements... 4-bit CountMinSketch with periodic aging... reset operation by TinyLfu' - so the aging claim is correct (sourced to code/TinyLFU TOS 2017, not this wiki page). The one inaccuracy: the claim's '~1% admission window' is no longer how Caffeine works - the current wiki states 'The size of the window vs main space is adaptively determined using a hill climbing optimization.' The fixed ~1% is a paper-era detail (academic W-TinyLFU), so the claim conflates the paper with the implementation. Hence corrected rather than confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: SIEVE keeps a FIFO queue with a moving hand that skips recently-accessed objects (lazy promotion / quick demotion), avoiding per-hit list relinking; integrated into 5 production cache libraries with <20 LOC each.
prior_art_claims.value: Reduces FIFO miss ratio ~21% mean (>42% on 10% of traces); reduces ARC miss ratio up to 63.2% (mean 1.5%); better thread scalability than optimized LRU
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Figures quoted from USENIX abstract/paper summary.

verification.corrected_value: Reduces FIFO miss ratio mean 21% (>42% on 10% of traces on CDN1); reduces ARC miss ratio up to 63.2% (mean 1.5%); better thread scalability than optimized LRU. (Award note: won the NSDI'24 Community Award, i.e. best paper with publicly available code/data, not a generic 'best paper'.)
verification.note: Verified by downloading the NSDI'24 PDF. Body (lines 645-652): SIEVE 'reduces FIFO's miss ratio by more than 42% on 10% of the traces (top whisker) with a mean of 21% on the CDN1 dataset using the large cache size... reduces ARC miss ratio by up to 63.2% with a mean of 1.5%.' Abstract: 'implemented in five production cache libraries, requiring fewer than 20 lines of code changes on average.' Scalability: 'achieves 17% and 125% higher throughput than optimized LRU at 1 and 16 threads' (cache hits require no locking). FIFO queue with moving hand skipping visited objects (lazy promotion/quick demotion) confirmed (Fig.1 pseudocode). All numbers match. Minor: it is the NSDI'24 Community Award (best paper with public code/datasets) per CMU CSD / USENIX, which the claim loosely calls 'best paper' - directionally correct.

prior_art_claims.system: Queueing analysis of cache hit path
prior_art_claims.claim: For LRU/SLRU the per-hit list operation (delinkT) is a contended bottleneck, so raising hit ratio can LOWER throughput; for FIFO, higher hit ratio always raises throughput. Validated by queueing model + implementation + simulation.
prior_art_claims.value: FIFO: throughput monotonically increases with hit ratio; LRU/SLRU: throughput rises then drops at high hit ratio
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Thesis and per-policy direction confirmed via abstract/search; exact throughput numbers not extracted (PDF binary).

verification.corrected_value: FIFO/CLOCK/S3-FIFO: throughput monotonically increases with hit ratio; LRU/SLRU/2Q: throughput rises then drops at high hit ratio due to the contended per-hit delink operation
verification.note: Verified by extracting the PDF (saved by WebFetch). Title 'Can Increasing the Hit Ratio Hurt Cache Throughput?', authors Ziyue Qiu, Juncheng Yang, Mor Harchol-Balter (CMU). Abstract confirms increasing hit ratio can hurt throughput for many caching algorithms via a 3-pronged approach (queueing model + simulation + implementation). FIFO: line 763 'the throughput of the FIFO cache always increases at higher hit ratios'; CLOCK/S3-FIFO likewise (lines 1199,1651,1680). LRU/SLRU: 'throughput initially rises with hit ratio and then drops'; line 1505 'Like LRU, SLRU also experiences reduced throughput with high hit ratios.' Bottleneck is the delink operation (Sdelink) on the hit path (lines 287-289,527-530). The claim's term 'delinkT' is a minor rendering of the paper's 'delink'/Sdelink. All elements verified; confidence raised to high.

mechanisms.name: GBT reuse-distance prediction (LRB / Learning Relaxed Belady)
mechanisms.what_it_does: A gradient-boosted decision tree predicts whether an object's next request lies beyond a 'Belady boundary'; objects predicted far-future are evicted. Training data and inference are engineered to be lightweight with bounded memory.
mechanisms.rationale: The PREDICTION SIGNAL (approximate reuse distance) is valuable, but tree inference per eviction candidate is too heavy for a microsecond hot path. Adapt: run a background GBT/online model that periodically emits cheap per-class or per-slab scores the hot path consumes as a static threshold; never call the model per request.

mechanisms.name: Regret-minimizing expert mixture (LeCaR)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Maintains weights over two eviction experts (LRU, LFU), samples the eviction from the weighted policies, and updates weights via regret minimization on observed misses (using a history/ghost list).
mechanisms.rationale: This is the ideal off-hot-path 'advisor' primitive: O(1), no neural net, tiny state, provably adaptive. IronCache can run a LeCaR-style controller in a background thread to pick the active hot-path policy (SIEVE vs W-TinyLFU vs LRU-sampled) per shard/workload.

mechanisms.name: Adaptive expert selection by workload primitive (CACHEUS)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Classifies the workload into LFU/LRU/scan/churn primitives and adaptively blends scan-resistant (SR-LRU) and churn-resistant (CR-LFU) experts.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the workload-primitive taxonomy as the advisor's feature space, but reject the heavier expert blending on the hot path; instead map a detected primitive to one cheap deterministic policy + knob set.

mechanisms.name: Neural imitation of Belady (Parrot)
mechanisms.what_it_does: An attention-based neural net trained by imitation learning approximates the optimal Belady eviction decision from past-access features.
mechanisms.rationale: Per-access neural inference is fundamentally incompatible with max-throughput-per-core and determinism. Useful only as an OFFLINE oracle/ceiling to measure how much hit-ratio headroom a cheap policy is leaving on the table.

mechanisms.name: ML flash admission + prefetch with 'episodes' (Baleen)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Groups accesses into residency episodes and trains ML admission/prefetch models optimized for a TCO/disk-head-time metric rather than raw hit ratio.
mechanisms.rationale: IronCache is in-memory, so flash-specific admission is out of scope, but the 'optimize the real cost metric, not hit ratio' insight and the episode-based offline training data construction directly inform IronCache's optional disk/SSD tier and its advisor's objective function.

mechanisms.name: W-TinyLFU admission via 4-bit Count-Min Sketch
mechanisms.what_it_does: A frequency sketch (4-bit counters, ~8 bytes/entry, aged) gates admission so a one-hit-wonder cannot evict a high-frequency resident; small LRU window absorbs recency.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the non-ML SOTA: near-Belady hit ratio, deterministic, ~8 bytes/entry, no inference. IronCache should implement this as the default admission filter and the baseline any ML advisor must beat before it's allowed to change anything.

mechanisms.name: Lazy promotion / quick demotion (SIEVE, S3-FIFO)
mechanisms.what_it_does: FIFO queue with a hand that retains recently-accessed objects and evicts the rest, avoiding the per-hit relink that bottlenecks LRU; quick demotion drops new objects fast.
mechanisms.rationale: Directly serves the throughput-per-core goal: avoids the delinkT lock contention that the queueing analysis identifies, scales across threads, <20 LOC. Strong default hot-path eviction structure.

mechanisms.name: Sampled approximate LRU/LFU with Morris counters
mechanisms.what_it_does: Samples maxmemory-samples (default 5) keys per eviction and evicts the worst; LFU uses a logarithmic Morris counter with time decay instead of exact counts.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the 'approximate, sampled, fixed-memory' philosophy and make sample count an ADVISOR-TUNED knob (per workload, trade accuracy vs CPU). Reject noeviction as the default for a cache product — IronCache should default to a real eviction policy.

mechanisms.name: RL/Bayesian knob autotuning (CDBTune / OtterTune)
mechanisms.what_it_does: CDBTune uses a DDPG RL agent (state = internal metrics, action = knob deltas, reward = throughput/latency delta) and OtterTune uses Gaussian-Process surrogate models to recommend DB configurations.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the closed-loop autotuning pattern for IronCache's background advisor (tune maxmemory-samples, LFU decay, slab/encoding/compression thresholds), but reject heavy RL training online; prefer cheap Bayesian/bandit optimization with safe rollback and bounded knob ranges.

mechanisms.name: LLM-agent prior-art mining + adversarial claim verification
mechanisms.what_it_does: Agent pipeline fans out web/source searches, extracts claims pinned to primary sources/versions, runs an adversarial verifier that tries to falsify each number, and auto-generates benchmark/trace replays to reproduce claimed gains before a mechanism is accepted.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the credible, safe 'AI' for IronCache: it raises engineering rigor without putting a model on the data path. Guardrails: deterministic trace replay, human merge gate, every claim carries source_url + version + confidence.

ironcache_implications: Hot path stays ML-free and deterministic. Default eviction = a SIEVE/S3-FIFO-style FIFO-with-reinsertion structure (lazy promotion, quick demotion) to avoid the per-hit relink contention that the ValueTools'24 queueing analysis shows can make higher hit ratio LOWER throughput. No neural inference per request, ever.

Default admission = W-TinyLFU (4-bit Count-Min Sketch, ~8 bytes/entry, periodic aging). This is the non-ML SOTA floor; ship it before any ML and require any advisor change to beat it on replayed traces.

Ship a real eviction default, not Redis's noeviction. Pick an allkeys policy by default (likely SIEVE+W-TinyLFU admission) since IronCache is positioned as a cache, while still supporting a noeviction mode for store-of-record users.

ML lives only in an off-hot-path 'advisor' thread. It observes counters (hit ratio, miss latency, scan/churn signals, eviction age) and retunes a small set of bounded, deterministic knobs: active policy choice, sample count (Redis-style), LFU log-factor/decay, slab/encoding thresholds, and per-value compression decisions. Hot path reads a published config snapshot atomically; no per-request model calls.

Advisor controller should be a LeCaR/CACHEUS-style expert-selection mechanism (regret minimization or a contextual bandit over a handful of cheap policies), per shard, with ghost-list accounting. This gives adaptivity with O(1) cost and no GPU/heavy-model dependency in the single static binary.

Per-value compression decision is a natural, low-risk first ML target: a cheap learned/heuristic classifier off the hot path decides whether a value class is worth compressing (and with which codec/level), set as a threshold the hot path applies deterministically. Measure CPU-per-byte vs memory-saved, not just ratio.

Treat learned-Belady (LRB GBT, Parrot NN) strictly as OFFLINE ceilings in the benchmark harness to quantify how much hit-ratio headroom cheap policies leave; do not ship them on the path. This keeps the 'AI' honest and measurable.

Optimize the right metric, per Baleen: the advisor's objective should be throughput-per-core and memory efficiency (and, for any disk tier, backend load/TCO), NOT raw hit ratio in isolation — directly aligned with IronCache's stated goal.

Build the AI-assisted development harness as first-class project infra: every prior-art claim pinned to a primary source + upstream version + confidence; an adversarial verifier agent that tries to falsify each number; auto-generated benchmark replays on public traces (Wikipedia, MSR Cambridge, Twitter Cluster, SocialNetwork) gating acceptance; humans gate merges. No agent-proposed mechanism enters a design doc without a reproduced measurement.

Safety guardrails for the advisor: bounded knob ranges, hysteresis/cooldown to prevent oscillation, automatic rollback on throughput/hit-ratio regression, and a hard kill-switch to a known-good static config — the cache must be fully correct and fast with the advisor disabled.

research_papers.title: Learning Relaxed Belady for Content Distribution Network Caching
research_papers.key_idea: A gradient-boosted decision tree approximates Belady by predicting whether an object's next access falls beyond a 'Belady boundary'; engineered for lightweight training/inference and bounded memory, deployed in an Apache Traffic Server prototype.
research_papers.relevance: Canonical 'learned eviction that actually shipped a prototype'; the 4-25% WAN reduction shows the ceiling, and its engineering of off-path training informs IronCache's advisor design.

research_papers.title: Driving Cache Replacement with ML-based LeCaR
research_papers.key_idea: Online regret minimization over two experts (LRU, LFU) with ghost lists; beats ARC by up to 18x at small cache/working-set ratios.
research_papers.relevance: The minimal, O(1), no-neural-net advisor primitive IronCache should adopt for background policy selection.

research_papers.title: Learning Cache Replacement with CACHEUS
research_papers.key_idea: Adaptive mixture of experts (SR-LRU scan-resistant, CR-LFU churn-resistant) selected per workload primitive (LFU/LRU/scan/churn) across 17,766 experiments.
research_papers.relevance: Provides the workload-primitive taxonomy and adaptivity model for IronCache's advisor feature space.

research_papers.title: An Imitation Learning Approach for Cache Replacement (Parrot)
research_papers.key_idea: Attention-based neural net imitates Belady from past accesses; ~20% miss-rate reduction on SPEC, ~61% hit-rate increase over LRU on web search.
research_papers.relevance: Defines the hit-ratio ceiling and demonstrates exactly why per-access neural inference is NOT hot-path-deployable — the boundary IronCache must respect.

research_papers.title: Baleen: ML Admission & Prefetching for Flash Caches
research_papers.key_idea: 'Episodes' residency model for training; ML admission + prefetch optimized for disk-head-time/TCO, ~12% peak disk-head time and ~17% TCO reduction.
research_papers.relevance: Teaches 'optimize the real cost metric, not hit ratio' and an offline training-data construction method relevant to any IronCache disk tier and the advisor objective.

research_papers.title: TinyLFU: A Highly Efficient Cache Admission Policy
research_papers.key_idea: Approximate-LFU admission via a compact aging Count-Min Sketch (4-bit counters), with a doorkeeper to filter one-hit-wonders; W-TinyLFU adds a small LRU window.
research_papers.relevance: The non-ML SOTA admission policy and the deterministic baseline IronCache should ship and benchmark every ML idea against.

research_papers.title: SIEVE is Simpler than LRU: an Efficient Turn-Key Eviction Algorithm for Web Caches
research_papers.key_idea: FIFO queue with a skipping hand (lazy promotion / quick demotion) avoids per-hit relinking; ~21% mean FIFO miss-ratio reduction with better thread scalability, <20 LOC integration.
research_papers.relevance: Strong default hot-path eviction structure that directly serves throughput-per-core and sidesteps the LRU relink bottleneck.

research_papers.title: Can Increasing the Hit Ratio Hurt Cache Throughput?
research_papers.key_idea: Queueing model + measurement show LRU/SLRU per-hit operations are a contended bottleneck, so higher hit ratio can reduce throughput; FIFO does not have this pathology.
research_papers.relevance: The load-bearing argument for keeping the hot path cheap and FIFO-like and for measuring throughput, not just hit ratio — the core tension in IronCache's design.

research_papers.title: An End-to-End Automatic Cloud Database Tuning System using Deep RL (CDBTune)
research_papers.key_idea: DDPG reinforcement-learning agent tunes DB knobs from internal-metric state with a throughput/latency reward, fine-tuned online.
research_papers.relevance: Reference architecture for IronCache's background knob autotuner; informs the choice of cheaper bandit/Bayesian methods over full online RL.

open_questions: What is the measured marginal hit-ratio gain of a LeCaR/CACHEUS-style advisor OVER a well-tuned W-TinyLFU + SIEVE baseline on in-memory KV traces (Twitter Cluster, Meta), and is that gain large enough to justify the advisor's complexity and the risk of mis-tuning?

Can the advisor retune knobs frequently enough to track workload shifts without causing throughput oscillation, and what hysteresis/cooldown and rollback policy guarantees no regression below the static baseline?

What is the actual per-request budget on the hot path (ns) at IronCache's target throughput-per-core, and does even a single sketch lookup + branch for admission fit within it across value-size distributions?

For per-value compression decisions, can a cheap offline-trained classifier beat a simple size/entropy heuristic enough to matter, and how is the decision encoded so the hot path stays branch-predictable?

How do we construct a trustworthy benchmark/trace corpus (which public traces, what mix) so that adversarially verified claims actually generalize to IronCache's target deployments rather than overfitting to CDN/flash traces?

What are the determinism and reproducibility requirements for the advisor (seeded, snapshot-versioned config) so that the cache's behavior is debuggable and a given input replay yields identical eviction decisions?

Where exactly is the human-in-the-loop merge gate in the AI-assisted-development pipeline, and what is the minimum reproduction bar (re-run on N traces, verifier sign-off) before an agent-proposed mechanism can enter the design tree?

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Hot path is ML-free and deterministic; ML only off-path as a knob advisor
proposed_issues.summary: Adopt the principle that no per-request ML inference runs on the data path. ML is confined to a background advisor that retunes bounded, deterministic knobs and selects among cheap policies. Capture rationale (throughput-per-core, determinism, single-binary, the hit-ratio-vs-throughput tradeoff).

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Quantify advisor headroom over a tuned W-TinyLFU + SIEVE baseline on in-memory KV traces
proposed_issues.summary: Replay public traces (Twitter Cluster, Meta/CacheLib, MSR, Wikipedia) to measure how much hit ratio a LeCaR/CACHEUS-style advisor adds over a well-tuned non-ML baseline, to decide whether the advisor is worth building.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Background advisor architecture (expert selection + bounded knob autotuning)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a per-shard advisor thread: LeCaR/contextual-bandit expert selection over {SIEVE, W-TinyLFU-admission, sampled-LRU/LFU}, plus CDBTune/OtterTune-style tuning of sample count, LFU log-factor/decay, slab/encoding/compression thresholds, with atomic config snapshots consumed by the hot path.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: W-TinyLFU admission filter as the default non-ML baseline
proposed_issues.summary: Design the default admission policy: aging 4-bit Count-Min Sketch (~8 bytes/entry) + small LRU window + doorkeeper, as the deterministic SOTA floor every ML idea must beat before acceptance.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: SIEVE/S3-FIFO-style hot-path eviction structure
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a FIFO-with-reinsertion eviction core (lazy promotion / quick demotion) that avoids per-hit list relinking and scales across threads, addressing the documented LRU throughput pathology.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Off-path per-value compression decision model
proposed_issues.summary: Design a cheap classifier (heuristic or offline-trained) that decides whether/how to compress a value class, emitting a deterministic threshold the hot path applies; objective is CPU-per-byte vs memory-saved, not ratio alone.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Reject per-request neural inference (Parrot-class) on the data path
proposed_issues.summary: Record as an explicit non-goal that learned-Belady neural policies run on the hot path; they are permitted only as offline oracles/ceilings in the benchmark harness.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Build the offline learned-Belady oracle/ceiling into the benchmark harness
proposed_issues.summary: Implement Belady-MIN and an LRB-style GBT oracle in the simulator to quantify the hit-ratio headroom cheap policies leave on each trace, without any production-path dependency.

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: AI-assisted development pipeline with adversarial claim verification
proposed_issues.summary: Stand up the LLM/agent pipeline that mines prior art, proposes mechanisms, auto-generates benchmark replays, and runs an adversarial verifier on every numeric claim, with a human merge gate. This is core project infrastructure, not a cache feature.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Adversarial claim-verification protocol (source-pinning, versioning, reproduction bar)
proposed_issues.summary: Define the verification contract: every claim carries source_url + upstream version + confidence; a verifier agent attempts falsification; mechanisms require reproduced measurements on N traces before entering the design tree.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Advisor safety guardrails (bounded knobs, hysteresis, rollback, kill-switch)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify safety: bounded knob ranges, oscillation hysteresis/cooldown, automatic rollback on regression, deterministic seeded/snapshot-versioned config, and a hard kill-switch to a known-good static configuration.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Define the advisor objective metric (throughput-per-core/memory, not raw hit ratio)
proposed_issues.summary: Following Baleen's 'optimize the real cost metric' lesson and the hit-ratio-vs-throughput result, define and validate the advisor's reward/objective so it does not chase hit ratio at the expense of IronCache's stated throughput-per-core and memory-efficiency goals.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Adversarial re-verification on 2026-06-13 using fresh primary sources. Methods: Redis defaults read directly from redis/redis@unstable via gh API (USENIX/MLR PDFs were downloaded via curl with a browser UA after WebFetch hit HTTP 403, then text-extracted with pdftotext; LeCaR and the ValueTools paper PDFs were similarly fetched). Each claim cross-checked against at least the cited primary source plus, where relevant, source code (Redis redis.conf + version.h/release tags; Caffeine FrequencySketch.java) or a second source (WebSearch for the SIEVE award).

8 of 9 claims CONFIRMED, several with confidence upgraded from medium to high because I read the exact primary text (LeCaR '>18x', Parrot 13 SPEC/61%, the ValueTools FIFO-monotonic vs LRU/SLRU result).

1 claim CORRECTED: [wtinylfu-caffeine-sketch]. The 4-bit Count-Min Sketch, ~8 bytes/entry, periodic aging, and 'near-optimal, competitive with ARC/LIRS' are all confirmed (the periodic-aging/reset is real, documented in FrequencySketch.java and the TinyLFU TOS 2017 paper). BUT the '~1% admission window' is no longer accurate for Caffeine - the current wiki states the window is adaptively sized via hill-climbing, not fixed at 1%. The fixed ~1% is a detail of the original academic W-TinyLFU paper; the claim conflates paper and implementation.

Two minor characterization notes that did not rise to a verdict change: (a) SIEVE won the NSDI'24 Community Award (best paper whose code/datasets are public) rather than a generic 'best paper'; (b) the ValueTools claim's 'delinkT' is a slight rendering of the paper's 'delink'/Sdelink term. Both claims still confirmed.

Two original-source URLs (the two USENIX PDFs and MLR PDF) block automated WebFetch with HTTP 403; they are valid and accessible via direct download with a normal browser user-agent, so the cited URLs are sound. No claim was rubber-stamped without reading primary text or code.

dimensions.summary:
The credibility problem for IronCache is twofold: proving it is "the most efficient cache in the world" without committing the classic benchmark-war sins, and proving Redis-wire correctness rigorously enough that operators trust a from-scratch reimplementation. On the benchmarking side, the standard tools are memtier_benchmark (the Redis-maintained generator), redis-benchmark/valkey-benchmark (simpler, single-process), and YCSB (cross-store, academically cited). memtier defaults are non-trivial and load-bearing for honesty: SET:GET ratio 1:10, pipeline=1, data-size=32B, key-pattern R:R (uniform random), 4 threads x 50 clients x 10000 requests, percentiles 50/99/99.9. Crucially, memtier DOES support Zipfian (key-pattern Z with --key-zipf-exp, default 1) and Gaussian (G, default stddev = range/6) distributions — a fact widely misreported — so realistic skew is achievable without YCSB. redis-benchmark's notorious trap is that without -r it hammers a single key, which masks cache-miss and contention behavior; this is the canonical example of a benchmark that flatters an in-memory store.

The published vendor numbers are a minefield of methodology asymmetries. Dragonfly's headline "25x faster than Redis" is measured single-instance on a c6gn.16xlarge with memtier configured to give Dragonfly 64 threads but Redis only 2 threads — comparing a multi-threaded store against a deliberately single-threaded one, and never against a Redis Cluster (Redis's actual horizontal-scaling story). Dragonfly's "30% less memory / 3x lower peak during snapshot" comes from io_uring async snapshotting vs Redis's fork()-based BGSAVE memory spike — a real architectural win but measured under update traffic during BGSAVE specifically. Valkey's 8.x numbers (e.g. ~1.19M RPS on c7g.4xlarge with I/O threads, vs ~360K for 7.2; ~678K SET with 6 io-threads vs 239K without) are more transparent but still depend heavily on io-threads, host vs Docker-NAT networking, and pinning. The deepest methodological landmine is coordinated omission (Gil Tene): closed-loop generators like default redis-benchmark/memtier under-report tail latency because when the server stalls, the client stops issuing requests, so the stall's victims are never timed. Open-loop / constant-rate generation (wrk2 style) plus HdrHistogram with expectedInterval correction is the honest fix. The Valkey perf team's own methodology stresses bare-metal, taskset pinning, loopback to remove the network as the bottleneck, and isolating CPU — the right primitives for a throughput-per-core metric.

For IronCache the efficiency thesis ("max throughput per core, minimal memory, smart compression") demands metrics that vendors usually avoid: throughput-per-core (not just aggregate RPS on a 64-core box), memory-at-fixed-hit-ratio (RSS to sustain a target hit ratio under a Zipfian working set — directly tests compression and overhead-per-key), and tail latency p99/p999/p9999 under open-loop load at a fixed offered rate. A defensible benchmark harness must publish: the exact tool+flags, hardware, pinning, warmup, distribution and zipf exponent, pipeline depth (and report pipelined vs non-pipelined separately because pipelining inflates RPS by amortizing syscalls), hit ratio, and CO-corrected histograms. Comparisons must be apples-to-apples on thread count, or explicitly framed as "per-core" to neutralize the Dragonfly-style thread asymmetry.

On correctness, the field has matured well past "run the unit tests." The Redis/Valkey TCL suite (tests/unit/, tests/integration/) is the de-facto conformance oracle, with a precise assertion vocabulary — assert_equal, assert_error, assert_encoding (checks OBJECT ENCODING: listpack/intset/quicklist/hashtable/skiplist/embstr/int), assert_type, assert_replication_stream. IronCache should run a per-command conformance suite either by porting these tests or by differential testing against a real redis-server (send identical command streams, diff RESP replies and OBJECT ENCODING). Distributed correctness is best proven Jepsen-style: the 2020 Redis-Raft analysis found 21 issues including split-brain, lost updates, stale reads, and total data loss on failover, using the Elle checker (black-box, cycle-detection over Adya dependency graphs, list-append workload, near-linear in history length and constant in concurrency — unlike Knossos which blows up exponentially). Elle detects G0/G1a/G1b/G1c/G-single/G2 and lost update. The most powerful modern technique is Deterministic Simulation Testing (FoundationDB Flow, TigerBeetle VOPR, now commercially via Antithesis): run the entire system single-threaded over simulated time/network/disk/RNG driven by one seed, inject partitions/crashes/clock-skew, check invariants, and replay any failure exactly from its seed. This is a strong fit for a green-field Rust codebase if concurrency and I/O are abstracted behind a deterministic runtime from day one.

The practical correctness gate stack for IronCache: (1) coverage-guided fuzzing of the RESP2/RESP3 parser (cargo-fuzz/libFuzzer or AFL++) — the parser is the attack surface and a frequent CVE source; (2) property-based tests (proptest/quickcheck/bolero) for data-structure invariants and a model-based oracle comparing IronCache against an in-memory reference model of each type; (3) a per-command conformance suite as a CI gate, ideally differential against pinned redis-server versions; (4) a DST harness as the architecture matures, with seeds archived in CI; (5) Jepsen+Elle for any clustering/replication mode before it ships. The cross-cutting decision is that determinism must be designed in early (single-threaded-per-shard or an abstracted async runtime), because retrofitting DST onto an arbitrary-threaded codebase is the expensive failure mode every DST adopter warns about.

prior_art_claims.claim: Default SET:GET ratio is 1:10
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from upstream man page source and corroborated by README

verification.note: Man page line: '--ratio ... Set:Get ratio (default: 1:10)'. Independently corroborated in source: memtier_benchmark.cpp config_init_defaults sets cfg->ratio = config_ratio("1:10").

prior_art_claims.claim: Default pipeline depth is 1 (no pipelining unless --pipeline set)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from upstream man page; pipelining honesty is central to the dimension

verification.note: Man page: '--pipeline ... Number of concurrent pipelined requests (default: 1)'. Source confirms: if (!cfg->pipeline) cfg->pipeline = 1.

prior_art_claims.claim: Default value/data size is 32 bytes
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from upstream man page

prior_art_claims.claim: Default key access pattern is R:R (uniform random for both SET and GET)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from upstream man page; uniform-random default is itself a methodology pitfall vs realistic skew

verification.corrected_value: R:R (uniform random)
verification.note: Man page: '--key-pattern ... Set:Get pattern (default: R:R)' with 'R for uniform Random'. Source confirms: if (!cfg->key_pattern) cfg->key_pattern = "R:R".

prior_art_claims.claim: Key-pattern supports Z (Zipf), G (Gaussian), R (uniform random), S (sequential), P (parallel sequential subset); Zipf exponent set by --key-zipf-exp (default 1, valid range (0,5))
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Quoted verbatim from man page source via grep; contradicts secondary sources that claim no Zipf support

verification.corrected_value: Z/G/R/S/P; --key-zipf-exp default 1
verification.note: Man page lists exactly: G=Gaussian, R=uniform Random, Z=zipf (limits to positive), S=Sequential, P=Parallel (sequential subset per client). --key-zipf-exp 'limit to (0, 5)' and '(default is 1, though any number >2 seems insane)'. All five letters and the default=1 / range (0,5) match verbatim.

prior_art_claims.claim: Gaussian access default stddev is key-range/6 and default median is the center of the key range
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Quoted from man page via grep

prior_art_claims.claim: Default printed latency percentiles are p50, p99, p99.9
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from man page; relevant because p9999 is NOT printed by default

verification.note: Man page --print-percentiles: 'by default prints percentiles: 50,99,99.9'. Source confirms config_quantiles("50,99,99.9").

prior_art_claims.claim: Defaults: 4 threads, 50 clients per thread, 10000 requests per client
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from man page

prior_art_claims.claim: Default key range is key-minimum=0 to key-maximum=10000000
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: From WebFetch summary of man page, not re-grepped verbatim

verification.note: Man page: '--key-minimum ... (default: 0)' and '--key-maximum ... (default: 10000000)'. Source confirms key_maximum=10000000 default. Upgrades cited 'medium' confidence to high; value is correct.

prior_art_claims.claim: By default redis-benchmark runs against a single key; -r N is required to randomize keys across a keyspace of size N
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated explicitly in official Redis benchmark docs

verification.corrected_value: single key unless -r used
verification.note: Redis docs state verbatim 'By default the benchmark runs against a single key' and that -r <keyspacelen> uses random keys (expands __rand_int__ to a number in [0, keyspacelen-1]) for SET/GET/INCR. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.version: Cooper et al. SoCC 2010 / current wiki
prior_art_claims.claim: Core workloads: A=50/50 read/update, B=95/5 read/update, C=100% read, D=read-latest (95% read newest + 5% insert), E=95% scan + 5% insert, F=read-modify-write
prior_art_claims.value: A 50/50; B 95/5; C 100R; D read-latest; E scan; F RMW
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Confirmed from YCSB wiki; widely cited

verification.corrected_value: A 50/50; B 95/5; C 100R; D read-latest (95R/5insert); E scan (95scan/5insert); F RMW (50R/50RMW)
verification.note: Verified against the authoritative workload property files (not just the wiki prose): A read=.5/update=.5; B read=.95/update=.05; C read=1; D read=.95/insert=.05 requestdistribution=latest; E scan=.95/insert=.05; F read=.5/readmodifywrite=.5. All ratios and descriptors match the claim exactly.

prior_art_claims.claim: Default Zipfian distribution constant is 0.99
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: From YCSB issue discussion citing source default; not re-read from ZipfianGenerator.java directly

verification.note: Code-anchored: ZipfianGenerator.java line 43 'public static final double ZIPFIAN_CONSTANT = 0.99;'. Better primary source than the cited issue #685 (a question thread). Confidence upgraded from medium to high; value correct.

prior_art_claims.version: dragonflydb.io blog 'Redis vs Dragonfly Scalability and Performance' (accessed 2026-06-13)
prior_art_claims.claim: The '25x faster than Redis' throughput claim uses memtier with 64 threads for Dragonfly but only 2 threads for Redis, single-instance, on c6gn.16xlarge, 10M keys, 256B values, 30 clients/thread — never vs a Redis Cluster
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Thread counts and instance read directly from Dragonfly's own benchmark blog

verification.corrected_value: 25x; Dragonfly 64 threads vs Redis 2 threads
verification.note: Cited blog states 'Dragonfly throughput is 25X higher than Redis for both GET and SET' and shows the memtier cmd with '--threads=<2 for Redis, 64 for Dragonfly>'. Also confirms c6gn.16xlarge, 10M keys, 256B values, 30 clients/thread, single Redis instance (not a cluster). All specifics match.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dragonfly claims ~30% lower memory at idle and ~3x lower peak vs Redis during BGSAVE, attributed to io_uring async snapshotting vs Redis fork()-based BGSAVE memory spike; measured on r6gd.4xlarge with ~5GB data under update traffic
prior_art_claims.value: ~30% idle, up to 3x peak during snapshot
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from Dragonfly's own blog; vendor-reported, not independently reproduced

verification.corrected_value: ~30% lower idle and ~3x lower peak during snapshot are stated in the Dragonfly README (test: r6gd? + 'debug populate 5000000 key 1024' ~5GB + memtier update traffic + bgsave), NOT in the cited scaling blog. The cited scaling blog instead reports ~14% idle (Dragonfly 48 GiB vs Redis 56 GiB), Redis 'almost doubles' (~2x, not 3x) at snapshot peak, on r6gd.4xlarge with 'debug populate 100000000 key 500' (~50GB), with no memtier traffic during snapshot.
verification.note: Values (~30% idle, ~3x peak, ~5GB via 5M x 1024B, memtier update traffic) are accurate but the claim attributes them to the wrong source. They live in the README ('Dragonfly was 30% more memory efficient than Redis in the idle state ... At peak, Redis memory use increased to almost 3X that of Dragonfly'; 'debug populate 5000000 key 1024'). The cited scaling blog describes a DIFFERENT memory test with different numbers (~2x, ~14%, ~50GB). Corrected on source + setup conflation; the io_uring/fork() attribution is directionally right.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey 8.0 reached ~1.19M RPS on c7g.4xlarge (~230% over 7.2's ~360K RPS); separately SET throughput went 239K (no io-threads) to 678K with 6 io-threads, p99 1.68ms to 0.93ms
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Numbers from Valkey blog/community summaries; depend heavily on io-threads and instance, not re-derived

verification.corrected_value: ~1.19M RPS (8.0) vs ~360K (7.2) = ~230%, measured on c7g.16xlarge (NOT c7g.4xlarge); 239K->678K SET with 6 io-threads and p99 1.68ms->0.93ms. Both number sets are real but neither appears at the cited URL.
verification.note: The cited valkey.io perf-methodology blog contains NEITHER number set. The 1.19M/360K/230% figures are in valkey.io/blog/unlock-one-million-rps/ ('rising from 360K to 1.19M requests per second compared to Valkey 7.2'), and the stated test instance there is c7g.16xlarge, not c7g.4xlarge as the claim says (one secondary source mis-cited it as 4xlarge). The 239K->678K SET / p99 1.68->0.93ms figures are from the Momento 'Valkey Turns One' article (gomomento.com), reflecting a 6-io-threads SET benchmark. Corrected: values right, source wrong, plus the c7g.4xlarge instance detail is wrong (should be 16xlarge).

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey perf methodology mandates bare-metal (not VMs), taskset process pinning, loopback/local network to remove the NIC as bottleneck, and isolating changes to measure CPU impact (perf, VTune)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from Valkey methodology blog

verification.corrected_value: bare-metal + taskset + loopback + perf/VTune
verification.note: Cited blog states verbatim: bare-metal servers rather than VMs, process pinning using taskset, local network interfaces (loopback) for client-server communication, and using perf and Intel VTune Profiler to identify hot code paths. All four elements confirmed at the cited URL.

prior_art_claims.claim: Closed-loop generators (default redis-benchmark/memtier) under-report tail latency via coordinated omission; open-loop constant-rate generation (wrk2 -R) + HdrHistogram recordValues(value, expectedInterval) corrects it
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Well-documented by Tene; wrk2 README and HdrHistogram API confirm the mechanism

verification.corrected_value: closed-loop hides tails; -R + expectedInterval corrects
verification.note: wrk2 README describes coordinated omission (closed-loop generators coordinate with the server during high-latency periods, under-reporting tails) and corrects it via constant-throughput -R rate plus measuring latency from when transmission *should* have occurred, recorded losslessly in HdrHistogram. Example given: 99th pct 1.27s corrected vs 6.04ms uncorrected (~200x). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Jepsen found 21 issues in Redis-Raft including split-brain with lost updates, stale reads, aborted reads, infinite loops, crashes, and total data loss on failover/membership change; tested with Elle on 5-node clusters with partitions, pauses, clock skew, membership changes
prior_art_claims.value: 21 issues; split-brain, lost updates, total data loss on failover
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from the Jepsen analysis directly

verification.corrected_value: 21 issues; split-brain, lost updates, total data loss on failover
verification.note: Analysis states 'twenty-one distinct issues' including split-brain & lost updates (#17/#28/#44), stale reads (#19), aborted reads (#23), infinite loop (#13), multiple crashes, and total data loss on failover (#14). Tested on five-node clusters (LXC + EC2) with process pauses, crashes, network partitions, clock skew, membership changes, using Elle. Independently corroborated by the Jepsen blog post of the same date. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: WAIT does not make Redis strongly consistent overall; default async replication can lose acknowledged writes on failover
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Quoted from Jepsen analysis

verification.corrected_value: WAIT != strong consistency
verification.note: Jepsen analysis notes the WAIT command 'does not make the system strongly consistent overall' and quotes Redis docs that default async replication 'can lose acknowledged writes during some types of network failures' (small windows of acknowledged-write loss on failover). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Elle is a black-box transactional safety checker using cycle detection over Adya-style dependency graphs; detects G0, G1a (aborted read), G1b (intermediate read), G1c, G-single (read skew), G2; near-linear in history length and constant in concurrency (vs Knossos exponential); uses list-append workload
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from Elle README and VLDB paper summary

verification.corrected_value: detects G0/G1a/G1b/G1c/G-single/G2; ~linear
verification.note: Elle README confirms black-box checker using cycle detection over Adya-style dependency graphs, detecting G0 (write cycle), G1a (aborted read), G1b (intermediate read), G1c (cyclic info flow), G-single (read skew), G2 (anti-dependency cycle); '~linear in history length, and ~constant ... with respect to concurrency' vs Knossos exponential; uses list-append workload (elle.list-append). All elements confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: The conformance assertion vocabulary includes assert_equal, assert_error, assert_match, assert_range, assert_morethan, assert_type, assert_refcount, and assert_encoding (which runs OBJECT ENCODING key and matches against expected encoding e.g. listpack/intset/quicklist/hashtable/skiplist)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read assert_encoding/assert_type proc bodies directly from test.tcl

verification.corrected_value: assert_encoding -> OBJECT ENCODING match
verification.note: Verified in tests/support/test.tcl (valkey unstable): assert_match (L31), assert_equal (L55), assert_lessthan (L61), assert_morethan (L73), assert_range (L85), assert_error (L91), assert_encoding (L99: 'set val [r object encoding $key]; assert_match $enc $val'), assert_type (L107), assert_refcount (L111). All claimed procs present in the cited file; assert_encoding does run OBJECT ENCODING and match. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Deterministic Simulation Testing runs the system single-threaded over simulated time/network/disk/RNG so any bug is reproducible from a single seed; FDB uses Flow, TigerBeetle uses VOPR, Antithesis provides a deterministic hypervisor; requires designing out non-determinism early
prior_art_claims.value: single-thread + simulated I/O + one seed = replayable
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Consistent across TigerBeetle docs, FDB write-ups, and Antithesis docs

verification.corrected_value: single-thread + simulated I/O + one seed = replayable
verification.note: Cited deepwiki page confirms the core DST principle verbatim: 'All I/O and time are simulated, making bugs reproducible from a single seed', plus TimeSim, storage simulation, fault injection, seeded randomization. The cited page does NOT itself mention VOPR/FoundationDB-Flow/Antithesis, but those attributions are independently correct: TigerBeetle's docs/internals/vopr.md confirm VOPR is the deterministic simulator reproducible from seed + git commit, and multiple sources confirm TigerBeetle's DST was inspired by FoundationDB's Flow and Antithesis' deterministic hypervisor. Verdict confirmed; only caveat is the bundled FDB/VOPR/Antithesis names are not on the single cited URL.

mechanisms.name: memtier_benchmark as primary load generator
mechanisms.what_it_does: Multi-threaded, multi-client generator with configurable SET:GET ratio, pipeline depth, data-size distributions, key patterns (uniform/sequential/Gaussian/Zipf), arbitrary --command support, cluster mode, and HDR histogram output (--hdr-file-prefix).
mechanisms.rationale: It is the Redis-ecosystem standard, supports Zipfian (--key-zipf-exp) and Gaussian skew natively, emits HdrHistogram files for honest tails, and can drive arbitrary commands. IronCache should adopt it as the canonical generator so numbers are directly comparable to Redis/Valkey/Dragonfly published runs.

mechanisms.name: redis-benchmark default single-key workload
mechanisms.what_it_does: Simple generator that, without -r, repeatedly operates on one key, and reports throughput plus basic latency.
mechanisms.rationale: The single-key default produces inflated, unrealistic numbers (no contention/keyspace pressure) and it is closed-loop with weak tail reporting. Use it only as a smoke test, never as a headline benchmark; prefer memtier + open-loop tooling for published claims.

mechanisms.name: YCSB core workloads A-F with Zipfian
mechanisms.what_it_does: Standardized cross-store workloads (50/50, 95/5, read-only, read-latest, scan, RMW) with uniform/zipfian/latest distributions, default zipfian constant 0.99.
mechanisms.rationale: Valuable for cross-database, academically-citable comparisons and for the read-latest distribution. But YCSB's JVM client is a throughput bottleneck and lacks pipelining; use it for distribution definitions and cross-store credibility, not for peak single-node throughput-per-core claims.

mechanisms.name: Open-loop / constant-rate load + coordinated-omission correction
mechanisms.what_it_does: Issues requests at a fixed offered rate regardless of server progress and records latency into an HdrHistogram with expected-interval correction so stalls are attributed to all delayed requests.
mechanisms.rationale: Required to report honest p99/p999/p9999. Without it, IronCache (and every competitor) systematically hides tail latency. IronCache's benchmark gate must include open-loop runs at fixed rates with CO-corrected histograms; this is a credibility differentiator.

mechanisms.name: Throughput-per-core and memory-at-fixed-hit-ratio metrics
mechanisms.what_it_does: Bare-metal + taskset pinning + loopback to isolate CPU; measure aggregate RPS divided by cores, and RSS required to sustain a target hit ratio under a Zipfian working set.
mechanisms.rationale: These two metrics directly encode IronCache's thesis (max throughput per core, minimal memory, smart compression) and neutralize the Dragonfly-style thread-count asymmetry. They are the primary scoreboard, not aggregate RPS on a fat box.

mechanisms.name: Redis/Valkey TCL conformance suite + assert_encoding
mechanisms.what_it_does: Per-command TCL tests with assertions assert_equal/assert_error/assert_type/assert_encoding (validates OBJECT ENCODING transitions like listpack->quicklist, intset->hashtable) and assert_replication_stream.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the de-facto Redis behavior oracle. Port/adapt it (or run it against IronCache via the Valkey harness) as a CI conformance gate. Adapt rather than borrow verbatim because IronCache's internal encodings differ; encoding assertions should map to IronCache's own OBJECT ENCODING reporting or be marked as compatibility-shimmed.

mechanisms.name: Differential testing against pinned redis-server
mechanisms.what_it_does: Send identical fuzzed/random command streams to IronCache and a pinned real redis-server, diff RESP replies, error messages, and OBJECT ENCODING.
mechanisms.rationale: The strongest, lowest-effort way to prove wire/semantic compatibility for a reimplementation: the real server is the oracle. Catches subtle reply-shape and error-string mismatches that hand-written tests miss.

mechanisms.name: RESP2/RESP3 parser coverage-guided fuzzing
mechanisms.what_it_does: Feeds mutated byte streams into the protocol parser to find panics, OOB reads, integer overflow in bulk-length handling, and DoS via malformed multibulk.
mechanisms.rationale: The parser is the primary remote attack surface and a recurring CVE source in caches. In Rust this is cheap (cargo-fuzz) and catches the bugs unit tests cannot. Must be a CI fuzz-gate with a seed corpus from real RESP captures.

mechanisms.name: Property-based + model-based testing of data structures
mechanisms.what_it_does: Generates random operation sequences against each type (string/list/set/zset/hash/stream) and asserts invariants and equivalence to a simple in-memory reference model.
mechanisms.rationale: Property tests with a reference-model oracle catch encoding-transition and edge-case bugs (e.g. listpack overflow thresholds, ziplist->skiplist) far more thoroughly than examples. bolero can also run the same properties under libFuzzer for extended campaigns.

mechanisms.name: Deterministic Simulation Testing (Flow/VOPR/Antithesis style)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Runs the whole system single-threaded over simulated time/network/disk/RNG from one seed, injects partitions/crashes/clock-skew/disk-faults, checks invariants, and replays any failure exactly.
mechanisms.rationale: Gold standard for distributed correctness and the highest-leverage long-term investment, but it constrains architecture (must abstract I/O/time/concurrency behind a deterministic runtime). Adopt for multi-node IronCache, and design the runtime for determinism in M1 so it is not a retrofit. For single-node M2, a lighter deterministic test harness suffices.

mechanisms.name: Jepsen + Elle for clustering/replication
mechanisms.what_it_does: Black-box consistency testing: drives a real cluster under faults, collects client-observed histories, and uses Elle's cycle detection to find G0/G1a/G1b/G1c/G-single/G2 and lost-update anomalies.
mechanisms.rationale: Any IronCache clustering/replication mode must pass a Jepsen test before claiming a consistency level, given Redis-Raft itself shipped 21 issues including split-brain. Elle is near-linear and black-box, so it works without instrumenting IronCache internals.

ironcache_implications: Publish a frozen, scripted benchmark harness (exact memtier flags, hardware, kernel, pinning, warmup, distribution + zipf exponent, pipeline depth, hit ratio) so every claim is reproducible; treat unreproducible vendor numbers as the anti-pattern to avoid.

Make throughput-per-core and memory-to-sustain-a-target-hit-ratio (under a Zipfian working set) the headline metrics, not aggregate RPS on a high-core box — this directly encodes the efficiency thesis and immunizes IronCache against Dragonfly-style thread-count comparisons.

Always report tail latency p99/p999/p9999 from open-loop, constant-rate load with HdrHistogram coordinated-omission correction; never headline a closed-loop p99. Ship CO-corrected histograms as build artifacts.

Report pipelined and non-pipelined throughput as separate, explicitly-labeled numbers; pipelining amortizes syscalls and can inflate RPS by an order of magnitude, so mixing them is a benchmark-war trap.
When comparing to Redis/Valkey/Dragonfly, fix thread counts symmetrically OR frame everything per-core, and always include a Redis Cluster comparison for any multi-node claim (since vendors omit it).

Adopt the Redis/Valkey TCL conformance vocabulary as a CI gate: run a per-command suite and, where encodings differ, expose IronCache's own OBJECT ENCODING so assert_encoding-style checks remain meaningful; supplement with differential testing against pinned redis-server versions.
Stand up parser fuzzing (cargo-fuzz on the RESP2/RESP3 decoder) and property/model-based tests for every data type from the first prototype; seed the fuzz corpus with real RESP captures.

Decide the determinism stance in M1: abstract time/network/disk/RNG behind a runtime so Deterministic Simulation Testing is feasible later without a rewrite; archive failing seeds in CI for exact replay.

Gate any clustering/replication mode behind a passing Jepsen+Elle run before publicly claiming a consistency level (e.g. linearizable, causal), and document the exact failure modes (split-brain, lost writes on failover) that the mode does and does not tolerate.

Define explicit non-goals: e.g. IronCache will not claim a consistency guarantee it has not Jepsen-tested, and will not publish a headline throughput number without a CO-corrected tail-latency companion and per-core normalization.

research_papers.title: Benchmarking Cloud Serving Systems with YCSB
research_papers.key_idea: Defines the YCSB framework: core workloads A-F, request distributions (uniform, zipfian default ~0.99, latest), and a tier-1 (performance) / tier-2 (scaling) methodology for cross-store comparison.
research_papers.relevance: Canonical reference for realistic key-distribution and read/write mixes; IronCache should cite it for distribution definitions and cross-store credibility.

research_papers.title: Elle: Inferring Isolation Anomalies from Experimental Observations
research_papers.key_idea: Black-box transactional safety checker using cycle detection over Adya dependency graphs; detects G0/G1a/G1b/G1c/G-single/G2 in near-linear time using a list-append workload, vastly outscaling Knossos.
research_papers.relevance: The checker IronCache's clustering tests should use; defines the precise anomaly taxonomy any consistency claim must rule out.

research_papers.title: Jepsen: Redis-Raft 1b3fbf6
research_papers.key_idea: Empirical analysis finding 21 issues (split-brain, lost updates, stale/aborted reads, total data loss on failover) in a Raft-based Redis, and clarifying that WAIT does not give strong consistency.
research_papers.relevance: Direct evidence that Redis-lineage clustering is hard to get right; sets the bar IronCache's clustering must clear before any consistency claim.

research_papers.title: FoundationDB: A Distributed Unbundled Transactional Key Value Store
research_papers.key_idea: Describes Flow, the deterministic single-threaded actor runtime enabling whole-system simulation testing where any failure is reproducible from one seed across simulated network/disk/clock faults.
research_papers.relevance: The foundational case for Deterministic Simulation Testing and why determinism must be an architectural decision, not a bolt-on, for IronCache.

research_papers.title: How NOT to Measure Latency (Coordinated Omission)
research_papers.key_idea: Closed-loop load generators systematically omit the latency of requests delayed by server stalls; constant-rate open-loop generation plus HdrHistogram expected-interval correction restores honest tail percentiles.
research_papers.relevance: The single most important methodology fix for IronCache to report credible p99/p999/p9999 instead of flattering closed-loop numbers.

open_questions: Single-threaded-per-shard vs an abstracted async runtime: which concurrency model best supports both max throughput-per-core AND Deterministic Simulation Testing without forcing a rewrite later?

What exactly is IronCache's compatibility contract — bit-identical RESP replies and error strings, or behavioral equivalence only? This decides whether differential testing must match error message text verbatim.

Should IronCache expose Redis-style OBJECT ENCODING values (listpack/quicklist/etc.) for conformance compatibility even if its internal representations differ, or report its own encodings and shim the test suite?
Which Redis/Valkey versions to pin as the conformance/differential oracle, and how to track behavioral drift as upstream evolves (Redis vs Valkey divergence post-fork)?
What is the canonical published hardware/config matrix for headline benchmarks (instance types, core counts, pinning, kernel/io_uring settings) so results stay comparable over time?
For memory-at-fixed-hit-ratio, what reference working-set generator and zipf exponent define the 'standard' IronCache memory benchmark, and how is hit ratio held constant across implementations?
Do we build our own deterministic simulator (Flow/VOPR-style) or pay for Antithesis, and at what milestone does the cost/benefit cross over?
Which fuzzing harness (cargo-fuzz/libFuzzer vs AFL++ vs bolero) and what RESP3 push/out-of-band coverage is needed beyond the request parser (e.g. inline commands, RESP3 attributes, pub/sub frames)?

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: Benchmarking & correctness gates for IronCache
proposed_issues.summary: Umbrella tracking the reproducible benchmark harness, the efficiency metrics (throughput-per-core, memory-at-fixed-hit-ratio, CO-corrected tail latency), and the correctness stack (conformance, fuzzing, property tests, DST, Jepsen). Defines what 'most efficient' and 'correct' mean for IronCache claims.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Adopt memtier_benchmark as canonical load generator; redis-benchmark smoke-test only
proposed_issues.summary: Standardize on memtier_benchmark (Zipf via --key-zipf-exp, HDR output via --hdr-file-prefix) for all published numbers; redis-benchmark relegated to smoke tests because its default single-key, closed-loop behavior is misleading.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Reproducible benchmark harness spec (flags, hardware, pinning, warmup, distribution)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a scripted harness pinning exact memtier flags, instance/kernel, taskset pinning, loopback isolation, warmup, zipf exponent, pipeline depth, and hit ratio, emitting machine-readable results plus HdrHistogram artifacts for every run.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Open-loop constant-rate latency harness with coordinated-omission correction
proposed_issues.summary: Add a wrk2-style fixed-rate generator path (or memtier + HDR expected-interval correction) so p99/p999/p9999 are reported honestly under load; closed-loop p99 is banned from headline claims.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Headline metrics are throughput-per-core and memory-at-fixed-hit-ratio
proposed_issues.summary: Define the primary scoreboard as per-core throughput and RSS needed to sustain a target hit ratio under a Zipfian working set, with symmetric thread counts in all comparisons, to neutralize thread-asymmetry and fat-box inflation.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Audit published Dragonfly/KeyDB/Valkey/Redis numbers and catalog methodology traps
proposed_issues.summary: Document each vendor's exact methodology (Dragonfly 64-vs-2-thread single-instance 25x, Valkey io-threads RPS, snapshot memory) and the traps (CO, single key, pipelining, no-cluster comparison) so IronCache's claims avoid them.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Per-command RESP conformance suite as CI gate
proposed_issues.summary: Adapt the Redis/Valkey TCL test vocabulary (assert_equal/assert_error/assert_type/assert_encoding) into a per-command conformance suite that gates merges, mapping encoding assertions to IronCache's OBJECT ENCODING reporting.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Differential testing against pinned redis-server
proposed_issues.summary: Build a harness that replays identical (including fuzzed) command streams to IronCache and pinned redis-server versions and diffs RESP replies, error strings, and encodings to prove wire/semantic compatibility.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Coverage-guided fuzzing of the RESP2/RESP3 parser
proposed_issues.summary: Stand up cargo-fuzz/libFuzzer (and optionally AFL++) targets on the protocol decoder with a seed corpus of real RESP captures; make it a CI fuzz-gate covering multibulk length overflow, inline commands, and RESP3 frames.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Property-based + model-based tests for every data type
proposed_issues.summary: Use proptest/bolero to generate random operation sequences per type and assert invariants and equivalence to an in-memory reference model, especially around encoding-transition thresholds (listpack/intset/quicklist/skiplist).

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Design the runtime for determinism to enable DST
proposed_issues.summary: Abstract time, network, disk, and RNG behind a runtime so Deterministic Simulation Testing (Flow/VOPR/Antithesis style) is feasible later; commit to single-thread-per-shard or a deterministic async executor before code lands.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Build-vs-buy deterministic simulation testing (own simulator vs Antithesis)
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate building a Flow/VOPR-style simulator versus using Antithesis's deterministic hypervisor, including cost, integration effort, and what invariants/faults each can exercise.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Jepsen + Elle test plan for clustering/replication
proposed_issues.summary: Define a Jepsen suite (partitions, pauses, clock skew, membership changes) checked by Elle for any IronCache multi-node mode; passing it is a precondition for publicly stating a consistency level.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: No consistency or efficiency claim without its test/correction
proposed_issues.summary: IronCache will not advertise a consistency guarantee that has not passed Jepsen+Elle, nor a throughput headline lacking a coordinated-omission-corrected tail-latency companion and per-core normalization.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Method: For each claim I fetched the cited primary source plus at least one independent source, and for code-default claims I read the actual source on the default branch via raw.githubusercontent.com.

CONFIRMED (17): All six memtier defaults (man page + cross-checked against memtier_benchmark.cpp config_init_defaults), redis-benchmark single-key default, YCSB core workloads (verified against the actual workload property files, stronger than the wiki prose), YCSB zipfian 0.99 (code-anchored to ZipfianGenerator.java L43 — upgrade the cited issue #685 to the source file), Dragonfly 25x thread-asymmetry (every specific matches the cited scaling blog), Valkey methodology, coordinated omission (wrk2), Jepsen 21 issues, WAIT != strong consistency, Elle anomalies, valkey assert_encoding vocab (test.tcl L99), and the DST single-seed principle.

CORRECTED (2):
1) dragonfly-memory-snapshot-claim — the ~30% idle / ~3x peak / ~5GB-via-5M-keys / memtier-update-traffic figures are REAL but come from the Dragonfly README, NOT the cited scaling blog. The cited scaling blog describes a different memory test with materially different numbers: ~14% idle (48 vs 56 GiB), Redis 'almost doubles' (~2x, not 3x) at peak, on r6gd.4xlarge with 100M keys x 500B (~50GB), and no memtier traffic during snapshot. The claim conflated two Dragonfly benchmarks. Best source = README.
2) valkey-io-threads-throughput — the cited valkey.io perf-methodology blog contains NEITHER number set. The 1.19M/360K/230% numbers live at valkey.io/blog/unlock-one-million-rps/ (and the instance there is c7g.16xlarge, not c7g.4xlarge as claimed — an instance-size error). The 239K->678K SET / p99 1.68ms->0.93ms numbers come from Momento's 'Valkey Turns One' article. Values are accurate; source attribution and one instance detail are wrong.

Recurring failure mode: this record's two weakest items both attach correct-sounding numbers to the wrong URL. The numbers survive scrutiny but the citations don't — both were corrected on provenance (and Valkey additionally on the 4xlarge vs 16xlarge detail). The two corrections were the originally medium-confidence items, which is consistent. Note: 'today' is 2026-06-13 per environment; memtier master man page and valkey unstable test.tcl were read live at that point.

dimensions.summary:
IronCache's compression strategy should exploit a gap that Redis leaves wide open: Redis ships NO transparent value compression. The only compression in vanilla Redis is RDB persistence, which uses LZF (rdbcompression yes by default, "Compress string objects using LZF when dump .rdb databases?"). In-memory values are stored uncompressed; clients must compress before SET. This is by design (low CPU, predictable latency) but leaves 2-4x RAM on the table for compressible workloads — and third-party modules (apersson/redis-compression-module, VladRodionov/mcdc-redis) confirm 2x-4x RAM cuts and ratios up to 7.7x with zstd dictionaries on real data. IronCache, being efficiency-obsessed, should make threshold-gated transparent value compression a first-class, opt-in feature.

The algorithm landscape is well-characterized by the official lz4/zstd lzbench numbers on Silesia (Core i7-9700K @ 4.9GHz): LZ4 1.10.0 hits ratio 2.10 at ~675 MB/s compress / 3850 MB/s decompress; Snappy 1.2.1 ratio 2.09 at 520/1500; zstd 1.5.7 -1 ratio 2.90 at 510/1550; zstd --fast=3 ratio 2.24 at 635/1980; lzf 3.6 ratio 2.08 at 410/820. The clear conclusion for a cache: zstd at low/negative levels dominates the ratio-vs-speed frontier (40% better ratio than LZ4 at comparable-enough speed for an in-memory cache where decompression on the hot path is what matters most), while LZ4 wins only when decompression throughput is the single binding constraint. zstd's decompression speed is nearly level-independent, which is ideal — a cache compresses once on SET but decompresses on every GET. The pure-Rust lz4_flex crate (v0.12) is within ~10% of the C lz4 in safe mode (1272 vs 1469 MiB/s compress, 4540 vs 5313 MiB/s decompress on 66KB JSON) and supports zero-unsafe builds, which fits the single-static-binary, memory-safe goal. The zstd Rust crate (v0.13.3, bundling zstd C 1.5.7 via zstd-sys 2.0.16+zstd.1.5.7) is a C binding; a fully pure-Rust zstd compressor (KillingSpark/zstd-rs) exists but does not yet match speed/ratio/configurability.

The single biggest lever for a CACHE specifically is dictionary compression (ZDICT). Caches hold huge numbers of small, structurally-similar values (JSON session blobs, serialized protobufs, user records) — exactly the regime where standalone block compression fails because there's no window to build a dictionary from. Facebook's canonical result: 1000 GitHub-user records (~850 KB raw) compress to ~300 KB (2.8x) with plain gzip/zstd, but to 122 KB (6.9x) with a one-time pre-shared trained dictionary. ZDICT_trainFromBuffer (fastCover algorithm) with a default ~110 KB dictionary is the mechanism; RocksDB applies the same idea per-SST-file (max_dict_bytes, zstd_max_train_bytes). IronCache should train per-keyspace/per-prefix dictionaries from a sample of live values and attach a small dictionary-ID to each compressed value. This is the differentiator that turns "a cache with compression" into "the most memory-efficient cache."
