Beyond byte-stream compression, Redis demonstrates that compact in-memory ENCODINGS matter as much as compression: listpack (a contiguous, allocation-free serialization replacing ziplist) for small hashes/sets/zsets/lists, and intset for all-integer sets. Defaults: hash-max-listpack-entries 512 / -value 64; set-max-intset-entries 512; set-max-listpack-entries 128 / -value 64; zset-max-listpack-entries 128 / -value 64; list-max-listpack-size -2 (8 KB). These give large constant-factor RAM wins for small aggregates with zero CPU decompression cost and should be borrowed wholesale, adapted to Rust (a listpack analog plus an intset analog), as they compose with — and are cheaper than — value compression for small collections.

Design synthesis for IronCache: (1) ship a threshold-gated transparent value compressor, default OFF or with a conservative size threshold (spymemcached's BaseSerializingTranscoder uses a 16384-byte default compression threshold; EVCache compresses above a configurable size); (2) make zstd the default codec at a low level (e.g. -1 or --fast) with LZ4/lz4_flex as a "max-throughput" option and "none" always available; (3) invest heavily in ZDICT dictionary training as the headline memory feature, with per-prefix dictionaries and online sampling; (4) implement listpack/intset-style compact encodings for small aggregate types; (5) gate compression on a cheap entropy/compressibility probe to avoid wasting CPU on already-compressed or incompressible values (store an incompressible flag); (6) consider per-segment/per-slab compression as an alternative to per-value to amortize dictionary and framing overhead and improve ratio on tiny values. The CPU-vs-RAM tradeoff must be measured per workload and exposed as policy, not hardcoded.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis performs no transparent in-memory value compression; value compression is left to clients. The only built-in compression is RDB persistence, which uses LZF.
prior_art_claims.value: in-memory: none; RDB: LZF (rdbcompression yes by default)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from redis.conf; comment states 'Compress string objects using LZF when dump .rdb databases?'

verification.corrected_value: in-memory: none; RDB: LZF (rdbcompression yes by default)
verification.note: redis.conf 7.4.0 has rdbcompression yes with comment 'Compress string objects using LZF when dump .rdb databases'. No directive performs transparent in-memory value compression; the only relevant in-memory option is list-compress-depth (default 0 = off) which compresses interior quicklist nodes, not arbitrary values. Value compression is left to clients. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: rdbcompression defaults to yes and uses the LZF algorithm for RDB string compression.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Verbatim from redis.conf comment and default

verification.note: redis.conf 7.4.0: 'rdbcompression yes' with comment explicitly naming LZF for RDB string compression. Confirmed verbatim.

All listed feature flags (bindgen, pkg-config, zstdmt, experimental, zdict_builder, no_asm, legacy, thin, fat-lto) exist in zstd 0.13.3 Cargo.toml. Only the zstd-sys patch number is corrected.

prior_art_claims.claim: A transparent zstd-dictionary Redis module reports compression_ratio 7.70 (1.62 GB -> 211 MB) across 100,000 objects; default dictionary target size 100 KB; training leaks ~6 MB per op and blocks synchronously.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Third-party module README; numbers are author-reported on their own dataset

verification.corrected_value: ratio 7.70; default DICTSIZE 100 KB
verification.note: README confirmed verbatim: compress_compression_ratio 7.70; compress_uncompressed_size 1623962898 (~1.62 GB) -> compress_compressed_size 210842569 (~211 MB); compress_objects 100000; 'default target size for dictionaries is 100 KB ... DICTSIZE option'; 'Training a dictionary leaks about 6 MB of memory' and 'runs synchronously and can therefor block other operations'. All sub-claims confirmed; upgrades original 'medium' confidence.

prior_art_claims.claim: BaseSerializingTranscoder compresses stored values larger than a default 16384-byte threshold (GZIP); EVCache compresses above a configurable size threshold.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Javadoc returned 403 to fetch; value corroborated by multiple secondary sources and well-known spymemcached default

verification.corrected_value: default compression threshold = 16384 bytes (GZIP)
verification.note: Verified directly from source at tag 2.12.3: 'public static final int DEFAULT_COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD = 16384;' and 'protected int compressionThreshold = DEFAULT_COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD;'. Compression uses GZIPOutputStream/GZIPInputStream (java.util.zip). Values larger than the threshold are compressed. The javadoc URL returned HTTP 403 but the canonical GitHub source confirms. Confirmed; upgrades original 'medium' confidence. EVCache configurable-threshold claim is plausible but not independently re-verified here.

prior_art_claims.claim: RocksDB block-based SST blocks default to 4 KB, too small to build a dictionary; max_dict_bytes / zstd_max_train_bytes preset a per-SST dictionary sampled across blocks for better cross-block ratio.
prior_art_claims.value: block size 4 KB default; per-file trained dictionary via zstd_max_train_bytes
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: RocksDB wiki; confirms per-segment dictionary pattern, though exact ratio gains not published there

mechanisms.name: Transparent value compression (client-side in Redis)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Redis never compresses in-memory values; applications/clients compress before SET and decompress after GET. Only RDB persistence compresses (LZF).
mechanisms.rationale: Reject the 'push it to the client' philosophy but keep the safety instinct. IronCache should offer SERVER-SIDE transparent compression (opt-in, threshold-gated) because that is the headline efficiency win Redis leaves on the table, while keeping a 'none' mode for latency-critical users.

mechanisms.name: LZF for RDB string compression
mechanisms.what_it_does: Fast byte-stream LZ77 variant used to shrink RDB snapshots; ratio ~2.08 on Silesia.
mechanisms.rationale: LZF is dominated by both LZ4 (faster, similar ratio) and zstd (much better ratio at acceptable speed). No reason to adopt LZF for a greenfield Rust system; use zstd/LZ4 for persistence too.

mechanisms.name: listpack compact encoding
mechanisms.what_it_does: Contiguous, allocation-light serialization for small hashes/sets/zsets/lists (replacing ziplist), avoiding per-element object/pointer overhead below entry/value thresholds.
mechanisms.rationale: Huge constant-factor RAM savings for small aggregates with zero decompression CPU. A Rust listpack analog composes with value compression and is strictly cheaper for small collections. Borrow the design, reimplement safely in Rust.

mechanisms.name: intset compact encoding
mechanisms.what_it_does: Sorted packed array of integers (16/32/64-bit) for all-integer sets up to set-max-intset-entries (512), avoiding hashtable overhead.
mechanisms.rationale: Cheap, well-proven, ideal for ID sets common in caches. Borrow with a Rust implementation and the same auto-upgrade-to-hashtable threshold semantics.

mechanisms.name: zstd negative/low compression levels
mechanisms.what_it_does: --fast=N and level -1 trade ratio for speed; decompression speed is nearly level-independent (~1.5-2 GB/s).
mechanisms.rationale: Ideal cache default: a cache compresses once on write, decompresses on every read. zstd -1/--fast gives ~40% better ratio than LZ4 while keeping fast, level-independent decompression. Make zstd the default codec.

mechanisms.name: ZDICT dictionary training (fastCover)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Trains a small (~110 KB) dictionary from many small samples so each tiny value compresses against shared structure; lifts small-record ratio from 2.8x to ~6.9x.
mechanisms.rationale: This is THE differentiator for a cache full of small similar values. Train per-prefix/per-keyspace dictionaries online from sampled live values and tag each value with a dictionary-ID. Directly enables 'most memory-efficient cache' positioning.

mechanisms.name: zstd long-distance matching (--long, windowLog 27)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Large match window (default 128 MiB) finds repeats across far-apart data; decompressor must know the window.
mechanisms.rationale: Per-value cache entries are small and independent; a 128 MiB window provides no benefit per value and inflates decompressor memory. Reject for per-value path; possibly reconsider only for whole-snapshot persistence.

mechanisms.name: LZ4 / lz4_flex pure-Rust codec
mechanisms.what_it_does: Extremely fast LZ77 codec; lz4_flex is pure-Rust, no-unsafe by default, within ~10% of C lz4.
mechanisms.rationale: Offer LZ4 (via lz4_flex) as the 'max-throughput / minimal-CPU' codec option, not the default. Use the safe (no-unsafe) build to honor the memory-safe single-binary goal; allow unsafe build behind a feature flag for benchmarking.

mechanisms.name: Per-SST dictionary compression
mechanisms.what_it_does: Presets a dictionary trained by sampling data blocks across an SST file because 4 KB blocks are too small to self-train; configured via max_dict_bytes / zstd_max_train_bytes.
mechanisms.rationale: Validates the per-segment dictionary pattern. Adapt to IronCache by training dictionaries per memory segment/slab or per key-prefix rather than per persisted file, applying it to the in-memory hot set.

mechanisms.name: Threshold-gated compression (spymemcached/EVCache)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Only compress values above a size threshold (spymemcached default 16384 bytes; EVCache configurable), to avoid CPU cost where it doesn't pay.
mechanisms.rationale: Keep the threshold idea but make the gate smarter: combine a size floor with a cheap compressibility/entropy probe and store an 'incompressible' flag so already-compressed blobs aren't re-attempted. Default threshold likely lower than 16 KB given zstd dictionaries help small values.

ironcache_implications: Ship server-side transparent value compression as a first-class opt-in feature — this is the single biggest memory win Redis structurally refuses to provide (Redis compresses only RDB, with LZF).

Make zstd the default codec at a low/negative level (-1 or --fast) because a cache decompresses on every GET and zstd's decompression speed is nearly level-independent (~1.5 GB/s) while ratio beats LZ4/Snappy by ~40%.

Offer a codec policy enum per keyspace: none (latency-critical), lz4 via lz4_flex (max throughput), zstd (default, best ratio), zstd+dict (max memory savings). Expose as runtime config, not compile-time.

Invest in ZDICT dictionary training as the headline differentiator: per-prefix online-trained dictionaries can lift small-value ratio from ~2.8x to ~6.9x (Facebook GitHub-users result). Tag each compressed value with a dictionary-ID; version and hot-swap dictionaries safely.

Implement Redis-style compact in-memory encodings in safe Rust: a listpack analog (small hashes/sets/zsets/lists) and an intset analog (all-integer sets), with auto-upgrade thresholds. These give constant-factor RAM wins at zero decompression CPU and compose under value compression.

Gate compression with a size floor PLUS a cheap entropy/compressibility probe; persist an 'incompressible' flag so already-compressed/binary values are never re-attempted. Start threshold lower than spymemcached's 16384 bytes since dictionaries make small values worth compressing.

Use the pure-Rust lz4_flex (safe, no-unsafe default, within ~10% of C) to honor the memory-safe single-static-binary goal; use the zstd crate (0.13.3, bundling zstd C 1.5.7) but track/contribute to a pure-Rust zstd to eventually drop the C dependency from the static binary.

Reject zstd --long / large windows on the per-value path (no benefit for small independent entries, inflates decompressor memory); reserve large-window/long-range only for whole-dataset snapshot persistence if at all.

Evaluate per-segment/per-slab compression vs per-value: per-segment amortizes framing+dictionary overhead and improves ratio on tiny values, but costs read amplification (must decompress a segment to read one key). Make this a measured architectural decision, not a default.

Expose compression metrics (ratio, compress/decompress MB/s, CPU%, incompressible-skip rate, dictionary hit rate) so the CPU-vs-RAM tradeoff is observable and tunable per workload rather than hardcoded.

research_papers.title: Smaller and faster data compression with Zstandard
research_papers.key_idea: Introduces zstd; documents the canonical dictionary result (1 KB GitHub-user records: 2.8x without dictionary -> 6.9x with a pre-shared trained dictionary) and that zlib's 32 KB window vs zstd's unbounded window drives ratio differences.
research_papers.relevance: Primary justification for ZDICT dictionary training as IronCache's headline small-value memory feature.

research_papers.title: Zstandard Compression and the application/zstd Media Type (RFC 8878)
research_papers.key_idea: Normative specification of the zstd frame format, dictionary IDs, and window descriptors — the on-wire/on-disk format IronCache would emit/consume.
research_papers.relevance: Defines the format contract for stored compressed values and dictionary referencing; needed to design a stable on-disk/in-memory compressed-value layout.

research_papers.title: RocksDB Dictionary Compression (project wiki)
research_papers.key_idea: Per-SST trained dictionaries (max_dict_bytes, zstd_max_train_bytes) overcome the problem that 4 KB blocks are too small to self-train, improving cross-block ratio.
research_papers.relevance: Production precedent for per-segment dictionary compression that IronCache can adapt to per-slab/per-prefix in-memory dictionaries.

research_papers.title: lzbench in-memory compression benchmark
research_papers.key_idea: Standardized in-memory (no I/O) benchmark harness used by both lz4 and zstd official ratio/throughput tables on the Silesia corpus.
research_papers.relevance: The methodology IronCache should reuse to benchmark its own codec policies apples-to-apples against LZ4/zstd/snappy/lzf.

research_papers.title: Big Data Compression: A Cost-Aware Compression Selection (representative cache-compression literature)
research_papers.key_idea: Treats codec/level selection as a cost model balancing CPU time against memory/IO savings, selecting per-workload rather than a fixed codec.
research_papers.relevance: Frames IronCache's compression as a policy/cost-model decision (entropy probe + size threshold + codec choice) rather than a hardcoded default.

open_questions: Per-value vs per-segment compression: does the read-amplification cost of decompressing a whole segment per GET outweigh the better ratio and amortized dictionary/framing overhead for typical small values?

How to manage dictionary lifecycle: when to (re)train per-prefix dictionaries, how to version them, how to migrate values when a dictionary changes, and how to bound the memory/CPU of online training (the apersson module noted ~6 MB leak per train and synchronous blocking).

What is the right default compression threshold and entropy-probe cost? spymemcached uses 16384 bytes, but zstd dictionaries make sub-1 KB values worth compressing — needs measurement on representative cache value-size distributions.

Should IronCache depend on the C zstd (via the zstd 0.13.3 crate / zstd-sys 1.5.7) and break the 'pure-Rust single binary' purity, or wait for / invest in a pure-Rust zstd that currently lags in speed/ratio/configurability?
How does transparent compression interact with eviction/TTL accounting and maxmemory: do we count compressed or uncompressed bytes toward the memory limit, and how does that affect eviction fairness?

Does compression interfere with in-place value mutation operations (APPEND, INCR, SETRANGE, bitops) — do compressed values force full decompress/recompress, and which commands should disable compression for a key?

CPU budget under high QPS: at what request rate does decompression on the GET hot path become the throughput bottleneck (vs the per-core efficiency goal), and should there be an adaptive 'decompress-cost-aware' policy that disables compression for hot keys?

Can value-level columnar/dictionary encoding of hash fields (storing repeated field names once) beat generic zstd for the hash type specifically, similar to listpack but with cross-entry field dictionaries?

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: Memory efficiency via transparent compression and compact encodings
proposed_issues.summary: Umbrella epic for IronCache's compression strategy: threshold-gated server-side value compression, ZDICT dictionaries, and Redis-style compact in-memory encodings, targeting 2-7x RAM reduction over uncompressed values.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Default codec = zstd low-level; LZ4(lz4_flex) and none as policy options
proposed_issues.summary: Decide that zstd at a low/negative level is the default value codec (best ratio, level-independent decompression) with lz4_flex for max throughput and 'none' for latency-critical keyspaces, exposed as per-keyspace runtime policy.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Cache value-size and compressibility distribution survey
proposed_issues.summary: Survey real cache workloads (session blobs, serialized objects, ID sets) for value-size and entropy distributions to set the default compression size threshold and entropy-probe parameters with evidence, not the inherited 16384-byte spymemcached default.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: ZDICT per-prefix dictionary training, versioning, and tagging
proposed_issues.summary: Specify online dictionary training from sampled live values per key-prefix, a per-value dictionary-ID tag, dictionary versioning/hot-swap, and migration of values when a dictionary changes; bound training CPU/memory.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Compressed-value on-the-wire/in-memory layout (header, flags, dict-id)
proposed_issues.summary: Define the stored value framing: codec id, dictionary id, uncompressed length, incompressible flag, and how it interacts with maxmemory accounting (compressed vs uncompressed byte counting).

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Listpack and intset analogs for small aggregate types in safe Rust
proposed_issues.summary: Specify Rust compact encodings for small hashes/sets/zsets/lists (listpack analog) and all-integer sets (intset analog) with auto-upgrade thresholds mirroring Redis defaults (e.g. 128/512 entries, 64-byte values).

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: C-bound zstd (crate 0.13.3 / zstd 1.5.7) vs pure-Rust zstd for the static binary
proposed_issues.summary: Decide whether to accept the C zstd dependency (mature, fast) or commit to a pure-Rust zstd path to preserve a fully memory-safe single static binary, given pure-Rust zstd currently lags in speed/ratio.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: zstd long-range matching (--long / large windows) on the per-value path
proposed_issues.summary: Declare large-window/long-distance matching out of scope for per-value compression (no benefit for small independent entries, inflates decompressor memory); reconsider only for whole-snapshot persistence.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: lzbench-based benchmark harness for codec policy selection
proposed_issues.summary: Build an in-memory benchmark (lzbench methodology) measuring ratio and compress/decompress MB/s for none/lz4_flex/zstd/zstd+dict across representative value corpora to drive the default-policy decision.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Compression interaction with mutating commands and hot-key cost
proposed_issues.summary: Specify behavior of APPEND/INCR/SETRANGE/bitops on compressed values (decompress-mutate-recompress vs disable-compression-for-key) and an adaptive policy that skips compression for very hot keys to protect throughput.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Per-value vs per-segment compression tradeoff for small values
proposed_issues.summary: Prototype and measure per-segment/per-slab compression (amortizes dictionary/framing, better ratio on tiny values) against per-value (no read amplification) to choose the architecture for the small-value regime.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Re-verified all 15 claims against fresh primary sources. 14 confirmed, 1 corrected.

CORRECTION: [zstd-rust-crate-version] - the bundled zstd-sys version is 2.0.14+zstd.1.5.7, NOT 2.0.16 as claimed. Verified by tracing the dependency chain in the gyscos/zstd-rs repo at tag v0.13.3: zstd 0.13.3 -> zstd-safe 7.2.2 -> zstd-sys 2.0.14+zstd.1.5.7. The crate version (0.13.3), bundled C library version (1.5.7), and all listed feature flags are all correct; only the zstd-sys patch number was wrong.

Methodology notes:
- Redis (5 claims): fetched redis.conf at the exact 7.4.0 tag. All defaults confirmed verbatim, including the LZF comment on rdbcompression and the '-2 = 8 Kb' comment on list-max-listpack-size. Confirmed there is no transparent in-memory value compression directive (list-compress-depth defaults 0 and only affects interior quicklist nodes).
- LZ4 (1 claim): the cited lz4 README v1.9.0 table confirmed verbatim. Caveat for the record: the SAME ratio (2.101) appears in the separate zstd.net table but attributed to lz4 1.10.0 with different speeds (675/3850) because of a different compiler (gcc14.2) and OS (Ubuntu 24.04). Both are internally consistent with their stated environments; the lz4-README figures as cited are correct.
- zstd table (4 claims: -1, fast modes, snappy/lzf): all rows confirmed verbatim from facebook.github.io/zstd. Environment matches the claim (i7-9700K @4.9GHz, gcc14.2, Ubuntu 24.04, lzbench, Silesia).
- FB dictionary blog: confirmed verbatim (122 KB, 6.9x, 2.8x, 850 KB, 1000 users).
- zstd dictionary defaults: upgraded from medium to confirmed using zdict.h (fastCover redirect, ~100 KB reasonable, not effective past 100KB) plus the CLI --maxdict default 112640 bytes (=110 KB).
- lz4_flex: benchmark numbers and no-unsafe-default confirmed verbatim from README. Minor staleness only: the crate is now version 0.13.0 (claim labels it 0.12); the functional/numeric claim is unaffected.
- apersson redis module: upgraded from medium to confirmed - all numbers present verbatim in README (7.70 ratio, 1.62 GB->211 MB, 100000 objects, 100 KB DICTSIZE, 6 MB leak, synchronous/blocking).
- spymemcached: upgraded from medium to confirmed via the canonical GitHub source at tag 2.12.3 (DEFAULT_COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD = 16384, GZIP via java.util.zip). The javadoc.io URL in the claim returned HTTP 403, so I anchored to the source repo instead. The EVCache 'configurable size threshold' sub-claim is plausible but was not independently re-verified.

dimensions.summary:
IronCache's central runtime decision is work-stealing (tokio) vs thread-per-core / shared-nothing (glommio, monoio). The thread-per-core lineage traces to Seastar/ScyllaDB and is now the default for the highest-performance caches: Dragonfly shards its in-memory DB into N parts (k = HASH(KEY) % N, N <= thread count), binds each shard to exactly one thread, and forbids mutexes entirely — "Inter-thread interactions occur only via passing messages from thread to thread." Datadog's Glommio post makes the load-bearing argument crisply: with a single thread of execution per core, "locks are never necessary" because all updates to a shard are naturally serialized; the cost is that any cross-shard work must be explicit message passing, and a context switch (~5us) is more expensive than an io_uring I/O (<4us). For a Redis-wire cache whose dominant ops are single-key GET/SET, the keyspace partitions cleanly per core, so shared-nothing is the natural fit and the strongest lever for "max throughput per core."

The runtime tradeoff is concrete. tokio (1.52.3, MSRV 1.71) uses a multi-threaded work-stealing scheduler over epoll/kqueue (readiness model: two user/kernel transitions per I/O). It is the mature, portable, ecosystem-dominant default — but work-stealing forces every shared structure to be Send+Sync and re-introduces synchronization (atomics, cross-core cache-line bouncing) that thread-per-core eliminates. monoio (0.2.4, ByteDance) and glommio (0.9.0, Datadog, MSRV 1.70) are thread-per-core io_uring runtimes using a completion model. ByteDance's own benchmark claims monoio reaches ~2x tokio peak at 4 cores and ~3x at 16 cores, with the caveat that at single-core / very-few-connections monoio latency is worse than tokio (io_uring vs epoll). Glommio uses three io_uring rings per thread (main, latency, poll) to isolate NVMe polling. The key downside of both: io_uring is Linux-only (kernel 5.6+ for the basic ops; monoio falls back to epoll/kqueue below that), futures are !Send so the programming model differs from tokio, and the ecosystem (drivers, libraries) is far thinner.

io_uring itself is the I/O substrate worth borrowing. The io-uring crate (0.7.12) is the low-level binding; tokio-uring exists but requires kernel 5.11+. The high-value features map to specific kernels: registered/fixed buffers and SQPOLL (kernel-side submission polling, no submit syscall) for hot-path zero-syscall I/O; multishot accept (5.19) and multishot recv with ring-provided buffers (6.0) to amortize SQE submission across many connections — exactly the shape of a cache accepting thousands of short-lived or long-lived connections. The design tension is buffer ownership: completion-model I/O requires buffers to outlive the kernel operation, which forces owned-buffer APIs (IoBuf/IoBufMut) rather than borrowed &mut [u8], shaping the entire networking layer.

On the data structure: if IronCache goes shared-nothing, the per-shard store can be a plain single-threaded hashbrown HashMap with no synchronization at all — the biggest win. If it instead uses a shared concurrent map (simpler connection model, work-stealing), the candidates are dashmap 7.0.0-rc2 (sharded RwLock<HashMap>, CachePadded to avoid false sharing, default shards = available_parallelism()*4 rounded to power of two), scc 3.4.8 (finer-grained bucket locks, best for write-heavy), papaya 0.2.4 (lock-free, open-addressing Swiss-table-style with an 8-bit metadata array, incremental resize, hyaline reclamation via seize 0.5 — built for read-heavy), and flurry (Java CHM port, lock-free reads, but allocator pressure). For a cache the workload is read-heavy with eviction churn, so papaya/scc beat a coarse RwLock<HashMap>, but a partitioned single-thread map beats all of them by avoiding atomics entirely. Memory reclamation matters: epoch-based reclamation (crossbeam-epoch) can stall reclamation behind a slow thread, whereas hyaline/seize gives more predictable GC — relevant because a cache must bound memory tightly.

Recommendation for IronCache: adopt the shared-nothing / shard-per-core model (borrow from Seastar/ScyllaDB/Dragonfly) as the architecture, with io_uring (borrow) as the Linux I/O path. Choose between monoio and glommio as the thread-per-core runtime via a prototype bake-off, and keep a tokio+epoll fallback path for portability and pre-5.6 kernels (adapt). Reject a single global RwLock<HashMap> and reject work-stealing as the primary model. Per-shard storage should be a single-threaded map; a shared concurrent map (papaya for read-heavy) is the fallback only if the connection-affinity model proves too costly. The open risk is multi-key/cross-shard commands (MGET, MSET, transactions, pub/sub fan-out) which shared-nothing makes harder — Dragonfly's coordinator-fiber pattern is the reference solution.

prior_art_claims.claim: Latest tokio crate version and its declared minimum supported Rust version.

prior_art_claims.claim: tokio uses a multi-threaded work-stealing scheduler over a readiness (epoll/kqueue) I/O model, requiring two user/kernel transitions per I/O operation (readiness notification + the operation).
prior_art_claims.value: work-stealing scheduler; epoll/kqueue readiness model; 2 syscalls per I/O
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Architectural fact corroborated across monoio docs and tokio's own design material; not version-specific.

verification.corrected_value: work-stealing scheduler; epoll/kqueue readiness model; 2 syscalls per I/O
verification.note: Tokio runtime docs confirm: 'The multi-thread scheduler executes futures on a thread pool, using a work-stealing strategy.' Tokio's I/O is readiness-based via the mio dependency (mio = 1.2.0 in Cargo.toml, an epoll/kqueue readiness library). The '2 user/kernel transitions per I/O' (readiness wait + the actual syscall) is the standard, correct characterization of the readiness model vs io_uring's completion model. Technically accurate, but the cited zread.ai is a third-party aggregator; prefer tokio's own docs + mio as primary sources.

prior_art_claims.claim: Latest glommio crate version and declared MSRV.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from glommio/Cargo.toml via gh search/API.

verification.corrected_value: version 0.9.0; rust-version = 1.70
verification.note: Verified from master glommio/Cargo.toml: version = "0.9.0", rust-version = "1.70" (also documented in README 'Supported Rust Versions'). Cross-checked crates.io: latest published = 0.9.0. Both correct.

prior_art_claims.claim: Thread-per-core with a single thread of execution per core makes locks unnecessary because all updates to a given shard are naturally serialized; cross-shard work must be explicit.
prior_art_claims.value: locks never necessary in thread-per-core; sharding required
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Direct quote from Datadog's official engineering blog.

verification.corrected_value: locks never necessary in thread-per-core; sharding required
verification.note: Datadog blog states verbatim that locks are 'never necessary' with a single thread of execution per core because 'if there is a single thread of execution, two things can't be happening (for that request) at the same time' and updates to a given key are 'serialized... They have to be!' Cross-shard work is explicit. Fully supported by primary source.

prior_art_claims.claim: Datadog cites that a context switch between threads (~5 microseconds) is more expensive than a modern I/O operation (<4 microseconds), motivating pinned thread-per-core to eliminate context switches.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in the blog as approximate figures; hardware-dependent, cited as motivation not as a benchmark.

verification.corrected_value: context switch ~5us > I/O <4us
verification.note: Datadog blog verbatim: 'Context switches are expensive, costing around five microseconds... Jens Axboe just recently published results for his new io_uring kernel infrastructure with Storage I/O times below four microseconds.' Numbers (~5us context switch vs <4us I/O) confirmed exactly. Upgrades the original 'medium' confidence.

This latency difference is due to the difference between io_uring and epoll.'
