Anchored to the official seastar.io shared-nothing page; consistent with ScyllaDB architecture docs.

mechanisms.name: Thread-per-core / shared-nothing sharding
mechanisms.what_it_does: Pin one thread per core, partition the keyspace into N shards (k = HASH(KEY) % N), and let exactly one thread own each shard. All cross-shard work is explicit message passing; no shared mutable state, so no locks/atomics on the hot path.
mechanisms.rationale: For a Redis-wire cache the dominant ops (GET/SET) are single-key and partition cleanly per core. Eliminating atomics and cross-core cache-line bouncing is the single biggest lever for 'max throughput per core', and it is the proven architecture of the fastest caches (Dragonfly) and databases (ScyllaDB).

mechanisms.name: Work-stealing multi-threaded scheduler
mechanisms.what_it_does: A pool of worker threads each with a local run queue; idle workers steal tasks from busy workers' queues to balance load. Tasks must be Send so they can migrate across threads.
mechanisms.rationale: Work-stealing requires every shared structure to be Send+Sync, forcing locks/atomics and re-introducing the cache-coherency cost shared-nothing avoids. It optimizes for uneven CPU-bound task graphs, not for uniform per-key cache ops. Keep tokio only as a portability fallback runtime, not the primary model.

mechanisms.name: io_uring completion-model I/O
mechanisms.what_it_does: Submission/completion ring buffers shared with the kernel; submit batches of I/O and reap completions, avoiding a readiness syscall per op. Completion model means buffers must outlive the in-flight op (owned-buffer APIs).
mechanisms.rationale: Halves syscalls vs epoll and enables zero-syscall hot paths; it is the substrate behind monoio/glommio. The owned-buffer constraint shapes the networking layer but is acceptable for the Linux fast path.

mechanisms.name: SQPOLL (kernel submission polling)
mechanisms.what_it_does: A kernel thread polls the submission queue, so userspace can submit I/O without any io_uring_enter syscall, trading a busy kernel thread for lower latency.
mechanisms.rationale: Attractive for ultra-low-latency single-tenant deployments, but it burns a core and hurts efficiency-per-core under low load. Make it an opt-in flag, not the default, given IronCache's efficiency goal.

mechanisms.name: Multishot accept + multishot recv with ring-provided buffers
mechanisms.what_it_does: A single accept SQE posts a CQE per new connection; a single recv SQE repeatedly posts CQEs pulling buffers from a registered provided-buffer pool. Amortizes SQE submission across many connections/reads.
mechanisms.rationale: Ideal for a cache fronting thousands of connections, but require kernel 6.0; gate behind feature detection and fall back to one-shot ops on older kernels. Adopt registered/fixed buffers regardless for zero-copy hot paths.

mechanisms.name: Per-shard single-threaded hashbrown HashMap
mechanisms.what_it_does: Under shared-nothing, each shard's store is an ordinary single-threaded SwissTable HashMap with no locks, no atomics, no reclamation overhead.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the payoff of shared-nothing: the store needs zero synchronization, beating any concurrent map. Pair with an intrusive LRU/eviction structure also owned by the shard thread.

mechanisms.name: Sharded RwLock<HashMap> concurrent map
mechanisms.what_it_does: Array of CachePadded<RwLock<HashMap>> shards (default available_parallelism()*4, power-of-two); a key hashes to a shard and takes that shard's RwLock. CachePadded avoids false sharing.
mechanisms.rationale: Only relevant if we abandon shard-per-core. Even then a coarse RwLock per shard serializes writers within a shard and re-introduces lock contention under eviction churn; papaya/scc dominate it. Reject as the primary store; acceptable only for low-contention side metadata.

mechanisms.name: Lock-free open-addressing map with hyaline reclamation
mechanisms.what_it_does: Swiss-table-style open addressing with an 8-bit metadata array for cache-efficient probing, atomic table-pointer swaps for incremental resize, and hyaline-based memory reclamation (seize) so readers never block writers.
mechanisms.rationale: The best shared-map fallback for IronCache's read-heavy profile if the connection-affinity model proves too costly. Adapt because a cache also needs bounded memory and TTL/eviction, which papaya does not provide out of the box.

mechanisms.name: Aggressive bucket-lock sharding
mechanisms.what_it_does: Concurrent HashMap that shards locks at bucket granularity, far finer than dashmap, giving strong write-heavy throughput.
mechanisms.rationale: Write-heavy specialization is not IronCache's primary need (caches are read-heavy), and it still pays atomics/locks that shard-per-core avoids. Keep as a benchmark baseline only.

mechanisms.name: Epoch-based reclamation (EBR)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Defers freeing memory until all threads have advanced past the epoch in which it was unlinked, giving safe lock-free reclamation.
mechanisms.rationale: Workable but a stalled/slow thread can pin an epoch and stall reclamation, growing memory unboundedly — bad for a memory-bounded cache. Prefer hyaline/seize where lock-free reclamation is needed; under shard-per-core, reclamation is moot for the main store.

mechanisms.name: seqlock + cache-line padding for hot read-mostly state
mechanisms.what_it_does: A seqlock lets readers retry-read versioned data without taking a lock; CachePadded aligns hot atomics to a cache line to prevent false sharing between cores.
mechanisms.rationale: Useful for shared read-mostly state that must cross shards (config, stats counters). Cheap, standard, and complements shared-nothing for the rare genuinely-shared datum.

ironcache_implications: Adopt shard-per-core as the core architecture: pin one OS thread per core, partition the keyspace by k = HASH(KEY) % N (N = core count), and have each thread exclusively own its shard's HashMap + eviction structures so the GET/SET hot path takes zero locks and zero atomics.

Make the runtime a swappable trait/abstraction so a prototype bake-off between monoio (0.2.4) and glommio (0.9.0) is cheap, with tokio (1.52.3) + epoll retained as the portability fallback for non-Linux and pre-5.6 kernels.

Treat io_uring as Linux fast path only; require kernel 5.6+ for basic ops and feature-detect at startup for multishot accept (5.19) and multishot recv + ring-provided buffers (6.0), falling back to one-shot ops or epoll when absent.

Build the networking layer around owned buffers (IoBuf/IoBufMut style) and registered/fixed buffers from day one, since the io_uring completion model forbids borrowing &mut [u8] across an in-flight op.

Solve cross-shard / multi-key commands (MGET, MSET, MSETNX, transactions, pub/sub fan-out) with a Dragonfly-style coordinator: pin each connection to one thread's fiber that dispatches per-shard sub-commands and reassembles results, providing atomicity without a global lock.
Bind each client connection to a single thread for its lifetime; if a request touches a remote shard, route via a bounded SPSC/MPSC message channel rather than reaching into another thread's map.

If a shared concurrent map is ever needed (e.g. abandoning connection affinity), default to papaya 0.2.4 (read-heavy, hyaline reclamation) not dashmap's RwLock<HashMap>; reserve dashmap/scc as benchmark baselines.
Cache-line-pad (CachePadded) any genuinely shared atomics (global stats, epoch counters) and use seqlocks for read-mostly shared config to avoid false sharing across cores.
Make SQPOLL an opt-in latency mode, not default, because a dedicated kernel poll thread harms throughput-per-core under low/moderate load — directly counter to IronCache's efficiency goal.
Document the kernel-version support matrix prominently in the README, since the single-static-binary promise collides with io_uring feature fragmentation across 5.6 / 5.11 / 5.19 / 6.0.

research_papers.title: The Impact of Thread-Per-Core Architecture on Application Tail Latency
research_papers.key_idea: Empirically compares thread-per-core (shared-nothing) against work-stealing/shared designs, showing thread-per-core can improve tail latency substantially by eliminating cross-core synchronization and migration.
research_papers.relevance: Provides the academic backing for choosing shard-per-core over work-stealing for a latency-sensitive cache; the '71% tail-latency' figure Datadog cites traces here.

research_papers.title: Scalable Kernel TCP Design and Implementation for Short-Lived Connections (MegaPipe / affinity-accept lineage)
research_papers.key_idea: Per-core connection acceptance and CPU-affine connection handling remove accept-path contention and improve locality for many short-lived connections.
research_papers.relevance: Directly informs IronCache's accept/connection model: bind connections to the accepting core and use multishot accept to scale connection setup.

research_papers.title: Efficient Lock-Free Durable Sets / Hyaline: Fast and Transparent Lock-Free Memory Reclamation
research_papers.key_idea: Hyaline reclamation uses reference-counted retirement batches that are robust to stalled threads, avoiding the unbounded-garbage problem of epoch-based reclamation.
research_papers.relevance: Justifies papaya/seize's reclamation choice over crossbeam-epoch for any lock-free path in a memory-bounded cache.

research_papers.title: MICA: A Holistic Approach to Fast In-Memory Key-Value Storage
research_papers.key_idea: Partitions data across cores with exclusive (EREW) access, uses kernel-bypass NIC queues steered per-core, achieving tens of millions of ops/sec by avoiding cross-core sharing.
research_papers.relevance: Canonical proof that shard-per-core + per-core NIC steering is the path to maximum KV throughput per core — the IronCache north star.

research_papers.title: Scaling Memcached at Facebook
research_papers.key_idea: Real-world memcached scaling: lock granularity, slab allocation, and connection handling tradeoffs under read-heavy production load.
research_papers.relevance: Grounds expectations about read-heavy cache workload mixes and the operational cost of lock contention that shard-per-core removes.

open_questions: Does shard-per-core (monoio/glommio) actually beat tokio+epoll+papaya for IronCache's specific GET/SET mix on representative hardware, or only at high core counts? Needs an in-house bake-off, since vendor benchmarks are not apples-to-apples.
How costly is the cross-shard coordinator path for MGET/MSET/transactions/pub-sub, and at what fraction of multi-key traffic does shared-nothing stop winning vs a shared concurrent map?

monoio vs glommio: which has the more maintainable API, better Linux-kernel feature coverage (multishot, fixed buffers), and a thinner unsafe surface for a production cache? Both have small ecosystems.
How do we keep a true single-static-binary promise while supporting both io_uring (Linux 5.6+/6.0) and an epoll/kqueue fallback, including non-Linux dev machines? Compile-time vs runtime selection?
What is the per-shard memory bookkeeping cost for tight memory bounds, and does owning eviction (LRU/LFU/W-TinyLFU) per shard skew hit rates vs a global eviction policy?
Should connection-to-shard routing rebalance on hot keys (shard imbalance), and if so how, without re-introducing cross-core sharing? (ScyllaDB's 'hot shard' problem.)
Does SQPOLL or fixed-buffer registration interact badly with many shards each owning a ring (memlock limits, kernel thread count)?

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Shard-per-core / shared-nothing as IronCache's core concurrency model
proposed_issues.summary: Adopt thread-per-core with per-core keyspace sharding (k = HASH(KEY) % N) and no shared mutable hot-path state, following Seastar/ScyllaDB/Dragonfly, in preference to a work-stealing + shared-concurrent-map design. Record the rationale and the rejected alternatives.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Runtime bake-off — monoio vs glommio vs tokio+epoll on GET/SET workload
proposed_issues.summary: Build a minimal echo/KV harness on each runtime and measure throughput-per-core and tail latency at 1/4/16 cores with realistic connection counts, to validate (or refute) the ~2x/3x thread-per-core claims for our workload before committing.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Runtime abstraction layer to keep monoio/glommio/tokio swappable
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a thin runtime/IO trait so the cache core is decoupled from the concrete runtime, enabling the bake-off and preserving a tokio+epoll fallback for portability and pre-5.6 kernels.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: io_uring fast path with registered buffers, multishot accept/recv, kernel feature detection
proposed_issues.summary: Design the Linux I/O layer around the io-uring crate (0.7.12): owned/fixed buffers, multishot accept (5.19) and multishot recv with ring-provided buffers (6.0), with runtime feature detection and graceful fallback to one-shot ops.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Cross-shard coordinator for multi-key and transactional commands
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a Dragonfly-style per-connection coordinator fiber that fans multi-key commands (MGET/MSET/transactions/pub-sub) out to shard-owning threads via bounded channels and reassembles results with defined atomicity guarantees.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Per-shard single-thread HashMap vs shared concurrent map fallback
proposed_issues.summary: Decide that the primary per-shard store is an unsynchronized hashbrown HashMap, and document papaya 0.2.4 (read-heavy, seize/hyaline reclamation) as the designated shared-map fallback if shard affinity is abandoned; reject RwLock<HashMap> and scc as primary.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Memory reclamation strategy for any lock-free paths (seize/hyaline vs crossbeam-epoch)
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate whether any IronCache component needs lock-free structures, and if so compare hyaline (seize 0.5) vs epoch-based reclamation for predictable, memory-bounded GC suitable for a cache.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Kernel-version support matrix and single-binary fallback strategy
proposed_issues.summary: Document the io_uring feature/kernel matrix (5.6 basic, 5.11 tokio-uring, 5.19 multishot accept, 6.0 multishot recv) and define how a single static binary selects io_uring vs epoll/kqueue at runtime.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: General-purpose work-stealing task scheduling on the hot path
proposed_issues.summary: State explicitly that IronCache does not use work-stealing for hot-path command processing; work-stealing is out of scope except possibly for background/maintenance tasks.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Hot-shard / load-imbalance mitigation under shard-per-core
proposed_issues.summary: Investigate how to detect and mitigate skewed shard load (hot keys) without re-introducing cross-core sharing, drawing on ScyllaDB's hot-shard handling.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Methodology: verified each version/MSRV claim by reading the cited master Cargo.toml/source via the GitHub API (gh) AND cross-checking the canonical published version on crates.io; verified kernel/manpage and blog claims via WebFetch of the cited primary source plus at least one independent source (man7, Red Hat, lkml).

Net result: 20 confirmed, 1 corrected, 0 refuted, 0 uncertain.

The one real error:
- scc-version (CORRECTED to 3.7.3): the claim's 3.4.8 comes from the repo's master branch Cargo.toml, but master is stale; the actual current published crate is 3.7.3. This is the kind of trap where 'read the master Cargo.toml' diverges from 'latest released crate.' Also note scc's master now requires edition 2024 / rust-version 1.85.0.

Confirmed-with-important-nuance (values stand, but record-keepers should note):
- tokio-uring-min-kernel: the empirical 'kernel 5.4 fails, 5.11 works' is verbatim from the README and correct, but the crate's docs.rs/lib.rs declares the formal minimum as kernel 5.10. Use 5.10 as the declared floor, 5.11 as the empirically-verified-working figure.
- dashmap-internal-design: on master, 'HashMap<K,V>' is a crate-local alias for hashbrown's HashTable<(K,V)>, not std::collections::HashMap. Structure and false-sharing rationale are correct; 'standard HashMap' is imprecise.
- dashmap-version: 7.0.0-rc2 is correct for master, but the latest STABLE published dashmap is 6.2.1 (the 7.0.0 line is prerelease only). Claim was carefully scoped to 'on master' so it stands.
- monoio-vs-tokio-scaling: confirmed but these are ByteDance's own self-reported numbers (vendor benchmark), so treat as vendor claims rather than neutral third-party measurements; original 'low' confidence was appropriately cautious.

Source-quality flags: tokio-workstealing-readiness-model cited a third-party aggregator (zread.ai); the underlying technical claim is sound and I re-anchored it to tokio's own runtime docs + the mio dependency. io-uring-multishot-accept cited an lkml patch thread; I re-anchored to the man7 manpage (stronger primary source) plus Red Hat corroboration.

Versions are as of 2026-06-13: tokio 1.52.3, glommio 0.9.0, monoio 0.2.4, io-uring 0.7.12, dashmap master 7.0.0-rc2 (stable 6.2.1), papaya 0.2.4, seize 0.5.1, scc published 3.7.3 (master 3.4.8), tokio-uring 0.5.0.

dimensions.summary:
The dominant production design for a Redis-wire-compatible cache cluster is Redis Cluster's: a fixed key space of 16384 hash slots, with slot = CRC16(key, XMODEM variant) mod 16384, slots statically assigned to master shards, and a "smart client" routing model where clients cache a slot->node map and follow MOVED/ASK redirections rather than going through a proxy. Membership and config propagation ride a full-mesh "cluster bus" (base port + 10000) using a gossip heartbeat, with failure detection via PFAIL->FAIL escalation gated on cluster-node-timeout (default 15000 ms) and replica-promotion elections versioned by a configEpoch logical clock. Replication is asynchronous leader-follower by default (WAIT gives bounded sync). This design is operationally proven but has two well-known pain points: 16384 slots was deliberately chosen so the per-node slot bitmap is 2 KB (vs 8 KB at 65536) and caps practical clusters at ~1000 masters (antirez, redis#2576); and legacy slot migration (CLUSTER SETSLOT MIGRATING/IMPORTING + per-key MIGRATE) is non-atomic, slow (~hundreds of thousands of round-trips for large slots), and leaves slots in an inconsistent state on failure — which Valkey 9.0's fork-based atomic slot migration (snapshot via child process, stream incremental mutations, atomic ownership handoff) fixes and is the better model to follow.

On partitioning algorithms, the academic state of the art has moved well past Karger ring/ketama. Rendezvous/HRW hashing (Thaler & Ravishankar, 1996) is simpler and more general than consistent hashing, gives minimal-disruption reassignment (only the failed node's keys move) and is O(n) per lookup (O(log n) with a skeleton tree). Jump consistent hash (Lamping & Veach, 2014) is ~5 lines, needs zero memory beyond registers, runs in O(ln n), and distributes near-perfectly — but its fatal limitation is that buckets must be 0..N-1 sequential, so you can only add/remove the last bucket, making it unfit for clusters with arbitrary node removal. Newer algorithms (AnchorHash, MementoHash 2023) restore arbitrary node removal at minimal memory; MementoHash also removes AnchorHash's requirement to fix a maximum capacity up front. For IronCache, the right separation is: use a slot abstraction (like Redis, for clean ownership transfer and multi-key hash-tag locality) but make the slot count and slot->node assignment a first-class, consensus-managed map rather than gossip-propagated bitmaps.

On consistency, the spectrum runs from Redis Cluster's async leader-follower (fast, can lose acknowledged writes on the minority side of a partition) through Dynamo-style leaderless quorums (Amazon Dynamo 2007: N/R/W with R+W>N, sloppy quorum + hinted handoff for availability under partition, vector clocks for causality with conflict resolution pushed to the app — which the industry found painful) to CRDT-based active-active. Redis Enterprise CRDB and KeyDB active-replica implement active-active: CRDB maps strings to LWW registers, counters to op-based PN-counters (captures total increments since last sync, so concurrent INCRs both survive), and sets to Add-Wins Observed-Remove sets; KeyDB uses pure timestamp LWW on all types, which silently loses concurrent counter increments. The research lineage here is Shapiro et al. CRDTs and Berkeley's Anna KVS (coordination-free lattice/CRDT actors, up to 700x Masstree).

For membership/failure detection, Redis Cluster's full-mesh gossip works to ~1000 nodes but scales O(N^2) in connections and bitmap-heavy heartbeats. SWIM (Das, Gupta, Motivala, 2002) decouples failure detection (randomized direct ping + k indirect pings) from dissemination (infection-style gossip) and proves expected detection time, false-positive rate, and per-member message load are all independent of group size. HashiCorp memberlist/Serf is the battle-tested Rust/Go-portable embodiment, with the Lifeguard extensions (2017) cutting false positives ~50x under CPU/network stress. SWIM is the clear borrow for IronCache's membership layer.

For IronCache specifically (Rust, single static binary, most-efficient-cache-per-core, single-node-first then multi-node), the recommended path is: ship a rock-solid single node first; design the on-wire and storage layers so a slot abstraction can be bolted on later without a rewrite; for multi-node v1 adopt Redis-Cluster-compatible client semantics (16384 slots, CRC16, hash tags, MOVED/ASK) so existing cluster clients "just work," but internally manage the authoritative slot map and membership with a small Raft group (or a Raft-managed config plane + SWIM data-plane membership) rather than gossip bitmaps, and implement atomic, fork/copy-on-write-streamed slot migration from day one. Defer active-active/CRDT mode to a later milestone as an opt-in mode (LWW strings, PN-counters, OR-sets) clearly documented as a different consistency contract.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis Cluster partitions the key space into a fixed 16384 hash slots; the slot for a key is CRC16(key) mod 16384.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated verbatim in the official Redis cluster specification.

verification.corrected_value: 16384 slots; slot = CRC16(key) mod 16384
verification.note: Cluster-spec states verbatim: 'The cluster's key space is split into 16384 slots' and 'HASH_SLOT = CRC16(key) mod 16384'. Upper limit 16384 masters, suggested ~1000. Confirmed at current spec.

prior_art_claims.claim: The CRC16 used is the XMODEM/CRC-16-ACORN variant: poly 0x1021, init 0x0000, no input/output reflection, no output XOR; CRC16('123456789')=0x31C3.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Full CRC16 parameter table given in the official cluster spec.

verification.corrected_value: CRC-16/XMODEM, poly=0x1021, init=0x0000, refin/refout=false, xorout=0x0000, check(123456789)=0x31C3
verification.note: Cluster-spec Appendix A lists XMODEM (aka ZMODEM/CRC-16/ACORN): Poly 1021, Init 0000, RefIn false, RefOut false, XorOut 0000, output for '123456789' = 31C3. Independently corroborated by the reveng CRC catalogue (https://reveng.sourceforge.io/crc-catalogue/16.htm) which lists width=16 poly=0x1021 init=0x0000 refin=false refout=false xorout=0x0000 check=0x31c3 for CRC-16/XMODEM.

prior_art_claims.claim: 16384 (not 65536) slots were chosen so the per-node served-slots bitmap in each heartbeat is 2 KB instead of a prohibitive 8 KB, and because clusters are unlikely to scale beyond ~1000 master nodes.
prior_art_claims.value: 2 KB bitmap @16384 vs 8 KB @65536; ~1000 master cap
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Direct quote from antirez's comment fetched via gh api.

verification.corrected_value: 2 KB bitmap @16384 vs 8 KB @65536; ~1000 master cap
verification.note: antirez's comment (fetched via gh api repos/redis/redis/issues/2576/comments) states verbatim: the slots config in heartbeats 'uses 2k of space with 16k slots, but would use a prohibitive 8k of space using 65k slots' and 'it is unlikely that Redis Cluster would scale to more than 1000 master nodes'. Exact match.

prior_art_claims.claim: The cluster-node-timeout default is 15000 ms; a master unreachable longer than this can be failed over. PFAIL escalates to FAIL when a majority of masters concur within node-timeout*2.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Default value widely corroborated across sources; not re-read from the raw redis.conf in this session.

verification.corrected_value: 15000 ms default
verification.note: The 15000ms default value is CORRECT, but the cited scaling page does NOT state it (it only shows '5000' as an example). Verified the real default from Redis 7.4 source: redis.conf has '# cluster-node-timeout 15000' and src/config.c createLongLongConfig('cluster-node-timeout', ... default 15000). Value confirmed; source corrected to the redis.conf / config.c source of truth. The PFAIL->FAIL escalation within node-timeout*2 is documented in the cluster-spec, not the scaling page.

prior_art_claims.claim: Replication between master and replica is asynchronous by default; acknowledged writes can be lost on the minority side of a partition. WAIT requests bounded synchronous replication.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated explicitly in the cluster spec replication section.

verification.corrected_value: async default; WAIT for sync
verification.note: Cluster-spec: 'Redis Cluster uses asynchronous replication between nodes' and 'nodes do not wait for other nodes' acknowledgment of writes (if not explicitly requested using the WAIT command).' Also notes acknowledged writes can be lost, with larger windows in a minority partition. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Nodes form a full mesh on a cluster bus at base_port+10000 (or configurable cluster-port), gossiping ping/pong heartbeats; every node is pinged at least every node-timeout/2.
prior_art_claims.value: cluster bus = base port + 10000; full mesh; ping every node-timeout/2
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Cluster bus port and gossip cadence stated in the spec.

verification.corrected_value: cluster bus = base port + 10000 (or cluster-port); full mesh; ping every node-timeout/2
verification.note: Cluster-spec: bus port 'derived by adding 10000 to the data port or specified with cluster-port' (6379->16379); 'Redis Cluster is a full mesh where every node is connected with every other node'; 'every node makes sure to ping every other node that hasn't sent a ping or received a pong for longer than half the NODE_TIMEOUT time.' Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Hash tags route multiple keys to one slot: if a key contains {...} with a non-empty substring, only the first {..} substring is hashed; multi-key commands require all keys in the same slot or return CROSSSLOT.
prior_art_claims.value: first non-empty {tag} substring is hashed; else whole key
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Hash tag rules and examples enumerated in the spec.

verification.corrected_value: first non-empty {tag} substring is hashed; else whole key; CROSSSLOT for multi-key across slots
verification.note: Cluster-spec hash-tag algorithm: if key has '{', a '}' to its right, and >=1 char between them, only the substring between first '{' and first following '}' is hashed; else whole key. Examples confirm (foo{}{bar} hashes whole key; foo{bar}{zap} hashes 'bar'). Multi-key commands require same slot. Note: the spec page itself does not use the literal token 'CROSSSLOT' (it describes the requirement and shows -TRYAGAIN during resharding), but the CROSSSLOT error is the established behavior. Core claim confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Routing is client-side: a node returns -MOVED slot host:port for a permanently relocated slot (client updates its map) and -ASK during migration (client retries that one query at target after ASKING, without updating its map).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Redirection semantics defined verbatim in the spec.

verification.corrected_value: MOVED permanent (client updates map), ASK transient + ASKING (no map update)
verification.note: Cluster-spec: '-MOVED 3999 127.0.0.1:6381' means slot permanently served elsewhere, client should memorize it; 'ASK means to send only the next query to the specified node', prefixed by ASKING, and the client must NOT update its slot map. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Legacy resharding sets slot state via CLUSTER SETSLOT ... MIGRATING/IMPORTING and moves keys with per-batch MIGRATE; it is non-atomic and on failure must be reversed step by step.
prior_art_claims.value: CLUSTER SETSLOT MIGRATING/IMPORTING + MIGRATE per batch
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Migration command sequence given in the spec; non-atomic nature corroborated by Valkey.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey 9.0 ships atomic slot migration: a child process snapshots migrating slots, the source streams incremental mutations, then ownership is atomically handed off and broadcast — avoiding the legacy per-key approach's ~213000 round-trips and large-key OOM risk.
prior_art_claims.value: fork snapshot + stream + atomic handoff; replaces ~213k round-trips
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Mechanism and round-trip figure from the official Valkey engineering blog.

verification.corrected_value: fork/child-process snapshot + incremental stream + atomic ownership handoff & broadcast; replaces legacy ~213k (212,992) round-trips
verification.note: Valkey blog confirms: Valkey 9.0 'Atomic Slot Migration'; source sends point-in-time snapshot via a child process, then streams incremental changes, then briefly pauses and the target acquires ownership and broadcasts it. Legacy RTT formula 4096*(3*160/10+4)=212992 round-trips (~213k), and legacy approach could trigger out-of-memory conditions. All specifics confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey 8.0 replicates CLUSTER SETSLOT to replicas synchronously before applying on the primary and recovers slot-migration state across failover, plus adds a per-slot dictionary replacing a linked list.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: From Valkey 8.0 release blog; feature list summarized via search.

verification.corrected_value: synchronous SETSLOT replication before primary execution; failover slot-state recovery; per-slot dict replaces linked list
verification.note: Valkey 8.0 RC1 blog: 'All CLUSTER SETSLOT commands are now replicated synchronously to replicas before execution on the primary'; on failover Valkey 8.0 'automatically updates the slot migration states on source and target nodes'; introduces 'a new per-slot dictionary ... which replaces a linked list'. All three sub-claims confirmed (upgrading from the original medium confidence).

prior_art_claims.claim: Jump consistent hash maps a uint64 key and int32 num_buckets to a bucket using LCG step key=key*2862933555777941757+1 and j=(b+1)*(1<<31)/((key>>33)+1); ~5 lines, no storage, O(ln n) expected time.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Constant and formula corroborated by docs.rs jump-consistent-hash and Go/lithammer reference implementations matching the paper; raw PDF text was not parseable in-session.

verification.corrected_value: multiplier 2862933555777941757; j=(b+1)*((1LL<<31)/((key>>33)+1)); O(ln n) expected (< ln(n)+1 iterations); no storage / ~5 lines
verification.note: Verified from extracted PDF text (Figure 1): 'key = key * 2862933555777941757ULL + 1; j = (b + 1) * (double(1LL << 31) / double((key >> 33) + 1));'. Performance section: expected iterations 'less than ln(n) + 1' (O(ln n)); abstract: 'minimal memory ... requires no storage ... about 5 lines of code'. Note the slot formula uses (1LL<<31) not (1<<31). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Jump hash's main limitation is that buckets must be numbered sequentially 0..N-1, so only the last bucket can be added/removed — it cannot handle arbitrary node removal.
prior_art_claims.value: sequential buckets only; remove last only
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Limitation stated in the paper abstract and reiterated across implementations.

verification.corrected_value: buckets must be numbered sequentially 0..n-1; suited to data storage; effectively only the last bucket can be removed (no arbitrary removal)
verification.note: Abstract: 'Its main limitation is that the buckets must be numbered sequentially, which makes it more suitable for data storage applications than for distributed web caching.' Body confirms shards get ids in increasing order filling [0,num_buckets) and jump hash, unlike Karger/rendezvous, does not handle 'arbitrary buckets being removed'. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Rendezvous (HRW) hashing assigns object O to the node maximizing hash(O,node); it is O(n) per lookup (O(log n) with a skeleton tree), gives minimal disruption (only a removed node's keys move), and predates and generalizes consistent hashing.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Algorithm, complexity, and 1996 origin consistent across Wikipedia and the original paper references.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dynamo uses leaderless quorums with N/R/W where R+W>N, a sloppy quorum over the first N healthy nodes of the preference list, hinted handoff to stand-in nodes during outages, and vector clocks with app-level conflict resolution.
prior_art_claims.value: R+W>N; sloppy quorum; hinted handoff; vector clocks
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Core mechanisms of the canonical Dynamo paper, widely and consistently cited.

verification.corrected_value: R+W>N; sloppy quorum over first N healthy preference-list nodes; hinted handoff; vector clocks with app-level reconciliation
verification.note: Dynamo SOSP 2007: quorum-like N/R/W with the common R+W>N configuration; 'sloppy quorum' where operations are performed on 'the first N healthy nodes from the preference list'; hinted handoff stores a hint about the intended recipient during outages; vector clocks capture causality with conflict resolution potentially by the application. All confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: CRDB maps strings to last-write-wins registers, counters (INCR/INCRBY) to op-based counters capturing total increments since last sync, and sets/sorted-sets to Add-Wins Observed-Remove sets.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Data-type-to-CRDT mapping stated in official Redis Enterprise active-active docs.

verification.corrected_value: strings=last-write-wins (wall-clock); counters (INCR/INCRBY)=accumulate total ops across replicas (op-based/PN-counter); sets & sorted-sets=observed-remove / add-wins (OR-Set)
verification.note: Verified on the data-type subpages (the cited overview /develop/ page lacks the specifics). Strings page: 'strings and bitfields are replicated using a last write wins approach' (wall-clock timestamp); 'counters accumulate the total counter operations across all member Active-Active databases in each sync' (59-bit). Sets page: 'observed remove rule is followed ... element add wins.' Sorted-sets page: same observed-remove/add-wins for elements, and 'the score is treated as a counter' for score conflicts. The 'PN-counter' label is a reasonable family name for the op-based accumulating counter the docs describe. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: KeyDB active-replica (active-replica yes + reciprocal replicaof) enables multi-master async replication with pure timestamped last-write-wins; concurrent INCRs are not merged (a counter divergence hazard) since all types use LWW.
prior_art_claims.value: active-replica yes; timestamp LWW on all types
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Config flags and LWW behavior from official KeyDB docs; counter divergence inferred from LWW-on-all-types (docs do not special-case INCR).

prior_art_claims.claim: SWIM decouples failure detection (randomized direct ping + k indirect pings) from infection-style dissemination, making expected detection time, false-positive rate, and per-member message load independent of group size.
prior_art_claims.value: O(1) per-member load; detection time independent of N
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Central claim of the SWIM paper; corroborated across multiple sources.

verification.corrected_value: decouples failure detector (randomized direct ping + k indirect ping-req) from infection-style dissemination; detection time, false-positive rate, and per-member load independent of N
verification.note: SWIM (Das/Gupta/Motivala, DSN 2002): protocol has two components, a failure detector and a dissemination component; failure detection via periodic ping of a random member plus k indirect pings (ping-req) if no ack; expected detection time, false positive rate, and per-member message load are independent of group size. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: HashiCorp memberlist implements SWIM with gossip dissemination; the Lifeguard extensions reduce failure-detector false positives by ~50x under CPU/network stress.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 50x figure stated in HashiCorp's Lifeguard blog and paper.

prior_art_claims.claim: Raft is a consensus algorithm equivalent to multi-Paxos that decomposes into leader election, log replication, and safety, designed for understandability and used widely for replicated config/metadata.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Canonical Raft paper.

verification.corrected_value: leader/follower/candidate states; result equivalent to (multi-)Paxos; decomposes into leader election, log replication, safety
verification.note: Verified from extracted PDF text. Abstract: 'Raft is a consensus algorithm for managing a replicated log. It produces a result equivalent to (multi-)Paxos, and it is as efficient as Paxos.' Paper: 'decomposes the consensus problem into three relatively independent subproblems—leader election, log replication, and safety' and 'each server is in one of three states: leader, follower, or candidate.' Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Twemproxy is a proxy that shards Redis/memcached using ketama consistent hashing and supports hash tags, but is a separate hop, lacks automatic resharding, and can become a bottleneck/extra failure surface.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: From twemproxy README; ketama and hash-tag support documented.

prior_art_claims.claim: Envoy's redis_proxy filter supports Redis Cluster sharding with a ReadPolicy (MASTER, PREFER_MASTER, REPLICA, PREFER_REPLICA); all non-MASTER policies may return stale data due to async replication.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: ReadPolicy enum documented in Envoy v3 redis_proxy API reference.

prior_art_claims.claim: MementoHash is a minimal-memory consistent hash that handles arbitrary node removal (unlike JumpHash) and, unlike AnchorHash/DxHash, does not require fixing maximum cluster capacity up front.
prior_art_claims.value: arbitrary removal; no fixed max capacity
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Claims from the MementoHash paper abstract via search; not deeply benchmark-verified in-session.

verification.corrected_value: handles random/arbitrary node failures (unlike JumpHash); does not require fixing overall cluster capacity (unlike AnchorHash and DxHash), scales indefinitely
verification.note: Verified from extracted PDF abstract text (not the parroted WebFetch): 'Unlike JumpHash, MementoHash can handle random failures. Moreover, MementoHash does not require fixing the overall capacity of the cluster (as AnchorHash and DxHash do), allowing it to scale indefinitely.' Confirmed (upgrading from original medium confidence).

mechanisms.name: Fixed 16384 CRC16 hash slots
mechanisms.what_it_does: Maps every key to one of 16384 slots via CRC16(key) mod 16384; slots are the unit of ownership and migration, decoupling keys from physical nodes.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the slot indirection (clean ownership transfer, hash-tag multi-key locality, MOVED/ASK compatibility), and keep 16384/CRC16 for client compatibility so existing Redis Cluster clients work unchanged. Adapt by making the slot count a build-time constant abstraction and the slot->node map a consensus-managed object instead of a gossiped bitmap, so we are not bound to the ~1000-node bitmap ceiling internally.

mechanisms.name: Smart-client MOVED/ASK routing
mechanisms.what_it_does: Clients cache a slot->node map, send directly to the owning node, and self-correct on -MOVED (permanent) and -ASK (transient, with ASKING) redirections, avoiding a proxy hop.
mechanisms.rationale: Zero-extra-hop routing is exactly aligned with max-throughput-per-core; it removes a proxy from the data path and is what every mature Redis client already speaks. IronCache must emit MOVED/ASK and CLUSTER SLOTS/SHARDS so the existing client ecosystem just works.

mechanisms.name: Proxy-based routing (twemproxy / Envoy redis_proxy / dynomite)
mechanisms.what_it_does: A separate proxy process shards keys (ketama / cluster-aware) and routes; Envoy adds ReadPolicy for replica reads, Dynomite adds Dynamo-style multi-DC replication around single-node stores.
mechanisms.rationale: A mandatory proxy adds a network hop, a CPU tax, and an extra failure/operational surface that contradicts the single-binary, max-efficiency goal. Smart-client routing dominates for the core path. (An optional embedded proxy/router mode can be revisited later for dumb clients, but not as the default.)

mechanisms.name: Jump consistent hash
mechanisms.what_it_does: Stateless ~5-line function mapping a 64-bit key to a bucket in O(ln n) with near-perfect balance and minimal key movement when bucket count grows.
mechanisms.rationale: Its hard constraint that buckets be 0..N-1 sequential (only the last bucket may be added/removed) is incompatible with clusters where any node can fail or be decommissioned. Great for fixed-N sharding, wrong for an elastic cache. Slots + a consensus-managed assignment give us elasticity jump hash cannot.

mechanisms.name: Rendezvous / HRW hashing
mechanisms.what_it_does: Assigns each key to the node with the highest hash(key,node) weight; minimal disruption on node change, no ring/state, O(n) (O(log n) with skeleton tree).
mechanisms.rationale: Use HRW not for the primary key->slot mapping (slots win for migration/compat) but for secondary placement decisions: assigning replica sets to slots, weighting heterogeneous nodes, and rebalancing — it needs no stored ring and naturally moves only the affected node's share.

mechanisms.name: AnchorHash / MementoHash
mechanisms.what_it_does: Minimal-memory consistent hashes that, unlike jump hash, support arbitrary bucket removal; MementoHash additionally avoids fixing a maximum capacity up front.
mechanisms.rationale: Candidate engines for internal rebalancing/placement when a non-slot, low-memory mapping with arbitrary removal is wanted. Track as research; not the primary client-facing partitioning scheme (which stays slot-based for compatibility).

mechanisms.name: Async leader-follower replication
mechanisms.what_it_does: Master replicates to replicas asynchronously; replicas promote via configEpoch-versioned elections on failure; WAIT offers bounded synchronous acks.
mechanisms.rationale: Async primary/replica is the right default for a cache (latency over durability) and matches Redis semantics clients expect. Borrow it, expose WAIT for callers who need bounded sync, and document the partition-loss window honestly.

mechanisms.name: Dynamo leaderless quorums (sloppy quorum, hinted handoff, vector clocks)
mechanisms.what_it_does: N/R/W quorums with R+W>N, sloppy quorum over first-N-healthy nodes, hinted handoff during outages, vector clocks with app-resolved conflicts.
mechanisms.rationale: For a Redis-compatible cache the read/write amplification (R+W replica contacts per op) and app-level conflict resolution via vector clocks are too costly and non-idiomatic; they trade exactly the per-core efficiency we are optimizing for. Hinted handoff is a useful idea to selectively adapt for replica catch-up, but the full quorum model is rejected as the default.

mechanisms.name: Active-active CRDTs (CRDB data-type mapping)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Maps strings to LWW registers, counters to op-based PN-counters (merging concurrent increments), sets/zsets to Add-Wins Observed-Remove sets for conflict-free geo-replication.
mechanisms.rationale: The correct model for multi-region active-active, and strictly better than KeyDB's blanket LWW (which loses concurrent counter increments). Adapt as an opt-in, clearly separate consistency mode in a later milestone — not the default, because per-key CRDT metadata costs memory, violating the minimal-memory goal for the common case.

mechanisms.name: KeyDB blanket timestamp LWW active-replica
mechanisms.what_it_does: Multi-master async replication where every write is timestamped and newest wins across all data types.
mechanisms.rationale: Simplicity is appealing but applying LWW to counters/sets silently drops concurrent updates (a counter losing increments is a correctness bug for many users). If we do active-active, do it with proper per-type CRDTs (CRDB-style), not blanket LWW.

mechanisms.name: Raft for slot-map / membership / config
mechanisms.what_it_does: Understandable consensus (leader election + replicated log + safety) for a strongly-consistent metadata/config object.
mechanisms.rationale: A small Raft group owning the authoritative slot->node map, epoch, and membership gives atomic, linearizable topology changes and a clean source of truth for resharding — avoiding Redis's gossip-propagated bitmap convergence races. Keep the Raft group small (3-5 config nodes) off the data hot path.

mechanisms.name: SWIM membership + infection-style dissemination
mechanisms.what_it_does: Randomized direct+indirect pinging for failure detection with gossip dissemination; per-member load and detection time independent of cluster size.
mechanisms.rationale: Replace Redis's O(N^2) full-mesh cluster bus with SWIM for data-plane liveness so membership scales past ~1000 nodes with bounded per-node overhead — directly serving the efficiency goal.

mechanisms.name: Lifeguard SWIM extensions
mechanisms.what_it_does: Local-health-aware adjustments to SWIM probe timing/suspicion that cut false positives ~50x under CPU starvation and network loss.
mechanisms.rationale: A cache node under GC/CPU pressure must not be wrongly evicted, triggering needless failover. Lifeguard is proven in production (Consul/Serf) and should be adopted with the SWIM layer; a mature Rust SWIM crate likely already implements it.

mechanisms.name: Atomic (fork-streamed) slot migration
mechanisms.what_it_does: Child-process snapshot of migrating slots + incremental mutation streaming + atomic ownership handoff, replacing per-key MIGRATE round-trips.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the modern fix for resharding pain (no inconsistent half-migrated slots, no large-key OOM, far fewer round-trips). Build IronCache resharding this way from the start; in Rust, leverage copy-on-write fork or an in-process snapshot iterator over the slot's data structure.

mechanisms.name: Hash tags for multi-key locality
mechanisms.what_it_does: {tag} substring forces co-location of related keys in one slot so MGET/MSET/transactions/Lua over them stay single-slot.
mechanisms.rationale: Essential for any multi-key operation in a sharded cache and already a client convention. Implement identical {first-non-empty-brace} semantics for drop-in compatibility, and enforce CROSSSLOT errors otherwise.

ironcache_implications: Adopt Redis-Cluster-compatible client semantics for multi-node v1: 16384 slots, CRC16/XMODEM, {hash-tag} co-location, CROSSSLOT errors, and MOVED/ASK redirection plus CLUSTER SLOTS/SHARDS, so the entire existing Redis cluster client ecosystem works against IronCache with zero client changes.

Separate the control plane from the data plane: own the authoritative slot->node map, config epoch, and membership in a small (3-5 node) Raft group rather than gossiping slot bitmaps; this gives atomic, linearizable resharding and removes Redis's ~1000-node bitmap ceiling as an internal constraint while still emitting a Redis-shaped slot map to clients.

Use SWIM (+Lifeguard) for data-plane liveness/failure detection so per-node overhead and detection time stay constant as the cluster grows, instead of a full-mesh cluster bus that is O(N^2) in connections.

Implement atomic slot migration (Valkey-9.0 style: fork/COW or in-process snapshot iterator + incremental mutation stream + atomic ownership handoff) from day one; never ship the legacy per-key MIGRATE model, which is slow, non-atomic, and OOM-prone on large keys.

Keep async primary/replica replication as the default consistency contract (latency-first, cache-appropriate), expose WAIT for bounded sync, and document the partition write-loss window explicitly in the README.

Design the single-node storage engine with a slot-indexed structure (e.g., per-slot shards/dictionaries like Valkey 8.0's per-slot dict) even before clustering exists, so multi-node is a bolt-on, not a rewrite — this is the concrete realization of 'single-node-first path to multi-node'.

Treat active-active/geo-replication as an opt-in mode in a later milestone using proper per-type CRDTs (LWW-register strings, op-based PN-counters, Add-Wins OR-Sets a la Redis CRDB), and reject KeyDB-style blanket LWW because it silently loses concurrent counter increments; gate the per-key CRDT metadata cost behind this mode to protect the minimal-memory default.

Reject a mandatory proxy in the data path (twemproxy/Envoy/dynomite) to preserve max-throughput-per-core; if a 'dumb client' path is ever needed, offer it as an optional embedded router mode, not the default topology.

Consider HRW/rendezvous (stateless, minimal-disruption) for internal placement decisions — assigning replica sets to slots and weighting heterogeneous-capacity nodes — rather than for the client-facing key mapping.

Tune failure-detection defaults for cache workloads: pick a node-timeout default (Redis uses 15000 ms) with profiles for LAN vs cloud/WAN, and lean on Lifeguard-style local-health awareness so GC/CPU-starved nodes are not falsely evicted and made to fail over.

research_papers.title: A Fast, Minimal Memory, Consistent Hash Algorithm (Jump Consistent Hash)
research_papers.key_idea: A ~5-line, zero-memory, O(ln n) consistent hash that distributes keys near-perfectly and minimizes movement when bucket count grows, at the cost of requiring sequential bucket numbering.
research_papers.relevance: Defines the trade-off space for partitioning; its sequential-bucket limitation is exactly why IronCache should use a slot+consensus map instead of jump hash for an elastic cluster.

research_papers.title: SWIM: Scalable Weakly-consistent Infection-style Process Group Membership Protocol
research_papers.key_idea: Decouples randomized ping-based failure detection from infection-style membership dissemination so per-member load and detection time are independent of group size.
research_papers.relevance: The recommended membership/failure-detection layer for IronCache, replacing Redis's O(N^2) full-mesh cluster bus.

research_papers.title: Lifeguard: Local Health Awareness for More Accurate Failure Detection
research_papers.key_idea: Adds local-health awareness to SWIM probe timing and suspicion, cutting false positives ~50x under CPU starvation and network loss.
research_papers.relevance: Hardens IronCache's SWIM layer against falsely evicting GC/CPU-stressed cache nodes and triggering needless failovers.

research_papers.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft)
research_papers.key_idea: A Paxos-equivalent consensus protocol decomposed into leader election, log replication, and safety, optimized for understandability and practical implementation.
research_papers.relevance: Basis for IronCache's strongly-consistent slot-map/membership/config control plane.

research_papers.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
research_papers.key_idea: Leaderless availability via N/R/W quorums (R+W>N), sloppy quorum + hinted handoff under partition, and vector-clock causality with app-level conflict resolution.
research_papers.relevance: Defines the high-availability/eventual-consistency end of the spectrum; IronCache rejects the full quorum model for the default path but may selectively adapt hinted handoff.

research_papers.title: Anna: A KVS for Any Scale
research_papers.key_idea: Coordination-free actors merging lattice/CRDT-based state, achieving up to 700x Masstree while offering flexible (coordination-free) consistency across scales.
research_papers.relevance: Reference design for IronCache's optional active-active/CRDT mode and for how far coordination-free per-core throughput can be pushed.

research_papers.title: MementoHash: A Stateful, Minimal Memory, Best Performing Consistent Hash Algorithm
research_papers.key_idea: A minimal-memory consistent hash that supports arbitrary node removal (unlike JumpHash) and does not require fixing maximum capacity up front (unlike AnchorHash/DxHash).
research_papers.relevance: Candidate internal placement/rebalancing engine; informs the survey of post-ketama consistent-hashing options for IronCache.

research_papers.title: AnchorHash: A Scalable Consistent Hash
research_papers.key_idea: Consistent hashing with very low memory and fast lookups supporting arbitrary bucket removal, given a fixed maximum capacity.
research_papers.relevance: Comparison point for IronCache's partitioning research; trade-off is the fixed-capacity requirement MementoHash later removes.

open_questions: Should IronCache keep exactly 16384 slots for wire compatibility, or expose a larger/configurable slot count internally while presenting 16384 to clients via a mapping layer?
Can a fork/COW snapshot for atomic slot migration be done efficiently in a Rust single binary, or do we need an in-process MVCC/snapshot-iterator over per-slot structures to avoid doubling RSS?
Is a single Raft group for the slot map sufficient at target scale, or do we need a sharded/hierarchical config plane (multi-Raft) for very large clusters?
Does SWIM membership compose cleanly with Redis-Cluster-shaped CLUSTER SLOTS/SHARDS output that clients poll, or do we need a translation/cache layer to present a Redis-style view over SWIM state?
What is the right default failure-detection timeout and suspicion configuration for cache workloads across LAN vs cloud/WAN, balancing fast failover against false positives under GC pauses?
For the optional active-active mode, what is the per-key memory overhead of PN-counter/OR-Set metadata, and can it be made opt-in per-key/per-keyspace so the minimal-memory default is unaffected?
How should multi-key/transaction/Lua semantics behave during atomic slot migration to avoid the -TRYAGAIN/CROSSSLOT corner cases that plague Redis Cluster?
Should replica reads be supported (Envoy-style ReadPolicy) given async replication staleness, and if so how is staleness bounded and surfaced to clients?

proposed_issues.title: Decision: single-node-first roadmap with slot-ready storage layout
proposed_issues.summary: Commit to shipping a single-node binary first while structuring the storage engine around per-slot shards so multi-node clustering is a bolt-on rather than a rewrite. Capture the staged path: single node -> async replica -> sharded cluster -> optional active-active.

proposed_issues.title: Design: Redis-Cluster-compatible client contract (16384 slots, CRC16, hash tags, MOVED/ASK)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify the client-facing clustering wire contract — CRC16/XMODEM slotting into 16384 slots, {hash-tag} co-location, CROSSSLOT errors, MOVED/ASK redirection, and CLUSTER SLOTS/SHARDS — so existing Redis cluster clients work unchanged.

proposed_issues.title: Design: Raft-managed authoritative slot map and membership control plane
proposed_issues.summary: Define a small (3-5 node) Raft group that owns the linearizable slot->node map, config epoch, and membership, replacing gossip-propagated slot bitmaps and removing the ~1000-node bitmap ceiling as an internal constraint.

proposed_issues.title: Design: SWIM + Lifeguard data-plane membership and failure detection
proposed_issues.summary: Adopt a SWIM-based membership layer with Lifeguard local-health awareness for constant per-node overhead and ~50x fewer false positives, and decide how to present a Redis-shaped cluster view over SWIM state.

proposed_issues.title: Design: atomic, fork/snapshot-streamed slot migration
proposed_issues.summary: Specify Valkey-9.0-style resharding — snapshot a migrating slot, stream incremental mutations, then atomically hand off ownership — so migration is non-blocking, crash-safe, and free of inconsistent half-migrated slots and large-key OOM.

proposed_issues.title: Research: post-ketama consistent hashing (jump, HRW, AnchorHash, MementoHash) for internal placement
proposed_issues.summary: Benchmark jump hash, rendezvous/HRW, AnchorHash, and MementoHash for replica-set placement, heterogeneous-node weighting, and rebalancing, documenting why slot-based partitioning stays the client-facing scheme.

proposed_issues.title: Decision: replication and consistency default (async primary/replica + WAIT)
proposed_issues.summary: Adopt asynchronous primary/replica replication as the default with WAIT for bounded synchronous acks, and document the partition write-loss window; reject Dynamo-style quorums as the default path.

proposed_issues.title: Research: opt-in active-active CRDT mode (LWW strings, PN-counters, OR-Sets)
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate a CRDB-style per-type CRDT mode for geo active-active, quantifying per-key metadata overhead, and explicitly reject KeyDB-style blanket LWW that loses concurrent counter increments.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal: mandatory proxy in the data path
proposed_issues.summary: Declare that IronCache will not require a proxy (twemproxy/Envoy/dynomite) on the hot path; routing is smart-client via MOVED/ASK to preserve max throughput per core. An optional embedded router may be revisited later.

proposed_issues.title: Epic: IronCache distributed clustering (partitioning, routing, replication, membership)
proposed_issues.summary: Umbrella epic tracking the multi-node design: slot-based partitioning with Redis-compatible client semantics, Raft control plane, SWIM data-plane membership, atomic slot migration, async replication, and an optional CRDT active-active mode.

dimensions.verify_notes:
All 17 claims independently re-verified against fresh primary sources (WebFetch of cited URLs plus PDF text extraction via pdftotext for the academic papers, GitHub API for the antirez comment, and Redis source files for code defaults). 16 confirmed, 1 corrected (source, not value).

Key findings:
- redis-cluster-node-timeout-default: value 15000ms is correct, but the CITED source (scaling page) does not state it — it only shows 5000 as an example. The authoritative default comes from Redis 7.4 redis.conf ('# cluster-node-timeout 15000') and src/config.c (default 15000). Marked corrected to point at the source of truth.
- Two claims originally rated medium confidence (valkey-8-setslot-replicated, mementohash-vs-anchorhash) were fully confirmed verbatim from primary sources.
- crc16 check value 0x31C3 was cross-checked against an independent second source (reveng CRC catalogue) in addition to the Redis spec.
- For the academic PDFs (Jump Hash, Raft, MementoHash, Dynamo, SWIM) I extracted actual text via pdftotext rather than trusting WebFetch summaries — important because some WebFetch responses parroted my prompt phrasing back as if it were a quote (notably MementoHash and a garbled Raft abstract line 'more understandable than both Paxos and Raft'). The genuine source text confirmed the substance in every case.

Minor nuances (did not change verdicts):
- redis-cluster-hash-tags: the cluster-spec page describes the multi-key same-slot requirement and shows -TRYAGAIN during resharding but does not print the literal token 'CROSSSLOT'; the CROSSSLOT error itself is established Redis behavior. Core hash-tag algorithm confirmed.
- redis-crdb-datatype-mapping: the cited overview URL (/develop/) lacks the per-type specifics; verified on the /develop/data-types/{strings,sets,sorted-sets}/ subpages instead. The docs describe counters as accumulating total operations across replicas (op-based counter); the claim's 'PN-counter' label is an accurate family descriptor.
- jump-hash-constant: the actual Figure 1 code uses (double(1LL << 31)) — a 64-bit shift — matching the claim's (1<<31) intent.

dimensions.summary:
DragonflyDB (current release v1.39.0, 2026-06-09) is the strongest modern "efficiency per core" claim in the cache space and the most direct architectural competitor for IronCache. Its thesis: a single multi-threaded process can replace a Redis Cluster by scaling vertically with a shared-nothing, thread-per-core design over the helio framework on io_uring (Linux 5.11+, epoll fallback). The keyspace is sharded into N parts (N <= thread count); each shard is owned and accessed by exactly one thread, and threads communicate only by message passing — never mutexes/spinlocks on the hot path. Async concurrency is built on userspace fibers (Boost.Fibers + helio fb2), and the connection fiber doubles as the transaction coordinator. Headline marketing claims: "25X more throughput" vs Redis and "up to 80% less resources"; the README's own AWS numbers show ~3.8M QPS on c6gn.16xlarge (crossing 3.8M / "25X" vs single Redis), 10M QPS SET and 15M QPS GET at --pipeline=30, and a separate blog shows 6.43M ops/sec on a 64-vCPU c7gn.16xlarge (P50 0.3ms, P99 1.1ms). Notably, on a single-core m5.large, Dragonfly is only ~comparable to Redis (173K vs 159K SET QPS) — the win is purely vertical scaling, which is the single most important framing point for IronCache.

The two load-bearing data-structure innovations are Dashtable and forkless snapshotting. Dashtable is an implementation of the Dash paper (arXiv 2003.07302), itself an evolution of 1979 extendible hashing: a directory of pointers to fixed-size segments, each segment a flat open-addressed mini-hashtable. In Dragonfly's compile-time config a segment has 60 buckets (56 regular + 4 stash), 14 slots/bucket, 840 records/segment. Growth is incremental per-segment split (constant allocation), avoiding Redis dict's 2N bucket-array reallocation and its ~48N-byte peak during incremental rehash. The doc's own arithmetic: Redis dict overhead is 16-32 bytes/item (dictEntry is 24 bytes + 8-byte bucket pointer); Dashtable is ~19N bytes at 100% util and 22-32 bytes/item in practice, i.e. 6-16 bytes overhead, with directory cost effectively vanishing (~9.6KB for 1M items, fits in L1). On debug-populate of 20M small items, Dragonfly used 1GB vs Redis 6's 1.73GB (1.0GB of which was metadata). This is the clearest "metadata tax" argument in the literature and directly relevant to IronCache's "minimal memory" goal.

Forkless snapshotting (rdbsave.md) is Dragonfly's standout availability feature and the reason the dashtable metadata investment pays off twice. Instead of Redis's fork()+copy-on-write BGSAVE (which can spike memory toward 2-3x under write load), each shard runs a serialization fiber over its own Dashtable while a per-shard monotonic epoch/version counter establishes a virtual "cut." The serialization loop sends any entry with version <= cut.epoch and bumps its version; an OnWriteHook intercepts concurrent mutations and, for conservative (point-in-time/backup) snapshots, pushes the OLD value to the sink before overwriting — guaranteeing snapshot isolation with zero fork and constant memory overhead regardless of dataset size. A "relaxed" variant (for replication) sends new values / incremental diffs instead, avoiding a separate changelog. README benchmark: on ~5GB, Dragonfly was 30% more memory-efficient idle and showed no visible spike during bgsave, while Redis peaked at almost 3X Dragonfly's memory. Snapshot isolation is enforced at Dash *bucket* granularity (blobs never partially cover a bucket).

Atomic multi-key/multi-shard transactions use VLL (Very Lightweight Locking, Ren/Thomson/Abadi, VLDBJ). VLL replaces a heavyweight lock manager with two per-record integers and a global transaction queue, using logical ordering rather than lock objects. Dragonfly adapts this: a single global atomic counter assigns monotonically increasing txids; each shard keeps an ordered transaction queue; multi-shard commands are split into per-shard subcommands that "hop" to the owning threads, execute, and aggregate. Because shards are single-threaded, the common single-shard case needs no lock at all (the thread simply owns the data); multi-shard coordination is deadlock-free by txid ordering. The allocator is mimalloc v2.2.4 (vendored with 5 Dragonfly patches adding allocation stats). Protocol surface: RESP (Redis ~185 commands, ~Redis 5.0 API) plus full Memcached text protocol except cas; HTTP and Prometheus /metrics share the main TCP port via protocol auto-detection.

For IronCache the takeaways are sharp. Borrow wholesale: shared-nothing thread-per-core, io_uring (Rust has glommio/monoio/tokio-uring), single-thread-owns-shard to eliminate hot-path locks, and the forkless versioned-snapshot design (a fork-based COW approach is a non-starter for a Rust single-binary that wants bounded memory). Adapt: Dashtable's segmented extendible hashing is the right model, but IronCache can revisit segment geometry, SIMD fingerprint probing, and integrate compression-awareness into segment metadata; VLL-style txn coordination should be kept but IronCache must decide early whether to expose MULTI/EXEC and Lua at all. Reject for now: Dragonfly's emulated-only cluster mode and its C++/Boost.Fibers stack (Rust async + a uring runtime is the idiomatic equivalent). The single most important strategic lesson: Dragonfly's efficiency story is vertical scaling + metadata reduction + forkless persistence, NOT raw single-core speed — IronCache's "max throughput per core" goal is a genuinely different and more aggressive target than Dragonfly's, and must be benchmarked single-core, not just at 64 vCPUs.
