mechanisms.name: Shared-nothing thread-per-core sharding
mechanisms.what_it_does: Partitions the keyspace into N shards (N <= thread count); each shard is owned and accessed by exactly one thread. Threads never share a data structure; they communicate only via message passing. Key routing is k = HASH(KEY) % N.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the canonical way to hit 'max throughput per core' with no hot-path locks and is exactly IronCache's target architecture. Rust enforces this even better than C++ via ownership (a shard struct moved into a per-core task is statically un-shareable).

mechanisms.name: io_uring async I/O with epoll fallback
mechanisms.what_it_does: All network and disk I/O is submitted through io_uring (Linux 5.11+), including registered buffers and multishot receives; falls back to epoll on older kernels.
mechanisms.rationale: Batched syscall submission/completion is the lowest-overhead I/O path on Linux. Rust has mature equivalents (monoio, glommio, tokio-uring). Must keep an epoll/kqueue fallback so the single binary still runs on macOS dev boxes and pre-5.11 kernels.

mechanisms.name: Userspace fibers (Boost.Fibers + helio fb2)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Concurrency is expressed as cheap stackful fibers scheduled per thread; the connection fiber also acts as the transaction coordinator. std::thread/std::mutex are banned to avoid deadlocks and contention.
mechanisms.rationale: The model (cooperative per-core scheduling) is right, but IronCache should use Rust async tasks (stackless, no per-fiber stack memory) on a thread-per-core runtime rather than stackful fibers — better memory footprint, which matters for the 'minimal memory' goal.

mechanisms.name: Dashtable (segmented extendible hashing)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Directory of pointers to fixed-size segments; each segment is a flat open-addressed mini-hashtable (56 regular + 4 stash buckets, 14 slots each, 840 records). Growth is per-segment split (constant allocation), not full 2N rehash. Open addressing + small fingerprints avoid per-entry dictEntry, cutting overhead to 6-16 bytes/item vs Redis's 16-32.
mechanisms.rationale: Directly serves IronCache's minimal-memory + low-tail-latency goals: no rehash latency spikes, L1-resident directory, stateless scan, and metadata small enough to hang extra bits (versioning, cache rank) off each entry. This is the single most copyable idea.

mechanisms.name: Stash buckets + neighbor probing
mechanisms.what_it_does: Each segment reserves 4 stash buckets to absorb spillover from the 56 regular buckets; an item probes its home bucket, the right neighbor, then stashes, before declaring the segment full and splitting.
mechanisms.rationale: Raises load factor before splitting (less memory waste), but the exact 56/4/14 geometry should be re-tuned for IronCache's cache lines and SIMD width; consider AVX/NEON fingerprint scan over a bucket's 14 slots in one vector op.

mechanisms.name: VLL transaction coordination
mechanisms.what_it_does: Global atomic counter assigns monotonic txids; each shard has an ordered transaction queue; multi-shard commands split into per-shard subcommands that hop to owning threads, run, and aggregate. Single-shard ops need no lock (thread owns data); multi-shard is deadlock-free by txid order.
mechanisms.rationale: Keep the lock-free single-shard fast path and txid-ordered multi-shard hop. But IronCache should decide whether to support MULTI/EXEC/WATCH and Lua at all in M1 — full transactional generality is a large cost the 'most efficient cache' may not need.

mechanisms.name: Forkless versioned snapshot
mechanisms.what_it_does: Per-shard monotonic epoch sets a cut; a serialization fiber emits entries with version <= cut and bumps their version; an OnWriteHook pushes the OLD value to the sink before any concurrent mutation (conservative) or the new value/diff (relaxed). No fork(), constant memory overhead, bucket-granularity isolation.
mechanisms.rationale: Redis fork()+COW can spike memory ~3X under write load — fatal for a Rust single binary targeting bounded memory. The versioned-cut design gives point-in-time RDB-compatible snapshots with O(1) extra memory and is enabled precisely because Dashtable has spare metadata bits.

mechanisms.name: mimalloc allocator
mechanisms.what_it_does: Uses Microsoft mimalloc v2.2.4 (vendored, 5 patches for allocation stats / heap-collect fixes) as the process allocator for low fragmentation and fast thread-local alloc.
mechanisms.rationale: A sharded, low-fragmentation allocator is essential. In Rust, evaluate mimalloc-rust vs jemallocator vs a custom slab; the choice interacts with per-shard arenas and with returning freed pages to the OS (RSS accuracy matters for the memory claim).

mechanisms.name: RESP + Memcached + HTTP on one port
mechanisms.what_it_does: Auto-detects protocol on the main TCP port (6379): RESP (~185 Redis cmds, ~Redis 5 API), Memcached text protocol (all but cas), and an HTTP console + Prometheus /metrics.
mechanisms.rationale: Redis-wire compat is a hard IronCache requirement; sharing the port with HTTP/metrics is ergonomic for a single-binary story. IronCache should target a newer Redis API baseline (RESP3, Redis 7 commands) and decide whether Memcached compat is in scope (likely a non-goal initially).

mechanisms.name: Unified adaptive cache eviction (cache_mode)
mechanisms.what_it_does: A single adaptive eviction policy (claimed higher hit rate than LRU/LFU with zero memory overhead) that only evicts near maxmemory, with eviction metadata folded into Dashtable entries.
mechanisms.rationale: 'Zero memory overhead' eviction by piggybacking on hashtable metadata is exactly the kind of efficiency IronCache wants, but the claim is unquantified — IronCache should design its own policy (e.g., TinyLFU/W-TinyLFU/S3-FIFO) and benchmark hit-rate honestly rather than copy an opaque heuristic.

mechanisms.name: Emulated-only cluster mode
mechanisms.what_it_does: Per the README, cluster_mode supports only 'emulated' — the cluster command API on a single process — rather than true multi-node sharding (which has since evolved in newer releases).
mechanisms.rationale: IronCache's stated path is single-node to multi-node as a real distributed system; an emulated single-process cluster is a stopgap, not a model to copy. Design real multi-node from the architecture spec instead.

ironcache_implications: Adopt shared-nothing thread-per-core as the core architecture: one Rust task/runtime per physical core, each owning a disjoint shard. Use Rust ownership to make cross-shard data access a compile error, going beyond Dragonfly's convention-only enforcement.

Benchmark single-core first. Dragonfly's own README shows near-parity with Redis at 1 vCPU (173K vs 159K SET QPS); its 25X is purely vertical scaling. IronCache's 'max throughput per core' goal demands a single-core target (e.g. beat Redis 7 and Dragonfly per-core), not just a 64-vCPU headline.

Implement a Dashtable-equivalent: directory + fixed-size open-addressed segments with stash buckets, incremental per-segment split, and SIMD fingerprint probing across a bucket's slots. Target <=16 bytes/item overhead and reserve spare metadata bits per entry for versioning + eviction rank from day one.

Make snapshotting forkless and versioned, never fork()+COW. Use a per-shard epoch cut + write-hook that emits the pre-image (or diff) of concurrently-mutated entries, giving point-in-time snapshots with O(1) extra memory — this is what lets IronCache claim bounded memory under write load.

Use io_uring with an epoll/kqueue fallback so the single static binary runs on Linux 5.11+ at peak speed but still starts on older kernels and macOS dev machines. Pick the runtime (monoio/glommio vs custom) as an explicit M1 decision.
Prefer Rust stackless async tasks over stackful fibers to cut per-connection/per-coroutine memory — directly serving the minimal-memory goal where Dragonfly's Boost.Fibers stacks cost more.

Decide the transaction surface early. Keep VLL's lock-free single-shard fast path and txid-ordered multi-shard hop, but treat full MULTI/EXEC/WATCH/Lua as a scoped decision — a pure cache may ship a smaller transactional surface to stay lean.
Treat Memcached protocol and emulated cluster as likely non-goals; commit instead to RESP3 / modern Redis API compatibility and a real (not emulated) single-node-to-multi-node story.

Choose the allocator deliberately (mimalloc-rust vs jemallocator vs per-shard slab arenas) and measure RSS return-to-OS, since the 30%+ idle memory-efficiency claim lives or dies on fragmentation and page reclaim, not just per-entry overhead.

Design an honest, quantified eviction policy (S3-FIFO / W-TinyLFU) with metadata folded into hashtable entries ('zero overhead'), and publish hit-rate numbers — avoid Dragonfly's unquantified 'better than LRU/LFU' claim.

research_papers.title: Dash: Scalable Hashing on Persistent Memory
research_papers.key_idea: Extendible-hashing variant with fixed-size segments, fingerprints, stash/bucket balancing, and optimistic concurrency designed for persistent memory; achieves high load factor with cheap incremental resize and stateless scan.
research_papers.relevance: Direct basis for Dragonfly's Dashtable and the strongest template for IronCache's in-memory hash index; the fingerprint + segment-split mechanics translate directly to a SIMD-probed Rust implementation.

research_papers.title: VLL: a lock manager redesign for main memory database systems
research_papers.key_idea: Replaces a heavyweight lock manager with two per-record integers (CX/CS) plus a global transaction queue, using logical ordering rather than lock objects; separate contention-free and contention execution paths.
research_papers.relevance: The transactional foundation Dragonfly cites; informs how IronCache can offer atomic multi-key ops on a shared-nothing layout without mutexes, and where to draw the line on transactional generality.

research_papers.title: Extendible Hashing - A Fast Access Method for Dynamic Files
research_papers.key_idea: Original directory-of-buckets scheme that grows incrementally by splitting buckets and doubling the directory, avoiding full-table rehash.
research_papers.relevance: The 47-year-old root idea behind Dashtable; understanding it clarifies which incremental-growth and directory-doubling tradeoffs IronCache inherits and can improve.

research_papers.title: S3-FIFO: FIFO queues are all you need for cache eviction
research_papers.key_idea: A simple three-FIFO eviction scheme that matches or beats LRU/LFU hit rates with O(1) operations and tiny metadata, scaling well under concurrency.
research_papers.relevance: A concrete, published, quantified alternative to Dragonfly's opaque adaptive cache_mode; a strong candidate for IronCache's 'zero-overhead' eviction policy that can be benchmarked honestly.

open_questions: What is Dragonfly's actual single-core throughput vs Redis 7 / Valkey on identical hardware in 2026? The README's 1-vCPU parity numbers are from m5.large and Redis 6 — IronCache needs fresh per-core data to know the real bar to beat.

How much per-entry metadata does Dashtable actually reserve today (versioning + eviction + expiry bits), and does it still hit the doc's ~19N bytes once all Dragonfly features are enabled? The 6-16 byte overhead may be optimistic in the shipped binary.

Does the relaxed (replication) snapshot's incremental-diff path require an extended RDB format, and how does that interact with point-in-time correctness under heavy multi-key writes? IronCache must decide if it needs both conservative and relaxed variants.

What concrete eviction algorithm does cache_mode implement, and is the 'higher than LRU/LFU with zero memory overhead' claim reproducible? No paper or pseudocode is published; IronCache should not copy it blind.
Stackful fibers vs stackless Rust async: what is the real per-connection memory cost difference at, say, 1M idle connections, and does Rust's stackless model meaningfully improve the memory claim?
How does mimalloc's page reclaim behave under churn for Dragonfly's RSS, and which Rust allocator best preserves the 'memory returns to OS' property that underpins the idle-efficiency claim?

Is there an up-to-date third-party (non-vendor) benchmark of Dragonfly vs KeyDB vs Valkey on per-core efficiency, since most numbers (incl. KeyDB winning on SET/mixed) come from blogs of varying rigor?

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: Shared-nothing thread-per-core architecture for IronCache
proposed_issues.summary: Define IronCache's core as one Rust runtime/task per physical core, each owning a disjoint shard with no shared mutable state, communicating via message passing. Establish this as the foundational architecture decision against which all other designs are measured.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Segmented extendible-hashing index (Dashtable/Dash-style) with SIMD fingerprint probing
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a directory + fixed-size open-addressed segment hash index with stash buckets and incremental per-segment split, targeting <=16 bytes/item overhead and reserving per-entry metadata bits for version, expiry, and eviction rank. Define segment geometry and SIMD slot scan.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Benchmark single-core throughput vs Redis 7 / Valkey / Dragonfly
proposed_issues.summary: Establish the real per-core performance bar. Dragonfly is only ~at parity with Redis at 1 vCPU; IronCache's 'max throughput per core' goal needs fresh, identical-hardware single-core numbers across the field before architecture is frozen.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Forkless versioned point-in-time snapshotting
proposed_issues.summary: Adopt Dragonfly's per-shard epoch-cut + write-hook design (emit pre-image on concurrent mutation) to produce RDB-compatible snapshots with O(1) extra memory and no fork(). Specify conservative (backup) vs relaxed (replication) variants and bucket-granularity isolation.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Transaction surface — VLL single-shard fast path, and whether to support MULTI/EXEC/Lua
proposed_issues.summary: Keep VLL's lock-free single-shard fast path and txid-ordered multi-shard hop, but decide explicitly how much Redis transactional/scripting surface (MULTI/EXEC/WATCH, Lua) IronCache commits to, since full generality is a large cost for a cache.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Async runtime and io_uring strategy with epoll/kqueue fallback
proposed_issues.summary: Choose between monoio/glommio/tokio-uring/custom for the thread-per-core runtime, mandate io_uring on Linux 5.11+ with an epoll/kqueue fallback so the single static binary runs everywhere, and prefer stackless async tasks over stackful fibers for memory.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Eviction policy selection (S3-FIFO / W-TinyLFU) with zero-overhead metadata
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate published, quantified eviction algorithms whose metadata folds into the hash index entries, instead of copying Dragonfly's opaque adaptive cache_mode. Produce honest hit-rate benchmarks vs LRU/LFU.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Allocator choice and RSS return-to-OS behavior
proposed_issues.summary: Decide between mimalloc-rust, jemallocator, and per-shard slab arenas, measuring fragmentation and page reclaim, since IronCache's idle/under-load memory-efficiency claims depend on returning freed memory to the OS, not just per-entry overhead.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: Memcached protocol and emulated single-process cluster mode
proposed_issues.summary: Scope out Memcached text-protocol compatibility and Dragonfly-style emulated cluster mode for the initial milestones; commit instead to RESP3 / modern Redis API and a real single-node-to-multi-node distribution design.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Audit Dragonfly headline claims (25X, 80% less resources, 6.43M RPS) for benchmark conditions
proposed_issues.summary: Document the exact conditions behind Dragonfly's marketing numbers (instance, pipelining, value size, version) and the single-core counter-evidence, so IronCache's own claims and comparisons are framed honestly in the README and vision EPIC.

dimensions.verify_notes: All 19 claims independently re-verified against fresh primary sources on 2026-06-13. README/docs were fetched from raw.githubusercontent.com on the main branch (HEAD commit 2aea1a77bb80909adcd6420b1e012e4c12f36042, dated 2026-06-13), not via cached blob renders. Source-code/default claims (mimalloc version, io_uring kernel floor, fiber primitives) were checked directly against src/external_libs.cmake and AGENTS.md. The latest-version claim was anchored to the GitHub releases API via gh (authoritative), which confirmed v1.39.0 (2026-06-09) as the newest non-prerelease, non-draft release; a stale WebSearch index only knew up to v1.38.1 but that is not a refutation. The two academic citations were corroborated by independent sources: Dash via arXiv:2003.07302 (authors Lu/Hao/Wang/Lo) and VLL via dblp RenTA15 + Springer VLDB Journal 24(5) (authors Ren/Thomson/Abadi). Every claim was confirmed; no corrections or refutations were warranted. Three claims originally tagged 'medium'/'medium' confidence (redis-dictentry-size, dragonfly-cluster-emulated, dragonfly-cache-mode-eviction) and the medium-confidence Graviton3 blog claim were all upgraded to confirmed after direct primary-source verification. Minor note on redis-dictentry-size: the Dragonfly doc frames the dictEntry/rehash analysis against Redis 6 (its benchmarks use Redis 6); the 24-byte/3-pointer figure and 48N peak are presented as Dragonfly's own characterization of the Redis dict, which is what the claim states."}

dimensions.summary:
The eviction literature has bifurcated into two camps that IronCache must reconcile. The first camp optimizes hit ratio with sophisticated recency/frequency hybrids: ARC (FAST'03) self-tunes a parameter p that splits a 2c-entry directory into recency (T1/B1) and frequency (T2/B2) lists, keeps no frequency counts, and "substantially outperforms LRU" while being parameter-free; LIRS (SIGMETRICS'02) uses inter-reference recency to get scan resistance; 2Q, MQ, CAR (the CLOCK variant of ARC), and CLOCK-Pro round out this family. W-TinyLFU (Caffeine) is the strongest of these for in-memory KV: a ~1% LRU admission window feeds a ~99% SLRU main region, gated by a frequency-based admission filter built on a 4-bit Count-Min Sketch (~8 bytes/entry) with a doorkeeper and periodic halving (aging) of all counters; Caffeine adds hill-climbing to adapt the window size. These deliver excellent hit ratios but pay a structural tax: every cache HIT mutates a doubly-linked list under a lock (eager promotion), which caps multicore throughput.

The second, newer camp (the one most aligned with IronCache's "max throughput per core" thesis) abandons LRU's per-hit list surgery in favor of lazy promotion + quick demotion on FIFO queues. S3-FIFO (SOSP'23) uses three fixed-size FIFOs — a 10% small probationary queue, a 90% main queue, and a ghost queue (object IDs only) — with a 2-bit frequency counter capped at 3. On eviction from the small queue, objects with freq>1 are promoted to main; freq≤1 are demoted to ghost; main-queue eviction reinserts-with-decrement if freq>0. The insight: the median one-hit-wonder ratio jumps to 72% when measured over short sequences (first 10% of unique objects), so a small FIFO filters them cheaply. S3-FIFO had the lowest mean miss ratio on 10/14 datasets and top-3 on 13/14 across 6594 traces / 856B requests, with ~6x higher throughput than highly-optimized LRU at 16 cores. SIEVE (NSDI'24) is even simpler: one FIFO + a "hand" pointer that scans tail→head, clearing per-object visited bits and evicting the first unvisited object (a hit just sets the bit, no list move). SIEVE needs only 12-21 lines to retrofit into existing caches (C++, Go, Rust, etc.), is ~16% faster single-threaded and >2x faster at 16 threads than optimized LRU, and has the lowest miss ratio on >45% of web traces (next best: 15%).

For IronCache the concurrency story is decisive. LRU/LFU/ARC/W-TinyLFU all require taking a lock (or doing CAS-heavy list splicing) on the hot read path; under Redis-like single-shard-per-core or sharded designs this is the throughput ceiling. FIFO-based policies need only an atomic flag set on hit, enabling lock-free or near-lock-free reads. SIEVE and S3-FIFO match or beat W-TinyLFU on web/KV hit ratio while being radically more scalable, which is exactly IronCache's goal. The catch: neither maintains the stack property — they are non-monotonic (a bigger cache can, pathologically, give a worse miss ratio), SIEVE has no ghost list so it cannot quickly re-admit a recently-evicted hot object and degrades at very small cache sizes and on scan-heavy block workloads. S3-FIFO's ghost queue mitigates this. SIEVE's middle-of-list eviction also breaks the simple circular-array implementation, requiring a real linked list (still O(1) per op, but pointer-chasing).

Learned/adaptive policies (LeCaR, CACHEUS, LRB, LHD, Hyperbolic, GDSF) are mostly out of scope for an in-memory KV hot path. LeCaR (HotStorage'18) uses regret-minimization over LRU+LFU experts and beats ARC by up to 18x at tiny cache sizes; CACHEUS (FAST'21) generalizes this with SR-LRU and CR-LFU experts. LRB (NSDI'20) approximates Belady's MIN with gradient-boosted trees over per-object feature vectors, cutting CDN WAN/byte traffic ~4-25%, but its per-object feature tracking and model inference are far too heavy for nanosecond-scale in-memory lookups. LHD (NSDI'18) ranks objects by expected hits-per-space via online conditional-probability estimation. IronCache should treat these as research inspiration for an optional, AI-driven offline/background tuner (e.g., choosing window sizes or admission thresholds), not as the primary online policy. Recommendation: ship a SIEVE-or-S3-FIFO-class FIFO policy as the default for lock-friendliness, keep an optional W-TinyLFU-style frequency admission filter (CM-sketch + doorkeeper) for frequency-skewed workloads, expose a ghost queue for re-admission, and reserve learned methods for a background "auto-tuner" service.

prior_art_claims.claim: S3-FIFO partitions the cache into a small probationary FIFO of 10% and a main FIFO of 90% of cache capacity, plus a ghost FIFO that stores only object IDs (no data).
prior_art_claims.value: small=10%, main=90%, ghost stores IDs only
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated identically in author's own blog and confirmed by paper text (10% small queue filters one-hit wonders).

verification.corrected_value: small=10%, main=90%, ghost stores IDs only
verification.note: Paper PDF (re-extracted with pdftotext): 'choose S to use 10% of the cache space ... M then uses 90% of the cache space. The ghost queue G stores the same number of ghost entries (no data) as M.' Corroborated by source code libCacheSim S3FIFO.c DEFAULT_CACHE_PARAMS: fifo-size-ratio=0.10, ghost-size-ratio=0.90. Cited blog also confirms.

prior_art_claims.claim: Each object carries a 2-bit frequency counter capped at a maximum value of 3; on small-queue eviction freq>1 promotes to main, else to ghost; on main-queue eviction freq>0 reinserts with decremented freq, freq==0 evicts.
prior_art_claims.value: 2-bit counter, max=3; promote if freq>1; main reinsert-with-decrement if freq>0
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Author (Juncheng Yang) blog states 2-bit capped at 3 explicitly; a WebFetch PDF helper mis-read it as 3-bit, but author's primary description governs.

verification.corrected_value: 2-bit counter, max=3; promote if freq>1; main reinsert-with-decrement if freq>0
verification.note: Paper: 'two bits per object ... similar to a capped counter with frequency up to 3'; small tail 'moved to M if accessed more than once or G if not'; main 'reinserted with one bit set to 0 (similar to decreasing frequency by 1).' Source code confirms exactly: move-to-main-threshold=2 (so freq>1 -> main, else ghost); main eviction 'if (freq >= 1)' reinsert with new freq = MIN(freq,3)-1, else freq==0 evicts. Cap of 3 visible as MIN(freq,3).

prior_art_claims.claim: On 6594 traces from 14 datasets (856B requests), at 10%-of-objects cache size S3-FIFO had the lowest mean miss ratio on 10 of 14 datasets and was top-3 on 13 of 14; the next best algorithm (LIRS) was best on only a few datasets.
prior_art_claims.value: best on 10/14 datasets, top-3 on 13/14; 6594 traces; 856B requests
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Extracted directly from paper PDF text via pdftotext.

verification.corrected_value: best on 10/14 datasets, top-3 on 13/14; 6594 traces; 856B requests; next best (LIRS) wins on 2 datasets
verification.note: Paper (re-extracted): '6594 production traces from 14 sources ... 856 billion requests'; 'most efficient algorithm on 10 out of the 14 datasets and among the top three most efficient algorithms on 13 datasets. As a comparison, the next best algorithm (LIRS) obtains the highest efficiency on only 2 datasets.' Claim said LIRS 'best on only a few' which is exactly 2 - confirmed and slightly sharpened.

prior_art_claims.claim: S3-FIFO achieves ~6x higher throughput than a highly-optimized LRU implementation (in CacheLib) at 16 cores/threads.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in abstract and intro of paper PDF.

verification.corrected_value: ~6x vs optimized LRU at 16 threads/cores
verification.note: Abstract: 'FIFO queues enable S3-FIFO to achieve good scalability with 6x higher throughput compared to optimized LRU at 16 threads.' Body: 'implemented a prototype in Cachelib ... more than 6x higher throughput than the highly-optimized LRU implementation on 16 cores.'

prior_art_claims.claim: Measured over sequences comprising the first 10% of unique objects in each trace, the median one-hit-wonder ratio is 72%, motivating cheap FIFO filtering of objects accessed only once.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Exact number read from paper PDF text.

verification.corrected_value: median one-hit-wonder ratio = 72% (on sequences comprising 10% of unique objects); 26% on full trace
verification.note: Paper: 'The median one-hit-wonder ratio of all traces, when considering the entire trace, is 26%. However, when focusing on sequences that comprise 10% of the unique objects in each trace, the median one-hit-wonder ratio skyrockets to 72%.' Exact match.

prior_art_claims.claim: SIEVE uses a single FIFO queue and a 'hand' pointer that moves tail→head; new objects are inserted at the head; a cache hit only sets the object's visited bit; on eviction the hand clears visited bits until it finds an unvisited object to evict (the eviction candidate may be in the middle of the list).
prior_art_claims.value: 1 FIFO + hand pointer + 1 visited bit per object; insert at head; evict first unvisited under moving hand
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Described consistently across author blog and paper; mechanism is well-documented.

verification.corrected_value: 1 FIFO + hand pointer (moves tail->head) + 1 visited bit per object; insert at head; evict first unvisited under moving hand (candidate can be mid-list)
verification.note: Cited blog confirms all four parts: 'hand ... moves from the tail to the head'; 'new object is always inserted into the head'; 'cache hit ... sets the visited bit ... to true'; on eviction it resets visited bits and 'continues ... until it encounters an object that has not been visited, and it evicts the object', and 'the eviction candidate in SIEVE is an object that can be in the middle of the list.'

prior_art_claims.claim: SIEVE is ~16% faster than optimized LRU (and ~17% vs TwoQ) single-threaded, and >2x higher throughput than optimized LRU and TwoQ at 16 threads on Meta production traces.
prior_art_claims.value: ~16% faster single-thread; >2x at 16 threads vs optimized LRU
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in SIEVE official site and author blog with matching figures.

verification.corrected_value: ~16% faster single-thread vs optimized LRU (~17% vs TwoQ); >2x at 16 threads vs optimized LRU and TwoQ on Meta trace
verification.note: NSDI'24 paper (re-extracted): 'At a single thread, SIEVE is 16% (17%) faster than the optimized LRU (TwoQ) ... At 16 threads, SIEVE shows more than 2x higher throughput than the optimized LRU and TwoQ on the Meta trace.' Contribution list separately reports 17%/125% higher than LRU at 1/16 threads (Cachelib prototype). All numbers in the claim match.

prior_art_claims.claim: SIEVE has the lowest miss ratio among nine+ state-of-the-art algorithms on more than 45% of evaluated web-cache traces, while the next best algorithm leads on only ~15%; it reduces FIFO's miss ratio by a mean of 21% (up to >42% at the top decile) on a major CDN dataset.
prior_art_claims.value: lowest miss ratio on >45% of traces (next best ~15%); -21% mean vs FIFO
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Consistent across USENIX login article, official site, and author blog.

verification.corrected_value: lowest miss ratio on >45% of 1559 traces vs 9 SOTA algorithms (next best/runner-up TwoQ 15%); reduces FIFO miss ratio mean 21%, >42% at top decile
verification.note: Paper: 'SIEVE has a lower miss ratio than 9 state-of-the-art algorithms on more than 45% of the 1559 traces, while the next best algorithm only has a lower miss ratio on 15%' (runner-up = TwoQ). Login.usenix article (cited URL) was HTTP 403 to the fetcher, but the underlying NSDI PDF is the stronger primary source and confirms. The -21% mean / >42% top-decile FIFO reduction is corroborated by web search of the same article ('reduces FIFO's miss ratio by more than 42% on 10% of the traces ... with a mean of 21%').

prior_art_claims.claim: SIEVE required only 12-21 lines of code to retrofit into five existing cache libraries (C++, Go, JavaScript, Python, Rust); it does NOT maintain the stack property (non-monotonic), has no ghost list, and degrades at very small cache sizes and on scan-heavy block workloads because new objects are evicted before proving popularity.
prior_art_claims.value: 12-21 LOC to integrate; no stack property; no ghost list; weak at tiny cache / scans
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: LOC and limitations stated in official SIEVE materials and corroborated by independent analyses (brooker.co.za).

verification.corrected_value: 12-21 LOC to integrate into FIVE libraries (C++/Cachelib, Go, JavaScript, Python, Rust); no ghost list; degrades at small cache / scan-heavy block workloads. STACK-PROPERTY claim NOT supported by the cited source.
verification.note: Confirmed by NSDI paper: five open-source libraries 'Cachelib, groupcache ... languages: C++, Golang, JavaScript, Python, and Rust', Table 2 LOC = 12-21 (groupcache 21, mnemonist 12, lru-rs 16, lru-dict 21), '<20 lines on average'; no ghost cache; 'SIEVE is not scan-resistant ... when the cache size is small ... having a ghost is critical.' CORRECTION: the assertion that SIEVE 'does NOT maintain the stack property (non-monotonic)' is NOT found in the cited cachemon blog, the jasony blog, OR the NSDI paper text (no occurrence of 'stack property' / 'monotonic'). It is plausibly true of SIEVE but is unanchored to any cited primary source, so that sub-claim is removed/unsupported.

prior_art_claims.claim: W-TinyLFU splits the cache into a small LRU admission window (default ~1%) and a large main region (~99%) implemented as a Segmented LRU; an entry evicted from the window is admitted to main only if TinyLFU's frequency estimate says it is more valuable than the main victim. Caffeine adaptively resizes the window via hill-climbing on observed hit rate.
prior_art_claims.value: window ~1% LRU, main ~99% SLRU; adaptive window via hill climbing
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Window/main split and SLRU + hill-climbing confirmed from Caffeine wiki; exact 1%/99% default is the documented starting point but Caffeine self-tunes it.

verification.corrected_value: window ~1% LRU (PERCENT_MAIN=0.99), main ~99% Segmented LRU; admit window victim to main only if TinyLFU frequency estimate beats main victim; adaptive window via hill climbing on hit rate
verification.note: Caffeine source code is the strongest primary source: BoundedLocalCache.java 'static final double PERCENT_MAIN = 0.99d' and window = max - PERCENT_MAIN*max = 1%; comments describe hill-climbing window adaptation on the hit-rate curve. Cited wiki confirms the admission decision ('TinyLfu estimates the frequency of the window's victim and the main's victim, choosing to retain the entry with the highest historic usage') and 'uses hill climbing to sample the hit rate.' Wiki does not state the 1%/99% default split numerically, but the source does (claim was confidence:medium).

prior_art_claims.claim: TinyLFU stores frequency in a 4-bit Count-Min Sketch costing ~8 bytes per cache entry; it uses a doorkeeper (single-bit minimum-increment bloom filter) to avoid counting one-hit items, and periodically halves (ages) all counters once the running sample count reaches a threshold proportional to cache size.
prior_art_claims.value: 4-bit CM-sketch, ~8 bytes/entry; doorkeeper; periodic counter halving (aging)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 4-bit sketch and 8 bytes/entry stated verbatim in Caffeine wiki; doorkeeper + aging are core to the TinyLFU paper.

verification.corrected_value: 4-bit Count-Min Sketch (~8 bytes/entry); periodic aging that HALVES all counters when sample count = 10x cache max. NO doorkeeper in Caffeine's production W-TinyLFU.
verification.note: FrequencySketch.java confirms 4-bit CM sketch (max freq 15), aging that 'periodically halves the popularity of all elements' via reset() when size==sampleSize=10*maximum (proportional to cache size); wiki confirms '4-bit CountMinSketch, which requires 8 bytes per cache entry.' CORRECTION: the 'doorkeeper (single-bit minimum-increment bloom filter)' is NOT part of Caffeine's production design. The cited wiki does not mention a doorkeeper, and the production FrequencySketch has none; a doorkeeper exists only in Caffeine's research simulator (admission/countmin4 + reference.conf, non-default), and conservative/minimum-increment is also a non-default simulator option. The doorkeeper belongs to the original TinyLFU paper, not Caffeine/W-TinyLFU as deployed.

prior_art_claims.claim: ARC maintains a cache directory of twice the cache size (~2c entries) split across four lists T1/B1 (recency, seen once) and T2/B2 (frequency, seen >=2 recently), self-tunes a single adaptation parameter p that is the target size for T1, keeps NO frequency counts, is parameter-free ('no magic'), and substantially outperforms LRU.
prior_art_claims.value: 4 lists, ~2c directory, self-tuned p = target |T1|, no frequency counts
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from ARC paper PDF text: parameter p is target size for T1; maintains no frequency counts; remembers twice the pages; substantially outperforms LRU.

verification.corrected_value: 4 lists T1/B1/T2/B2; directory ~2c (twice cache size); self-tuned parameter p = target size for T1; maintains NO frequency counts; self-tuning/parameter-free; outperforms LRU
verification.note: Paper (re-extracted): directory 'remembers twice as many pages as in the cache memory'; one list seen once 'recently', other seen 'at least twice recently' (T1/B1 recency, T2/B2 frequency); 'the parameter [p] is the target size for [T1]'; explicitly 'The policy ARC maintains no frequency counts'; self-tuning vs '2Q, LRFU, LIRS require user-defined parameters.' The claim's quoted phrase 'no magic' is not verbatim in this PDF (paper uses 'self-tuning'/'empirically universal'); the substance is fully correct.

prior_art_claims.claim: ARC's space overhead is essentially double that of LRU because it keeps ghost entries (B1/B2) for as many pages as the cache holds, in addition to resident pages.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Paper text states 'essentially double the space overhead of LRU.'

prior_art_claims.claim: LIRS uses Inter-Reference Recency (IRR, the recency distance between the last two references of a block) instead of recency alone to classify blocks as Low-IRR (hot) or High-IRR (cold), giving scan resistance with overhead comparable to LRU.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Core LIRS definition; corroborated across multiple primary sources.

prior_art_claims.claim: LeCaR is a learning framework using regret minimization over just two experts (LRU and LFU); at small cache sizes relative to the working set it outperforms ARC by more than 18x.
prior_art_claims.value: >18x better than ARC at small cache sizes; experts = {LRU, LFU}
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 18x figure from search summary of USENIX abstract; not independently re-read from the PDF body.

verification.corrected_value: >18x better than ARC at small cache sizes (relative to working set); experts = {LRU, LFU}; regret minimization
verification.note: HotStorage'18 PDF (re-extracted): 'the LeCaR framework outperforms ARC using only two fundamental eviction policies, LRU and LFU, by more than 18x when the cache size is small relative to the size of the working set.' Also 'LeCaR outperforms ARC by as much as 18x in hit rate across the 8 production storage I/O workloads from FIU.' Exact match.

prior_art_claims.claim: CACHEUS adaptively combines expert policies to handle four workload primitive types (LFU-friendly, LRU-friendly, scan, churn), introducing SR-LRU (scan-resistant LRU) and CR-LFU (churn-resistant LFU) as new experts alongside ARC/LIRS/LFU.
prior_art_claims.value: experts include SR-LRU and CR-LFU; targets 4 workload primitives
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: From search summary of FAST'21 paper/abstract; not re-read from PDF body.

verification.corrected_value: experts SR-LRU (scan-resistant LRU) and CR-LFU (churn-resistant LFU); targets 4 workload primitive types: LRU-friendly, LFU-friendly, scan, churn
verification.note: FAST'21 PDF (re-extracted): 'four workload primitive types - LFU-friendly, LRU-friendly, scan, and churn'; 'two new ones - SR-LRU, a scan-resistant version of LRU, and CR-LFU, a churn-resistant version of LFU.' The claim's 'alongside ARC/LIRS/LFU' is loosely supported: the headline CACHEUS experts are LRU/LFU + SR-LRU/CR-LFU, with ARC and LIRS appearing as alternative candidate experts in the broader design/evaluation; core claim confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: LRB approximates Belady's MIN oracle by learning a 'relaxed Belady' objective with gradient-boosted decision trees over per-object features, reducing CDN WAN/byte traffic by roughly 4-25% vs a typical production CDN cache design.

prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 4-25% figure from search summary of NSDI'20 abstract; direct PDF fetch returned 403 so not re-verified against paper body.

verification.corrected_value: Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM, LightGBM-style GBDT) over per-object features; approximates relaxed Belady (mimics Belady MIN); reduces WAN traffic by 4-25% vs typical production CDN (Bloom-filter-protected LRU)
verification.note: NSDI'20 PDF (re-extracted): 'Learning Relaxed Belady (LRB) to mimic a Relaxed Belady algorithm'; 'Model: Gradient Boosting Machines. LRB uses Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM)' (ref = LightGBM, a GBDT); 'reduces WAN traffic by 4-25% compared to a typically deployed CDN cache - i.e., using LRU replacement protected with a Bloom filter.' Exact match including 4-25%.

prior_art_claims.claim: LHD evicts the object with the least 'hit density' (expected hits per unit space-time), estimated online via conditional probability over object age/class, and reports consistently outperforming prior policies on its evaluated traces.
prior_art_claims.value: rank by expected hits per space-time (hit density)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Concept from paper title/abstract; specific quantitative wins not re-read from PDF body.

Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis does not implement true LRU/LFU; it uses approximate sampling (default maxmemory-samples 5) over keys, with allkeys-lru / allkeys-lfu / volatile-* policies, to bound metadata cost — a pragmatic precedent for approximate eviction in a KV store.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Redis approximate-LRU sampling default is well established; not re-fetched in this session so version-pin is approximate.

verification.corrected_value: approximate LRU/LFU via sampling; default maxmemory-samples 5; policies noeviction, allkeys-lru/lfu/random, volatile-lru/lfu/random/ttl
verification.note: Official Redis docs confirm: 'The Redis LRU algorithm uses an approximation ... It samples a small number of keys at random'; LFU 'is approximated like LRU' via a Morris probabilistic counter with decay; default directive 'maxmemory-samples 5'; full policy list present (allkeys-lru/lfu/random, volatile-lru/lfu/random/ttl, noeviction). Confirmed.

mechanisms.name: SIEVE (FIFO + hand + visited bit)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Single FIFO list with a roving hand pointer; a hit sets a 1-bit visited flag (no list move), eviction scans clearing flags and removes the first unvisited object. Lazy promotion + quick demotion.
mechanisms.rationale: Best concurrency-to-hit-ratio tradeoff in the literature: hit path is a single atomic bit set (lock-free reads), 12-21 LOC, >2x LRU throughput at 16 threads and lowest miss ratio on >45% of web traces. This is the closest match to IronCache's 'max throughput per core' thesis. Ship as a default policy.

mechanisms.name: S3-FIFO (three static FIFOs + 2-bit freq + ghost)
mechanisms.what_it_does: 10% small probationary FIFO filters one-hit-wonders, 90% main FIFO holds promoted objects, ghost FIFO (IDs only) enables fast re-admission; 2-bit freq capped at 3 drives promotion/reinsertion.
mechanisms.rationale: Adds a ghost queue (which SIEVE lacks) for quick re-admission of recently-evicted hot keys, fixing SIEVE's small-cache weakness, while staying FIFO-friendly (~6x LRU throughput at 16 cores) and winning on 10/14 datasets. Strong default/alternative; the small-FIFO filter also reduces churn from scans.

mechanisms.name: W-TinyLFU frequency admission (CM-sketch + doorkeeper + aging)
mechanisms.what_it_does: 4-bit Count-Min Sketch (~8 B/entry) estimates frequency; doorkeeper bloom filter ignores one-hit items; periodic counter halving ages the sketch; admission compares window victim vs main victim frequency.
mechanisms.rationale: Keep the CM-sketch+doorkeeper as an OPTIONAL admission filter in front of a FIFO main policy (frequency-skewed workloads), but reject the LRU window + SLRU main + hill-climbing scaffolding because the per-hit SLRU promotion reintroduces lock contention. Use the sketch only at admission/eviction, not on every read.

mechanisms.name: ARC self-tuning recency/frequency balance
mechanisms.what_it_does: Four lists (T1/B1 recency, T2/B2 frequency) with a self-tuned parameter p (target |T1|) and ~2c ghost directory; parameter-free, no frequency counts.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the self-tuning idea (adapt the split between recency and frequency from ghost-hit feedback) and the ghost-directory concept, but reject the four-list LRU implementation: every hit moves an entry between lists under a lock, and it doubles metadata. Patent history (IBM) is also a caution for verbatim implementation.

mechanisms.name: CLOCK / second-chance
mechanisms.what_it_does: Circular buffer with reference bits approximating LRU at O(1) and low metadata; hit just sets a bit.
mechanisms.rationale: CLOCK's per-hit 'set a bit' is exactly the lazy-promotion primitive SIEVE generalizes; treat SIEVE as the modern, scan-resistant CLOCK and adopt that lineage rather than vanilla CLOCK (which is not scan-resistant and lacks quick demotion).

mechanisms.name: Approximate sampled LRU/LFU
mechanisms.what_it_does: Samples N keys (default 5) and evicts the approximate LRU/LFU victim, avoiding global ordering structures.
mechanisms.rationale: For Redis-wire compatibility IronCache must expose allkeys-lru/lfu/random/volatile-* policy NAMES, but should back them with O(1) FIFO-class internals rather than Redis's sampling. Sampling is a reasonable fallback for the LFU mode where exact recency ordering is not required.

mechanisms.name: LIRS / inter-reference recency
mechanisms.what_it_does: Classifies blocks by IRR to get scan/loop resistance superior to LRU at LRU-like cost.
mechanisms.rationale: Scan resistance is valuable but LIRS's stack pruning and HIR/LIR bookkeeping are complex and lock-heavy on the hot path; S3-FIFO's small-FIFO filter and SIEVE's visited-bit achieve adequate scan resistance far more cheaply for an in-memory KV cache.

mechanisms.name: LeCaR / CACHEUS expert ensembles
mechanisms.what_it_does: Online regret-minimization / adaptive weighting over expert policies (LRU, LFU, SR-LRU, CR-LFU) to track shifting workloads.
mechanisms.rationale: Running multiple shadow simulators and reweighting on every request is too expensive for nanosecond-scale lookups and complicates the lock-free path. The ADAPTIVITY idea is worth keeping, but realized as a slow background tuner that switches/parameterizes a single fast policy, not as an online per-request ensemble.

mechanisms.name: LRB (Learning Relaxed Belady, GBDT)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Gradient-boosted trees predict each object's relaxed-Belady eviction value from feature vectors to approximate the MIN oracle.
mechanisms.rationale: Per-object feature collection plus tree inference per eviction is appropriate for CDN/flash byte-miss optimization, not for a memory-bandwidth-bound in-memory cache. Keep as inspiration for an optional offline 'Belady-distance' analysis to benchmark how far IronCache's online policy is from optimal.

mechanisms.name: LHD / Hyperbolic / GDSF (size & cost aware)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Rank objects by expected hit density / hyperbolic priority / greedy-dual size-frequency-cost to handle variable object sizes and miss costs.
mechanisms.rationale: Size-aware value functions matter most for variable-size web/CDN objects; for a Redis-style KV cache object sizes vary less and per-eviction floating-point scoring hurts throughput. Revisit only if IronCache targets large-value or tiered (compressed/SSD) storage where size-cost tradeoffs dominate.

ironcache_implications: Default eviction policy should be a FIFO-class lazy-promotion / quick-demotion algorithm (SIEVE for simplicity, S3-FIFO when a re-admission ghost is wanted) so the read hot path is a single atomic visited-bit set, enabling lock-free or sharded-lock reads and maximizing throughput per core.

Add an optional S3-FIFO-style ghost queue (object keys/hashes only, not values) to recover SIEVE's main weakness — inability to quickly re-admit a recently-evicted hot key and poor behavior at very small cache sizes.

Offer a W-TinyLFU-style frequency admission filter as a pluggable front-end (4-bit Count-Min Sketch ~8 B/entry + doorkeeper bloom filter + periodic counter halving) for frequency-skewed workloads, but invoke the sketch only at admission/eviction, never on every read, to preserve the lock-free read path.

Because FIFO-based policies are non-monotonic (no stack property), do NOT rely on miss-ratio-curve / hit-ratio-curve assumptions for capacity planning; instead expose live hit-ratio counters and a shadow-cache sampler for sizing.

Implement SIEVE with a real intrusive doubly-linked list (not a circular array) because eviction removes from the middle; keep per-entry overhead tiny (1 visited bit packed into existing flag bits, 2 pointers). S3-FIFO needs a 2-bit counter + ghost-entry hash set.

Maintain Redis-wire compatibility by exposing the standard policy names (noeviction, allkeys-lru, allkeys-lfu, allkeys-random, volatile-ttl, volatile-lru, volatile-lfu, volatile-random) as configuration aliases that map onto IronCache's superior internal FIFO-class engine, rather than reimplementing Redis's sampled approximation.

Reserve learned/adaptive methods (LeCaR/CACHEUS-style switching, LRB-style Belady approximation) for an optional background 'auto-tuner' that periodically retunes a single fast policy's knobs (window %, freq threshold, ghost size) from sampled traces — never an online per-request ensemble.

Provide an offline Belady-MIN (and sampled-Belady) analysis tool so IronCache can report how close its online policy is to optimal on a customer's trace, turning 'most efficient cache' into a measurable, defensible claim.

Design the eviction policy as a trait/interface from day one (admit(), on_hit(), evict_candidate()) so SIEVE, S3-FIFO, and W-TinyLFU can be swapped and benchmarked on identical traces; pick the default empirically from a standard trace corpus (the 6594-trace / cachemon corpus).

research_papers.title: FIFO Queues are All You Need for Cache Eviction (S3-FIFO)
research_papers.key_idea: Three static FIFO queues (10% small + 90% main + ghost) with a 2-bit freq counter cap 3 implement quick demotion of one-hit-wonders; lowest mean miss ratio on 10/14 datasets and ~6x LRU throughput at 16 cores.
research_papers.relevance: Primary candidate for IronCache's default policy; the ghost queue and small-FIFO filter are directly adoptable for a lock-friendly, scan-resistant cache.

research_papers.title: SIEVE is Simpler than LRU: an Efficient Turn-Key Eviction Algorithm for Web Caches
research_papers.key_idea: One FIFO + hand pointer + per-object visited bit; hits set a bit (no list move), eviction scans for an unvisited object. 12-21 LOC to integrate, >2x LRU throughput at 16 threads, lowest miss ratio on >45% of web traces.
research_papers.relevance: Strongest match to IronCache's throughput-per-core goal; minimal metadata and lock-free reads. Note: no ghost list, non-stack-property, weak at very small caches / scans.

research_papers.title: TinyLFU: A Highly Efficient Cache Admission Policy
research_papers.key_idea: Frequency-based admission via a 4-bit Count-Min Sketch with a doorkeeper bloom filter and periodic counter halving (aging); admits only items whose estimated frequency beats the eviction victim.
research_papers.relevance: Source of the optional frequency-admission filter for IronCache; the sketch+doorkeeper+aging design is the part worth borrowing (not the per-hit LRU/SLRU window).

research_papers.title: ARC: A Self-Tuning, Low Overhead Replacement Cache
research_papers.key_idea: Self-tunes parameter p (target T1 size) to balance recency vs frequency using ghost-list hits, parameter-free, no frequency counts, ~2c directory; substantially outperforms LRU.
research_papers.relevance: Reference for self-tuning and ghost-feedback adaptivity; rejected for hot-path implementation (lock-heavy four-list LRU, 2x metadata, patent history).

research_papers.title: LIRS: An Efficient Low Inter-reference Recency Set Replacement Policy
research_papers.key_idea: Uses Inter-Reference Recency (distance between last two references) to classify hot/cold blocks, giving scan/loop resistance at near-LRU cost.
research_papers.relevance: Benchmark for scan resistance; informs what FIFO-class policies must match, but its bookkeeping is too heavy for IronCache's hot path.

research_papers.title: Driving Cache Replacement with ML-based LeCaR
research_papers.key_idea: Regret-minimization online learning over two experts (LRU, LFU) beats ARC by >18x at small cache sizes.
research_papers.relevance: Inspiration for an adaptive auto-tuner; rejected as an online per-request mechanism due to cost.

research_papers.title: Learning Cache Replacement with CACHEUS
research_papers.key_idea: Adaptive expert ensemble (incl. new SR-LRU and CR-LFU) targeting four workload primitives: LFU-friendly, LRU-friendly, scan, churn.
research_papers.relevance: Defines workload-primitive taxonomy IronCache can use to design its trace test-suite and auto-tuner switching logic.

research_papers.title: Learning Relaxed Belady for Content Distribution Network Caching (LRB)
research_papers.key_idea: Gradient-boosted trees approximate Belady's MIN via a 'relaxed Belady' objective over per-object features; ~4-25% WAN/byte-traffic reduction for CDNs.
research_papers.relevance: Inspiration for an offline Belady-gap benchmark; rejected for online in-memory use (feature tracking + inference too heavy).

research_papers.title: LHD: Improving Cache Hit Rate by Maximizing Hit Density
research_papers.key_idea: Evicts the object with least expected hits-per-space-time (hit density), estimated online by conditional probability over object age/class.
research_papers.relevance: Size/cost-aware value-function reference for a future tiered or large-value IronCache mode; not for the default KV hot path.

research_papers.title: Can Increasing the Hit Ratio Hurt Cache Throughput? (Long Version)
research_papers.key_idea: Quantifies the tension between higher hit ratio and higher throughput, showing complex high-hit-ratio policies can reduce overall system throughput.
research_papers.relevance: Directly supports IronCache's bias toward simple FIFO-class policies: argues efficiency must be measured as end-to-end throughput, not hit ratio alone.

open_questions: Which default policy wins on a representative Redis/KV workload corpus once value sizes, TTLs, and write ratios are included — SIEVE, S3-FIFO, or W-TinyLFU-fronted FIFO? Needs a head-to-head benchmark on the cachemon 6594-trace corpus plus KV-specific traces.
How should eviction interact with TTL expiry and with active-expiration sweeps (Redis volatile-* semantics) without adding a second lock on the hot path?

What is the right ghost-queue sizing and representation (full key vs 8-byte hash) to balance re-admission accuracy against memory in a 'minimal memory' cache, and what false-positive rate is acceptable?

Under a sharded single-thread-per-core architecture, is a per-shard SIEVE list sufficient, or is a global frequency sketch needed for cross-shard hot-key detection — and how is the sketch shared without contention?
How do FIFO-class policies behave under adversarial / scan / churn workloads compared to LIRS/ARC, and do we need an explicit scan-detection guard beyond the small-FIFO filter?
Can an offline Belady-MIN gap be turned into a continuously-reported online metric cheaply enough to ship as a product feature?
Does the non-stack (non-monotonic) property of FIFO-class policies cause user-visible surprises during cache resizing / autoscaling, and how do we document/guard against it?

For the optional AI auto-tuner, what is the minimal set of tunables (window %, freq threshold, ghost size, small/main split) and what cadence/feedback signal (sampled hit ratio) makes retuning safe and stable?

proposed_issues.title: Decision: default eviction policy = SIEVE vs S3-FIFO vs W-TinyLFU-fronted FIFO
proposed_issues.summary: Choose IronCache's default online eviction policy based on the throughput-per-core thesis and a trace-driven benchmark. Leading candidates are SIEVE (simplest, lock-free reads) and S3-FIFO (adds ghost re-admission); record the decision and rationale.

proposed_issues.title: Research: benchmark SIEVE/S3-FIFO/W-TinyLFU/ARC on the cachemon 6594-trace corpus + KV traces
proposed_issues.summary: Reproduce hit-ratio and multi-thread throughput comparisons across FIFO-class and LRU-class policies on a standard trace corpus plus Redis-like KV traces (with value sizes/TTLs), to ground the default-policy decision in data.

proposed_issues.title: Design: pluggable EvictionPolicy trait (admit / on_hit / evict_candidate / on_evict)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a policy interface so SIEVE, S3-FIFO, W-TinyLFU, and approximate-LRU/LFU can be swapped and benchmarked on identical data structures, with the hot read path constrained to an atomic flag set (no lock).

proposed_issues.title: Design: optional S3-FIFO-style ghost queue for fast hot-key re-admission
proposed_issues.summary: Define a ghost queue storing key hashes (not values) to recover SIEVE's small-cache weakness; specify sizing, hash width, false-positive tolerance, and memory budget under the 'minimal memory' goal.

proposed_issues.title: Design: optional W-TinyLFU frequency admission filter (CM-sketch + doorkeeper + aging)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a 4-bit Count-Min Sketch (~8 B/entry) with doorkeeper bloom filter and periodic counter halving, invoked only at admission/eviction (never per-read), as a pluggable front-end for frequency-skewed workloads.

proposed_issues.title: Design: map Redis maxmemory-policy names onto IronCache's internal FIFO-class engine
proposed_issues.summary: Define how allkeys-lru/lfu/random, volatile-*, and noeviction Redis policy names alias onto IronCache's superior internal policies while preserving wire-level expectations and volatile/TTL semantics.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal: no online per-request learned ensemble (LeCaR/CACHEUS/LRB) on the hot path
proposed_issues.summary: Declare that online ML ensembles and per-eviction model inference are out of scope for the default engine due to throughput cost; learned methods are confined to an optional offline/background tuner.

proposed_issues.title: Research: offline Belady-MIN (and sampled-Belady) gap analysis tool
proposed_issues.summary: Build a tool to compute the optimal (Belady MIN) miss ratio on a customer trace and report how close IronCache's online policy is, making the 'most efficient cache' claim measurable.

proposed_issues.title: Design: AI-driven background auto-tuner for policy knobs (window %, freq threshold, ghost size)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a low-frequency background tuner that adjusts a single fast policy's parameters from sampled hit-ratio feedback (CACHEUS-style adaptivity without per-request cost), including stability/safety guards.

proposed_issues.title: Task: implement SIEVE prototype with intrusive linked list + packed visited bit
proposed_issues.summary: Prototype SIEVE in Rust using an intrusive doubly-linked list (middle removal) with the visited bit packed into existing entry flags, validating O(1) ops and lock-free reads on a sharded design.

proposed_issues.title: Research: scan/churn resistance of FIFO-class policies vs LIRS/ARC
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate whether the S3-FIFO small-FIFO filter and SIEVE visited-bit provide sufficient scan/loop resistance, or whether an explicit scan-detection guard is needed for adversarial workloads.

proposed_issues.title: Meta: eviction-algorithms prior-art doc and decision log
proposed_issues.summary: Seed the prior-art document with the claims/sources gathered here and maintain a running decision log for eviction/admission choices as benchmarks come in.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Method: re-verified every claim against fresh primary sources. WebFetch could not parse several PDFs (returned binary) and login.usenix + the NSDI presentation pages returned HTTP 403; I worked around this by downloading the canonical paper PDFs with curl and extracting text with pdftotext, and by reading source code via the gh API. Each verdict is anchored to the paper's own PDF text, the project's source code, or official docs - not to secondary summaries.

14 of 16 claims CONFIRMED (most strengthened with a second independent source - e.g. paper PDF + source code for S3-FIFO; Caffeine wiki + Caffeine source for W-TinyLFU). All S3-FIFO numbers (10/90 split, ghost IDs-only, 2-bit/cap-3 counter, freq>1 promote / freq>0 reinsert, 6594 traces / 14 datasets / 856B requests, 10-of-14 best, 13-of-14 top-3, LIRS wins 2, 6x throughput, 72% one-hit-wonder) verified verbatim against the SOSP'23 PDF AND libCacheSim S3FIFO.c (DEFAULT_CACHE_PARAMS fifo-size-ratio=0.10, ghost-size-ratio=0.90, move-to-main-threshold=2; main reinsert MIN(freq,3)-1).

TWO CORRECTIONS, both bundled 'compound' claims where the headline value is right but a sub-assertion is unsupported by the cited primary source:
1) [sieve-loc-and-stack-property]: LOC (12-21), five libraries/languages (incl. C++ via Cachelib), no ghost list, and weakness at small cache / scans are all CONFIRMED by the NSDI'24 PDF. But the embedded claim that SIEVE 'does NOT maintain the stack property (non-monotonic)' appears in NEITHER cited source NOR the NSDI paper (the strings 'stack property'/'monotonic' do not occur). Likely true in the abstract but not anchored - so that sub-claim is unsupported.
2) [wtinylfu-cmsketch-4bit]: 4-bit CM sketch, ~8 bytes/entry, and periodic counter halving/aging (threshold = 10x cache max) are CONFIRMED by Caffeine's FrequencySketch.java and wiki. But the 'doorkeeper (single-bit minimum-increment bloom filter)' is NOT part of Caffeine's production W-TinyLFU - it is not in the cited wiki, not in FrequencySketch.java, and exists only in Caffeine's research SIMULATOR (admission/countmin4, non-default in reference.conf). The doorkeeper is from the original TinyLFU paper, conflated here with Caffeine's design.

Minor (left as confirmed): ARC paper does not contain the verbatim phrase 'no magic' (claim's parenthetical) but is unambiguously self-tuning/parameter-free with no frequency counts. CACHEUS 'alongside ARC/LIRS/LFU' is loosely supported - the headline experts are LRU/LFU + SR-LRU/CR-LFU; ARC/LIRS are alternative candidate experts. Redis doc OCR rendered 'allkeys-lru' as 'allkeys-lrm' in one fetch - artifact, not a real policy.

dimensions.summary:
Microsoft Garnet is the closest existing system to IronCache's stated goal: a RESP-wire-compatible remote cache-store engineered for high throughput-per-core, low and stable tail latency, larger-than-memory tiering, and single-node-to-cluster scale-out. Its core insight — and the thing IronCache should study hardest — is a clean layering: a "narrow-waist" storage API (Read / Upsert / Delete / atomic RMW with async callbacks) over the Tsavorite engine (a fork of FASTER), with the full rich RESP surface implemented on top of just those four primitives. This is exactly the boundary IronCache wants between its wire layer and its store. Garnet is C#/.NET, so the runtime (GC, SocketAsyncEventArgs, SslStream, thread pool) is not portable, but the algorithms and data structures are.

The storage engine is the deepest source of ideas. Tsavorite/FASTER combines a lock-free hash index (cache-aligned 64-byte buckets, 8 entries each, with a "tentative" bit for lock-free insertion) with a HybridLog: a single logical address space split into three regions by two moving markers. The mutable region (tail down to ReadOnlyAddress) supports in-place updates of the hot set with no allocation or log growth — this is what lets FASTER beat pure in-memory structures and is the single most important mechanism for IronCache's "max throughput per core." The read-only region (ReadOnlyAddress down to HeadAddress) is immutable and updated via read-copy-update (append a new version at the tail). The stable region (below HeadAddress) lives on disk, giving larger-than-memory storage. Concurrency is governed by an epoch-protection framework (global epoch counter, thread-local epochs, a drain list of trigger actions fired when all threads pass an epoch) rather than fine-grained latches — region-boundary shifts, page flushes, and memory reclamation are all deferred until safe. Locking, where needed, is at hash-bucket granularity (15 shared-lock bits + 1 exclusive bit) rather than per-record. FASTER reports up to 160M ops/sec on a single multi-core machine (YCSB).

Garnet's networking/threading model is its second major contribution and is highly portable as a design. Rather than Redis's single-threaded event loop, Garnet runs a shared-memory, thread-scalable server: connections are handled with async socket completions, and crucially TLS decryption, RESP parsing, and the storage operation all execute inline on the IO completion thread, avoiding a thread hand-off per request. I confirmed this in source: the non-TLS receive path calls OnNetworkReceiveWithoutTLS synchronously inside the SocketAsyncEventArgs.Completed callback in a do/while(!ReceiveAsync) loop, so when data is already buffered the same thread keeps parsing and serving with zero context switches. This "process on the thread that received the bytes" design plus batching is what gives Garnet better scaling across cores and across payload sizes than Redis/KeyDB, and far more stable p99/p99.9 than Dragonfly in MSR's published comparisons. IronCache, being natively threaded Rust with no GC, can implement the same shared-state-threaded model (e.g. io_uring or epoll with per-core acceptor + inline processing) without .NET's overheads.

Durability and ops features round out the picture: append-only-file (AOF / write-ahead log, off by default), non-blocking checkpoints in two flavors — Fold-Over (move ReadOnly marker to tail, persist incrementally within the hybrid log) and Snapshot (separate full in-memory snapshot file) — built on Concurrent Prefix Recovery (CPR, SIGMOD'19), which commits "all operations until time t per session" via async incremental checkpointing instead of a classic WAL. Cluster mode adds Redis-Cluster-compatible hash-slot sharding, gossip, replication, failover, and dynamic key migration (including a FastMigrate path that writes straight to the network buffer). Log compaction (Lookup strategy recommended; Scan discouraged due to transient memory blow-up) and "revivification" (free-record reuse) manage space.

For IronCache the takeaways are concrete. Borrow: the narrow-waist store API, the HybridLog mutable/read-only/stable tri-region with in-place hot-set updates, epoch-based deferred reclamation, and inline-on-IO-thread request processing. Adapt: tiered storage and checkpointing (Rust has no managed GC, so memory management and the object store for composite types must be redesigned around explicit ownership / arenas; F2's hot-log+cold-log split is a better blueprint than FASTER's single hybrid log for very skewed larger-than-memory workloads). Reject for v1: the .NET object store representation, AOF-per-op durability defaults, and full cluster mode — these are M2+ concerns. The defaults to note for parity decisions: port 6379, 16 GiB log, 90% mutable, storage tiering and read cache OFF by default, compaction None by default, AOF off by default.

verification.note: Cache-line-aligned 64-byte buckets confirmed: 'a 64-byte bucket consists of seven 8-byte hash bucket entries and one 8-byte entry to serve as an overflow bucket pointer.' The '8 entries/bucket' claim is imprecise/wrong: only SEVEN slots hold hash entries; the eighth 8-byte slot is the overflow-bucket pointer. The per-entry tentative bit is confirmed ('Each hash bucket entry consists of three parts: a tag (15 bits), a tentative bit, and the address (48 bits)') and is used by the latch-free two-phase insert. Corrected the entries-per-bucket from 8 to 7 (+1 overflow pointer).

Matches claim.
