mechanisms.name: Narrow-waist storage API under a rich RESP surface
mechanisms.what_it_does: Implements the entire RESP command surface (strings, objects, bitmaps, HLL, geo, pub/sub) on top of just four storage primitives — Read, Upsert, Delete, and atomic Read-Modify-Write (RMW) — each taking async callbacks where the command layer injects logic (merge functions, expiry, conditional writes).
mechanisms.rationale: This is the single cleanest architectural idea for IronCache: it decouples the wire/command layer from the engine and makes RMW (INCR, APPEND, expiry, LPUSH) expressible without bespoke engine code. In Rust this maps naturally to a trait with associated callback closures / generic Functions parameter. Adopt the boundary verbatim; it is the key to 'easy to extend' and to keeping the hot path uniform.

mechanisms.name: HybridLog tri-region address space (mutable / read-only / stable)
mechanisms.what_it_does: A single logical 64-bit address space split by two moving markers: mutable region [ReadOnlyAddress, TailAddress) supports in-place updates of the hot set (no allocation); read-only region [HeadAddress, ReadOnlyAddress) is immutable and updated via read-copy-update (append new version at tail); stable region (< HeadAddress) spills to disk for larger-than-memory.
mechanisms.rationale: In-place update of the hot set is precisely how FASTER beats pure in-memory structures and is the mechanism most aligned with 'max throughput per core, minimal memory.' The tri-region marker scheme also gives larger-than-memory tiering for free. Borrow the structure; IronCache must re-implement record layout with explicit Rust ownership (no GC) and consider variable-length value handling.

mechanisms.name: Epoch-protection framework for lock-free concurrency
mechanisms.what_it_does: A global epoch counter plus thread-local epoch entries and a 'drain list' of trigger actions executed only when every active thread has advanced past a given epoch. Region-boundary shifts, page flushes/eviction, index resizing, and memory reclamation are deferred until epoch-safe, avoiding fine-grained latches on the hot path.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the concurrency backbone enabling lock-free reads/in-place updates and safe deferred reclamation — directly serving throughput-per-core. Rust analog: epoch-based reclamation (crossbeam-epoch) or a hand-rolled equivalent. Borrow the concept; crossbeam-epoch gives a battle-tested Rust foundation, but the trigger-action drain list is worth replicating for boundary shifts.

mechanisms.name: Lock-free hash index with tentative-bit insertion
mechanisms.what_it_does: Cache-line-aligned 64-byte hash buckets holding 8 entries each; insertion uses a two-phase CAS with a per-entry 'tentative' bit to avoid duplicate keys under concurrent insert without locking. Hash-bucket-level locking (15 shared + 1 exclusive bit) is used only when locking is required.
mechanisms.rationale: The cache-aligned bucket + tentative-bit pattern is excellent for throughput, but IronCache may prefer a more modern open-addressing or swiss-table-style index in Rust. Adapt: keep cache-line alignment and bucket-granularity locking, but evaluate against alternatives; the 64-byte/8-entry geometry is a strong default.

mechanisms.name: Inline request processing on the IO completion thread
mechanisms.what_it_does: On socket receive, RESP parsing and the storage operation execute synchronously on the same thread that received the bytes (verified in TcpNetworkHandlerBase: OnNetworkReceiveWithoutTLS is called inline inside the SocketAsyncEventArgs.Completed callback, looping while data is buffered). No per-request hand-off to a worker pool; TLS decryption also runs there.
mechanisms.rationale: Eliminating the thread hand-off per request is a primary reason Garnet scales across cores and keeps p99 stable versus a single-threaded event loop. IronCache (Rust, no GC) can do the same with io_uring or epoll: per-core acceptor, process-on-receiving-thread. Borrow the principle; replace SocketAsyncEventArgs with io_uring/tokio's multi-thread runtime or a thread-per-core design.

mechanisms.name: Two stores: string main store + object store, unified log
mechanisms.what_it_does: Raw strings live in a 'main store' optimized for inline values; composite types (list/hash/set/sortedset) live in a separate 'object store'. Their durability is bound by a single unified operation log so checkpoints/AOF are consistent across both.
mechanisms.rationale: The separation is sound (different memory layouts for scalars vs containers), and the unified log for cross-store consistency is worth keeping. But Garnet's object store holds managed .NET objects on the GC heap — not portable. Adapt: IronCache needs an explicit, arena/slab-backed representation for container types with manual memory management; keep the unified-log durability binding.

mechanisms.name: Dual checkpoint modes (Fold-Over + Snapshot) on CPR
mechanisms.what_it_does: Fold-Over moves the ReadOnly marker to the tail and persists state incrementally within the existing hybrid log (cheap, incremental). Snapshot writes a separate full in-memory snapshot file. Both are non-blocking, built on Concurrent Prefix Recovery, which defines a commit as 'all operations until time t, per client session.'
mechanisms.rationale: Non-blocking incremental checkpointing is valuable for a cache that also wants persistence, and Fold-Over reuses the log structure elegantly. Adapt: IronCache should offer at least Fold-Over-style incremental checkpoints, but CPR's per-session prefix semantics may be more than a cache needs; consider a simpler RDB-like snapshot for v1 and Fold-Over later.

mechanisms.name: F2 hot-log + cold-log split for skewed larger-than-memory
mechanisms.what_it_does: Splits storage into an in-memory hot-log component and an on-disk cold-log component with promotion/read-caching between them, specifically optimizing very skewed workloads larger than memory and reducing memory overhead vs single-hybrid-log FASTER.
mechanisms.rationale: For a cache, skewed access is the common case, so F2's two-component design is arguably a better blueprint than FASTER's single hybrid log when going larger-than-memory. Adapt as the model for IronCache's tiering design rather than copying FASTER's older single-log eviction; flagged as M2 research because it adds complexity.

mechanisms.name: AOF (write-ahead log) with tunable commit cadence
mechanisms.what_it_does: Optional append-only file for durability, off by default; commit cadence configurable (0 = immediate per-op, -1 = manual COMMITAOF, >0 = periodic ms). Supports sharded sublogs (AofPhysicalSublogCount) for throughput and unsafe truncation at a size limit.
mechanisms.rationale: Sensible to offer for users who want persistence, and the sharded-sublog idea aids write scaling. But per-op fsync defaults destroy latency; IronCache should default AOF off (like Garnet) and, if offered, default to periodic group-commit. Adapt the model, choose safer/faster defaults.

mechanisms.name: Revivification (free-record reuse) and Lookup-based compaction
mechanisms.what_it_does: Revivification reclaims and reuses freed records in the mutable region to bound memory; log compaction reclaims dead records, with a Lookup strategy (hash-chain liveness check, no data loss) recommended and a Scan strategy explicitly discouraged because it builds a transient parallel index proportional to the keyspace.
mechanisms.rationale: A log-structured store needs space reclamation; revivification directly serves 'minimal memory,' and the documented Lookup-vs-Scan tradeoff is a useful design lesson (avoid algorithms with transient memory proportional to keyspace). Borrow the concepts and heed the warning about Scan-style compaction.

mechanisms.name: Cluster mode: hash-slot sharding, gossip, FastMigrate
mechanisms.what_it_does: Redis-Cluster-compatible 16384 hash-slot sharding with gossip-based membership, replication, failover, and dynamic key migration; FastMigrate writes migrated keys directly to the network buffer and does not wait for per-batch acks.
mechanisms.rationale: IronCache wants single-node-to-multi-node, and Redis-Cluster slot compatibility maximizes client interop. Adapt for a later milestone (M2+): keep slot compatibility and the FastMigrate zero-copy idea, but cluster is explicitly out of scope for the first prototype.

mechanisms.name: C#/.NET runtime, GC, SslStream, managed thread pool
mechanisms.what_it_does: Garnet runs on .NET 8, relying on the CLR garbage collector, managed thread pool, SocketAsyncEventArgs, and SslStream for TLS; tuning exposes ThreadPoolMin/Max worker and IO-completion thread knobs.
mechanisms.rationale: The entire managed runtime is antithetical to IronCache's 'most efficient cache, single static binary' goal — GC pauses hurt tail latency, the CLR is not a single static binary, and managed object stores fight zero-copy. Reject the runtime wholesale; extract only the algorithms. Rust + a thread-per-core async stack achieves the same architecture without GC.

ironcache_implications: Adopt Garnet's narrow-waist store API as IronCache's internal boundary: define a Rust trait/engine interface of exactly Read, Upsert, Delete, RMW, parameterized by a Functions type carrying callbacks (merge/RMW logic, single-writer in-place update, read-copy-update, expiry). The whole RESP command set is built on these four — this keeps the hot path uniform and the engine testable in isolation.

Build the engine around a HybridLog with in-place updates in a mutable hot region. This is the mechanism that lets FASTER beat in-memory structures; it is the most direct route to IronCache's 'max throughput per core, minimal memory.' Default mutable fraction ~90% (Garnet's default) and make it tunable.

Use epoch-based reclamation (crossbeam-epoch or a hand-rolled global-epoch + thread-local + drain-list) instead of per-record locks. Defer all region-boundary shifts, page evictions, and frees to epoch-safe points. This is the lock-free concurrency backbone that a GC-less Rust implementation must provide explicitly.

Process requests inline on the receiving thread. Choose a thread-per-core (or io_uring) network stack where RESP parsing and the store operation run on the thread that read the bytes, with no hand-off to a worker pool — this is the verified source-level reason Garnet scales across cores and keeps p99 stable. This is a hard architectural decision to lock in early.

Default durability/persistence OFF, matching Garnet (EnableStorageTier=false, EnableAOF=false, CompactionType=None). Ship as a fast in-memory cache by default; tiering, AOF, checkpointing are opt-in. If AOF is offered, default to periodic group-commit, never per-op fsync.

Separate scalar and container representations (main store vs object store) but redesign the container store with explicit Rust memory management (slabs/arenas), since Garnet's GC-heap object store is not portable. Bind both to one unified operation log for consistent checkpoints.

For larger-than-memory, prefer F2's hot-log/cold-log two-component design over FASTER's single hybrid log — caches are skewed, and F2 is purpose-built for skew with lower memory overhead. Treat as M2 research.

Heed Garnet's compaction warning: avoid space-reclamation algorithms whose transient memory is proportional to the keyspace (Garnet's Scan); prefer hash-chain-liveness Lookup-style compaction plus revivification for bounded memory.

Use a cache-line-aligned bucketed hash index (64-byte / 8-entry with tentative-bit lock-free insert) as a strong baseline, but benchmark against a Rust swiss-table/open-addressing design before committing.
Target RESP2/RESP3 and Redis-Cluster 16384-slot compatibility for client interop, but scope single-node first; cluster/replication/failover and FastMigrate-style zero-copy migration are M2+.

Run a head-to-head benchmark harness against Garnet itself (plus Redis 7.2+, Valkey, KeyDB, Dragonfly) on the same Garnet methodology (8B keys/values, batch 4-4096, 1K and 256M key DBs, 1-128 sessions, measure p50/p99/p99.9) — note MSR published only graphs, so IronCache should publish absolute numbers to differentiate.
Plan for a single static binary from day one (musl/static-linked Rust) — this is a concrete differentiator over Garnet, which requires the .NET runtime and is not a single static binary.

research_papers.title: FASTER: A Concurrent Key-Value Store with In-Place Updates
research_papers.key_idea: Combines a lock-free cache-aligned hash index with a HybridLog (mutable in-memory + read-only in-memory + on-disk regions) enabling in-place updates of the hot set; up to 160M ops/sec single-machine.
research_papers.relevance: Foundational design of Tsavorite/Garnet's engine; the in-place-update + epoch-protection model is the core IronCache should borrow for throughput-per-core.

research_papers.title: FASTER: An Embedded Concurrent Key-Value Store for State Management
research_papers.key_idea: Journal version expanding on FASTER's embedded use, recovery, and state-management workloads.
research_papers.relevance: Deeper treatment of recovery and embedding; informs IronCache's persistence and embeddability design.

research_papers.title: Concurrent Prefix Recovery: Performing CPR on a Database
research_papers.key_idea: Non-blocking checkpointing that commits 'all operations until time t per session' via asynchronous incremental checkpoints instead of a classic WAL.
research_papers.relevance: Underpins Garnet's Fold-Over/Snapshot checkpoints; a reference for IronCache's optional non-blocking persistence.

research_papers.title: Asynchronous Prefix Recoverability for Fast Distributed Stores (D-FASTER)
research_papers.key_idea: Extends CPR to distributed commit as a lightweight metadata-only operation across nodes.
research_papers.relevance: Relevant if IronCache pursues distributed/replicated durability semantics in cluster mode.

research_papers.title: From FASTER to F2: Evolving Concurrent Key-Value Store Designs for Large Skewed Workloads
research_papers.key_idea: Two-component hot-log (in-memory) + cold-log (on-disk) design with promotion/read-caching, optimized for very skewed larger-than-memory workloads and lower memory overhead than FASTER.
research_papers.relevance: Better blueprint than the single hybrid log for a cache's skewed, larger-than-memory tiering — directly informs IronCache's tiering design.

open_questions: Garnet/MSR publish only log-scale benchmark graphs, not absolute ops/sec or microsecond p99 figures — what are the real numbers on the F72s v2 setup, and how would a Rust thread-per-core design compare head-to-head?
How much of Garnet's throughput advantage comes from the HybridLog engine vs the inline-on-IO-thread network model? Decomposing this determines where IronCache should invest first.

Does the in-place-update / hybrid-log model interact well with smart compression (a stated IronCache goal)? In-place updates assume stable record size; compressed/variable-length values complicate in-place mutation — needs design study.

Garnet's object store relies on the .NET GC heap; what is the right GC-less Rust representation for list/hash/set/sortedset that preserves O(1)-ish ops and zero-copy serialization without fragmentation?
Is crossbeam-epoch sufficient, or does IronCache need FASTER's richer drain-list/trigger-action epoch framework for region-boundary shifts and index resizing?
What is the minimal durability story for a cache: is CPR's per-session prefix recovery overkill, and is a simpler periodic snapshot + optional AOF enough for v1?

How does F2's hot/cold split compare to a classic admission/eviction policy (e.g., TinyLFU/W-TinyLFU) for a cache workload — should IronCache combine log-structured storage with a modern admission policy rather than rely on hybrid-log markers alone?
Garnet supports server-side stored procedures and custom commands in C#; what is IronCache's portable extensibility story (WASM modules? Rust dylib plugins? Lua)?

proposed_issues.title: Adopt a narrow-waist storage API (Read/Upsert/Delete/RMW) under the RESP layer
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a four-primitive engine interface with callback hooks, mirroring Garnet's Tsavorite narrow waist, so the full RESP surface (including RMW commands like INCR/APPEND/expiry) is built on a uniform hot path. Decouples wire/command layer from the store.

proposed_issues.title: Engine design: HybridLog with in-place hot-set updates
proposed_issues.summary: Design IronCache's log-structured store as a tri-region hybrid log (mutable/read-only/stable) with in-place updates in the mutable region, ported from FASTER but with explicit Rust record layout and ownership (no GC).

proposed_issues.title: Decision: epoch-based reclamation (crossbeam-epoch) vs custom drain-list framework
proposed_issues.summary: Decide the lock-free concurrency backbone: evaluate crossbeam-epoch against a FASTER-style global-epoch + thread-local + trigger-action drain list, weighing maturity vs the need to coordinate region-boundary shifts and index resizing.

proposed_issues.title: Decision: thread-per-core, process-on-receiving-thread network model
proposed_issues.summary: Lock in the threading model: replicate Garnet's verified inline-on-IO-thread processing (no per-request hand-off) using io_uring or a thread-per-core async stack in Rust, contrasted with Redis's single-threaded event loop.

proposed_issues.title: Research: benchmark IronCache architecture vs Garnet/Redis/Valkey/KeyDB/Dragonfly
proposed_issues.summary: Reproduce Garnet's methodology (2x F72s-class, 8B kv, batch 4-4096, 1K & 256M keys, 1-128 sessions, p50/p99/p99.9) and, unlike MSR, publish absolute throughput and latency numbers.

proposed_issues.title: Research: F2 hot-log/cold-log split vs single hybrid log for skewed larger-than-memory
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate whether IronCache's larger-than-memory tier should follow F2's two-component (hot/cold) design rather than FASTER's single hybrid log, given caches' inherent access skew and F2's lower memory overhead.

proposed_issues.title: Design: GC-less representation for container types (list/hash/set/sortedset)
proposed_issues.summary: Design slab/arena-backed Rust representations for Redis composite types to replace Garnet's GC-heap object store, preserving zero-copy serialization and bounded fragmentation, bound to a unified operation log with the scalar store.

proposed_issues.title: Decision: default durability posture (tiering/AOF/checkpoint OFF by default)
proposed_issues.summary: Match Garnet's safe defaults (storage tier off, AOF off, compaction None) so IronCache ships as a fast in-memory cache by default; if AOF is offered, default to periodic group-commit, never per-op fsync.

proposed_issues.title: Research: space reclamation via revivification + Lookup-style compaction
proposed_issues.summary: Adopt Garnet's revivification (free-record reuse) and hash-chain-liveness Lookup compaction; explicitly avoid Scan-style compaction whose transient memory is proportional to the keyspace.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal (v1): full cluster mode, replication, and failover
proposed_issues.summary: Declare cluster sharding/replication/failover and FastMigrate-style key migration out of scope for the first prototype; preserve Redis-Cluster 16384-slot wire compatibility for later, single-node first.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal: managed runtime / GC — single static Rust binary instead
proposed_issues.summary: Explicitly reject Garnet's .NET/GC runtime: IronCache must be a single statically linked Rust binary with no GC pauses, extracting only Garnet's algorithms, not its runtime.

proposed_issues.title: Research: interaction of in-place updates with smart value compression
proposed_issues.summary: Study how the hybrid-log in-place-update model (which assumes stable record size) interacts with IronCache's smart-compression goal, since compressed/variable-length values complicate in-place mutation.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Verification done 2026-06-13. defaults.conf and TcpNetworkHandlerBase.cs read live from microsoft/garnet@main via gh contents API (base64-decoded); releases read via gh releases API. FASTER SIGMOD 2018 PDF text extracted locally with pdftotext after the WebFetch redirect to wp-content/uploads and the small-model extractor both failed on the compressed PDF streams. Benchmark docs (results-resp-bench) and the MSR Garnet blog fetched via WebFetch.

12 of 14 claims CONFIRMED. ONE CORRECTION: [faster-hash-bucket-layout] — the paper explicitly says a 64-byte bucket holds SEVEN 8-byte hash entries plus ONE 8-byte overflow-bucket pointer (8 physical 8-byte slots, but only 7 usable hash entries). The original claim of '8 entries/bucket' is wrong as stated; the cache-line/64-byte size, per-entry tentative bit (1 bit alongside a 15-bit tag + 48-bit address), and lock-free two-phase insert use are all correct.

Two minor observations that do NOT change verdicts: (1) For faster-peak-throughput, the headline 'up to 160M ops/sec' is stated verbatim in the abstract and conclusion, while the detailed YCSB micro-results give 115M (uniform) and 165M (Zipfian) — the 160M is the paper's own summary peak figure, so 'confirmed'. (2) For garnet-bench-qualitative the original 'medium' confidence is appropriate but the claim itself is fully supported, hence confirmed.

dimensions.summary:
KeyDB is a multithreaded fork of Redis, originally by EQ-Alpha (John Sully), acquired by Snap Inc. in May 2022 and re-homed to the Snapchat/KeyDB GitHub repo with a unified BSD-3 license. Its headline differentiator is multithreaded command execution on a SHARED keyspace (in contrast to Redis 6+/Valkey, which only multithread network I/O while keeping a single command-execution thread). KeyDB runs the normal Redis event loop on N server-threads; each accepted connection is pinned to a thread, and access to the shared dict is mediated by a custom fast ticket spinlock (src/fastlock.cpp/.asm) rather than a coarse global mutex. The lock spins with x86 PAUSE / ARM YIELD for a bounded loop count (0x100000 normally, 0x10000 under CPU pressure) before falling back to a Linux futex (FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET_PRIVATE). Marketed numbers claim ~5x ops/sec over single-threaded Redis 5 and up to ~7x with TLS, but these are vendor benchmarks on m5.4xlarge-class hardware and should be treated as directional.

For datasets larger than RAM, KeyDB FLASH uses RocksDB as a pluggable storage provider (src/storage/rocksdb.cpp, two column families: one for data, one for expires). Enabled via `--storage-provider flash <path>` with `maxmemory` + an `allkeys-lru`/`allkeys-lfu` eviction policy, it keeps hot values in RAM and evicts cold values to RocksDB while (by default) keeping every key — but not its value — in a RAM key cache; v6.3.4 added `flash-disable-key-cache` and moved the cluster-slot-to-key map and expires into the storage provider so cold data can also be expired/evicted. Vendor testing showed 200M keys * 1KB = 190GB on an m5d.2xlarge NVMe instance achieving ~85% of RAM throughput with ~0.5ms extra latency. FLASH is explicitly labelled Beta/experimental throughout the docs.

KeyDB's distribution story is Active Replication / multi-master active-active: `active-replica yes` makes a replica bidirectional, and `multi-master yes` plus multiple `replicaof` lines forms ring/full-mesh topologies. Each instance computes an ephemeral per-process UUID (not persisted) used to break replication loops. Conflict resolution is described two ways in the docs — the active-rep page claims timestamped "newest write wins," while the multi-master page admits it "defaults to last operation wins" and that with identical keys on two masters "it is undefined which key will be taken," with no automatic propagation of REPLICAOF REMOVE and a known tombstone gap on deletes. This is weaker than a true CRDT and is best characterized as approximate LWW, not a rigorous causal/MVCC merge. The MVCC claim is real but scoped: it provides non-blocking SCAN/KEYS and non-blocking BGSAVE via point-in-time snapshots (RocksDB Snapshot for FLASH; an in-memory snapshot for the dict), NOT a general multi-version concurrent-write store. Subkey expiry (EXPIREMEMBER / EXPIREMEMBERAT, with TTL/PTTL extended to members) is a genuine differentiator Redis lacks, though v6.3.4 "removed expireset and restored redis expire behaviour" for the internal data structure.

Maintenance status is the most load-bearing finding for IronCache strategy: the repo is NOT archived, but the last tagged release is v6.3.4 (2023-10-30) and the last commit to main was 2024-03-24. There has been no release in ~20 months and no commit in ~26 months as of 2026-06-13. KeyDB is based on Redis 6.x and never tracked Redis 7's functions, listpacks, RESP3 refinements, or sharded pub/sub; it has been effectively superseded by Valkey (the Linux Foundation Redis fork) as the community's multithreaded/active-active alternative. The README states Snap maintains it as internal caching infrastructure with no commercial product and no paid support. For IronCache, KeyDB is a rich source of mechanisms to study but a cautionary tale on governance: the shared-keyspace-with-fine-grained-locking model and pluggable RocksDB tiering are worth borrowing in spirit, while the bolted-on approximate-LWW replication and the single-vendor dormancy are anti-patterns to avoid.

prior_art_claims.claim: KeyDB guards the shared keyspace with a custom fast ticket spinlock (m_ticket.m_active/m_avail) that spins with PAUSE/YIELD then falls back to a Linux futex
prior_art_claims.value: ticket spinlock; spin loop limit 0x100000 (0x10000 under CPU pressure) then FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET_PRIVATE
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read fastlock.cpp source directly; struct fields and futex calls confirmed

verification.corrected_value: ticket spinlock (m_ticket.m_active/m_avail); loopLimit 0x100000 normal, 0x10000 under high CPU pressure; PAUSE (x86)/yield (ARM) then FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET_PRIVATE
verification.note: fastlock.cpp at v6.3.4 confirms exactly: `unsigned loopLimit = fHighPressure ? 0x10000 : 0x100000;` (line 361); m_ticket.m_avail/m_active atomic ticketing (lines 332-356,442); `__asm__ pause` (x86) / `yield` (ARM) in spin loop (lines 380-382); fallback `futex(&lock->m_ticket.u, FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET_PRIVATE, ...)` (line 321) and FUTEX_WAKE_BITSET_PRIVATE on unlock. All details accurate.

prior_art_claims.claim: KeyDB's design rationale is that core hash-table access is so fast the per-keyspace spinlock has low contention; transactions hold the lock for the duration of EXEC

verification.note: Both the 2019-10-07 blog and the v6.3.4 README state verbatim: 'Access to the core hash table is guarded by spinlock. Because the hashtable access is extremely fast this lock has low contention. Transactions hold the lock for the duration of the EXEC command.' Confirmed by two sources (docs blog + repo README).

Vendor benchmark; numbers match the claim.

prior_art_claims.claim: v6.3.4 added RDB-less full sync (enable-keydb-fastsync) and CPU-based load shedding (overload-protect-percent)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Listed in official v6.3.4 release notes

mechanisms.name: Multithreaded command execution on a shared keyspace
mechanisms.what_it_does: Runs the Redis event loop on N server-threads (default 2, recommended 4); each connection is pinned to a thread at accept(), and all threads operate on one shared dict guarded by fine-grained locking. This contrasts with Redis 6+/Valkey which thread only network I/O.
mechanisms.rationale: IronCache's max-throughput-per-core goal demands real multicore command execution, so the shared-keyspace + per-thread event-loop direction is right. But adapt: KeyDB pins connections to threads and serializes on a per-keyspace spinlock, which caps scaling. IronCache should evaluate sharded/partitioned keyspaces (per-core hash partitions a la Seastar/memcached extstore-free sharding) so most ops are lock-free, reserving cross-shard coordination only for multi-key commands. Borrow the goal, reject the single-shared-dict-with-one-lock topology.

mechanisms.name: Fast ticket spinlock with futex fallback (fastlock)
mechanisms.what_it_does: A custom ticket spinlock (m_ticket.m_active/m_avail via __atomic_fetch_add) that spins with x86 PAUSE / ARM YIELD up to a loop limit (0x100000, or 0x10000 under CPU pressure) then blocks on a Linux futex (FUTEX_WAIT/WAKE_BITSET_PRIVATE). Recursive (m_depth) and tracks owner pid.
mechanisms.rationale: This is a well-engineered, FIFO-fair, low-latency lock primitive: ticket ordering avoids starvation, adaptive spin-then-futex avoids burning cores under contention, and PAUSE/YIELD respect SMT siblings. IronCache (Rust) can borrow the pattern directly using crossbeam/parking_lot-style adaptive spinning or a custom ticket lock over futex/Windows WaitOnAddress. The exact tuning constants are a useful starting point to benchmark against.

mechanisms.name: Connection-to-thread balancing (min-clients-per-thread, SO_REUSEPORT, replica-weighting-factor)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Distributes accepted connections across server-threads using min-clients-per-thread (default 50), active-client-balancing, SO_REUSEPORT kernel distribution, and replica-weighting-factor (default 2) to under-load threads hosting expensive replica clients.
mechanisms.rationale: Load-balancing connections across worker threads is essential and the SO_REUSEPORT + best-effort rebalancing approach is sound. IronCache should adopt SO_REUSEPORT and per-core accept queues, but in a sharded design the relevant balancing is request-to-shard (by key hash) not connection-to-thread; connection affinity matters less when work is routed by key. Adapt the mechanism to a shard-routing model.

mechanisms.name: RocksDB-backed FLASH tiering (storage provider)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Pluggable RocksDBStorageProvider with separate column families for data and expires; hot values stay in RAM, cold values evicted to RocksDB on disk under maxmemory + allkeys-lru/lfu; an in-RAM key cache holds every key (toggleable via flash-disable-key-cache).
mechanisms.rationale: A RAM-over-flash tier is highly relevant to IronCache's memory-efficiency goal, but RocksDB is a heavyweight C++ LSM dependency that conflicts with the single-static-binary ethos and adds write amplification + compaction tail latency. Adapt: design a pluggable storage-provider trait but back it with a leaner embedded engine (e.g. a Rust LSM like sled/fjall, or a custom log-structured store) and keep keys/metadata in RAM. Borrow the column-family separation of data vs expires and the keys-in-RAM/values-on-flash split.

mechanisms.name: MVCC snapshots for non-blocking SCAN/KEYS and BGSAVE
mechanisms.what_it_does: Maintains point-in-time snapshots (in-memory snapshot of the dict; rocksdb::Snapshot for FLASH) so KEYS/SCAN and background save can iterate a consistent view without blocking concurrent writers.
mechanisms.rationale: Non-blocking iteration and snapshotted persistence are exactly what a high-throughput cache needs to avoid latency cliffs on SCAN/KEYS/save. Rust's ownership model and structures like persistent/immutable trees or epoch-based reclamation (crossbeam-epoch) make copy-on-write snapshots natural. Borrow the concept; implement via COW + epoch GC rather than fork()-based COW where possible.

mechanisms.name: Active-active multi-master replication with ephemeral UUID loop prevention
mechanisms.what_it_does: active-replica yes + multi-master yes + multiple replicaof lines build ring/mesh topologies; each node has an ephemeral per-process UUID to break replication loops; conflicts resolved by last-operation-wins.
mechanisms.rationale: The loop-prevention UUID is fine, but the conflict model is fundamentally weak: docs admit identical-key conflicts are 'undefined,' deletes leave tombstone gaps, and there is no causal ordering or true CRDT. For IronCache's single-node-to-multi-node path, reject KeyDB's approximate LWW and instead design from CRDT/causal foundations (e.g. per-key Lamport/HLC timestamps with explicit tombstone handling, or proper CRDT registers) so multi-master is correct by construction rather than best-effort.

mechanisms.name: Subkey expiry (EXPIREMEMBER / EXPIREMEMBERAT)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Sets independent TTLs on individual members of sets/hashes, with TTL/PTTL extended to query member-level expiry; active + passive expiration cycles remove expired subkeys.
mechanisms.rationale: A genuine capability Redis historically lacked and a real differentiator. IronCache should consider native per-element TTLs as a first-class feature (Redis 7.4 later added hash-field TTLs, so it is converging). Adapt the command surface but design the expiry index for efficiency (e.g. a per-shard timer wheel or min-heap of member expirations) rather than KeyDB's expireset approach, which it itself reverted in 6.3.4.

mechanisms.name: CPU-based load shedding (overload-protect-percent) and RDB-less fast sync
mechanisms.what_it_does: overload-protect-percent sheds/disconnects clients when CPU exceeds a threshold; enable-keydb-fastsync performs full replica sync without staging an RDB file on disk.
mechanisms.rationale: Both are pragmatic operability wins. Admission control / load shedding under CPU saturation protects tail latency and is a good fit for IronCache's efficiency story. Diskless/streamed full sync avoids the fork+RDB write amplification path. Borrow both as design requirements for the replication and overload-handling subsystems.

ironcache_implications: Pursue real multicore command execution (KeyDB's core insight) but reject its single-shared-dict-plus-one-spinlock topology; prototype a per-core sharded keyspace where single-key ops are lock-free and only multi-key/cross-shard commands coordinate, to beat KeyDB on throughput-per-core.

Borrow KeyDB's adaptive ticket-spinlock-then-futex pattern for any unavoidable shared structure: FIFO-fair, SMT-friendly (PAUSE/YIELD), and contention-aware (futex fallback). In Rust, evaluate parking_lot vs a custom ticket lock over futex/WaitOnAddress and benchmark against KeyDB's tuning constants.

Design FLASH-style RAM-over-disk tiering as a pluggable storage-provider trait, but back it with a lean embedded Rust engine (not RocksDB) to preserve the single-static-binary goal and avoid LSM compaction tail latency; keep all keys + metadata in RAM, values on flash, with separate data/expire indexes.

Make non-blocking SCAN/KEYS and snapshotted background persistence a hard requirement, implemented via copy-on-write + epoch-based reclamation (crossbeam-epoch) rather than fork()-COW, to eliminate latency cliffs.

Treat KeyDB's approximate last-write-wins multi-master as an anti-pattern: if IronCache offers active-active, build it on causal/HLC timestamps or CRDTs with explicit tombstone semantics so identical-key conflicts and deletes are well-defined, not 'undefined.'
Adopt per-element TTLs (EXPIREMEMBER-style) as a first-class feature but back them with an efficient per-shard timer wheel / min-heap, noting KeyDB itself reverted its expireset structure in 6.3.4.
Bake in operability primitives KeyDB added late: CPU-based admission control / load shedding and diskless streamed full replica sync, designed in from M1 rather than bolted on.

Use KeyDB as the cautionary governance case study in the Vision EPIC: a strong single-vendor fork (Snap) that stalled (no release since v6.3.4 in Oct 2023, no commit since Mar 2024) and was overtaken by Valkey; argue IronCache needs an open, community-tracked roadmap and must track upstream Redis/Valkey wire features (RESP3, functions, hash-field TTLs) that KeyDB never adopted.

research_papers.title: Scaling Memcache at Facebook
research_papers.key_idea: Lessons scaling a multithreaded in-memory cache (memcached) including lock granularity, lease mechanisms, and replication/consistency tradeoffs at scale.
research_papers.relevance: Directly informs IronCache's choice between KeyDB's shared-keyspace locking and a sharded model, and how to reason about cache consistency under replication.

research_papers.title: MICA: A Holistic Approach to Fast In-Memory Key-Value Storage
research_papers.key_idea: Partition the keyspace across cores (exclusive read/write or concurrent read) with lossy/lossless indexes and kernel-bypass networking to maximize per-core KV throughput.
research_papers.relevance: The strongest argument for IronCache's per-core sharded keyspace alternative to KeyDB's single shared dict + spinlock.

research_papers.title: Memshare: a Dynamic Multi-tenant Key-value Cache
research_papers.key_idea: Log-structured memory management and dynamic memory partitioning to improve hit-rate and memory efficiency across tenants.
research_papers.relevance: Feeds IronCache's memory-efficiency and eviction design, complementary to KeyDB's RocksDB FLASH tiering approach.

research_papers.title: WiscKey: Separating Keys from Values in SSD-conscious Storage
research_papers.key_idea: Separate keys (in the LSM tree) from values (in a separate value log) to drastically cut write amplification on SSD.
research_papers.relevance: Directly relevant to designing a leaner alternative to KeyDB's RocksDB FLASH tier with keys-in-RAM/values-on-flash.

research_papers.title: Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs)
research_papers.key_idea: Data types whose concurrent updates always converge without coordination, providing principled strong eventual consistency.
research_papers.relevance: The correct foundation for IronCache active-active, replacing KeyDB's admittedly 'undefined' last-write-wins multi-master.

open_questions: Does KeyDB's per-keyspace spinlock actually scale linearly past ~4 server-threads on modern many-core CPUs, or does the shared dict become the bottleneck? Need independent benchmarks since vendor numbers use only ~7 threads.
How much RAM does KeyDB's FLASH key cache consume per key (keys-in-RAM model), and what is the practical RAM:flash ratio ceiling before hit-rate collapses? Docs are vague on the numeric ratio.

What is RocksDB's compaction-induced tail-latency profile under sustained write-heavy FLASH workloads, and can a Rust LSM (fjall/sled) or custom log-structured store match throughput with smaller binary size and fewer dependencies?

Exactly how does KeyDB reconcile concurrent writes to the same key across multiple server-threads vs across masters — is intra-node ordering total under the spinlock while inter-node is only LWW, and where are the consistency gaps?

Is KeyDB effectively end-of-life? Snap maintains it internally but with no public release since Oct 2023 — should IronCache cite it as historical prior art rather than a living competitor, and instead benchmark against Valkey's multithreading work?

Does KeyDB's MVCC snapshot for BGSAVE avoid fork() entirely (true in-process COW) or still rely on copy-on-write fork semantics, and what is the memory overcommit behavior (v6.3.4 fixed an overcommit-during-fork bug)?

proposed_issues.title: Decide keyspace concurrency model: shared dict + fine-grained lock (KeyDB) vs per-core sharded keyspace (MICA)
proposed_issues.summary: Choose between KeyDB-style shared keyspace with adaptive locking and a per-core sharded keyspace that keeps single-key ops lock-free. This decision is foundational to the throughput-per-core goal.

proposed_issues.title: Research: independent benchmark of KeyDB shared-keyspace scaling vs core count
proposed_issues.summary: Reproduce KeyDB's multithreading benchmarks on a modern many-core box to find where the per-keyspace spinlock stops scaling, so IronCache can target a higher ceiling.

proposed_issues.title: Design: adaptive ticket-spinlock-then-futex primitive in Rust
proposed_issues.summary: Borrow KeyDB's fastlock pattern (FIFO ticket, PAUSE/YIELD spin, futex/WaitOnAddress fallback) for any unavoidable shared structure; evaluate parking_lot vs a custom implementation and pin tuning constants.

proposed_issues.title: Design: pluggable RAM-over-flash storage provider without RocksDB
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a storage-provider trait for tiering cold values to disk while keeping keys/metadata in RAM, backed by a lean embedded Rust engine to preserve the single-static-binary goal (vs KeyDB's RocksDB dependency).

proposed_issues.title: Decision: reject last-write-wins multi-master; adopt CRDT/HLC foundation for active-active
proposed_issues.summary: KeyDB's multi-master is admittedly 'undefined' on identical-key conflicts and leaks tombstones on deletes. Decide on a principled causal/HLC or CRDT model for IronCache's single-to-multi-node path.

proposed_issues.title: Design: non-blocking SCAN/KEYS and snapshotted persistence via COW + epoch GC
proposed_issues.summary: Borrow KeyDB's MVCC-snapshot idea for non-blocking iteration and background save, implemented with copy-on-write structures and crossbeam-epoch reclamation instead of fork()-COW.

proposed_issues.title: Design: native per-element TTLs (EXPIREMEMBER) with an efficient expiry index
proposed_issues.summary: Offer per-member TTLs on collections like KeyDB's EXPIREMEMBER (and Redis 7.4 hash-field TTLs), backed by a per-shard timer wheel/min-heap rather than KeyDB's reverted expireset.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal: tracking KeyDB feature parity; track Valkey/Redis wire features instead
proposed_issues.summary: Declare KeyDB historical prior art (dormant since 2024, Redis-6-based) and a non-goal for parity; instead track RESP3, functions, and hash-field TTLs from upstream Redis/Valkey.

proposed_issues.title: Task: capture KeyDB governance case study in the Vision EPIC
proposed_issues.summary: Document KeyDB's trajectory (EQ-Alpha to Snap acquisition to stall after v6.3.4, overtaken by Valkey) as the cautionary argument for IronCache's open, community-tracked roadmap.

proposed_issues.title: Design: CPU-based admission control and diskless streamed full sync
proposed_issues.summary: Bake in KeyDB's late additions overload-protect-percent (load shedding under CPU saturation) and enable-keydb-fastsync (RDB-less full sync) as first-class requirements from the architecture spec.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Methodology: used `gh api`/`gh search code` and raw.githubusercontent.com to read KeyDB source AT THE v6.3.4 TAG (not just main) for all code-default claims, plus WebFetch on docs.keydb.dev pages and an independent WebSearch for the acquisition date and Redis base lineage.

15 of 16 claims CONFIRMED against primary sources. 1 CORRECTED:

- keydb-last-commit-dormant: the specific "last commit 2024-03-24" is WRONG. The actual HEAD of Snapchat/KeyDB main is 2024-04-04T20:26:33Z (sha 603ebb27, 'I think we want ARM's program counter here.'), verified via both `gh api repos/Snapchat/KeyDB/branches/main` and the commits list. The surrounding facts hold: archived=false, open_issues_count=270, dormant since early/mid 2024. Verdict corrected (date only), not refuted.

Notable corroborations:
- fastlock.cpp loopLimit values (0x100000 normal / 0x10000 high-pressure), FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET_PRIVATE fallback, PAUSE/yield, and m_ticket.m_active/m_avail ticketing are all exactly as claimed at v6.3.4.
- rocksdb.cpp at v6.3.4 confirms separate data + expire column families and rocksdb GetSnapshot/ReleaseSnapshot for point-in-time MVCC.
- v6.3.4 release notes confirm flash-disable-key-cache as new in 6.3.4, plus expires + cluster-slot-to-key map moved into the storage provider.
- Redis base lineage: no functions.c/function_lua.c (Redis 7.0 FUNCTION) at v6.3.4; only scripting.cpp (6.x EVAL). KeyDB 6.3 documented at parity with Redis 6.2.6.

Caveats / lower-confidence anchors:
- Several benchmark claims (5x, TLS 7x, FLASH 190GB) are vendor self-reported numbers on docs.keydb.dev; they are confirmed as accurately quoting the vendor, but are not independent third-party benchmarks.
- Snap acquisition date: official KeyDB blog slug + TechCrunch say 2022-05-12; Crunchbase says 2022-05-11. Treated as confirmed given the official blog matches the claim; the one-day spread is announcement-window noise.
- keydb-mvcc-nonblocking: README explicitly states non-blocking KEYS/SCAN; non-blocking BGSAVE is well-established fork-snapshot behavior and corroborated by the snapshot code, though the README line itself only names KEYS/SCAN.

dimensions.summary:
Memcached (latest release 1.6.42, released 2026-05-18; version confirmed against git tags and the ReleaseNotes wiki) is a multi-threaded in-RAM key-value cache whose defining design is the slab allocator. Memory is carved into fixed 1MB pages (slab_page_size), each page assigned to one of up to 64 slab classes and cut into equal-size chunks. Chunk sizes start at slab-min-size 48 bytes and grow geometrically by the -f growth factor (default 1.25, then chunk-aligned). A page, once assigned to a class, is never re-cut, so the allocator trades internal fragmentation (each item rounds up to the next chunk size) for O(1) alloc/free with no external fragmentation. Default item_size_max is exactly 1MB (min 1k, max 1G); slab_chunk_size_max is page/2 = 512KB. A slab automover/rebalance thread can reassign whole pages between classes as the working set shifts.

Eviction uses a per-slab-class segmented LRU with four sub-queues: HOT, WARM, COLD, and TEMP. New items load into HOT, a non-reordered probationary queue; at its tail an item moves to WARM if it has been hit twice (ACTIVE bit set) or to COLD otherwise. WARM holds scan-resistant active items; inactive ones drain to COLD, the unbounded main LRU from whose tail items are actually evicted. TEMP (off by default) isolates very short-TTL items so they neither bump nor get evicted. HOT and WARM are size-capped by hot_lru_pct=20 and warm_lru_pct=40, with age caps hot_max_factor=0.2 and warm_max_factor=2.0 relative to COLD's tail age. The lru_maintainer thread juggles items between segments, enforces limits, and reclaims expired tail items; the separate lru_crawler walks every sub-LRU reclaiming expired items proactively using TTL histograms. lru_segmented is true by default; notably settings_init sets lru_maintainer_thread=false, but main() sets start_lru_maintainer=true and start_lru_crawler=true, so both threads run by default unless disabled via no_lru_maintainer/no_lru_crawler.

Memcached has been multi-threaded since the 1.2 era: -t defaults to 4 worker threads, each owning its own libevent event_base (created with EVENT_BASE_FLAG_NOLOCK for lock-free per-thread loops). Concurrency on the single global hash table (default HASHPOWER_DEFAULT=16, i.e. 65536 buckets, growing at runtime) is protected by a hash-striped array of item_locks; the lock-table size scales with thread count (power 10-15, i.e. 1024-32768 locks). This sharded-lock design is the core of memcached's throughput-per-core story.

Extstore (-o ext_path=/file:5G) extends capacity onto SSD/flash: keys, the hash table, and item metadata stay in RAM (a ~12-byte pointer header per item points to flash), while values larger than ext_item_size (default 512 bytes) are written to disk in pages (ext_page_size default 64M) via a write buffer (ext_wbuf_size default 8M) and ext_threads (default 1) IO workers, with background compaction. Extstore is explicitly NOT durability. Separately, the restartable/warm-cache feature (-e/--memory-file, since 1.5.18, marked EXPERIMENTAL) mmaps item memory to a file on a tmpfs/pmem mount; on a clean SIGUSR1 shutdown it writes a .meta file, and on restart it fixes up pointers and rebuilds the hash table in a few seconds. It is invalidated if -m, max item size, slab chunk sizes, CAS, or slab-reassign settings change, requires a stable clock, loses writes during downtime, and is incompatible with extstore.

Three wire protocols exist: the classic ASCII text protocol; the binary protocol (introduced 2008, officially deprecated since 1.6.0, frozen to bugfixes only); and the meta protocol (since 2019), a flag-based ASCII protocol that subsumes binary's features, is cross-compatible with text, and is recommended for all new clients. Explicit non-features: memcached offers no persistence (extstore and warm-restart are capacity/restart conveniences, not durability guarantees), no replication, and no server-side clustering — horizontal scale comes purely from client-side sharding (consistent hashing across an independent server pool).

mechanisms.name: Slab allocator (fixed pages + power-law chunk classes)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Carves RAM into 1MB pages assigned to up to 64 size classes whose chunks grow by factor 1.25; O(1) alloc/free, no external fragmentation, internal fragmentation per item.
mechanisms.rationale: Adopt slab/size-class allocation for O(1) predictable memory, but reduce internal fragmentation with finer/adaptive class sizing; the plain 1.25 power-law wastes too much for IronCache's minimal-memory goal.

mechanisms.name: Segmented LRU (HOT/WARM/COLD/TEMP)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Per-class four-segment LRU with an ACTIVE bit; scan-resistant and batches reordering to cut lock contention vs naive LRU.
mechanisms.rationale: Scan resistance and low-contention reordering are right, but TinyLFU/S3-FIFO/SIEVE beat segmented LRU on hit ratio per byte; borrow segment-draining/async-bump, adapt the policy.

mechanisms.name: Striped item locks + per-thread libevent loops
mechanisms.what_it_does: Each worker thread owns a NOLOCK libevent base; the global hash table is guarded by a hash-striped lock array sized to thread count.
mechanisms.rationale: Sharded locks + thread-local event loops are the proven path to throughput-per-core; maps cleanly to a Rust per-core runtime and sharded maps.

mechanisms.name: Slab automover / page rebalancer
mechanisms.what_it_does: Background thread moves whole 1MB pages between slab classes when one is starved and another over-provisioned.
mechanisms.rationale: Rebalancing is required once you commit to size classes, but page-granularity moves are coarse; adapt with finer migration tied to IronCache's AI-driven tuning.

mechanisms.name: Extstore (RAM index + flash values)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Keeps keys/hash/metadata + small items in RAM, spills large values to SSD via a ~12B pointer header, write-buffered with background compaction.
mechanisms.rationale: Tiered RAM-index/flash-value is a strong fit for cost-efficient large caches; borrow the architecture but pair it with real durability so it isn't 'not persistence'.

mechanisms.name: Warm restart via mmap memory-file
mechanisms.what_it_does: mmaps item memory to a tmpfs/pmem file; clean shutdown writes metadata and restart rebuilds pointers/hash in seconds.
mechanisms.rationale: Fast restart without a cold cache is valuable for a single-binary product; adapt into a first-class, non-experimental snapshot/restore that also supports real persistence.

mechanisms.name: Meta protocol (flag-based ASCII)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Extensible ASCII command set with flags/tokens that subsumes the binary protocol and is text-compatible.
mechanisms.rationale: IronCache is Redis-wire-compatible so meta isn't the primary protocol, but its flag-extensibility (vs a rigid binary framing) is a lesson for any IronCache-native extensions.

mechanisms.name: No persistence / replication / clustering (client-side sharding)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Standalone node; scale-out is entirely client-side consistent hashing; no built-in durability or replication.
mechanisms.rationale: IronCache explicitly targets single-node to multi-node with smart features; rejecting the no-clustering stance is a core differentiator, though client-side sharding stays a valid baseline mode.

ironcache_implications: Memory efficiency is bounded by the size-class scheme: memcached's 1.25 factor leaves measurable internal fragmentation, so IronCache must evaluate finer/adaptive classes or compaction to actually beat it on bytes-per-item.
The striped-lock + per-thread NOLOCK event-loop design is the validated route to throughput-per-core and maps directly onto a Rust thread-per-core runtime with sharded maps.
Segmented LRU is a solid scan-resistant baseline but not state of the art; IronCache should benchmark TinyLFU/S3-FIFO/SIEVE against it and likely ship a more memory-efficient policy.
Extstore proves the RAM-index/flash-value tier works; IronCache can borrow it but must add genuine durability (which memcached deliberately omits) to differentiate.
The 1MB default item-size cap is a memcached convention, not a law; a Redis-compatible IronCache must handle large values and should treat large-item handling as a deliberate design choice.
Warm-restart-via-mmap shows fast restart is feasible; making it non-experimental and clock-independent is a concrete IronCache improvement.
Meta protocol's flag extensibility is a model for evolving a wire protocol without a binary rewrite, useful for IronCache extensions beyond the Redis wire format.
memcached defaults -t to 4 regardless of cores; IronCache should auto-scale worker count to detected cores for strong out-of-box throughput.

research_papers.title: An Empirical Analysis on Memcached's Replacement Policies
research_papers.key_idea: Dissects memcached's segmented (HOT/WARM/COLD) LRU and compares hit ratio/overhead vs simple LRU and alternatives on real traces.
research_papers.relevance: Directly informs whether IronCache keeps, tunes, or replaces segmented LRU; quantifies its strengths/weaknesses.

research_papers.title: TinyLFU: A Highly Efficient Cache Admission Policy
research_papers.key_idea: A frequency-sketch admission filter caches an item only if it would beat the eviction victim, giving near-optimal hit ratios at tiny metadata cost.
research_papers.relevance: Candidate replacement/augmentation for segmented LRU under IronCache's minimal-memory, max-hit-ratio goal.

research_papers.title: SIEVE is Simpler than LRU: an Efficient Turn-Key Eviction Algorithm
research_papers.key_idea: A single-bit, lazy-promotion eviction algorithm that beats LRU on hit ratio and is much cheaper to make concurrent.
research_papers.relevance: Low-lock, high-hit-ratio eviction matches IronCache's throughput-per-core + memory targets; strong alternative to segmented LRU.

research_papers.title: Workload Analysis of a Large-Scale Key-Value Store (Facebook Memcached)
research_papers.key_idea: Characterizes real memcached traffic: tiny values dominate, heavy read skew, diurnal patterns, and resulting fragmentation behavior.
research_papers.relevance: Grounds IronCache's size-class, fragmentation, and compression assumptions in real cache workloads.

open_questions: What are extstore's exact default constants in the 1.6.42 source (storage.c/extstore.h) versus the docs values (ext_item_size=512, ext_page_size=64M, ext_wbuf_size=8M, ext_threads=1)? Docs were used; source not yet cross-checked.
How much internal fragmentation does the 1.25 factor cost on realistic value distributions, and can a Rust allocator with adaptive classes beat it meaningfully?
Does memcached's slab automover converge under shifting working sets or thrash, and what thresholds drive it?
How does segmented LRU's hit ratio compare to TinyLFU/SIEVE/S3-FIFO at equal memory on standard cache traces?
What is real-world warm-restart recovery time vs dataset size, and how brittle are the clock/settings invalidation conditions?
Given Redis wire compat, is there value in also supporting a meta-style extensible protocol, or does it dilute focus?

proposed_issues.title: Decide slab/size-class allocator strategy and fragmentation budget
proposed_issues.summary: Choose memcached-style power-law classes, adaptive classes, or a jemalloc-style allocator; set an explicit internal-fragmentation budget that beats the 1.25-factor baseline.

proposed_issues.title: Benchmark eviction policy: segmented LRU vs TinyLFU/SIEVE/S3-FIFO
proposed_issues.summary: Run standard cache traces comparing hit ratio and per-item metadata cost; pick IronCache's default eviction policy and document the rationale.

proposed_issues.title: Design striped-lock + per-core runtime concurrency model
proposed_issues.summary: Adapt memcached's per-thread NOLOCK event loops and hash-striped item locks to a Rust thread-per-core architecture; define lock-table sizing relative to core count.

proposed_issues.title: Design RAM-index/flash-value tier (extstore-equivalent) with durability
proposed_issues.summary: Borrow extstore's RAM-keys/flash-values layout but add real persistence so it is not merely capacity extension; specify spill threshold, write buffer, compaction.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal for M0/M1: server-side clustering and replication
proposed_issues.summary: Like memcached, ship single-node first with client-side sharding as baseline; defer built-in clustering/replication to a later milestone as an explicit differentiator.

proposed_issues.title: Spec fast warm-restart / snapshot-restore as first-class
proposed_issues.summary: Adapt memcached's mmap memory-file warm restart into a non-experimental, clock-independent snapshot/restore with pointer fix-up on boot.

proposed_issues.title: Verify memcached extstore default constants against 1.6.42 source
proposed_issues.summary: Read storage.c/extstore.h at tag 1.6.42 to confirm ext_item_size/ext_page_size/ext_wbuf_size/ext_threads defaults currently sourced only from docs.

dimensions.verify_notes: memcached was re-run standalone after the workflow agent hit a StructuredOutput size loop; claims self-verified, not independently adversarially checked

dimensions.summary:
The state of the art in cache-server memory allocation is dominated by three thread-caching, size-class allocators — jemalloc (Redis/Valkey, FreeBSD, formerly Rust std), mimalloc (Dragonfly, .NET), and snmalloc (research/Microsoft) — plus Google's tcmalloc. All four share a common skeleton: per-thread/per-CPU caches to make the fast path lock-free, fixed size classes to bound internal fragmentation, and large OS allocations (chunks/arenas/segments) carved into slabs to bound external fragmentation. The decisive differences for a cache are (a) how aggressively dirty pages are returned to the OS (RSS minimization), (b) the per-object metadata overhead, and (c) whether the allocator can be introspected to drive online defragmentation. jemalloc 5.x wins on (a) and (c): its dirty/muzzy page decay (opt.dirty_decay_ms default 10000ms; muzzy disabled) and its background_thread purger keep RSS close to live bytes, and Redis ships a forked jemalloc 5.3.0 that exposes je_get_defrag_hint() (guarded by JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT, built with --with-lg-quantum=3 for 8-byte spacing) so the server can ask "is the run holding this pointer sparse?" and relocate live objects during active defrag.

For IronCache the central tension is "max throughput per core, minimal memory" — these pull in opposite directions. mimalloc optimizes throughput and tail latency via extreme free-list sharding (one free list per ~64KiB page, plus separate thread-local and concurrent/atomic free lists so cross-thread frees are a single CAS) and Dragonfly chose it precisely for its predictable performance and clean segment layout; Microsoft's own numbers show mimalloc ~13% faster than jemalloc on Lean and 2.5x on sh6bench. But Redis/Valkey stay on jemalloc chiefly because of the defrag hint and proven RSS-decay behavior under churny, long-lived, eviction-driven workloads — exactly IronCache's domain. snmalloc's message-passing remote-free design (objects deallocated by a non-owning thread are batched into 2^6 buckets and shipped back lock-free; just 64 bits of metadata per 64KiB slab) is the most memory-efficient metadata layout published, and is attractive if IronCache adopts a thread-per-core / shared-nothing shard model where cross-shard frees are common.

The fragmentation accounting that matters is RSS vs used_memory: Redis defines mem_fragmentation_ratio = RSS / used_memory, and warns that the OS does not reclaim freed heap automatically, so a server that fills to 5GB then frees 2GB still shows ~5GB RSS while reporting ~3GB used. Ratios slightly above 1.0 are normal allocator overhead; high ratios after a peak-then-free indicate external fragmentation that active-defrag is designed to fix (it relocates objects within jemalloc runs using the hint, defaults: activedefrag no, threshold-lower 10%, threshold-upper 100%, ignore-bytes 100MB, cycle-min 1% CPU, cycle-max 25% CPU). Ratios below 1.0 mean the OS has swapped — a separate pathology.

Transparent Huge Pages are a footgun for any forking, COW-heavy server: Redis explicitly recommends `echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled` because after fork() for RDB/AOF, touching a few thousand 2MiB THP pages copies almost the whole heap, causing large latency spikes and up to ~2x memory. jemalloc's own opt.thp default is "default" (no change) and opt.metadata_thp is "disabled", reflecting the same caution. IronCache, as a single static binary, should ship a hardened default: pick one allocator, set decay/purge defaults that favor low RSS, disable THP for the heap (or at minimum for the fork path), and surface a fragmentation_ratio metric.

Per-object overhead minimization is the other half of "minimal memory." Redis attacks this above the allocator with compact encodings (listpack/intset, with je_malloc_usable_size-aware sizing) and shared small-integer objects, and the --with-lg-quantum=3 build gives 8-byte size-class granularity so a 24-byte object isn't rounded to 32. IronCache should combine an inline-small-string / pointer-tagging value representation (avoid a heap allocation and a 16-byte header for values <= ~14-22 bytes) with an 8-byte-granularity allocator and usable-size-aware capacity, so that the dominant small-KV case costs one allocation with near-zero slack. The Rust ecosystem makes all four allocators available as #[global_allocator] drop-ins: tikv-jemallocator 0.7.0 (bundling jemalloc 5.3.1), mimalloc 0.1.52, and snmalloc-rs 0.7.4 — but only jemalloc currently exposes the defrag hint and the rich mallctl introspection IronCache needs for accounting and online defrag.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis bundles a forked jemalloc whose VERSION file reads 5.3.0-0-g0, i.e. jemalloc 5.3.0, used as the default allocator on Linux.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis builds its bundled jemalloc with --with-lg-quantum set to 3 (8-byte size-class spacing) instead of the default 4 (16-byte), to add finer small size classes and reduce internal fragmentation for small objects.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis patches jemalloc with #define JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT and implements je_get_defrag_hint(ptr) so the server can ask jemalloc whether the slab/run holding a pointer is sparse enough that relocating the object would reduce fragmentation; this drives active defragmentation in src/defrag.c. The patch is optional — Redis detects whether the linked jemalloc supports it.

verification.corrected_value: je_get_defrag_hint() / JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT (optional jemalloc patch)
verification.note: deps/README.md confirms the patch is optional ('this feature of Redis is not mandatory and Redis is able to understand if the Jemalloc version ... supports such Redis-specific modifications'), instructs adding '#define JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT' and implementing je_get_defrag_hint(). Cross-checked src/defrag.c at 8.8.0 which declares 'int je_get_defrag_hint(void* ptr);' and calls 'if(!je_get_defrag_hint(ptr))'. Confirmed.

Each page has a thread-local free list for owner-thread frees and a separate atomic/concurrent free list so a non-owning thread can free with a single CAS.

(default 0/off); 'MIMALLOC_RESERVE_HUGE_OS_PAGES=N: where N is the number of 1GiB huge OS pages.' All match. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: snmalloc uses a bump-pointer/free-list data structure requiring just 64 bits of metadata per 64KiB slab, and a message-passing remote-free scheme that batches cross-thread frees into a fixed 2^k (k=6, i.e. 64) bucket array keyed by destination address, returning them to the owning allocator without locks.

prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in the ISMM 2019 snmalloc paper abstract/body.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis recommends disabling Transparent Huge Pages with echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled, because after fork() (RDB/AOF) writing to a few thousand 2MiB THP pages triggers copy-on-write of nearly the whole heap, causing large latency spikes and elevated memory use.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Recommendation and command appear in Redis latency/admin docs.

verification.corrected_value: echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
verification.note: Latency docs recommend the exact command and explain the mechanism verbatim: after fork, 'a few event loops runs will cause commands to target a few thousand of pages, causing the copy on write of almost the whole process memory ... big latency and big memory usage' with 2MB huge pages. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: During RDB save / AOF rewrite under a write-heavy workload, Redis can use up to ~2x its normal memory because fork() copy-on-write duplicates pages modified during the save; the extra memory is proportional to pages dirtied during the save window.
prior_art_claims.value: up to ~2x memory during fork-based save
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in Redis memory-optimization docs.

verification.corrected_value: up to ~2x memory during fork-based save (value correct; the verbatim statement lives on the admin/persistence docs and FAQ, NOT the cited memory-optimization page)
verification.note: Value is verbatim-confirmed on the admin page: 'while saving an RDB file on disk or rewriting the AOF log, Redis can use up to 2 times the memory normally used. The additional memory used is proportional to the number of memory pages modified by writes during the saving process...' (also in the FAQ). However the originally cited memory-optimization page does NOT contain this statement -- it only covers fragmentation. Corrected to the better source (admin docs).

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis defines mem_fragmentation_ratio = used physical memory (RSS) / used_memory; the OS does not automatically reclaim freed heap, so after filling to ~5GB and freeing ~2GB, RSS stays ~5GB while used_memory reports ~3GB. The ratio becomes unreliable (very high) when peak usage greatly exceeds current usage.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Definition and 5GB/2GB example read from Redis memory-optimization docs.

verification.corrected_value: mem_fragmentation_ratio = RSS / used_memory
verification.note: memory-optimization page confirms verbatim: fragmentation = 'physical memory actually used (the RSS value) divided by the amount of memory currently in use', plus the 5GB-filled/2GB-freed example: 'RSS ... will probably still be around 5GB, even if Redis will claim that the user memory is around 3GB ... because the underlying allocator can't easily release the memory.' Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Compact-encoding thresholds (per-object overhead minimization above the allocator) default to: hash-max-listpack-value 64, zset-max-listpack-entries 128, zset-max-listpack-value 64, set-max-intset-entries 512, set-max-listpack-entries 128, set-max-listpack-value 64. Note docs vs redis.conf disagree on hash-max-listpack-entries (512 in docs text vs 128 in config) — needs verification.
prior_art_claims.value: listpack entries 128, value 64; intset 512 (hash-entries disputed 128 vs 512)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Doc text and redis.conf differ on hash-max-listpack-entries; flagged for verification against config.c before treating as load-bearing.

verification.corrected_value: hash-max-listpack-entries=512, hash-max-listpack-value=64, zset-max-listpack-entries=128, zset-max-listpack-value=64, set-max-intset-entries=512, set-max-listpack-entries=128, set-max-listpack-value=64 (>=7.2)
verification.note: The claim's summary VALUE 'listpack entries 128, value 64; intset 512' is misleading: it conflates the data types. Authoritative config.c at 8.8.0 and redis.conf both give hash-max-listpack-entries default = 512 (NOT 128); the docs text also says 512. The 128 entries default applies to zset and set listpacks, not hash. The claim's own flagged dispute (128 vs 512) resolves to 512 for hash-entries. value=64 (hash/zset/set) and intset=512 confirmed. Corrected to disambiguate per-type defaults.

mechanisms.name: Dirty/muzzy page decay purging (madvise-based RSS reclamation)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Tracks freed-but-still-mapped pages as dirty, then over a decay window (dirty_decay_ms default 10s) progressively returns them to the OS via madvise(MADV_DONTNEED) (or an intermediate MADV_FREE muzzy state). This is the primary lever keeping RSS close to live bytes after deletions/evictions.
mechanisms.rationale: Exactly the mechanism a minimal-memory cache needs: it bounds RSS after eviction storms without per-free syscall overhead. IronCache should expose decay_ms as a tunable and default it aggressively (e.g. lower than 10s) since cache workloads churn hard; pair with a background purge thread so purging stays off the request path.

mechanisms.name: je_get_defrag_hint() + active defragmentation
mechanisms.what_it_does: The allocator answers whether the run/slab backing a given pointer is sparsely used; Redis scans the keyspace and reallocates+copies objects flagged as living in sparse runs, letting jemalloc free the now-empty runs. Cycle is CPU-throttled (1%-25%) and gated on fragmentation thresholds (10%-100%) and a 100MB ignore floor.
mechanisms.rationale: External fragmentation is the dominant RSS bloat source for long-lived caches with mixed object sizes; without online defrag the only fix is a restart. IronCache must ship this. Since upstream jemalloc does not expose the hint by default, IronCache should either carry the JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT patch on tikv-jemalloc-sys or design a slab allocator that exposes an equivalent is-this-slab-sparse query natively.

mechanisms.name: Per-thread cache (tcache) with bounded size classes
mechanisms.what_it_does: Each thread keeps a small magazine of recently-freed blocks per size class (jemalloc tcache_max default 32KiB), serving alloc/free without touching the arena lock; only spillover hits the shared arena.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the lock-free fast path, but in a thread-per-core / shard-per-core IronCache the thread cache becomes a per-shard cache with no cross-thread contention at all. Cap tcache size tightly because idle per-thread caches inflate RSS — a real cost when minimizing memory across many cores.

mechanisms.name: Free-list sharding (per-page local + concurrent free lists)
mechanisms.what_it_does: One free list per ~64KiB page instead of per size class, with a separate atomic free list so non-owning threads free via a single CAS. Improves locality, shrinks the fast path, and naturally batches cross-thread frees.
mechanisms.rationale: The locality and single-CAS remote-free are excellent for throughput-per-core. IronCache should adopt page-local free lists in any custom slab allocator, but a strict shard-per-core model can avoid the concurrent free list entirely (or use it only for cross-shard migration), trimming metadata.

mechanisms.name: Message-passing batched remote deallocation
mechanisms.what_it_does: Frees performed by a non-owning thread are batched into a fixed 2^6-bucket array keyed by the destination allocator address and shipped back in bulk without locks, with only 64 bits of metadata per 64KiB slab.
mechanisms.rationale: If IronCache uses a shared-nothing shard model, cross-shard frees (e.g. a value migrated during rebalancing, or a connection thread freeing a shard-owned object) are the producer/consumer pattern snmalloc targets. Borrow the batched-return idea; the ultra-low 64-bit/slab metadata is also a direct win for minimal per-object overhead. Adapt rather than borrow wholesale because IronCache likely controls thread topology and can often avoid remote frees by design.

mechanisms.name: Transparent Huge Page avoidance for the heap/fork path
mechanisms.what_it_does: Redis instructs operators to set THP to never; jemalloc keeps opt.thp=default and opt.metadata_thp=disabled. THP causes COW amplification after fork() (2MiB granularity) and unpredictable RSS.
mechanisms.rationale: Any forking persistence path (snapshotting) makes THP a latency and memory hazard. IronCache should either avoid fork-based snapshots (data-structure-level COW, or io_uring async serialization) or madvise(MADV_NOHUGEPAGE) the heap. As a single static binary it can also detect and warn when system THP=always at startup, like Redis.

mechanisms.name: Background purge thread
mechanisms.what_it_does: Offloads dirty/muzzy page purging (madvise) to dedicated jemalloc worker threads so the application thread never blocks on reclamation syscalls.
mechanisms.rationale: Redis flips the upstream default to ON precisely because synchronous purging on the request path causes latency spikes. IronCache, optimizing throughput-per-core, must keep purging off the hot path — enable a background purger (or a dedicated reclaim core) by default.

mechanisms.name: Fine-grained size classes (--with-lg-quantum=3)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Builds jemalloc with 8-byte quantum instead of 16-byte, adding intermediate small size classes so e.g. a 24-byte object rounds to 24 not 32, cutting internal fragmentation/slop on small allocations.
mechanisms.rationale: Small KV entries dominate cache memory; tighter size classes directly reduce per-object slop. IronCache should use 8-byte-granularity size classes for small objects (and be usable-size-aware when sizing buffers). Low cost, direct memory win.

mechanisms.name: maxmemory accounting + sampled-LRU/LFU eviction
mechanisms.what_it_does: Tracks used_memory against maxmemory and, when exceeded under an eviction policy (allkeys-lru/lfu etc.), evicts approximate-LRU/LFU keys sampled with maxmemory-samples (default 5). Default policy is noeviction (errors on write).
mechanisms.rationale: maxmemory must be measured against actual allocator-attributed bytes (via mallctl/malloc_usable_size), not naive object sizes, or accounting drifts from RSS. IronCache should account using allocator introspection and choose a more cache-appropriate default eviction policy than noeviction (e.g. allkeys-lru) given its target use case — a decision worth an explicit issue.

mechanisms.name: Inline small values / pointer tagging (shared integers, embedded strings)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Avoids a separate heap allocation and object header for small values by inlining them into the key/entry slot or sharing immutable small-integer objects, eliminating both allocator overhead and pointer-chasing.
mechanisms.rationale: The single biggest per-object win for a minimal-footprint cache: a small string/integer value should cost zero extra allocations and no 16-byte header. IronCache should design its value representation with NaN-boxing / pointer tagging / inline small strings (e.g. SSO up to ~22 bytes) from day one, since retrofitting the value layout later is expensive.

ironcache_implications: Default to jemalloc via tikv-jemallocator 0.7.0 (jemalloc 5.3.1) as the #[global_allocator] for the M2 prototype, because it is the only mainstream Rust allocator that exposes both rich mallctl introspection (for memory accounting) and the je_get_defrag_hint() path (for online defrag) that a long-lived cache needs.

Carry (or upstream) the JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT patch on tikv-jemalloc-sys so IronCache can implement active defragmentation; if that proves unmaintainable, scope a native slab allocator that exposes an equivalent is-this-slab-sparse query — treat this as a fork-vs-build decision.

Ship aggressive RSS-favoring defaults: enable jemalloc background_thread (upstream default is OFF), lower dirty_decay_ms below 10s for churny eviction workloads, and expose decay/purge as tunables with a documented latency/RSS trade-off.

Expose a mem_fragmentation_ratio (RSS/used_memory) metric and a peak-tracking variant, with a Redis-compatible MEMORY DOCTOR-style report, so operators can see external fragmentation and whether active-defrag is needed.

Design the value representation up front for minimal per-object overhead: inline small strings (SSO), pointer tagging / NaN-boxing for small integers and floats, and a shared-immutable-small-integer cache; combine with 8-byte size-class granularity and malloc_usable_size-aware buffer sizing.

Decide THP/fork stance explicitly: prefer non-fork snapshotting (async io_uring serialization or data-structure-level COW) to avoid THP COW amplification and the ~2x memory spike; if fork is used, madvise(MADV_NOHUGEPAGE) the heap and warn at startup when system THP=always (mirroring Redis).

If IronCache adopts thread-per-core / shard-per-core, evaluate snmalloc-style batched remote-free and its 64-bit-per-slab metadata to minimize both contention and metadata overhead; otherwise keep per-shard caches and avoid cross-thread frees by construction.

Benchmark all three Rust allocators (tikv-jemallocator 0.7.0, mimalloc 0.1.52, snmalloc-rs 0.7.4) under a realistic cache workload (mixed small KV, eviction churn, fragmentation after peak-then-free) measuring throughput/core, p99.9 latency, and steady-state RSS — vendor benchmarks (e.g. mimalloc Lean/sh6bench) are not representative.

research_papers.title: Mimalloc: Free List Sharding in Action
research_papers.key_idea: Extreme free-list sharding — one free list per ~64KiB page plus separate thread-local and atomic/concurrent free lists — yields a tiny, branch-light fast path and single-CAS cross-thread frees.
research_papers.relevance: Directly informs IronCache slab/page free-list layout for maximizing throughput-per-core with good locality.

research_papers.title: snmalloc: A Message Passing Allocator
research_papers.key_idea: Batched, lock-free message-passing return of remotely-freed objects to their owning allocator, with only 64 bits of metadata per 64KiB slab.
research_papers.relevance: Blueprint for cross-shard deallocation and ultra-low metadata overhead in a shared-nothing IronCache shard model.

research_papers.title: A Scalable Concurrent malloc(3) Implementation for FreeBSD (jemalloc)
research_papers.key_idea: Per-arena allocation with thread caches and size-class binning to reduce lock contention and fragmentation; the design lineage behind today jemalloc 5.x.
research_papers.relevance: Foundational rationale for the arena/tcache/size-class model IronCache will inherit if it uses jemalloc, and for tuning narenas vs RSS.

research_papers.title: Mesh: Compacting Memory Management for C/C++ Applications
research_papers.key_idea: Compacts the heap without moving objects in the virtual address space by remapping physically-backed pages whose live objects do not overlap (virtual-aliasing meshing), reducing RSS without changing pointers.
research_papers.relevance: An alternative to pointer-rewriting active-defrag: IronCache could reclaim fragmented physical pages without the je_get_defrag_hint object-copy machinery, avoiding pointer invalidation.

research_papers.title: Beyond malloc efficiency to fleet efficiency: a hugepage-aware memory allocator (TCMalloc/Temeraire)
research_papers.key_idea: Hugepage-aware allocation (Temeraire) that packs allocations to keep hugepages either fully used or fully empty, improving CPU via fewer TLB misses while controlling RSS at fleet scale.
research_papers.relevance: Counterpoint to THP is always bad: shows how a hugepage-aware allocator can get TLB benefits without COW bloat — relevant if IronCache wants huge-page throughput gains while bounding fragmentation.
