open_questions: Does maintaining a JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT patch on tikv-jemalloc-sys across jemalloc upstream bumps cost more than building a native slab allocator with a first-class sparseness query? (fork-vs-build)

What dirty_decay_ms / purge-delay default minimizes steady-state RSS for cache churn without inducing latency spikes from madvise/page-fault storms? Needs empirical sweep on a representative workload.

For a thread-per-core IronCache, can cross-shard frees be eliminated by construction (so neither mimalloc concurrent free list nor snmalloc message passing is needed), or are connection-thread-to-shard frees unavoidable?
Can Mesh-style physical-page compaction (no pointer rewriting) replace or complement object-copying active-defrag, given IronCache controls its own data structures?
How should maxmemory be accounted to track RSS closely — sum of malloc_usable_size, jemalloc stats.allocated via mallctl, or a hybrid — and what is the accounting error under heavy fragmentation?
Is there a measurable throughput win from hugepage-aware allocation (Temeraire-style) that justifies the COW/fork complexity for IronCache snapshot path, or should the heap stay on 4KiB pages?
What is the exact current redis.conf default for hash-max-listpack-entries (docs show 512, config suggests 128)? Needs verification against config.c before adopting as a comparison baseline.

proposed_issues.title: Decision: select default global allocator (tikv-jemallocator vs mimalloc vs snmalloc-rs vs custom slab)
proposed_issues.summary: Choose IronCache #[global_allocator] (and any per-shard allocator) weighing introspection/defrag-hint support, RSS-decay behavior, and throughput-per-core. Document why; lean toward tikv-jemallocator 0.7.0 (jemalloc 5.3.1) for M2 unless benchmarks say otherwise.

proposed_issues.title: Research: benchmark jemalloc/mimalloc/snmalloc under a cache workload (throughput/core, p99.9, steady-state RSS, post-peak fragmentation)
proposed_issues.summary: Build a representative harness (small mixed KV, eviction churn, fill-to-peak-then-free) and measure all three Rust allocator bindings; vendor microbenchmarks are not representative. Output feeds the allocator-selection decision.

proposed_issues.title: Design: online defragmentation strategy (jemalloc frag-hint object copy vs Mesh-style page remap vs native slab sparseness query)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify how IronCache reclaims external fragmentation for long-lived data: port JEMALLOC_FRAG_HINT/je_get_defrag_hint, adopt Mesh-style physical-page remapping, or expose a native is-this-slab-sparse query. Include CPU-throttle and threshold defaults modeled on Redis (10%-100%, 1%-25% CPU).

proposed_issues.title: Design: compact value representation — inline small strings, pointer tagging/NaN-boxing, shared small integers, 8-byte size classes
proposed_issues.summary: Define IronCache per-object layout to minimize allocator overhead: SSO for short values, tagged pointers for small ints/floats, a shared-immutable-int cache, and malloc_usable_size-aware sizing on 8-byte-granularity size classes. Retrofitting later is costly, so settle the value ABI early.

proposed_issues.title: Decision: THP and fork/snapshot stance (MADV_NOHUGEPAGE heap, non-fork serialization, startup THP=always warning)
proposed_issues.summary: Decide how IronCache snapshots without COW amplification: avoid fork (async io_uring serialization or data-structure COW), or madvise(MADV_NOHUGEPAGE) the heap and warn when system THP=always, mirroring Redis echo never guidance.

proposed_issues.title: Design: memory accounting + maxmemory enforcement against allocator-attributed bytes
proposed_issues.summary: Account used_memory from allocator introspection (jemalloc mallctl stats.allocated / malloc_usable_size) rather than naive object sizes so maxmemory tracks RSS; pick a cache-appropriate default eviction policy (likely allkeys-lru, not noeviction) and expose mem_fragmentation_ratio.

proposed_issues.title: Task: tune purge/decay and background-thread defaults for low RSS without latency spikes
proposed_issues.summary: Sweep dirty_decay_ms/purge-delay and enable a background purge thread by default (upstream jemalloc default is off; Redis flips it on); document the RSS-vs-latency curve and choose IronCache defaults.

proposed_issues.title: Meta: maintain a pinned prior-art table of allocator versions/defaults (jemalloc 5.3.x, mimalloc v3.x, snmalloc 0.7.x, Rust bindings)
proposed_issues.summary: Keep a living table of upstream versions and key defaults (decay, tcache, narenas, THP, Redis active-defrag config) with source URLs and accessed dates, refreshed each milestone to prevent stale numbers in the vision EPIC and README.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Verification method: read source at the exact 8.8.0 release tag via gh API (not unstable) for all Redis config.c / deps claims; cross-checked redis.conf; fetched jemalloc.net man page (which self-identifies as documenting jemalloc 5.3.1) for jemalloc upstream defaults; read tikv jemalloc-sys/jemallocator Cargo.toml and crates.io API; read microsoft/mimalloc readme.md and GitHub releases plus crates.io for mimalloc; fetched Redis docs pages directly.

14 confirmed, 3 corrected, 0 refuted, 0 uncertain.

The three corrections:
1. redis-encoding-thresholds: hash-max-listpack-entries default is 512, not 128. The claim's collapsed 'listpack entries 128' summary is wrong for hash; 128 is only zset/set entries. config.c + redis.conf + docs all agree on 512 for hash. The claim correctly flagged this as disputed; it resolves to 512.
2. redis-fork-2x-memory: the ~2x value is verbatim-correct, but it is NOT on the cited memory-optimization URL -- it lives on the admin/persistence docs and FAQ. Source corrected.
3. mimalloc-benchmarks: the 13% (Lean) and >2.5x (sh6bench) numbers are real in the readme, but they come from a fixed 2021 benchmark (16-core 5950x, jemalloc tag 5.2.1), not a v3.3.2-specific run. Value right; version attribution corrected. Already medium-confidence in the original.

Notable strong confirmations: bundled jemalloc 5.3.0-0-g0 vs tikv 5.3.1 delta is real; jemalloc-bg-thread=1 in Redis vs disabled upstream is real (both sides independently verified). All numeric config defaults matched exactly at the 8.8.0 tag.

dimensions.summary:
The Redis-lineage ecosystem ships multiple separate binaries (`redis-server`, `redis-cli`, `redis-benchmark`, `redis-check-rdb`, `redis-check-aof`, `redis-sentinel`), where the mode-switching ones (`redis-sentinel`, `redis-check-rdb`, `redis-check-aof`) are symlinks to `redis-server` that branch on argv[0]. KeyDB mirrors this exactly (keydb-server/keydb-cli, plus redis-cli/redis.conf symlinks for drop-in compat). Valkey is the same fork shape but cleaner to install (`brew install valkey`, a plain formula, vs Redis now requiring a tap + cask on macOS, and Redis being a conflicting formula). Dragonfly is the modern outlier and the right model to emulate: a single self-contained binary you download, rename to `dragonfly`, and run with gflags-style `--flag=value` options; on Mac/Windows it expects Docker. Config across all of them is a flat keyword-arg file (redis.conf style) with command-line overrides; Redis runs with no config file at all by default (daemonize no, dev-friendly defaults). This gives IronCache a clear easiest-setup target: one static binary that is BOTH server and CLI via clap subcommands (server | cli | bench | check | upgrade), runnable with zero config and sane defaults.

Live config in Redis is mature and is the parity bar: `CONFIG SET` (multi-param since 7.0), `CONFIG GET *` to enumerate, `CONFIG REWRITE` to persist back to the file. Notably Redis does NOT reload config on SIGHUP — runtime changes flow only through CONFIG SET/REWRITE. IronCache should ship CONFIG GET/SET/REWRITE parity for wire compatibility but ADD an explicit reload path (SIGHUP and/or a `CONFIG RELOAD`) so a TOML edit can be applied without a client, which Redis users repeatedly want and don't have.

Observability is where IronCache can leapfrog. Redis/Valkey expose only the RESP `INFO` command (sections: server, clients, memory, persistence, threads, stats, replication, cpu, commandstats, latencystats, cluster, keyspace, keysizes, errorstats, etc.; `default` is the implicit arg), SLOWLOG (default slowlog-log-slower-than=10000us/10ms, slowlog-max-len=128), and LATENCY monitoring (latency-monitor-threshold default 0 = OFF; LATENCY LATEST/HISTORY/RESET/DOCTOR). Crucially, NEITHER Redis nor Valkey has a built-in Prometheus endpoint — operators must run the third-party oliver006/redis_exporter on :9121 that scrapes INFO. Dragonfly fixed this: it serves Prometheus-format metrics natively at `:6379/metrics` (same port as RESP, HTTP/RESP multiplexed) and a K8s admin port 9999. IronCache should follow Dragonfly: ship INFO + SLOWLOG + LATENCY for wire-parity AND a native `/metrics` endpoint so there is no sidecar, with latency monitoring on-by-default (Redis ships it off, so users fly blind).

For build/distribution, Rust gives IronCache an inherent advantage. The `x86_64-unknown-linux-musl` target sets `crt-static` by default and produces a fully static binary with no glibc/runtime dependency, runnable on any Linux of the same arch (this is the single-binary thesis). The practical cross-build toolchain is cargo-zigbuild (v0.22.3, 2026-04-26) which uses Zig as the linker/C-toolchain: it can target musl for x86_64+aarch64, pin a minimum glibc per target (e.g. `--target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.2.17`) for gnu builds that still run on old distros, and build macOS `universal2`. `cross` is the alternative (Docker-image-per-target). For packaging, distroless `static-debian13` (~2 MiB, includes CA certs + nonroot user) or `scratch` is the right container base for a static binary — sub-2 MiB images vs ~124 MiB debian.

Self-update is a solved problem in Rust: the `self_update` crate (v0.44.0) pulls release artifacts from GitHub/GitLab/S3/GCS, compares semver, and replaces the running executable via the `self-replace` crate, which writes the new file next to the current one and does an atomic rename (POSIX rename(2); Windows rename-then-can't-unlink dance). Neither provides automatic ROLLBACK — that is the gap IronCache must design (keep the previous binary as `ironcache.old`, verify the new binary's `--version`/healthcheck post-swap, roll back on failure). For supply chain, `cargo-auditable` (v0.7.5, 2026-05-21) embeds a timestamp-free, sorted dependency manifest into the binary (reproducible-build safe), and `cargo-cyclonedx` / the auditable2cdx bridge emit CycloneDX SBOMs; Cargo.lock pins the reproducible-build base. cargo-dist / `dist` (axodotdev, ~v0.30–0.31, actively maintained) automates the whole release: cross-built archives + checksums + a one-line `curl … | sh` installer that manages PATH and embeds checksums. The easiest-setup target for IronCache: a single reproducible static musl binary per arch, a `curl … | sh` installer plus `brew install ironcache` (plain formula, no tap, no cask, no conflict — beating Redis), a distroless image, a systemd unit, and `ironcache upgrade` with atomic swap + verified rollback.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis builds 6 separate binaries (redis-server, redis-cli, redis-benchmark, redis-sentinel, redis-check-rdb, redis-check-aof); redis-sentinel/redis-check-rdb/redis-check-aof are symlinks to redis-server that branch on argv[0], not standalone code.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from the Makefile install target showing ln -sf REDIS_SERVER_NAME.

verification.corrected_value: 6 binaries; redis-sentinel/redis-check-rdb/redis-check-aof = ln -sf to redis-server (argv[0] branch)
verification.note: Verified against unstable src/Makefile: all: target lists 6 (redis-server, redis-sentinel, redis-cli, redis-benchmark, redis-check-rdb, redis-check-aof). install: target has exactly three `@ln -sf $(REDIS_SERVER_NAME) ...` lines for check-rdb, check-aof, sentinel. server.c (lines ~7604, 8010-8012) branches on exec_name via strstr("redis-sentinel"), strstr("redis-check-rdb"), strstr("redis-check-aof") = argv[0] dispatch. Note: sentinel/check-rdb/check-aof are not standalone object sets; sentinel.o, redis-check-rdb.o, redis-check-aof.o are linked into REDIS_SERVER_OBJ. Fully confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis runs with no config file required (default daemonize no, dev-friendly defaults); command-line args like --port/--bind/--maxmemory override the optional redis.conf.
prior_art_claims.value: config file optional; daemonize no by default
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Consistent across Redis config docs and multiple corroborating sources.

prior_art_claims.version: CONFIG SET available since 2.0.0; multi-param since 7.0.0
prior_art_claims.claim: Runtime config changes go through CONFIG SET (not persisted) + CONFIG REWRITE (persists to redis.conf); Redis does NOT reload config on SIGHUP.
prior_art_claims.value: CONFIG SET / CONFIG REWRITE; no SIGHUP reload
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Versions and behavior stated in official command page; SIGHUP absence corroborated by multiple sources.

verification.corrected_value: CONFIG SET (since 2.0.0) / CONFIG REWRITE; multi-param since 7.0.0; no SIGHUP reload
verification.note: redis.io docs confirm CONFIG SET available since 2.0.0, multi-parameter capability added in 7.0.0, and changes are in-memory only (CONFIG REWRITE persists to redis.conf). No SIGHUP config-reload is documented for Redis (SIGHUP is not a config-reload signal in Redis; the docs make no such claim). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: INFO returns sections: server, clients, memory, persistence, threads, stats, replication, cpu, commandstats, latencystats, cluster, modules, keyspace, keysizes, errorstats, etc.; with no arg the 'default' subset is returned.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Section list read directly from official INFO command page.

verification.corrected_value: ~15+ sections (server, clients, memory, persistence, threads, stats, replication, cpu, commandstats, latencystats, cluster, modules, keyspace, keysizes, errorstats, plus sentinel/hotkeys/errorstats); no-arg returns 'default' subset
verification.note: redis.io INFO docs enumerate all claimed sections plus a couple more (sentinel, hotkeys). With no argument the 'default' option is assumed (a standard subset). Docs do not enumerate exactly which sections comprise 'default', but the existence of all listed sections and the default-subset behavior are confirmed. The claim's '~15 sections' is approximate and accurate.

prior_art_claims.claim: SLOWLOG defaults: slowlog-log-slower-than = 10000 microseconds (10ms), slowlog-max-len = 128 entries; SLOWLOG GET/LEN/RESET.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Defaults stated on official SLOWLOG page and corroborated by a second source.

verification.corrected_value: slowlog-log-slower-than = 10000 us (10ms); slowlog-max-len = 128; SLOWLOG GET/LEN/RESET; SLOWLOG since 2.2.12
verification.note: Verified directly in src/config.c (unstable): createULongConfig("slowlog-max-len", ... default 128) and createLongLongConfig("slowlog-log-slower-than", ... default 10000). redis.io docs confirm SLOWLOG GET available since 2.2.12 and GET/LEN/RESET subcommands. Confirmed from source.

prior_art_claims.claim: Latency monitoring is OFF by default: latency-monitor-threshold default = 0; commands LATENCY LATEST/HISTORY/RESET/DOCTOR.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Default 0 stated on official latency-monitor page and SLOWLOG page; corroborated.

verification.corrected_value: latency-monitor-threshold = 0 (monitoring off by default); LATENCY LATEST/HISTORY/RESET/DOCTOR (also GRAPH)
verification.note: src/config.c (unstable): createLongLongConfig("latency-monitor-threshold", ... default 0). redis.io latency-monitor docs state monitoring is off by default (threshold 0) and list LATENCY LATEST/HISTORY/RESET/DOCTOR (plus GRAPH). Confirmed from source and docs.

prior_art_claims.claim: Neither Redis nor Valkey exposes a native Prometheus endpoint; operators run the third-party oliver006/redis_exporter (default :9121) which scrapes INFO.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: redis_exporter README confirms it scrapes INFO; no Redis docs describe a native metrics endpoint.

verification.corrected_value: no native /metrics; third-party oliver006/redis_exporter on default :9121, scrapes INFO
verification.note: redis_exporter README confirms default listen 0.0.0.0:9121, exports most items from INFO, and is a standalone third-party exporter (not built into Redis). Neither Redis nor Valkey exposes a native Prometheus endpoint (no /metrics in core; confirmed by the existence/necessity of the external exporter). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dragonfly serves Prometheus-format metrics natively at :6379/metrics (HTTP multiplexed on the RESP port), no external exporter; K8s admin port 9999 also exposes /metrics.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Quoted directly from Dragonfly monitoring docs.

verification.corrected_value: :6379/metrics built-in (HTTP multiplexed on RESP port); K8s admin :9999/metrics
verification.note: Dragonfly docs: 'Dragonfly allows HTTP access via its main TCP port (i.e, 6379) and it exposes Prometheus compatible metrics on :6379/metrics.' For Kubernetes, metrics are available via admin port 9999 (:9999/metrics). No external exporter needed. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dragonfly ships as a single self-contained binary (download, rename to 'dragonfly', run ./dragonfly --logtostderr) for aarch64 and x86_64, plus .deb; uses gflags-style --flag=value and --flagfile; Mac/Windows expect Docker. Min 4GB RAM, kernel >= 4.19.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from official binary install docs.

verification.corrected_value: single self-contained binary; x86_64 + aarch64 + .deb (arm64/amd64); gflags --flag=value and --flagfile; ./dragonfly --logtostderr; min 4GB RAM, kernel >= 4.19; Mac needs Docker, Windows WSL works
verification.note: Dragonfly binary docs confirm single executable for Aarch64 and X86-64 plus ARM64/AMD64 .deb packages, gflags-style --flag=value and --flagfile, run via ./dragonfly --logtostderr, minimum 4GB RAM and Linux kernel 4.19+, and Docker required on macOS (Windows WSL also works). All claim elements confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: KeyDB ships keydb-server/keydb-cli/keydb-sentinel/keydb-benchmark/keydb-check-aof/keydb-check-rdb and creates redis-cli + redis.conf symlinks to keydb-cli/keydb.conf for drop-in Redis compatibility.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in KeyDB getting-started docs via search synthesis; not line-verified in source.

verification.corrected_value: keydb-server/keydb-cli/keydb-sentinel/keydb-benchmark/keydb-check-aof/keydb-check-rdb; redis-cli + redis.conf symlinks to keydb-cli/keydb.conf
verification.note: KeyDB src/Makefile (unstable) confirms binary names keydb-server, keydb-cli, keydb-sentinel, keydb-benchmark, keydb-check-rdb, keydb-check-aof (plus internal ln -sf for sentinel/check-rdb/check-aof to keydb-server). The redis-cli + redis.conf -> keydb-cli/keydb.conf compatibility symlinks are documented by KeyDB ('KeyDB has included symbolic links for both redis-cli and redis.conf so they are linked to keydb-cli and keydb.conf'), confirmed via docs.keydb.dev compatibility/getting-started. Original confidence was 'medium'; now confirmed. Note these compat symlinks come from packaging/docs, not the src/Makefile install target.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey installs as a plain Homebrew formula (`brew install valkey`); Redis now requires `brew tap redis/redis` + `brew install --cask redis` and conflicts with valkey (cannot coexist).
prior_art_claims.value: valkey = plain formula; redis = tap+cask, conflicting
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Valkey formula page confirms plain formula; Redis tap/cask + conflict from docs/search, not double-checked on brew page.

verification.corrected_value: valkey = plain Homebrew formula (brew install valkey); redis = brew tap redis/redis + brew install --cask redis; Conflicts with: redis
verification.note: formulae.brew.sh/formula/valkey shows 'brew install valkey' and 'Conflicts with: redis'. redis.io Homebrew install docs confirm Redis OSS on macOS now uses 'brew tap redis/redis' then 'brew install --cask redis' (cask, not formula; not integrated with brew services). Both halves confirmed; original confidence 'medium' upgraded.

prior_art_claims.claim: The x86_64-unknown-linux-musl target enables crt-static by default and produces a fully static binary with no glibc runtime dependency, runnable on any same-arch Linux.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: RFC 1721 defines crt-static; musl default behavior corroborated by rust-lang issues and docs.

verification.corrected_value: crt-static defaults ON for x86_64-unknown-linux-musl (and most musl targets), but NOT all musl targets (e.g. arm-unknown-linux-musleabi defaults OFF)
verification.note: RFC 1721 is accurate that x86_64-unknown-linux-musl has crt-static on by default, producing a static binary. However the claim's blanket 'on by default for *-linux-musl' is an overgeneralization: the RFC explicitly states 'x86_64-unknown-linux-musl will have it on by default, whereas arm-unknown-linux-musleabi will have it turned off by default.' Corrected to scope the default to x86_64 (and most) musl targets, not every musl target. The core point (x86_64 musl => static, no glibc dep) stands.

prior_art_claims.claim: cargo-zigbuild v0.22.3 uses Zig as linker/C-toolchain; supports musl + gnu for x86_64/aarch64, per-target minimum glibc pinning (e.g. --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.2.17), and macOS universal2 builds.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Version from releases page; glibc-suffix + universal2 syntax read from README.

verification.corrected_value: v0.22.3 (2026-04-26); Zig as linker/C-toolchain; musl + gnu for x86_64/aarch64; glibc-version suffix (e.g. --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.2.17); universal2-apple-darwin
verification.note: GitHub releases/latest confirms v0.22.3 published 2026-04-26. README confirms zig as linker for cross-compiling, glibc version pinning via target suffix (aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.2.17), and universal2-apple-darwin support (Rust 1.64.0+). musl + gnu cross targets are core documented features. Note glibc-suffix pinning applies to gnu targets specifically (musl is statically linked), consistent with the claim. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: self_update v0.44.0 fetches release artifacts from GitHub/GitLab/S3/GCS/DO Spaces, semver-compares, and replaces the running binary via the self-replace crate (atomic rename); no built-in rollback.
prior_art_claims.value: v0.44.0; multi-backend; atomic replace; no rollback
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Version and backend list read from docs.rs; rollback absence consistent with docs.

verification.corrected_value: v0.44.0; GitHub/GitLab/S3/GCS/DO Spaces backends; semver-compare; atomic replace via self_replace; no built-in rollback
verification.note: crates.io API confirms max_version 0.44.0. docs.rs confirms multiple backends (GitHub, GitLab, and S3 backend covering Amazon S3, Google GCS, DigitalOcean Spaces, any S3-compatible server), semver version checks, atomic replacement via the self_replace crate, and no documented built-in rollback. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: self-replace places the new file next to the current executable and performs an atomic rename swap; on Unix you cannot write into a running executable but can swap it; Windows allows rename-but-not-unlink of a running exe.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Mechanism described on docs.rs page.

verification.corrected_value: places new file next to current exe and atomic rename swap; Unix can swap (unlink) but not write into running exe; Windows can rename-but-not-unlink a running exe (uses post-shutdown cleanup)
verification.note: docs.rs self_replace confirms: for replacing, a new file is placed right next to the current executable and an atomic move with rename is performed; Unix allows unlink/swap of running file; Windows running exe can be renamed but not deleted, requiring a spawned helper for post-shutdown cleanup. Platform-specific behavior confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: cargo-auditable v0.7.5 embeds a dependency manifest into the binary with no timestamps and sorted JSON so output is byte-identical across builds (reproducible-build safe); auditable2cdx converts it to CycloneDX.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Version from gh api releases/latest; reproducibility properties from project README/docs.

verification.corrected_value: v0.7.5 (2026-05-21); embeds JSON dep tree in a linker section; no timestamps + sorted JSON => reproducible-build safe; auditable2cdx -> CycloneDX
verification.note: GitHub releases/latest confirms v0.7.5 published 2026-05-21. README: 'The data format is specifically designed not to disrupt reproducible builds. It contains no timestamps, and the generated JSON is sorted to make sure it is identical between compilations.' auditable2cdx converts the embedded data to CycloneDX. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: cargo-dist automates cross-built release archives with SHA256 checksums and generates a one-line `curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 -LsSf <url>/installer.sh | sh` installer that manages PATH (APPNAME_NO_MODIFY_PATH) and embeds checksums to validate the tarball before unpacking.
prior_art_claims.value: ~v0.31.0; curl|sh installer with embedded checksums + PATH mgmt
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Active maintenance and installer behavior confirmed via search; exact current version (0.30.3 vs 0.31.0) not pinned to a single release page.

verification.corrected_value: latest is dist v0.32.0 (2026-05-22), not ~0.31.0/2025 (project rebranded cargo-dist -> 'dist'); shell installer is `curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 -LsSf <url> | sh` with embedded SHA256 checksums validated before unpacking and PATH mgmt via *_NO_MODIFY_PATH
verification.note: Functional claims confirmed: cargo-dist generates a shell (curl|sh) installer with the --proto =https --tlsv1.2 -LsSf flags, embeds checksum info into the installer and validates the tarball before unpacking, and supports PATH management with a NO_MODIFY_PATH env var. Version is off: GitHub releases/latest is v0.32.0 published 2026-05-22 (273 releases, actively maintained, now branded 'dist'), so the claimed '~v0.30.3-0.31.0 (2025)' is stale/low. Corrected the version; original 'medium' confidence justified.

prior_art_claims.claim: gcr.io/distroless/static-debian13 is ~2 MiB (vs alpine ~5 MiB, debian ~124 MiB) and bundles CA certs + a nonroot user, making it the practical minimal base for a fully static Rust binary; scratch is the absolute-minimum alternative.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Sizes from distroless docs/community write-ups via search; not byte-measured here.

verification.corrected_value: gcr.io/distroless/static-debian13 ~2 MiB (vs alpine ~5 MiB, debian ~124 MiB); bundles ca-certificates, tzdata, /etc/passwd (root + nonroot user via :nonroot tag), /tmp
verification.note: distroless README confirms static-debian13 is ~2 MiB, ~50% of Alpine (~5 MiB) and <2% of Debian (124 MiB), and is the minimal base for static binaries (scratch being absolute-minimum). The static image contains ca-certificates, tzdata, /etc/passwd and /tmp; a nonroot user/group is provided (selectable via the :nonroot tag). All claim elements confirmed; original 'medium' confidence upgraded.

mechanisms.name: Single static musl binary (crt-static default)
mechanisms.what_it_does: x86_64-unknown-linux-musl (and aarch64) produce a fully static binary with zero glibc/runtime dependency; Dragonfly proves the download-rename-run UX in this space.
mechanisms.rationale: This is IronCache's core thesis (single static binary, easy setup). Rust gives it for free on musl targets; nothing simpler exists for the operator.

mechanisms.name: Binary-is-both-server-and-CLI via argv[0] symlinks
mechanisms.what_it_does: One compiled binary behaves as server, sentinel, or checker depending on the name it's invoked as (ln -sf to redis-server).
mechanisms.rationale: Keep the single-binary economy, but use explicit clap SUBCOMMANDS (ironcache server | cli | bench | check | upgrade | config) instead of argv[0] magic — clearer UX, --help discoverability, and avoids fragile symlink installs. Still ship `ironcache-cli`-style symlink/alias for redis-cli muscle memory.

mechanisms.name: redis-cli RESP client interop
mechanisms.what_it_does: A small RESP client (redis-cli) and redis.conf symlinks ship alongside so existing tooling works unchanged.
mechanisms.rationale: Wire compatibility is a hard requirement; existing redis-cli and clients must Just Work against IronCache. Provide our own `ironcache cli` but never break stock redis-cli.

mechanisms.name: CONFIG SET / GET / REWRITE live config
mechanisms.what_it_does: Mutate config at runtime (multi-param since 7.0), enumerate with GET *, persist back to file with REWRITE; no SIGHUP reload.
mechanisms.rationale: Implement CONFIG GET/SET/REWRITE for wire parity, but ADD a real reload path (SIGHUP + CONFIG RELOAD) so a TOML file edit can be applied without a client — closing a long-standing Redis gap.

mechanisms.name: INFO sectioned introspection
mechanisms.what_it_does: RESP INFO returns server/clients/memory/persistence/stats/cpu/commandstats/latencystats/keyspace etc.; 'default' subset when no arg.
mechanisms.rationale: INFO is the universal Redis observability contract every dashboard/exporter parses; IronCache must emit byte-compatible sections to drop into existing redis_exporter + Grafana setups.

mechanisms.name: Native Prometheus /metrics on the data port
mechanisms.what_it_does: Serves Prometheus text-format metrics at :6379/metrics (HTTP multiplexed on RESP) with no external exporter; admin port 9999 in K8s.
mechanisms.rationale: Eliminates the redis_exporter sidecar that Redis/Valkey force on operators. A self-contained binary with self-contained metrics matches the easiest-setup goal exactly.

mechanisms.name: SLOWLOG + LATENCY monitoring
mechanisms.what_it_does: SLOWLOG (default 10ms/128 entries) records slow commands; LATENCY LATEST/HISTORY/RESET/DOCTOR tracks latency spikes (threshold default 0 = off).
mechanisms.rationale: Ship SLOWLOG + LATENCY for parity, but enable latency monitoring with a sane non-zero default and surface percentiles in /metrics — Redis ships it OFF, leaving users blind by default.

mechanisms.name: cargo-zigbuild cross-compilation
mechanisms.what_it_does: Uses Zig as linker to cross-build musl + gnu for x86_64/aarch64 from one host, pin minimum glibc per target, and build macOS universal2.
mechanisms.rationale: Simplest reliable matrix build for x86_64 + aarch64 static binaries from CI without a Docker-per-target setup; glibc-pinning gives a portable gnu fallback if musl ever bites.

mechanisms.name: self_update + self-replace (atomic swap)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Pull a release artifact (GitHub/S3/GCS), semver-compare, and atomically rename the new binary over the running one.
mechanisms.rationale: Adopt for `ironcache upgrade`, but the crates have NO rollback — IronCache must add: keep previous binary as ironcache.old, post-swap health/version check, automatic rollback on failure, and signature verification.

mechanisms.name: cargo-auditable + cargo-cyclonedx (SBOM/supply-chain)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Embeds a timestamp-free, sorted dependency manifest in the binary (reproducible-build safe) and emits CycloneDX SBOMs.
mechanisms.rationale: Reproducible builds + embedded SBOM are table stakes for a serious infra binary in 2026 and cost almost nothing to wire into CI; Cargo.lock already pins inputs.

mechanisms.name: cargo-dist curl|sh installer
mechanisms.what_it_does: Generates cross-built archives + checksums and a one-line curl|sh installer that validates checksums and manages PATH.
mechanisms.rationale: Use the pattern (one-line installer + GitHub Releases) but treat the curl|sh script as one of several install paths; pin/own the installer rather than depend on a third party for a security-sensitive bootstrap.

mechanisms.name: Distroless/scratch container image
mechanisms.what_it_does: Minimal base (static-debian13 ~2 MiB with CA certs + nonroot) for shipping a static binary as a tiny, low-CVE-surface image.
mechanisms.rationale: A static Rust binary on distroless static (or scratch) yields a sub-5 MiB image with almost no OS attack surface — directly serves minimal-footprint and easy-deploy goals.

mechanisms.name: gflags --flagfile style config
mechanisms.what_it_does: All config is --flag=value, optionally loaded from a --flagfile with one flag per line.
mechanisms.rationale: gflags is an Abseil-ism with no typing, no comments structure, and no nesting. IronCache should use TOML (idiomatic Rust, typed, commentable, nestable) as the file format, with flags overriding TOML overriding defaults.

mechanisms.name: Redis runs with no config file by default
mechanisms.what_it_does: Starts with built-in dev defaults and daemonize no, no config file needed.
mechanisms.rationale: Zero-config first-run is the easiest-setup ideal; IronCache should boot on `ironcache server` with safe defaults and require config only to deviate.

ironcache_implications: Ship ONE static musl binary per arch (x86_64 + aarch64) that is server + CLI + bench + check + upgrade via clap subcommands; default `ironcache server` boots with zero config and safe defaults (bind localhost, protected mode, sane maxmemory policy).
Provide redis-cli wire interop: stock redis-cli and existing clients must connect unmodified; ship `ironcache cli` as the native client and optionally a redis-cli-named alias for muscle memory.

Config precedence: built-in defaults < TOML file < CLI flags < runtime CONFIG SET. Implement CONFIG GET/SET/REWRITE for Redis parity but ADD SIGHUP + `CONFIG RELOAD` to apply TOML edits without a client (the gap Redis never closed).

Native Prometheus /metrics served on the data port (like Dragonfly's :6379/metrics) plus an optional separate admin port, so there is NO redis_exporter sidecar; also emit Redis-compatible INFO sections so existing redis_exporter + Grafana dashboards still work as a fallback.

Observability defaults flipped to ON: ship SLOWLOG (10ms/128 parity defaults) and LATENCY monitoring enabled with a sensible non-zero threshold, and surface command latency percentiles directly in /metrics — don't make users discover they were flying blind.

Build pipeline: cargo-zigbuild in CI for the x86_64/aarch64 musl matrix (with a glibc-pinned gnu fallback target), reproducible builds via pinned Cargo.lock, cargo-auditable embedding the SBOM, and cargo-cyclonedx emitting a CycloneDX artifact attached to each release.

`ironcache upgrade`: use self_update/self-replace for the atomic rename swap but BUILD the missing rollback — verify minisign/cosign signature of the artifact, keep the prior binary as ironcache.old, run a post-swap `--version`+health probe, auto-rollback on failure, and never auto-update a running server without an explicit flag.

Distribution surface aiming to beat Redis on ease: (1) one-line `curl … | sh` installer (own/pin it), (2) `brew install ironcache` as a PLAIN formula — no tap, no cask, no conflicting-formula, which is strictly easier than today's Redis on macOS, (3) distroless-static / scratch Docker image under 5 MiB, (4) a hardened systemd unit (NoNewPrivileges, ProtectSystem, dynamic user).
Adopt TOML (not gflags --flagfile) as the config file format for typed, commentable, nestable config that round-trips cleanly with CONFIG GET/SET/REWRITE.

research_papers.title: Reproducible Builds: Increasing the Integrity of Software Supply Chains
research_papers.key_idea: Bit-for-bit reproducible builds let independent parties verify that a binary corresponds to its source, defending against build-time tampering.
research_papers.relevance: Directly motivates IronCache's reproducible static-binary + embedded-SBOM strategy (Cargo.lock + cargo-auditable) so the single binary users download is verifiable.

research_papers.title: in-toto: Providing farm-to-table guarantees for bits and bytes
research_papers.key_idea: A framework that cryptographically attests every step of a software supply chain from source to packaged artifact.
research_papers.relevance: Informs how IronCache should sign and attest its release artifacts and the curl|sh installer, beyond just a checksum.

research_papers.title: The Update Framework (TUF): Survivable Key Compromise in Software Update Systems
research_papers.key_idea: A signing/role/threshold model for software update systems that remains secure even when individual keys are compromised.
research_papers.relevance: Design reference for a hardened `ironcache upgrade` self-update channel (signature verification, key rotation, rollback protection) since self_update/self-replace provide none of this.

open_questions: Can a single binary HTTP-multiplex Prometheus /metrics on the RESP port (Dragonfly does) without hurting hot-path throughput, or should /metrics live on a dedicated admin port by default?
TOML vs redis.conf-flat-format for the on-disk config: how do we round-trip CONFIG REWRITE into TOML while preserving comments and ordering?
What is the rollback contract for `ironcache upgrade` — keep N previous binaries? where (alongside binary vs a state dir)? what health probe constitutes 'new binary is good'?

musl allocator penalty: musl's malloc is notably slower under multithread contention; do we statically link a better allocator (mimalloc/jemalloc) into the musl build, or ship a glibc-pinned gnu build as the performance default and musl as the portable default?
Signing: minisign vs sigstore/cosign for release artifacts and the self-update channel — which gives the best verify-on-a-fresh-box UX with no extra tooling?
How do we keep INFO byte-compatible enough for stock redis_exporter while also exposing IronCache-specific fields without breaking parsers?
Windows story: Redis/Dragonfly punt to WSL/Docker on Windows — does IronCache ship a native Windows binary (self-replace supports it) or also punt to containers for M2?

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: Single static binary that is both server and CLI
proposed_issues.summary: Track the operability thesis: one reproducible static musl binary per arch exposing server/cli/bench/check/upgrade subcommands, zero-config first run, and easiest-in-class install. Umbrella for the issues below.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: clap subcommands vs argv[0] symlink mode-switching
proposed_issues.summary: Decide between Redis-style argv[0] symlink dispatch and explicit clap subcommands (ironcache server|cli|bench|check|upgrade|config). Recommendation: clap subcommands + an optional redis-cli-named alias for interop.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Native Prometheus /metrics endpoint (no exporter sidecar)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a built-in Prometheus text-format endpoint à la Dragonfly (:port/metrics on the data port and/or an admin port), the metric set, and how it coexists with Redis-compatible INFO so existing redis_exporter dashboards still work.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: INFO / SLOWLOG / LATENCY wire-parity with sane defaults
proposed_issues.summary: Define byte-compatible INFO sections, SLOWLOG (10ms/128 parity), and LATENCY commands, but flip latency monitoring ON by default (Redis defaults it to 0/off) and feed percentiles into /metrics.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: TOML config with CONFIG GET/SET/REWRITE parity + live reload
proposed_issues.summary: Specify defaults<TOML<flags<CONFIG SET precedence, CONFIG GET/SET/REWRITE wire parity, REWRITE round-tripping into TOML, and a SIGHUP/CONFIG RELOAD path that Redis lacks. Reject gflags --flagfile.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: ironcache upgrade — atomic swap with verified rollback
proposed_issues.summary: Build on self_update/self-replace (atomic rename) but add the missing pieces: signature verification, keep ironcache.old, post-swap health/version probe, and automatic rollback on failure; never auto-update a live server implicitly.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: CI cross-build matrix via cargo-zigbuild (musl x86_64+aarch64, glibc-pinned gnu fallback)
proposed_issues.summary: Stand up cargo-zigbuild (v0.22.x) in CI to produce static musl binaries for x86_64 and aarch64 plus a glibc-version-pinned gnu fallback, with SHA256 checksums attached to releases.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Reproducible builds + embedded SBOM (cargo-auditable, cargo-cyclonedx)
proposed_issues.summary: Pin Cargo.lock, build reproducibly, embed a timestamp-free SBOM with cargo-auditable (v0.7.x), and publish a CycloneDX SBOM artifact per release.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: musl allocator penalty vs glibc — which to ship as default
proposed_issues.summary: Benchmark musl's malloc vs statically-linked mimalloc/jemalloc under multithread contention to decide whether musl is the performance default or a portable fallback to a glibc-pinned build.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Artifact + self-update signing scheme (minisign vs cosign/sigstore)
proposed_issues.summary: Choose the signing/verification mechanism for release artifacts and the upgrade channel that gives the best verify-on-fresh-box UX with minimal extra tooling; reference TUF/in-toto for key compromise resilience.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Packaging — curl|sh installer, plain Homebrew formula, distroless image, systemd unit
proposed_issues.summary: Ship a one-line curl|sh installer (checksum-validated, PATH-managing), a PLAIN brew formula (no tap/cask/conflict — beating Redis on macOS), a sub-5MiB distroless-static/scratch image, and a hardened systemd unit.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: native Windows server binary for M2
proposed_issues.summary: Like Redis/Dragonfly, defer native Windows server support to Docker/WSL for the prototype; ship Linux (musl) + macOS first. Revisit post-M2.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Re-verified all 17 claims against fresh primary sources (GitHub source/releases, crates.io API, vendor docs). Methodology: read code defaults directly from src (Redis/KeyDB Makefile and config.c on unstable via gh api; cross-checked with redis.io docs), used gh releases/latest and crates.io API for version pins, and WebFetch/WebSearch for vendor docs plus an independent second source per claim.

15 confirmed, 2 corrected, 0 refuted, 0 uncertain.

Corrections:
1. [rust-musl-crt-static-default] -> corrected: RFC 1721 confirms x86_64-unknown-linux-musl defaults crt-static ON, but explicitly notes NOT all musl targets do (arm-unknown-linux-musleabi defaults OFF). The blanket '*-linux-musl' is an overgeneralization, though the practical conclusion (x86_64 musl => fully static, no glibc dep) holds.
2. [cargo-dist-installer-curl-sh] -> corrected: functional details (curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 -LsSf | sh installer, embedded SHA256 checksums validated before unpack, *_NO_MODIFY_PATH PATH mgmt) all confirmed, but the version is stale. Actual GitHub latest is dist v0.32.0 (2026-05-22), not ~0.30.3-0.31.0/2025; project rebranded cargo-dist -> 'dist'.

Strongest source-of-truth confirmations (read directly from code, not just docs):
- Redis: src/Makefile shows 6 build targets + exactly 3 `@ln -sf` symlinks at install (sentinel/check-rdb/check-aof); server.c branches on exec_name via strstr() = argv[0] dispatch. config.c gives slowlog-max-len=128, slowlog-log-slower-than=10000, latency-monitor-threshold=0.
- KeyDB: src/Makefile confirms keydb-* binary names; redis-cli/redis.conf compat symlinks confirmed from KeyDB docs (these are packaging/docs-level, not in the src/Makefile install target).

Version pins all verified current as of 2026-06-13: self_update 0.44.0 (crates.io), cargo-auditable v0.7.5 (2026-05-21), cargo-zigbuild v0.22.3 (2026-04-26). Two 'medium'-confidence claims (keydb symlinks, valkey/redis brew) were upgraded to confirmed; distroless static also fully confirmed (size + ca-certs/nonroot bundling).

One minor nuance worth flagging for the record (not a correction): in both Redis and KeyDB the sentinel/check-rdb/check-aof functionality is compiled INTO redis-server/keydb-server (sentinel.o, redis-check-rdb.o, redis-check-aof.o are part of REDIS_SERVER_OBJ); the separate 'binaries' are symlinks, so 'builds 6 separate binaries' is true at the link/install level but only 3 distinct executables exist on disk (server, cli, benchmark) plus 3 symlinks to server.

dimensions.summary:
The persistence design space for a cache splits into four points, in increasing cost: ephemeral-only (no disk, fastest, lose everything on restart — Memcached's historical default), snapshot (periodic point-in-time dump — Redis RDB), append-log (every write logged — Redis AOF), and tiered RAM->SSD (larger-than-RAM, hot in memory, cold on flash — FASTER/F2, KeyDB FLASH, Memcached extstore). IronCache's stated goal (max throughput per core, minimal memory, single binary) argues for a default of ephemeral-or-snapshot with an optional tiered backend, and a hard rejection of fork()-based copy-on-write snapshotting because it is the single biggest memory-efficiency footgun in the incumbent (Redis) design.

The most important mechanism to borrow is Dragonfly's fork-less, versioned point-in-time snapshot. Instead of fork()+COW (which can double RSS under write-heavy load, the exact behavior Redis exhibits and KeyDB budgets ~30% headroom for), Dragonfly maintains a monotonic version counter; each entry records its last-update version; snapshot start captures an epoch cut; a traversal fiber serializes all entries with version <= cut, and a mutation hook (the "conservative" variation, used for file backups) pushes the pre-image of any entry to the serialization sink before an in-flight write modifies it. Memory overhead is constant regardless of dataset size, and the push-on-write creates natural back-pressure. This is the single best-fit idea for a most-efficient-cache: it gives a consistent snapshot with no fork, no RSS spike, and integrates with io_uring async writes. The dependency is a sharded, single-writer-per-shard architecture (shared-nothing) so each shard serializes only its own data through a blocking channel — an architectural commitment IronCache should make regardless.

For larger-than-RAM, the two viable engine families are the FASTER/F2 hybrid log and an LSM (RocksDB). FASTER's HybridLog is a single logical address space split into a mutable region (in-place updates, no allocation), a read-only region (immutable, RCU to the tail on update), and a stable/disk region; epoch protection provides latch-free memory reclamation and lazy cross-thread synchronization. F2 evolves this into an explicit hot-log (memory) + cold-log (disk) two-tier design with a read cache, reporting up to 3.2x higher throughput than RocksDB on skewed workloads (original FASTER hit ~160M ops/sec in-memory on YCSB). The hybrid-log/epoch approach is a strong borrow because it is latch-free and Rust-implementable, and it avoids the LSM write-amplification tax. RocksDB-as-cold-backend (KeyDB FLASH, Memcached extstore both effectively do this) is rejected as a core engine: leveled compaction write amplification is "often larger than 10" (Facebook prod uses a size multiplier of 10), which is poison for SSD lifetime on a high-churn cache workload, and it imports a large C++ dependency that breaks the single-static-binary promise.

Memcached extstore is the cleanest minimalist tiering design and worth borrowing selectively: keep keys + a 12-byte [page, offset, version] pointer in RAM, write values to flash, with tunables ext_item_size (default 512B), ext_page_size (default 64M), ext_wbuf_size (default 8M, 32M reserved total), and compaction governed by ext_max_frag / ext_compact_under (default 1/4 of pages) / ext_drop_unread. The 12-bytes-per-resident-value model is the key efficiency insight: you can address far more flash than RAM with tiny per-item overhead. Notably extstore is incompatible with warm restart in Memcached, a constraint IronCache should design around rather than inherit.

Warm restart (Memcached 1.5.18+) is a high-value, low-cost feature to adapt: put item data in an mmap file on a tmpfs/DAX mount via -e, send SIGUSR1 for graceful shutdown, write a .meta state file, and on restart fix up internal pointers and regenerate the hash table — preserving cache contents across binary upgrades with zero disk-format serialization. For a single-binary cache that will be redeployed often, mmap warm-restart is more valuable than full durability and far cheaper than snapshotting. The constraints (clock must not jump; writes during the restart window are lost; incompatible with the external-storage path) are acceptable for a cache.

io_uring is the clear I/O substrate for any snapshot or tiering writes: registered/fixed buffers plus SQPOLL can remove the submit syscall from the hot path entirely, with reported throughput gains (e.g., 238k -> 546k tx/s with SQPOLL in one benchmark) and 20-40% p99 improvements over epoll for batched I/O. In Rust this is reachable via tokio-uring or glommio's thread-per-core model, which aligns with a shared-nothing shard design. The synthesis for IronCache: default ephemeral; opt-in fork-less versioned snapshot (Dragonfly-style) written via io_uring; opt-in mmap warm-restart for fast upgrades; and a longer-term FASTER-style hybrid-log tiered backend rather than an LSM, to honor the throughput-per-core and SSD-friendliness goals. Append-log (AOF-style) durability is a possible later add for users who need bounded data loss (everysec = <=1s loss window), but it is not the efficiency-optimal default.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis default RDB save points trigger a snapshot after 1h/1 change, 5min/100 changes, or 60s/10000 changes.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from official Redis persistence docs which quote the redis.conf defaults verbatim.

verification.note: redis.conf 7.4 ships the single line 'save 3600 1 300 100 60 10000' with comments stating: after 3600s/1 change, 300s/100 changes, 60s/10000 changes. Matches the claim exactly. The docs/latest persistence page only gives an example (save 60 1000), so the source-of-truth is redis.conf, not the cited docs page.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis default AOF fsync policy is everysec, bounding data loss to ~1 second; fsync runs on a background thread.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated explicitly in official docs.

verification.corrected_value: appendfsync everysec (<=1s loss window)
verification.note: Docs (latest, fetched 2026-06-13) state everysec is 'the suggested (and default) policy', that 'fsync is performed using a background thread', and 'you can only lose one second worth of writes'. redis.conf 7.4 also has 'appendfsync everysec'. Fully confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis RDB/AOF-rewrite forks a child; copy-on-write can roughly double RSS under heavy write load because modified pages are duplicated.
prior_art_claims.value: up to ~2x RSS spike under write-heavy load
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Doubling is the worst-case bound; actual spike depends on write rate and page touch fraction. Behavior is well documented but the exact multiplier is workload-dependent.

verification.corrected_value: up to ~2x RSS spike under write-heavy load
verification.note: The cited Redis docs page mentions copy-on-write but does NOT state ~2x. Independent sources confirm: under a write-heavy BGSAVE the worst case (parent modifies every page) can temporarily double memory; THP raises COW granularity to 2MB worsening the spike. Claim is technically sound; medium confidence appropriate. Better source than the docs page given here.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis triggers automatic AOF rewrite at 100% growth over last rewrite with a minimum size floor.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Values match documented defaults but were summarized by the fetch model rather than read from redis.conf line-by-line.

verification.corrected_value: auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100; auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb (67108864 bytes)
verification.note: redis.conf 7.4 has 'auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100' and 'auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb'. 64MB = 67108864 bytes is correct. The cited docs/latest page does not list these defaults (only points to the example config), so redis.conf is the better source.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dragonfly snapshots without fork: a monotonic version counter, per-entry last-update version, an epoch cut (SnapshotShard.epoch = shard.epoch++), a traversal fiber serializing entries with version<=cut, and an OnWrite mutation hook that pushes the pre-image to the sink before a concurrent write.
prior_art_claims.value: versioned point-in-time; serialize if entry.version<=cut.epoch then set entry.version=cut.epoch+1
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from the upstream rdbsave.md design doc plus corroborating author blog.

verification.corrected_value: versioned point-in-time; serialize if entry.version<=cut.epoch then set entry.version=cut.epoch+1
verification.note: rdbsave.md (main) shows SnapshotShard.epoch = shard.epoch++; the traversal loop 'if (entry.version <= cut.epoch) { entry.version = cut.epoch + 1; SendToSerializationSink(entry); }'; and the OnWrite hook that pushes the pre-image to the sink before a concurrent write when entry.version <= cut.version. All elements of the claim match the doc verbatim.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dragonfly's snapshot memory overhead is constant regardless of dataset size and avoids the COW RSS spike, using push-on-write back-pressure instead of fork().
prior_art_claims.value: constant memory overhead during snapshot (no fork RSS doubling)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated explicitly by the system's author in the design blog.

verification.corrected_value: constant memory overhead during snapshot (no fork RSS doubling)
verification.note: Gershman's 2022-10-30 post states 'Dragonfly's memory overhead is constant during the snapshotting no matter how big the dataset size is' and that by avoiding fork() and pushing entries to the serialization sink during the update it 'establishes a natural back-pressure mechanism'. Fully supports the claim.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dragonfly has two snapshot variations: conservative (file backups, pushes previous value before modifying to keep point-in-time semantics) and relaxed (replication, sends new values with differential ops like hset/append).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Both variations described in upstream design doc.

prior_art_claims.claim: FASTER's HybridLog is one logical address space split into mutable (in-place updates), read-only (immutable, RCU to tail on update), and stable/on-disk regions; mutable+read-only are in RAM, stable on disk.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Core architecture of the SIGMOD paper; corroborated by multiple secondary sources.

verification.corrected_value: 3 regions: mutable | read-only | stable(disk)
verification.note: FASTER SIGMOD 2018 HybridLog splits one logical address space into mutable (in-place updates), read-only (immutable, RCU to tail on update), and stable (on-disk) regions; mutable+read-only reside in RAM, stable on disk. Confirmed via the paper. WebFetch PDF extraction was paraphrased, but the region structure is well-established and matches the paper's HybridLog section.

prior_art_claims.claim: FASTER uses an epoch-protection framework (global epoch counter, threads register an epoch, trigger actions/drain list) for latch-free, lazy cross-thread synchronization and safe memory reclamation, avoiding fine-grained latches on address updates.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Central mechanism of the paper.

verification.corrected_value: epoch-based protection (latch-free)
verification.note: FASTER uses an epoch-protection framework: global epoch counter, threads register/refresh their epoch, trigger actions executed when all threads pass an epoch (drain list), enabling latch-free lazy synchronization for safe memory reclamation and address-space transitions. Matches the paper's Epoch Protection Framework section.

prior_art_claims.claim: FASTER reported up to ~160 million operations/sec on YCSB-style in-memory workloads.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Widely cited headline figure; exact configuration/threads matter and were not re-read from the results table directly.

verification.corrected_value: ~160M ops/sec (YCSB, in-memory)
verification.note: Confirmed by independent sources: Microsoft Research project page and the paper state FASTER achieves up to 160 million operations per second on a single machine for in-memory YCSB-style workloads. Number is correct.

prior_art_claims.claim: F2 is a two-tier record-oriented store: a hot-log in memory and a cold-log on disk, with a read cache; records migrate hot->cold as they age, targeting large skewed workloads.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Abstract and intro of the F2 paper state the two-tier design and read cache.

verification.corrected_value: hot-log (RAM, with hash index) + cold-log (disk) + read cache
verification.note: Confirmed: F2 keeps write-hot keys in a hot log (in memory, alongside its hash index), a separate cold log for write-cold keys on disk, and a read cache between the hot-log index and hot log for disk-resident read-hot records; it targets larger-than-memory skewed workloads. Records migrate hot->cold via log compaction. Matches the paper.

prior_art_claims.claim: F2 achieves 2-11.9x better throughput than existing KV stores, and up to ~3.2x higher throughput than RocksDB on skewed workloads.
prior_art_claims.value: 2-11.9x vs KV stores; up to ~3.2x vs RocksDB
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 2-11.9x is from the abstract (high confidence); the specific ~3.2x-vs-RocksDB figure was extracted by the fetch model from the PDF body and not re-verified against the results table.

verification.corrected_value: 2-11.9x vs KV stores overall; ~11.8x vs RocksDB on average (NOT ~3.2x)
verification.note: The '2-11.9x better throughput compared to existing KV stores' headline is correct (KVell is the 11.9x max). But the RocksDB-specific figure is WRONG: the paper reports F2 outperforms FASTER 2.1x, LeanStore 2x, KVell 11.9x, SplinterDB 4.6x, and RocksDB ~11.8x on average. '~3.2x vs RocksDB' is not supported; the correct RocksDB multiple is ~11.8x. Note: 2305.01516 is the same work published at VLDB 2025 (vol18 p4910) titled 'From FASTER to F2', not 'VLDB 2024'.

prior_art_claims.claim: extstore keeps the hash table and keys in RAM and moves values to flash, storing a 12-byte [page, offset, version] location pointer in the in-memory item header.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from official Memcached flash storage docs.

verification.corrected_value: 12 bytes per flash-resident value (page,offset,version)
verification.note: Docs confirm extstore 'leaves the hash table and keys in memory, but moves values to external storage' and stores 'small (12 byte as of this writing) headers detailing where data lives in flash' = [PAGE ID, OFFSET IN PAGE, VERSION NUMBER]. Matches the claim.

prior_art_claims.claim: extstore defaults: ext_item_size=512B (min value size to flush), ext_page_size=64M, ext_wbuf_size=8M with 32M reserved for write buffers total; compaction governed by ext_max_frag, ext_compact_under (default 1/4 of pages), ext_drop_unread, ext_drop_under.
prior_art_claims.value: ext_item_size=512; ext_page_size=64M; ext_wbuf_size=8M; 32M reserved; ext_compact_under=1/4 pages
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: All values read from the official flash storage docs page.

verification.corrected_value: ext_item_size=512; ext_page_size=64M; ext_wbuf_size=8M; ~32M reserved for write buffers; ext_compact_under=1/4 of pages; ext_drop_under defaults to ext_compact_under
verification.note: Docs confirm ext_item_size defaults to 512 bytes, ext_page_size 64M, ext_wbuf_size 8M with 32MB reserved for write buffers by default, ext_compact_under defaults to 1/4th of pages, ext_drop_under defaults to the same value. ext_max_frag has no default stated in docs (docs say check --help / 'stats settings' for current values). Core defaults all confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Warm restart puts item data in an mmap file (-e /tmpfs_mount/memory_file on a RAM disk / DAX mount), shuts down on SIGUSR1, writes a .meta state file, and on restart fixes internal pointers and regenerates the hash table; earliest restartable version is 1.5.18.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from official warm restart docs.

verification.corrected_value: -e mmap file (on tmpfs/ramdisk or DAX) + SIGUSR1 + .meta; min version 1.5.18
verification.note: Docs confirm: '-e /tmpfs_mount/memory_file' on a ram disk, send SIGUSR1 to gracefully shut down, it creates 'memory_file.meta' on shutdown, and on restart 'fixes up the internal pointers and regenerates the hash table'. 'The earliest version you can restart from is 1.5.18.' All elements confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Memcached warm restart is presently incompatible with extstore, and writes (set/add/delete/incr) during the restart window are missed; system clock must not jump while down.
prior_art_claims.value: warm-restart XOR extstore; writes lost during restart window
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Constraints stated explicitly in official docs.

prior_art_claims.claim: KeyDB FLASH recommends maxmemory at 50% of RAM for data (up to 70% if equal-size swap is configured); RAM usage can rise up to ~30% above maxmemory during BGSAVE under heavy writes; RDB backup volumes should be >=2.5x the FLASH volume.
prior_art_claims.value: 50% (up to 70%) of RAM to data; +30% during BGSAVE; backup vol >=2.5x flash
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from official KeyDB FLASH sizing docs.

prior_art_claims.claim: KeyDB FLASH is built on RocksDB, serving hot data from RAM and cold data from flash with LRU/LFU eviction paging data in/out.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in official KeyDB FLASH docs.

verification.corrected_value: RocksDB-backed tiered RAM/flash
verification.note: Docs state 'KeyDB FLASH is built on RocksDB and is persistent', with all data written to flash and hot data remaining in memory; when memory is exceeded, data is evicted from RAM but stays on flash and accessible. Confirms RocksDB-backed tiered RAM/flash. (Docs describe LRU-style eviction; explicit LFU wording is configuration-dependent but the tiered behavior is confirmed.)

prior_art_claims.claim: RocksDB leveled compaction write amplification is often larger than 10x; Facebook production typically uses a level size multiplier (fanout) of 10 (a few use 8).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from the official RocksDB wiki Leveled Compaction page.

verification.corrected_value: write amplification often >10x; default size multiplier 10
verification.note: Leveled-Compaction wiki states leveled compaction write amplification 'is often larger than 10' and uses max_bytes_for_level_multiplier=10 as the default/example. The RocksDB Tuning Guide gives a worked example reaching ~33x write amp and shows configs using both 8 and 10. The 'Facebook production typically uses 10 (a few use 8)' phrasing is plausible and partially supported (examples use 8) but is not verbatim on the cited wiki page; the load-bearing numbers (>10x, multiplier 10) are confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: With SQPOLL and registered/fixed buffers, io_uring removes the submit syscall from the hot path; reported gains include ~238k->546.5k tx/s and 20-40% p99 improvement vs epoll for batched I/O.
prior_art_claims.value: submit syscall eliminated; ~2.3x tx/s in one benchmark; 20-40% p99 vs epoll
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Mechanism (SQPOLL/registered buffers) is from the man page (high); the specific throughput/p99 numbers are from a secondary benchmark blog and are workload-specific.

verification.corrected_value: submit syscall eliminated; registered buffers ~238k tx/s (+11%), SQPOLL ~546k tx/s (+32%); 20-40% p99 vs epoll
verification.note: SQPOLL eliminating the io_uring_enter() submit syscall from the hot path is documented in the io_uring man pages. The specific benchmark numbers (registered buffers ~238k tx/s, +11%; SQPOLL ~546k tx/s, +32% on YCSB) come from arXiv 2512.04859 ('io_uring for High-Performance DBMSs'), NOT the man7 page cited in the claim. Numbers are corroborated; cite the DBMS paper for the tx/s figures. The ~2.3x and 20-40% p99 framing is in the right ballpark (532k/238k ~= 2.3x). Medium confidence appropriate.

mechanisms.name: Fork-less versioned point-in-time snapshot
mechanisms.what_it_does: Monotonic version counter + per-entry last-update version + epoch cut; a traversal fiber serializes all entries <= cut, and an OnWrite hook pushes the pre-image of an entry to the serialization sink before a concurrent write mutates it. Memory overhead is constant, no fork, no COW RSS spike, with natural push-on-write back-pressure.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the single best mechanism for a most-efficient cache: a consistent snapshot with zero fork and no memory doubling. Directly serves IronCache's minimal-memory and throughput-per-core goals and pairs with io_uring writes. Requires committing to a shared-nothing per-shard single-writer architecture (which IronCache should do anyway).

mechanisms.name: fork()+copy-on-write snapshot (BGSAVE)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Forks a child that writes the dataset to disk while the parent serves traffic; modified pages are COW-duplicated.
mechanisms.rationale: COW can roughly double RSS under write-heavy load (the exact reason KeyDB budgets +30% headroom). This is the biggest memory-efficiency footgun in Redis and is antithetical to IronCache's minimal-memory goal. Replace with Dragonfly-style versioned snapshot.

mechanisms.name: HybridLog (mutable / read-only / stable regions)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Single logical address space partitioned into a mutable region (in-place updates, no allocation), a read-only region (immutable, RCU appends to the tail on update), and a stable on-disk region; recency determines region.
mechanisms.rationale: In-place updates in the mutable region give allocation-free hot writes (great for throughput-per-core), and the unified log naturally spills cold data to disk for larger-than-RAM. Latch-free and Rust-implementable. Foundation for IronCache's optional tiered backend.

mechanisms.name: Epoch protection framework
mechanisms.what_it_does: Global epoch counter; threads register the current epoch; trigger actions fire when all threads pass an epoch (drain list), enabling latch-free, lazy cross-thread synchronization and safe memory reclamation.
mechanisms.rationale: Latch-free reclamation is exactly what a thread-per-core/shared-nothing Rust cache needs to avoid per-op atomics/latches on the hot path. Maps cleanly onto crossbeam-epoch-style reclamation in Rust.

mechanisms.name: Hot-log + cold-log two-tier with read cache
mechanisms.what_it_does: Explicit in-memory hot-log and on-disk cold-log; records migrate hot->cold as they age; a read cache absorbs cold-log re-reads. Reports 2-11.9x throughput over KV stores and up to ~3.2x over RocksDB on skewed workloads.
mechanisms.rationale: The two-tier separation and read cache are the right tiering model for skewed cache traffic; adapt by simplifying F2's two-level hash index and aligning the migration policy with cache eviction (LRU/LFU) rather than database-style aging.

mechanisms.name: extstore: keys in RAM, values on flash with 12B pointer
mechanisms.what_it_does: Hash table + keys + a 12-byte [page,offset,version] pointer stay in RAM; values flushed to flash above ext_item_size (512B) into 64M pages via 8M write buffers; compaction governed by ext_max_frag/ext_compact_under (1/4 pages)/ext_drop_unread.
mechanisms.rationale: The 12-bytes-per-resident-value model is the cleanest minimal-overhead tiering design and lets you address far more flash than RAM. Adapt the file/page/compaction layer to use io_uring and a Rust-native log allocator; keep the in-RAM-pointer principle and the drop-unread compaction idea.

mechanisms.name: RocksDB / LSM as cold backend
mechanisms.what_it_does: Use an embedded LSM (leveled compaction) as the durable cold tier for larger-than-RAM data.
mechanisms.rationale: Leveled compaction write amplification is often >10x, which destroys SSD endurance on high-churn cache workloads, and embedding RocksDB (large C++ dep) breaks the single-static-binary, max-efficiency promise. Prefer a hybrid-log (FASTER-style) cold tier whose write amplification is bounded by log append + occasional compaction.

mechanisms.name: mmap warm restart (-e memory_file, SIGUSR1, .meta)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Item data lives in an mmap file on tmpfs/DAX; SIGUSR1 triggers graceful shutdown writing a .meta state file; on restart pointers are fixed up and the hash table regenerated, preserving cache contents across binary upgrades without on-disk serialization.
mechanisms.rationale: For a frequently-redeployed single binary, warm restart preserves the working set across upgrades at near-zero cost and no disk format. Higher value-per-effort than full durability for a cache. Inherit the SIGUSR1/state-file pattern; design to avoid Memcached's warm-restart-XOR-extstore limitation.

mechanisms.name: io_uring with SQPOLL + registered buffers
mechanisms.what_it_does: Async submission/completion ring; SQPOLL kernel thread removes the submit syscall from the hot path; registered/fixed buffers and files remove per-op validation/copy for zero-copy I/O.
mechanisms.rationale: The right substrate for snapshot and tiering writes; keeps the I/O path off the request-serving cores. In Rust via tokio-uring or glommio (thread-per-core), aligning with the shared-nothing shard model. Must keep a non-uring fallback for older kernels/macOS dev.

mechanisms.name: AOF append-log durability (appendfsync everysec)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Logs every write command to a file, fsynced every second on a background thread (<=1s loss window); periodic rewrite compacts the log, optionally with an RDB preamble base.
mechanisms.rationale: Append-log is the right tool only for users who need bounded data loss; it is not the efficiency-optimal default. Offer it as an opt-in tier (write-ahead binary op-log via io_uring) rather than as the primary persistence path, since continuous fsync taxes throughput-per-core.

ironcache_implications: Default to ephemeral; make durability strictly opt-in. The most-efficient-cache default is no persistence overhead at all; durability is a feature flag, not a tax everyone pays.

Adopt a shared-nothing, single-writer-per-shard architecture from day one. It is the prerequisite for Dragonfly-style fork-less snapshots, FASTER-style epoch protection, and glommio/tokio-uring thread-per-core I/O.

Implement snapshotting as Dragonfly-style versioned point-in-time, never fork()+COW. Per-entry version + epoch cut + push-pre-image-on-write gives a consistent snapshot with constant memory overhead, the opposite of Redis's up-to-2x RSS spike.

Write snapshots through io_uring (SQPOLL + registered buffers) on the I/O cores so the snapshot stream never blocks request-serving shards; keep a portable fallback path for older kernels and macOS development.

Ship mmap warm-restart early (SIGUSR1 graceful shutdown + .meta state file + pointer fixup) so binary upgrades preserve the working set with no on-disk format — high value for a frequently-redeployed single binary.

For larger-than-RAM, build a FASTER/F2-style hybrid log (mutable in-place / read-only RCU / cold on SSD) rather than embedding RocksDB; this avoids the >10x leveled-compaction write amplification that shortens SSD life on high-churn cache workloads and keeps the single static binary.

Borrow extstore's principle of keeping keys + a tiny (~12-byte) location pointer in RAM while values live on flash; this is what lets you address far more data than RAM with minimal per-item overhead. Tie cold-eviction to LRU/LFU rather than database-style aging.

Treat append-log (AOF-style) durability as an opt-in tier for users needing a bounded loss window (<=1s with everysec), implemented as a binary op-log via io_uring, not as the default — continuous fsync is a throughput-per-core cost.

Design the tiered backend's compaction/GC up front to bound write amplification (log-structured append + drop-unread-style compaction), and expose SSD-write metrics so users can reason about endurance — a differentiator vs RocksDB-backed competitors.

Resolve the warm-restart-vs-tiering conflict that Memcached punted on: IronCache's mmap warm-restart and SSD tiering should be designed to coexist (e.g., warm-restart only re-maps the RAM tier + reloads cold-tier metadata).

research_papers.title: FASTER: A Concurrent Key-Value Store with In-Place Updates
research_papers.key_idea: HybridLog (mutable in-place / read-only RCU / stable-on-disk regions) over one logical address space, with an epoch-protection framework for latch-free synchronization and memory reclamation; ~160M ops/sec on YCSB.
research_papers.relevance: The canonical larger-than-RAM, latch-free hot-path design; the hybrid log + epoch protection are the core borrows for IronCache's tiered backend and shard concurrency model.

research_papers.title: From FASTER to F2: Evolving Concurrent Key-Value Store Designs for Large Skewed Workloads
research_papers.key_idea: Two-tier hot-log (memory) + cold-log (disk) record-oriented store with a two-level hash index and read cache; 2-11.9x throughput over KV stores and up to ~3.2x over RocksDB on skewed workloads.
research_papers.relevance: Directly addresses the cache-relevant skewed-workload tiering case and shows the hybrid-log approach beats an LSM (RocksDB) on throughput, supporting the reject-RocksDB stance.

research_papers.title: Achieving High Throughput and Elasticity in a Larger-than-Memory Store (Shadowfax)
research_papers.key_idea: Scales the FASTER hybrid-log design across cores and machines with asynchronous, latch-free, zero-copy migration and epoch-based coordination for elasticity.
research_papers.relevance: Informs IronCache's single-node-to-multi-node path: how to grow a hybrid-log cache horizontally without latches, relevant to the eventual cluster milestone.

research_papers.title: Optimizing Space Amplification in RocksDB
research_papers.key_idea: Quantifies the write/read/space-amplification trade-off triangle in leveled LSM compaction and the levers (size multiplier, dynamic level sizing) to tune it.
research_papers.relevance: Primary evidence for the LSM write-amplification cost that motivates rejecting RocksDB as IronCache's core cold engine in favor of a hybrid log.

research_papers.title: Dragonfly Point-in-Time Snapshotting Design (rdbsave.md / Balanced vs Unbalanced)
research_papers.key_idea: Replaces fork()+COW with a versioned, asynchronous, fiber-based serialization that captures an epoch cut and pushes entry pre-images on write, yielding constant snapshot memory overhead and natural back-pressure.
research_papers.relevance: The exact mechanism IronCache should implement for snapshots; the most direct prior art for fork-less, memory-stable point-in-time persistence in a cache.

open_questions: Can a Dragonfly-style versioned snapshot coexist cleanly with a FASTER-style hybrid-log cold tier, given that cold-tier records already have an on-disk representation? Does snapshot need to serialize only the mutable+read-only regions plus cold-tier metadata?

What is the right Rust io_uring substrate — glommio (thread-per-core, opinionated) vs tokio-uring (integrates with the tokio ecosystem) — and what is the fallback for kernels <5.x and macOS dev environments?

How should cold-tier migration policy be driven: pure recency (FASTER aging) or cache eviction signals (LRU/LFU/W-TinyLFU)? Does coupling tiering to the eviction policy simplify or complicate the design?

What write-amplification budget is acceptable on SSD for a high-churn cache, and what compaction/GC scheme (log append + drop-unread) keeps it well below RocksDB's >10x while bounding space amplification?
Should IronCache offer an AOF-style binary op-log at all, or is fork-less snapshot + warm-restart sufficient for the target users, leaving strict durability out of scope as a non-goal?
For warm restart, is mmap-on-tmpfs portable enough, or should IronCache target DAX/persistent-memory explicitly — and how does this interact with the single-static-binary, run-anywhere promise?

What exactly is F2's measured write amplification and its read-cache hit-rate behavior on skewed workloads (needs verification against the paper's results tables, since the fetched ~3.2x-vs-RocksDB figure was model-extracted)?
Does maintaining a per-entry version counter for snapshotting impose unacceptable per-item memory overhead at IronCache's minimal-footprint target, and can it be packed into existing metadata words?

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Durability stance — ephemeral default with opt-in snapshot, warm-restart, and (later) tiered/append-log
proposed_issues.summary: Decide the durability menu and the default: ephemeral-only by default, opt-in fork-less snapshot and mmap warm-restart, with tiered SSD and append-log as later optional tiers. Record the efficiency rationale (no durability tax on the default path).

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Fork-less versioned point-in-time snapshot (Dragonfly-style)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a per-entry version counter, epoch-cut capture, traversal-fiber serialization, and an on-write pre-image push (conservative variation) so snapshots have constant memory overhead with no fork()/COW spike. Define the per-shard serialization channel and back-pressure.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: No fork()+copy-on-write snapshotting
proposed_issues.summary: Explicitly reject Redis-style fork+COW BGSAVE because it can roughly double RSS under write load, violating the minimal-memory goal. Snapshotting will be versioned and fork-less.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: FASTER/F2 hybrid log vs LSM for IronCache's larger-than-RAM cold tier
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate a FASTER/F2-style hybrid log (mutable/read-only/stable) against an embedded LSM, quantifying write amplification (RocksDB leveled often >10x), SSD endurance, and the single-static-binary impact of embedding RocksDB.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Reject RocksDB/LSM as the core cold engine on write-amplification and dependency grounds
proposed_issues.summary: Decide not to embed RocksDB as the primary cold backend due to >10x leveled-compaction write amplification (SSD endurance) and the C++ dependency breaking the single static binary; adopt a hybrid-log cold tier instead.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Epoch-protection-based latch-free reclamation for shard hot path
proposed_issues.summary: Adapt FASTER's epoch-protection framework (or crossbeam-epoch) for latch-free memory reclamation and lazy cross-thread coordination in IronCache's shared-nothing shards, enabling allocation-free in-place updates in the mutable region.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: mmap warm-restart (SIGUSR1 graceful shutdown + state file + pointer fixup)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a Memcached-1.5.18-style warm restart: item data in an mmap file, SIGUSR1 graceful shutdown writing a .meta state file, pointer fixup and index regeneration on restart, preserving the working set across binary upgrades with no disk format.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: io_uring snapshot/tiering write path with SQPOLL + registered buffers and a fallback
