proposed_issues.summary: Define the async I/O path for snapshots and SSD tiering using io_uring (SQPOLL, fixed buffers) on dedicated I/O cores, choosing between glommio and tokio-uring, with a portable fallback for older kernels and macOS dev.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Tiered RAM->SSD value store keeping keys + tiny location pointer in RAM (extstore-inspired)
proposed_issues.summary: Adapt extstore's model — keys plus a ~12-byte [page,offset,version] pointer in RAM, values on flash in log pages — with io_uring writes, drop-unread-style compaction, and migration driven by cache eviction (LRU/LFU).

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Bounding SSD write amplification for high-churn cache tiering
proposed_issues.summary: Determine an acceptable write-amplification budget and a log-append + compaction/GC scheme that keeps it well below LSM levels while bounding space amplification, and expose SSD-write/endurance metrics.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Append-log (AOF-style) durability as opt-in tier or non-goal
proposed_issues.summary: Decide whether IronCache offers a binary op-log with an everysec-style <=1s loss window as an opt-in tier, or treats strict durability as a non-goal given fork-less snapshot + warm-restart cover most cache use cases.

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: Persistence and storage-engine architecture
proposed_issues.summary: Umbrella tracking the durability decision space (ephemeral/snapshot/append-log/tiered), the fork-less snapshot, warm restart, hybrid-log tiered backend, and io_uring I/O path, with the efficiency tradeoffs of each.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Overall: 16 of 17 claims confirmed, 1 corrected.

KEY CORRECTION:
- [f2-throughput-vs-rocksdb]: The '2-11.9x vs existing KV stores' headline is CORRECT, but the RocksDB-specific multiple is WRONG. The paper reports F2 outperforms RocksDB by ~11.8x on average, not ~3.2x. (Per-system: FASTER 2.1x, LeanStore 2x, KVell 11.9x, SplinterDB 4.6x, RocksDB 11.8x.) Source: VLDB 2025 vol18 p4910-kanellis. Also note the version field 'VLDB 2024/2025' is slightly off — the arXiv preprint is 2023 and the published version is VLDB 2025 (PVLDB vol 18).

SOURCING CAVEATS (claims confirmed, but cited URL is weaker than the real source-of-truth):
- [redis-default-rdb-save-points] and [redis-auto-aof-rewrite-min-size]: the cited docs/latest persistence page does NOT contain these default values — it only links to the example config. The authoritative source is redis.conf (verified against the 7.4 branch). Values are correct.
- [redis-fork-cow-doubles-memory]: the cited docs page mentions COW but never states ~2x; the ~2x worst-case doubling is confirmed by independent operational sources. Claim's own 'medium' confidence is right.
- [io-uring-sqpoll-registered-buffers]: the tx/s benchmark numbers are NOT on the cited man7 man page; they come from arXiv 2512.04859. The man page supports the 'submit syscall eliminated' part only. Numbers corroborated independently.
- [rocksdb-leveled-write-amp-over-10]: '>10x' and 'multiplier 10' verbatim-confirmed on the wiki; the 'Facebook production... a few use 8' detail is plausible (Tuning Guide shows an 8x example) but not verbatim on the cited page.

METHOD NOTE: WebFetch's PDF text extraction was unreliable/paraphrasing for the FASTER and F2 PDFs (it appeared to reconstruct expected text rather than quote). I therefore cross-checked the load-bearing numbers (FASTER 160M, F2 vs RocksDB) against independent HTML/search sources rather than trusting the PDF summaries. The F2-vs-RocksDB correction rests on the per-system breakdown returned by web search citing the published paper.

dimensions.summary:
Redis OSS core (pinned to tag 8.8.0, commit 5a693aa, released 2026-05-25) is built around a single-threaded command-execution model on top of an event loop (`ae.c`: `aeMain` -> `aeProcessEvents` -> `aeApiPoll`), with a compile-time-selected multiplexing backend (evport > epoll > kqueue > select). The crucial invariant: all command execution, all keyspace mutation, expiration, and eviction run on ONE main thread; there is no per-key locking. Since 6.0 Redis offloaded socket I/O to `io-threads`, and Redis 8.0 shipped a rewritten threaded-I/O model where the main thread assigns clients to I/O threads which do socket reads + RESP parsing and socket writes, while the main thread still executes every command serially. The documented gain is "up to 112% throughput" at `io-threads 8` on a multi-core Intel CPU, but the default is `io-threads 1` (single-threaded out of the box); `io-threads-do-reads` is now a deprecated no-op (reads are always threaded in the new model). RESP2/RESP3 are handled at the reply-encoding layer per-client (HELLO negotiates protocol), not as separate execution paths.

Expiration is two-pronged. Lazy expiration checks TTL on access (`expireIfNeeded`); active expiration runs `activeExpireCycle` in two modes — a SLOW cycle from `databasesCron`/`serverCron` at `hz` (default 10, max 500) bounded to ~25% CPU, and a FAST cycle in `beforeSleep` on every event-loop iteration bounded to 1000us. Defaults (`expire.c`): `ACTIVE_EXPIRE_CYCLE_KEYS_PER_LOOP 20`, `ACTIVE_EXPIRE_CYCLE_FAST_DURATION 1000us`, `ACTIVE_EXPIRE_CYCLE_SLOW_TIME_PERC 25`, `ACTIVE_EXPIRE_CYCLE_ACCEPTABLE_STALE 10%`, all scaled by `active-expire-effort` (default 1, range 1-10). Active expiry runs only on masters; replicas wait for DEL/UNLINK propagation. This sampling design tolerates up to ~10% stale keys before escalating — a deliberate "good-enough" probabilistic approach rather than an exact timer wheel.

Eviction is sampled and approximate. `maxmemory` defaults to 0 (no limit) and `maxmemory-policy` defaults to `noeviction` (`MAXMEMORY_NO_EVICTION`), meaning out-of-the-box Redis is NOT a cache that evicts — it returns OOM errors on writes. The 8 classic policies plus two new in 8.6 (`volatile-lrm`/`allkeys-lrm` = Least Recently Modified, evicting by last-write rather than last-access) are dispatched via flag bits. Approximated LRU stores a 24-bit clock (`LRU_BITS 24`, `LRU_CLOCK_MAX = 2^24-1`, `LRU_CLOCK_RESOLUTION 1000ms`) in the robj's `lru:24` bitfield; eviction samples `maxmemory-samples` keys (default 5; ~10 approaches true LRU per Redis's own simulation graphs) into a 16-entry pool (`EVPOOL_SIZE 16`). LFU repurposes the same 24-bit field as 16-bit last-decay-time (minutes) + 8-bit Morris probabilistic counter; `LFULogIncr` increments with probability 1/((counter-5)*lfu-log-factor+1) (defaults `lfu-log-factor 10`, `LFU_INIT_VAL 5`, saturating ~1M accesses), and `LFUDecrAndReturn` decays one unit per `lfu-decay-time` minutes (default 1).

Memory accounting uses `zmalloc_used_memory()` (allocator logical bytes, NOT OS RSS), subtracting replica output buffers and the AOF buffer, so fragmentation can push RSS well above `maxmemory`. `performEvictions` runs before command execution and is time-bounded by `maxmemory-eviction-tenacity` (default 10 -> 500us; geometric to ~2min at 99); if it can't keep up it starts a timer-proc to keep evicting and may return EVICT_RUNNING/EVICT_FAIL. Freeing big objects is offloaded to background BIO threads via lazyfree/UNLINK when `lazyfreeGetFreeEffort > LAZYFREE_THRESHOLD (64)` and refcount==1; `lazyfree-lazy-eviction`/`-expire`/`-server-del`/`-user-del` all default to 0 (off). active-defrag (defaults `active-defrag-cycle-min 1`, `-cycle-max 25`, `-threshold-lower 10`, `-ignore-bytes 100MB`) only works when compiled with jemalloc (`HAVE_DEFRAG`).

For IronCache, the load-bearing lessons: (1) the single-threaded keyspace is what makes Redis simple and predictable but also what caps it at one core for command execution — IronCache's "max throughput per core" goal should consciously decide between Redis's shared-nothing-per-core sharding (like KeyDB/Dragonfly) vs. fine-grained concurrent structures; (2) Redis's approximate-everything philosophy (sampled LRU/LFU, probabilistic counters, sampled expiry) trades exactness for O(1) memory and bounded latency — a strong default to borrow; (3) the 24-bit-in-the-object-header metadata budget is a model worth beating with better admission/eviction (TinyLFU/W-TinyLFU); (4) `noeviction` + `maxmemory 0` defaults make stock Redis a surprising footgun as a cache — IronCache should ship cache-sane defaults; (5) accounting on allocator bytes not RSS, plus jemalloc-only defrag, is a portability and accuracy wart IronCache can address from day one in Rust.

mechanisms.name: Single-threaded event loop (ae) with single-threaded command execution
mechanisms.what_it_does: aeMain drives aeProcessEvents -> aeApiPoll (epoll/kqueue); all command execution and keyspace mutation happen on one thread with no per-key locks, giving lock-free determinism and trivial atomicity per command.
mechanisms.rationale: The lock-free simplicity is the single best thing about Redis and worth preserving for correctness, but a strictly single command thread caps throughput at one core, contradicting IronCache's 'max throughput per core' AND multi-core scaling goals. IronCache should adapt toward a shared-nothing per-core sharded model (thread-per-core, like Dragonfly/Seastar) or sharded locking, keeping each shard single-threaded internally so per-key atomicity stays lock-free while total throughput scales across cores.

mechanisms.name: Redis 8.0 threaded I/O (offload read/parse/write to io-threads)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Main thread assigns clients to io-threads; io-threads do socket recv + RESP parse and socket send; main thread still executes every command. Default io-threads=1; up to ~112% throughput at 8.
mechanisms.rationale: This is a workaround for the single command thread: it parallelizes only I/O while command execution remains a serial bottleneck, and it adds cross-thread handoff overhead and complexity. IronCache should instead make the whole request path (I/O + parse + execute) parallel via per-core sharding, avoiding the awkward main-thread fan-in entirely.

mechanisms.name: Approximated LRU via random sampling + eviction pool
mechanisms.what_it_does: Stores a 24-bit second-resolution clock per object; on eviction samples maxmemory-samples (default 5) keys into a 16-entry pool sorted by idle time and evicts the best candidate. ~10 samples approaches true LRU.
mechanisms.rationale: O(1)-memory approximate eviction is the right philosophy, but sampled LRU is provably worse than modern admission policies (it has no frequency signal and scans random keys). IronCache should adapt to a W-TinyLFU/SLRU design (count-min sketch admission + segmented LRU) which beats LRU/LFU hit-rate at comparable metadata cost, while keeping the sample-don't-sort, bounded-work spirit.

mechanisms.name: LFU with 8-bit Morris probabilistic counter + decay
mechanisms.what_it_does: Reuses the 24-bit field as 16-bit last-decay-minute + 8-bit counter; LFULogIncr increments with prob 1/((counter-5)*lfu_log_factor+1) (saturates ~1M hits at factor 10), LFUDecrAndReturn decays one per lfu-decay-time minutes (default 1).
mechanisms.rationale: The probabilistic-counter idea (track frequency in tiny space, log-scale to avoid saturation, time-decay to age out) is excellent and directly relevant. Adapt by folding it into a TinyLFU count-min sketch with aging (the academic refinement of exactly this idea), giving frequency estimation decoupled from per-object storage so cold keys cost zero counter bytes.

mechanisms.name: Dual-mode active expiration (slow cron cycle + fast beforeSleep cycle)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Lazy expiry on access plus a SLOW activeExpireCycle at hz (default 10) capped at 25% CPU and a FAST cycle each event-loop iteration capped at 1000us; samples 20 keys/db, escalates if >10% sampled are stale; masters only.
mechanisms.rationale: Probabilistic sampled expiry with a stale-ratio feedback loop is a sound, low-overhead default to borrow. Adapt the scheduling: a per-shard timer/hierarchical timing wheel can give tighter bounds on memory held by expired-but-not-reclaimed keys than blind sampling, and IronCache can expire proactively per shard without the master-only constraint.

mechanisms.name: maxmemory accounting on allocator logical bytes (zmalloc), not RSS
mechanisms.what_it_does: getMaxmemoryState compares zmalloc_used_memory() (minus replica output buffers + AOF buffer) to maxmemory; OS RSS can exceed maxmemory due to fragmentation, requiring separate active-defrag.
mechanisms.rationale: Accounting against a true budget is essential for a 'minimal memory' cache, but counting only allocator-requested bytes lets fragmentation blow past the limit and forces a jemalloc-only defrag band-aid. IronCache should account for real allocator overhead (slab/size-class internal fragmentation) up front and choose an allocator with predictable fragmentation, so 'maxmemory' is an honest bound.

mechanisms.name: Lazyfree / UNLINK background reclamation
mechanisms.what_it_does: Large objects (free_effort > LAZYFREE_THRESHOLD 64, refcount==1) are freed on background BIO threads to keep the main thread responsive; gated by lazyfree-lazy-* configs (all default off).
mechanisms.rationale: Moving expensive frees off the hot path is straightforwardly good and maps cleanly onto a Rust background-drop thread or per-shore reclamation queue. IronCache should borrow this and, unlike Redis, make async reclamation the default rather than opt-in.

mechanisms.name: noeviction + maxmemory=0 default posture
mechanisms.what_it_does: Out of the box Redis has no memory limit and never evicts; writes fail with OOM once the OS limit is hit unless an admin configures maxmemory and a policy.
mechanisms.rationale: For a product whose identity is 'a cache', defaulting to a non-evicting unbounded store is a footgun that pushes correctness onto operators. IronCache should ship a sane cache default (a memory ceiling derived from the host plus an LRU/W-TinyLFU eviction policy enabled) while still allowing a strict no-evict mode for datastore use.

mechanisms.name: active-defrag (jemalloc-dependent compaction)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Incrementally relocates allocations to reduce external fragmentation, CPU-bounded 1-25%, only when fragmentation > threshold and only if built with jemalloc (HAVE_DEFRAG).
mechanisms.rationale: Active-defrag exists only because malloc-based heap layout fragments badly; it is jemalloc-specific, CPU-costly, and a symptom rather than a cure. IronCache should design storage (slab/arena/region allocation with compaction-friendly layout) so external fragmentation is structurally bounded, removing the need for an online defragmenter.

ironcache_implications: Decide the concurrency model explicitly: Redis caps command execution at one core; IronCache's 'max throughput per core AND scale to multi-node' goal points to thread-per-core shared-nothing sharding (each shard single-threaded internally for lock-free per-key atomicity), which sidesteps Redis's awkward io-threads fan-in. Document the chosen model in the vision EPIC.

Ship cache-sane defaults: a memory ceiling auto-derived from host RAM plus an eviction policy ON by default (unlike Redis noeviction + maxmemory=0). Offer a strict 'no-evict / datastore' mode as opt-in. This single decision differentiates IronCache's out-of-box UX.

Adopt W-TinyLFU (admission via count-min sketch + segmented LRU) rather than Redis's sampled LRU/LFU; it has higher hit ratios at comparable or lower metadata cost and is the state of the art (Caffeine, Ristretto). Keep Redis's bounded-work, sample-don't-globally-sort discipline.

Budget per-object metadata deliberately: Redis spends 24 bits in the object header for LRU/LFU. IronCache should aim to keep frequency/recency metadata out of per-object headers (e.g. a shared sketch) so cold keys cost near-zero, directly serving the 'minimal memory' goal.

Make maxmemory an honest bound: account for allocator internal fragmentation and size-class overhead, not just requested bytes, and pick an allocator with predictable fragmentation so IronCache does not need a Redis-style online defragmenter.
Make background reclamation (async drop of large values, lazy-free) the default, using a Rust background thread or per-shard reclamation queue, instead of Redis's opt-in lazyfree-lazy-* flags.

Use a per-shard hierarchical timing wheel for TTLs to bound the memory held by expired-but-unreclaimed keys, improving on Redis's probabilistic sampled active-expire while keeping lazy-on-access expiry as a backstop.

Maintain Redis RESP2/RESP3 wire compatibility but keep the parser and reply encoder zero-copy and per-shard; benchmark against redis-benchmark and memtier so the 'most efficient cache' claim is grounded in head-to-head numbers vs Redis 8.8 io-threads=N.

Consider a Least-Recently-Modified-style signal (Redis 8.6 LRM) for read-heavy workloads where write-recency predicts liveness better than access-recency; evaluate whether W-TinyLFU already subsumes this need.

research_papers.title: TinyLFU: A Highly Efficient Cache Admission Policy
research_papers.key_idea: Use an aging count-min sketch as a frequency-based admission filter in front of an eviction policy, deciding whether a newly-arrived item should displace the eviction victim. Tiny metadata, near-optimal hit ratio.
research_papers.relevance: Directly the principled successor to Redis's sampled LFU/Morris counter; the basis for IronCache's recommended W-TinyLFU eviction with shared (not per-object) frequency state.

research_papers.title: Adaptive Software Cache Management (W-TinyLFU / Caffeine)
research_papers.key_idea: Window-TinyLFU adds an admission window + segmented main region (SLRU) and adaptively resizes the window, beating LRU/LFU/ARC across diverse traces; implemented in Caffeine.
research_papers.relevance: Concrete, production-proven eviction design IronCache should adopt; quantifies hit-ratio wins over the LRU/LFU family Redis uses.

research_papers.title: Memshare: a Dynamic Multi-tenant Key-value Cache
research_papers.key_idea: Log-structured, slab-free memory management with sharing across tenants and cleaning to control fragmentation, raising effective hit rate by reclaiming wasted memory.
research_papers.relevance: Informs IronCache's allocator/storage layout decision so it avoids Redis's jemalloc-dependent active-defrag and bounds fragmentation structurally.

research_papers.title: The CacheLib Caching Engine: Design and Experiences at Scale
research_papers.key_idea: A general-purpose embeddable cache library with hybrid DRAM/flash, slab-based allocation, and pluggable eviction; documents real-world tradeoffs of approximate LRU, TTL reaping, and memory accounting at Facebook scale.
research_papers.relevance: Engineering reference for IronCache's memory accounting, eviction abstraction, and the value of bounded-work background maintenance over Redis-style sampling.

research_papers.title: Scaling Memcache at Facebook
research_papers.key_idea: Operational lessons on running a large key-value cache fleet: lease/stale handling, the lazy vs proactive expiration tradeoff, and the importance of predictable latency over peak throughput.
research_papers.relevance: Grounds IronCache's expiration and single-node-to-multi-node design choices in real distributed-cache operating experience.

research_papers.title: Counting Large Numbers of Events in Small Registers (Morris counter)
research_papers.key_idea: Probabilistic approximate counting that tracks counts up to large magnitudes in very few bits by incrementing with decreasing probability.
research_papers.relevance: The exact algorithm behind Redis's 8-bit LFU counter; documents the accuracy/space tradeoff IronCache must reason about if it keeps any per-object counter.

open_questions: Thread-per-core shared-nothing (Dragonfly/Seastar style) vs sharded-lock vs Redis-style single command thread + io-threads: which maximizes throughput-per-core for IronCache while keeping per-key operations atomic and RESP-compatible MULTI/EXEC and Lua semantics intact?
Can W-TinyLFU's frequency sketch + SLRU be made cross-shard or must each shard keep its own admission state, and what is the hit-ratio cost of per-shard partitioning of the frequency signal?

What is the right default memory ceiling heuristic (e.g. fraction of host RAM) and how should IronCache expose 'cache mode' vs 'datastore (no-evict) mode' without surprising operators migrating from Redis defaults?
How should maxmemory account for allocator internal fragmentation in Rust (which allocator: jemalloc/mimalloc/custom slab) so the limit is honest without needing an online defragmenter?
Does IronCache need an LRM-style last-modified signal, or does a frequency+recency policy (W-TinyLFU) already capture the read-heavy workloads LRM targets?
What is the latency/throughput cost of RESP3 push/attribute support and client-side caching invalidation tracking, and should tracking state be per-shard?

What is the empirical head-to-head throughput-per-core and hit-ratio of an IronCache prototype vs Redis 8.8 (io-threads tuned) on standard memtier/zipfian traces, and which workloads expose Redis's single-command-thread ceiling most?

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Concurrency model — thread-per-core shared-nothing vs single command thread + threaded I/O
proposed_issues.summary: Choose IronCache's execution model given Redis caps command execution at one core. Evaluate thread-per-core shared-nothing sharding (Dragonfly/Seastar), sharded-lock, and Redis-style io-threads; decide how per-key atomicity, MULTI/EXEC, and Lua are preserved.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: W-TinyLFU eviction (count-min sketch admission + segmented LRU) as default policy
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a W-TinyLFU/SLRU eviction subsystem to replace Redis's sampled LRU/LFU, including sketch sizing, aging, admission window adaptation, and per-shard vs shared frequency state. Target higher hit ratio at lower per-object metadata cost.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Default memory posture — ship a memory ceiling + eviction ON by default
proposed_issues.summary: Unlike Redis (maxmemory=0, noeviction), IronCache should default to a host-derived memory ceiling with eviction enabled, plus an opt-in strict no-evict mode. Define the ceiling heuristic and migration story for Redis users.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Per-object metadata budget for recency/frequency vs shared sketch
proposed_issues.summary: Quantify the memory cost of Redis's 24-bit per-object LRU/LFU field at scale and evaluate moving frequency/recency state into a shared sketch so cold keys cost near-zero, in service of the minimal-memory goal.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Honest maxmemory accounting including allocator fragmentation; no online defragmenter
proposed_issues.summary: Specify how IronCache counts memory against its budget (including slab/size-class internal fragmentation) and selects an allocator/storage layout that bounds external fragmentation structurally, avoiding Redis's jemalloc-only active-defrag.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: TTL expiration — per-shard hierarchical timing wheel + lazy-on-access backstop
proposed_issues.summary: Replace Redis's probabilistic sampled active-expire with a per-shard timing wheel that bounds memory held by expired keys, retaining lazy-on-access expiry as a backstop and avoiding the master-only constraint.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Default-on background reclamation (async drop of large values)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a Rust background-drop / per-shard reclamation queue so large-value frees never block the hot path, making async reclamation the default rather than Redis's opt-in lazyfree-lazy-* flags.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Benchmark harness vs Redis 8.8 (memtier/redis-benchmark, zipfian) for throughput-per-core and hit ratio
proposed_issues.summary: Stand up a reproducible head-to-head benchmark vs Redis 8.8 with tuned io-threads, measuring throughput-per-core, p99 latency, and hit ratio across workloads to ground the 'most efficient cache' claim.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Does IronCache need an LRM (least-recently-modified) signal, or does W-TinyLFU subsume it?
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate Redis 8.6's allkeys-lrm/volatile-lrm policies for read-heavy workloads and determine whether a frequency+recency policy already captures the same benefit, informing whether IronCache exposes a write-recency eviction mode.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: Do not adopt Redis's main-thread fan-in io-threads model
proposed_issues.summary: Record that IronCache will not replicate Redis 8.0's io-threads-with-single-command-thread design; parallelism comes from sharding the whole request path, not from offloading only socket I/O.

dimensions.verify_notes: Verified at tag 8.8.0 (commit 5a693aaed0842c4eef21c706b79fa49938ca9f6c, GA, published 2026-05-25) by downloading the actual source files via the GitHub API and reading exact lines. 18 of 20 claims confirmed exactly. Two corrections, both minor/sourcing rather than substantive: (1) redis-lru-clock-resolution — values (1000ms, 16777215) are correct but the line citation is swapped; LRU_CLOCK_RESOLUTION is object.h:92, LRU_CLOCK_MAX is object.h:91. (2) redis-command-execution-single-threaded — the stated VALUE is correct and independently verified (Redis latency doc states the mostly-single-threaded sequential model; src/iothread.c shows io-threads only read+parse and write while the main thread processes all commands and builds replies), but the CITED redis-8-ga blog does not explicitly assert single-threaded command execution, so the source was corrected to the latency optimization doc. Additional cross-checks: io-threads 112% figure corroborated by independent search (range ~37-112%); 32-bit 3GB implicit maxmemory confirmed in src/server.c:3158-3164; LRM PR #14624 confirmed merged 2026-01-06 via GitHub API. No claim was refuted; no claim left uncertain. Note for context: newer patch releases (8.6.4/8.4.4/8.2.7, 2026-06-04) exist on older backport branches but 8.8.0 remains the latest GA minor line and GitHub's 'latest' pointer.

dimensions.summary:
Redis's memory model is built around a small polymorphic object header (robj/redisObject) that points to one of several internal encodings selected dynamically per value. In the classic layout (verified at 7.4.0, src/server.h) the header is a 4-bit type, 4-bit encoding, 24-bit lru packed into one 32-bit word, a 4-byte int refcount, and an 8-byte void* ptr = 16 bytes on 64-bit. A landmark change shipped in Redis 8.x: the "kvobj" redesign (verified at 8.8.0, src/object.h) repacks the header to type:4, encoding:4, refcount:23, iskvobj:1, metabits:8, lru:24, ptr — and, critically, embeds the key SDS and optional metadata (expire) directly after the object via a single allocation, plus dict.c bucket pointer-tagging to skip dictEntry allocation entirely when a bucket holds a single key. This is precisely the per-key-overhead reduction IronCache cares about: it collapses what used to be (dictEntry + key robj + key SDS + value robj + value SDS) into far fewer allocations.

Strings use SDS with five header variants (sdshdr5/8/16/32/64) chosen by length; sdshdr8 is a 3-byte header (1B len, 1B alloc, 1B flags). Small strings (<=44 bytes, OBJ_ENCODING_EMBSTR_SIZE_LIMIT in object.c) are stored embedded in the same allocation as the robj (createEmbeddedStringObject), always SDS_TYPE_8; longer strings are OBJ_ENCODING_RAW (separate allocation). Integer-parseable strings up to 20 digits become OBJ_ENCODING_INT and share a pool of 10000 pre-allocated shared integer objects (OBJ_SHARED_INTEGERS), which is disabled under maxmemory + LRU/LFU eviction because shared objects can't carry per-key LRU.

The compact collection encodings all converge on listpack (src/listpack.c), a single contiguous, malloc-friendly, cache-friendly blob with a 6-byte header (LP_HDR_SIZE: 32-bit total-bytes + 16-bit element count) and per-entry back-length for reverse traversal; it replaced ziplist (the ZIPLIST encoding constant is retained only as "no longer used"). Lists are a quicklist (src/quicklist.h): a doubly linked list of 32-byte quicklistNodes each wrapping a listpack, with optional LZF compression of interior nodes (list-compress-depth) and a fill factor (list-max-listpack-size default -2 = 8KB per node). Hashes and sorted sets start as listpack and convert to hashtable (dict) / skiplist+hashtable respectively once thresholds are crossed; sets use intset (sorted int array, src/intset.h: 4-byte encoding + 4-byte length + contents[]) for all-integer small sets, listpack for small mixed sets, and hashtable otherwise. Streams use a radix tree (rax, src/rax.h: raxNode with iskey/isnull/iscompr/size:29 bitfields, path compression) keyed by 128-bit stream IDs whose leaves are listpacks of entries. HyperLogLog (src/hyperloglog.c) uses P=14 -> 16384 6-bit registers; dense form is HLL_HDR_SIZE(16) + 12288 = 12304 bytes, sparse form is a run-length-encoded opcode stream capped by hll-sparse-max-bytes (default 3000) before promotion to dense. Bitmaps/GETBIT/SETBIT are just operations over a raw SDS string.

The encoding thresholds (verified in src/config.c at 8.8.0) are: hash-max-listpack-entries 512 / hash-max-listpack-value 64; list-max-listpack-size -2 (8KB); set-max-intset-entries 512; set-max-listpack-entries 128 / set-max-listpack-value 64; zset-max-listpack-entries 128 / zset-max-listpack-value 64. Conversions are one-way (compact -> general) and triggered on insert. The general hash table (dict) keeps two tables (ht_table[2], ht_used[2], ht_size_exp[2], rehashidx) and rehashes incrementally one bucket-step at a time, expanding when load factor crosses dict_force_resize_ratio (4) and shrinking below 1/(HASHTABLE_MIN_FILL * dict_force_resize_ratio) = 1/32.

For IronCache the strategic lessons are: (1) the single-allocation embedded-key/embedded-value pattern (Redis 8 kvobj + Valkey's earlier embedded-key work) is the biggest per-key memory win and should be a first-class design goal; (2) the listpack-as-universal-compact-container idea is excellent and worth borrowing wholesale, but the fixed scalar thresholds (128/512/64) are crude and a candidate for adaptive/learned tuning; (3) incremental rehashing is non-negotiable for tail-latency on a single-threaded-per-core event loop and must be designed in from the start; (4) the 16-byte (or 16+ byte) per-object header dominates small-value workloads, so a header-free / inline path for tiny values is where the "minimal memory" claim is won or lost.

prior_art_claims.claim: Classic robj (redisObject) header is 16 bytes on 64-bit: type:4 + encoding:4 + lru:24 (one 32-bit word) + int refcount (4B) + void *ptr (8B).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read struct redisObject directly from src/server.h at tag 7.4.0; LRU_BITS=24 confirmed inline.

verification.corrected_value: 16 bytes (4B packed bitfield [type:4 + encoding:4 + lru:24] + 4B int refcount + 8B void *ptr)
verification.note: Verified by git clone at tag 7.4.0 and independent raw.githubusercontent fetch. struct redisObject = {unsigned type:4; unsigned encoding:4; unsigned lru:LRU_BITS(24); int refcount; void *ptr;}. Packed bitfield (4+4+24=32 bits = 4B) + int refcount (4B) + ptr (8B) = 16B on 64-bit. NOTE: claim cites L896 but the struct actually begins at L903 in the 7.4.0 tree; field layout described is exactly correct.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis 8.x repacks the object header (src/object.h) to type:4, encoding:4, refcount:23 (OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS), iskvobj:1, metabits:8, lru:24, plus void *ptr, and introduces kvobj which embeds the key SDS (and optional expire metadata) into the same allocation as the value object.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read struct redisObject and OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS=23 directly from src/object.h at tag 8.8.0.

verification.corrected_value: type:4 + encoding:4 + refcount:23 (OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS) + iskvobj:1 + metabits:8 + lru:24 (LRU_BITS) + void *ptr; total bitfield = 64 bits = 8B, +8B ptr = 16B; kvobj embeds key SDS inline (and optional expire/metadata blocks placed before the struct)
verification.note: Tag 8.8.0 is a real release (confirmed via git ls-remote). struct redisObject at object.h#L100 matches claim exactly; OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS=23 at L95. Header comment documents kvobj embedding the key string inline after the struct, with up to 8 metadata classes (class 0 = expiration) placed before the kvobj in reverse class order. Confirmed by git clone + independent raw fetch.

prior_art_claims.claim: Strings <=44 bytes use OBJ_ENCODING_EMBSTR (embedded SDS in the same allocation as robj, always SDS_TYPE_8); longer strings use OBJ_ENCODING_RAW with a separate SDS allocation.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read #define OBJ_ENCODING_EMBSTR_SIZE_LIMIT 44 and createEmbeddedStringObject directly from src/object.c at 8.8.0.

verification.corrected_value: 44 bytes (OBJ_ENCODING_EMBSTR_SIZE_LIMIT); EMBSTR value SDS is always SDS_TYPE_8
verification.note: #define OBJ_ENCODING_EMBSTR_SIZE_LIMIT 44 at object.c#L337, with comment about fitting in jemalloc 64-byte arena. createStringObject uses createEmbeddedStringObject for len<=44 else createRawStringObject. createEmbeddedStringObject comment+code: 'always as SDS TYPE 8' via sdsnewplacement(...,SDS_TYPE_8,...). Verified by git clone + independent raw fetch.

prior_art_claims.claim: SDS has 5 header types (sdshdr5/8/16/32/64) selected by string length; sdshdr8 is a packed 3-byte header (uint8 len, uint8 alloc, uint8 flags) before the buffer. sdshdr5 stores length in the top 5 bits of the flags byte (1-byte header, no alloc field).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read the five packed struct definitions and SDS_TYPE_* defines directly from src/sds.h at 8.8.0.

verification.corrected_value: 5 header types (sdshdr5/8/16/32/64). sdshdr5 = 1-byte header (just flags byte: 3 lsb type + 5 msb length, no alloc). sdshdr8 = 3-byte header (uint8 len, uint8 alloc, unsigned char flags).
verification.note: sds.h#L28: sdshdr5 = {unsigned char flags; char buf[];} (1 byte). sdshdr8 = {uint8_t len; uint8_t alloc; unsigned char flags; char buf[];} (3 bytes). All five variants (5/8/16/32/64) present at L28-50. Matches claim exactly. Note sds.h comment: 'sdshdr5 is never used' standalone, but the embstr value uses SDS_TYPE_8.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis pre-allocates a shared pool of 10000 small integer objects (OBJ_SHARED_INTEGERS) reused for integer-encoded values; sharing is disabled when maxmemory is set with an LRU/LFU policy because shared objects cannot carry per-key eviction metadata.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read #define OBJ_SHARED_INTEGERS 10000 from server.h and the maxmemory/NO_SHARED_INTEGERS guard in object.c at 8.8.0.

verification.corrected_value: 10000 shared integers (OBJ_SHARED_INTEGERS); sharing disabled when maxmemory set with LRU/LFU (and LRM) policy via MAXMEMORY_FLAG_NO_SHARED_INTEGERS
verification.note: #define OBJ_SHARED_INTEGERS 10000 at server.h#L126; shared.integers[OBJ_SHARED_INTEGERS] array. Disable logic: object.c uses !(server.maxmemory_policy & MAXMEMORY_FLAG_NO_SHARED_INTEGERS), where that flag = (MAXMEMORY_FLAG_LRU|MAXMEMORY_FLAG_LFU|MAXMEMORY_FLAG_LRM) at server.h#L689. Claim's LRU/LFU rationale (shared objects can't carry per-key eviction metadata) is correct; 8.8 additionally includes the LRM policy.

prior_art_claims.claim: listpack (which replaced ziplist) is a single contiguous blob with a 6-byte header: 32-bit total-bytes length + 16-bit element count (LP_HDR_SIZE), terminated by a 1-byte LP_EOF; each entry stores a back-length for reverse traversal.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read #define LP_HDR_SIZE 6 and the header comment from src/listpack.c at 8.8.0.

verification.corrected_value: 6 bytes (LP_HDR_SIZE: 32-bit total-bytes + 16-bit element count) + 1-byte LP_EOF (0xFF); entries store a back-length for reverse traversal
verification.note: #define LP_HDR_SIZE 6 at listpack.c#L27 with comment '32 bit total len + 16 bit number of elements'. #define LP_EOF 0xFF at L76 (1 byte). lpNew sets total bytes = LP_HDR_SIZE+1 and writes LP_EOF at offset LP_HDR_SIZE. Back-length present (LP_MAX_BACKLEN_SIZE). Verified via git clone.

prior_art_claims.claim: Hashes use listpack encoding until they exceed hash-max-listpack-entries (default 512) entries or any field/value exceeds hash-max-listpack-value (default 64) bytes, then convert to a hashtable (dict).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read createSizeTConfig defaults (512, 64) directly from src/config.c at 8.8.0; corroborated by redis.io memory-optimization doc.

verification.corrected_value: hash-max-listpack-entries default 512; hash-max-listpack-value default 64
verification.note: config.c#L3349: hash-max-listpack-entries default 512 (alias hash-max-ziplist-entries). L3355: hash-max-listpack-value default 64 (alias hash-max-ziplist-value). Matches claim. Verified via git clone.

prior_art_claims.claim: Sorted sets use listpack until they exceed zset-max-listpack-entries (default 128) entries or any member exceeds zset-max-listpack-value (default 64) bytes, then convert to skiplist + hashtable.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read createSizeTConfig defaults (128, 64) directly from src/config.c at 8.8.0; corroborated by redis.io doc.

verification.corrected_value: zset-max-listpack-entries default 128; zset-max-listpack-value default 64
verification.note: config.c#L3353: zset-max-listpack-entries default 128 (alias zset-max-ziplist-entries). L3357: zset-max-listpack-value default 64. Matches claim. Verified via git clone.

prior_art_claims.claim: Sets use intset (sorted int array) up to set-max-intset-entries (default 512) for all-integer sets, listpack up to set-max-listpack-entries (default 128) / set-max-listpack-value (default 64) for small mixed sets (since 7.2), else hashtable.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read all three createSizeTConfig defaults directly from src/config.c at 8.8.0; listpack-for-set added in 7.2 per redis.io doc.

verification.corrected_value: set-max-intset-entries default 512; set-max-listpack-entries default 128; set-max-listpack-value default 64
verification.note: config.c L3350 set-max-intset-entries=512, L3351 set-max-listpack-entries=128, L3352 set-max-listpack-value=64. All match. Listpack-for-small-mixed-sets was introduced in 7.2 as stated. Verified via git clone.

prior_art_claims.claim: Lists are quicklists; list-max-listpack-size default is -2, which (per the negative-size table) caps each quicklist node's listpack at 8 KB rather than an element count.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read createIntConfig default -2 from src/config.c at 8.8.0; -2=>8KB mapping documented in redis.conf and quicklist.c.

verification.corrected_value: list-max-listpack-size default -2 = 8 KB (8192 bytes) per quicklist node
verification.note: config.c#L3271: list-max-listpack-size default -2 (alias list-max-ziplist-size). quicklist.c#L49 optimization_level[] = {4096, 8192, 16384, 32768, 65536}; for fill=-2, offset = -fill-1 = 1 -> optimization_level[1] = 8192 = 8 KB. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: A quicklistNode is a 32-byte struct (prev/next pointers, listpack ptr, sz, count, and bitfields encoding/container:2/recompress:1/attempted_compress:1); interior nodes may be LZF-compressed (container PLAIN=1 vs PACKED=2).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read the quicklistNode struct and its '32 byte struct' comment directly from src/quicklist.h at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: intset is a header of uint32 encoding + uint32 length followed by a sorted contents[] array of int16/int32/int64 (encoding upgrades in place); binary search lookup, O(n) insert.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read the intset struct (uint32_t encoding; uint32_t length; int8_t contents[]) directly from src/intset.h at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: Streams index entries by a radix tree (rax); raxNode header is a 32-bit bitfield iskey:1, isnull:1, iscompr:1, size:29 followed by inline child characters and child pointers, with path compression for single-child chains.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read the raxNode struct and layout comments directly from src/rax.h at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: HyperLogLog uses HLL_P=14 giving HLL_REGISTERS=16384 registers of HLL_BITS=6 bits each; dense form size = HLL_HDR_SIZE(16) + (16384*6+7)/8 = 12304 bytes; standard error ~0.81%.
prior_art_claims.value: P=14, 16384 registers x 6 bits, dense=12304 bytes
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read HLL_P/HLL_REGISTERS/HLL_BITS/HLL_DENSE_SIZE and HLL_HDR_SIZE=sizeof(hllhdr) directly from src/hyperloglog.c at 8.8.0; dense size arithmetic is exact.

verification.corrected_value: HLL_P=14, HLL_REGISTERS=16384, HLL_BITS=6; dense = HLL_HDR_SIZE(16) + (16384*6+7)/8 = 16 + 12288 = 12304 bytes; standard error ~0.81%
verification.note: hyperloglog.c L193 HLL_P=14, L196 HLL_REGISTERS=(1<<14)=16384, L198 HLL_BITS=6, L200 HLL_HDR_SIZE=sizeof(struct hllhdr). hllhdr = magic[4]+encoding(1)+notused[3]+card[8] = 16 bytes. HLL_DENSE_SIZE = 16 + (16384*6+7)/8 = 16 + 12288 = 12304. Standard error: code uses relerr = 1.04/sqrt(HLL_REGISTERS) = 1.04/128 = 0.008125 = 0.81%. All confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: HyperLogLog starts in sparse (run-length opcode) encoding and promotes to dense once the sparse representation would exceed hll-sparse-max-bytes (default 3000 bytes).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read createSizeTConfig hll-sparse-max-bytes default 3000 directly from src/config.c at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: The general hash table (dict) keeps two hash tables (ht_table[2], ht_used[2], ht_size_exp[2]) and a rehashidx; rehashing is incremental, moving buckets a step at a time on each operation while serving lookups from both tables until complete.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read dict struct fields and the rehash logic from src/dict.h and dict.c at 8.8.0; long-standing documented behavior.

verification.corrected_value: two-table incremental rehash: dictEntry **ht_table[2], unsigned long ht_used[2], signed char ht_size_exp[2], long rehashidx (-1 when not rehashing)
verification.note: dict.h: ht_table[2] (L165), ht_used[2] (L166), rehashidx (L168), ht_size_exp[2] (L175); dictIsRehashing(d) = (rehashidx != -1) at L232. Incremental rehash moving buckets a step at a time while serving from both tables is the long-standing design, unchanged. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: dict expands when used/buckets reaches dict_force_resize_ratio (=4) and shrinks when used/buckets falls below 1/(HASHTABLE_MIN_FILL * dict_force_resize_ratio) = 1/32; base load-factor expansion target is 1.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read 'static unsigned int dict_force_resize_ratio = 4' and the 1/32 shrink-threshold comment directly from src/dict.c at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis 8.x dict.c uses pointer tagging on bucket entries to store a key (kvobj) directly in the bucket without allocating a dictEntry when a bucket holds a single key, distinguishing direct-key pointers from dictEntry pointers via 8-byte-alignment bit tags.
prior_art_claims.value: no dictEntry allocation for single-key buckets (pointer tagging)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read the dict.h header-comment describing pointer tagging at 8.8.0; exact bit-tag scheme summarized from comment rather than tracing every code path.

verification.corrected_value: single-key buckets store the key (kvobj) directly with no dictEntry allocation; ENTRY_PTR_MASK=7 (low 3 bits, 8-byte alignment) distinguishes ENTRY_PTR_NORMAL(0)=dictEntry from tagged keys (ENTRY_PTR_IS_ODD_KEY=1, ENTRY_PTR_IS_EVEN_KEY=2)
verification.note: Upgraded from claim's medium to confirmed. dict.c#L129-133 defines ENTRY_PTR_MASK=7, ENTRY_PTR_NORMAL=0, ENTRY_PTR_IS_ODD_KEY=1, ENTRY_PTR_IS_EVEN_KEY=2. entryIsKey()/entryIsNormal() (L137-144), encodeMaskedPtr/decodeMaskedPtr (L155-161) use the low-bit tags on 8-byte-aligned pointers. dictGetKey returns the de pointer directly when it is a tagged key, avoiding a dictEntry allocation for single-key buckets. The 3-bit mask corresponds to the 8-byte-alignment tagging the claim describes.

mechanisms.name: Embedded-key / embedded-value single allocation (kvobj + embstr)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Collapses key SDS, value object header, and small value buffer into a single malloc by embedding the key into the value object (kvobj) and embedding strings <=44B into the robj (embstr), and uses dict bucket pointer-tagging to skip dictEntry allocation for single-key buckets.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the single largest per-key memory win available and directly serves IronCache's 'minimal memory' goal. Fewer allocations also means fewer cache misses and less allocator metadata overhead. IronCache should make a one-allocation-per-key layout the default from day one rather than retrofitting it as Redis had to.

mechanisms.name: SDS with multiple header sizes (sdshdr5/8/16/32/64)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Length-prefixed byte strings whose header (len+alloc fields) scales from 1 byte (sdshdr5) to 17 bytes (sdshdr64) based on string length, keeping small-string overhead minimal while supporting large strings and O(1) length.
mechanisms.rationale: The size-class header idea is excellent and worth keeping, but in Rust we'd implement it as a custom unsized type / NonNull<u8> with a variable-width prefix rather than C packed structs. Drop the legacy SDS_TYPE_5 path complexity; keep the embedded-vs-raw split tied to value-inlining.

mechanisms.name: listpack universal compact container
mechanisms.what_it_does: A single contiguous, allocation-friendly blob with a 6-byte header and self-describing variable-length entries (with back-length for reverse iteration) used as the small-collection encoding for hashes, sets, sorted sets, quicklist nodes, and stream entry groups.
mechanisms.rationale: One cache-friendly contiguous container shared across all small collection types is exactly the right primitive for max-throughput-per-core and low memory. IronCache should adopt a listpack-equivalent (fixing ziplist's O(n) cascade-update flaw, which listpack already largely addressed) as its universal compact encoding.

mechanisms.name: Fixed scalar encoding-conversion thresholds
mechanisms.what_it_does: Hard-coded per-type integer limits (128/512 entries, 64-byte values) that trigger a one-way promotion from compact encoding to the general hashtable/skiplist representation.
mechanisms.rationale: The mechanism (promote-on-threshold) is sound and must stay, but the magic constants are crude and workload-blind, and conversions are one-way (no demotion when a collection shrinks). IronCache should keep promotion but consider self-tuning thresholds (size/access-pattern aware) and optional demotion, aligning with the 'AI-driven innovation' goal.

mechanisms.name: quicklist (linked list of LZF-compressed listpacks)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Represents lists as a doubly linked list of 32-byte nodes each holding a listpack (default 8KB), with interior nodes optionally LZF-compressed (list-compress-depth) for cold middle data.
mechanisms.rationale: Good for huge lists and a nice compression hook, but the 32-byte node + pointer chasing hurts cache locality for medium lists, and LZF is dated. IronCache should keep the chunked-listpack idea but evaluate a more cache-friendly chunk index (e.g. a small B-tree/rope of chunks) and a modern codec (LZ4/zstd) for cold chunks, tying into the 'smart compression' goal.

mechanisms.name: intset (sorted packed integer array)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Stores all-integer sets as a sorted contiguous int16/int32/int64 array (encoding upgrades in place) with binary-search membership; extremely compact, no per-element pointers.
mechanisms.rationale: Near-optimal memory and cache behavior for integer sets, a very common workload (IDs). O(n) insert is acceptable up to the 512-entry cap. IronCache should keep this verbatim and possibly extend the cap or add a delta/bitset variant for dense integer ranges.

mechanisms.name: incremental two-table rehashing
mechanisms.what_it_does: Maintains two hash tables during growth/shrink and migrates a bounded number of buckets per operation (amortized), reading from both tables until migration completes, avoiding a stop-the-world rehash.
mechanisms.rationale: Essential for bounded tail latency in a single-threaded-per-core event loop; a monolithic rehash would cause multi-ms stalls on large keyspaces. IronCache must design its hashtable around incremental migration (or a concurrent/lock-free scheme) from the outset.

mechanisms.name: shared integer object pool (10000 objects)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Pre-allocates and reuses immutable robj instances for integers 0-9999 to avoid per-value allocation for common small integers; disabled under LRU/LFU maxmemory eviction.
mechanisms.rationale: The interning idea saves allocations for hot small integers, but Redis's 16-byte shared robj and the eviction incompatibility are baggage. In IronCache, if values are already inlined into the key object (no separate value allocation), interning yields little; better to inline small integers directly in the value slot and skip a shared pool entirely.

mechanisms.name: rax (radix tree) stream index
mechanisms.what_it_does: Path-compressed radix tree keyed on 128-bit stream IDs whose leaves point to listpacks of entries, giving ordered range scans and compact storage of sorted IDs.
mechanisms.rationale: A solid structure for ordered 128-bit-key indexes, but streams are a lower priority for an efficiency-first cache. IronCache should treat stream support as optional/later (non-goal for M0-M2) and, if built, reuse a generic ordered-map (e.g. a B-tree or ART) rather than a bespoke rax.

mechanisms.name: HyperLogLog dense/sparse dual encoding
mechanisms.what_it_does: Probabilistic cardinality with P=14 (16384 6-bit registers, ~0.81% error); starts in a run-length sparse encoding and promotes to a 12304-byte dense array past hll-sparse-max-bytes (3000).
mechanisms.rationale: Memory-efficient cardinality estimation with a clean sparse->dense ladder is exactly the kind of compact probabilistic structure that fits IronCache's efficiency thesis; the dual-encoding pattern is reusable. Keep P=14 default for wire compatibility but allow configurable precision.

ironcache_implications: Make single-allocation-per-key the default object layout: embed the key bytes, the small value, and per-key metadata (TTL, eviction bits) into one allocation, matching/exceeding Redis 8's kvobj + embstr + dict pointer-tagging. This is where the 'minimal memory' claim is won; target <16 bytes of fixed overhead per small key.

Adopt a listpack-equivalent as the single universal compact container for hashes/sets/zsets and list chunks, but fix ziplist's historical cascade-update pathology up front and design entry encoding for SIMD-friendly scans to maximize throughput-per-core.

Design the main hashtable around incremental rehashing (or a concurrent/lock-free resize) from the start; never allow a stop-the-world rehash. Specify the per-op migration budget as a tunable and verify p99.9 latency under continuous resize in benchmarks.

Replace Redis's fixed scalar conversion thresholds (128/512/64) with self-tuning, workload-aware promotion (and optional demotion) policy, surfaced as the 'AI-driven innovation' angle; ship Redis-compatible defaults but allow an adaptive mode.

Keep intset and a HyperLogLog sparse/dense ladder verbatim for wire compatibility and because they are already near-optimal; consider a dense-integer-range bitset variant and configurable HLL precision as efficiency extensions.
Inline small integers directly into the value slot rather than maintaining a 10000-entry shared-integer pool; with inlined values the pool saves little and adds eviction-policy complexity.

Modernize the cold-data compression hook: where Redis quicklist uses LZF on interior nodes, evaluate LZ4/zstd dictionary compression on cold chunks/values to serve the 'smart compression' goal, with a per-chunk compressed/uncompressed flag like quicklistNode.recompress.

Treat Streams/rax as an optional, post-prototype feature (non-goal for M0-M2); if implemented later, reuse a generic ordered map (B-tree or ART) instead of a bespoke radix tree to reduce code surface in a single static binary.

Document an explicit per-key and per-encoding memory model (header bytes + container bytes + allocator rounding) in the README/vision, with measured numbers, since per-key overhead is the headline metric IronCache competes on against Redis/Valkey/Dragonfly.

research_papers.title: HyperLogLog: the analysis of a near-optimal cardinality estimation algorithm
research_papers.key_idea: Cardinality estimation using m registers each tracking the max leading-zero count of hashed values, with a harmonic-mean estimator giving ~1.04/sqrt(m) relative error.
research_papers.relevance: Directly underpins Redis's HLL (P=14 -> m=16384 -> ~0.81% error). Defines the math IronCache must match for OSS-compatible PFADD/PFCOUNT semantics.

research_papers.title: HyperLogLog in Practice: Algorithmic Engineering of a State of The Art Cardinality Estimation Algorithm
research_papers.key_idea: Sparse representation, bias correction for small cardinalities, and 64-bit hashing to remove large-range correction; the basis for HLL++.
research_papers.relevance: Justifies Redis's sparse encoding and informs whether IronCache should adopt HLL++ improvements (bias correction, sparse->dense ladder) for better accuracy at low cardinality.

research_papers.title: Adaptive Radix Tree: ARTful Indexing for Main-Memory Databases
research_papers.key_idea: A radix tree with adaptive node sizes (Node4/16/48/256) and path compression that is both space-efficient and cache-friendly for in-memory ordered indexes.
research_papers.relevance: A concrete, more cache-efficient alternative to Redis's rax for any ordered-key index (streams, sorted structures) IronCache might build.

research_papers.title: Cuckoo Filter: Practically Better Than Bloom
research_papers.key_idea: A compact approximate-membership structure supporting deletion with better space efficiency than Bloom filters at low false-positive rates.
research_papers.relevance: Informs compact probabilistic set membership beyond Redis's intset/HLL; a candidate efficiency extension for IronCache's probabilistic data types.

research_papers.title: Scaling Memcache at Facebook
research_papers.key_idea: Production lessons on slab allocation, memory fragmentation, and per-item overhead at cache scale; the cost of per-item metadata dominates small-value workloads.
research_papers.relevance: Reinforces that per-key/per-item overhead and allocator behavior, not algorithmic cleverness, decide real-world cache memory efficiency — IronCache's core thesis.

open_questions: What is IronCache's exact target per-key fixed overhead (bytes) for a small string value, and how does it compare to measured Redis 8.x kvobj (~ header + embedded key SDS + embedded value) and Valkey?
Should the universal compact container be a listpack clone (byte-packed, reverse back-length) or a redesigned layout optimized for SIMD scanning and Rust's ownership model?
Will IronCache use jemalloc/mimalloc-style slab classes (like Redis's libc/jemalloc reliance) and how does allocator rounding interact with the single-allocation key layout to avoid hidden waste?
Should encoding conversions be one-way (Redis behavior, simpler) or support demotion when collections shrink, and what hysteresis avoids thrash near thresholds?

For incremental rehashing under a thread-per-core architecture, is a two-table amortized migration (Redis dict) sufficient, or should IronCache use a concurrent/lock-free hash table (e.g. open-addressing with epoch-based resize)?
Which probabilistic structures are in scope for M2 (HLL only, or also Cuckoo/Bloom/Top-K/t-digest) given the single-static-binary size budget?
How should IronCache report OBJECT ENCODING for Redis-wire compatibility when its internal encodings differ (e.g. expose synthetic 'listpack'/'hashtable' names)?
Is Streams (and thus a rax/ART index) in scope at all for the first releases, or an explicit non-goal?

proposed_issues.title: Decide IronCache's per-key object layout (embedded key + inlined small value, single allocation)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a one-allocation-per-key object layout that embeds the key bytes, small values, and per-key metadata (TTL, eviction bits), matching or beating Redis 8.x kvobj + embstr and dict bucket pointer-tagging. Set an explicit byte-overhead target for small keys.

proposed_issues.title: Design the universal compact collection container (listpack-equivalent)
proposed_issues.summary: Define a single contiguous, cache/SIMD-friendly compact container used by hashes, sets, zsets, and list chunks, with a small header and self-describing entries, avoiding ziplist's cascade-update pathology.

proposed_issues.title: Specify the main hashtable with incremental (non-stop-the-world) rehashing
proposed_issues.summary: Adopt incremental two-table or concurrent resize so growth/shrink never stalls the event loop; define per-operation migration budget and load-factor expand/shrink thresholds (Redis baseline: expand at 4, shrink below 1/32).

proposed_issues.title: Research: adaptive vs fixed encoding-conversion thresholds
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate replacing Redis's fixed thresholds (hash/zset/set-max-listpack-entries 128/512, value 64; set-max-intset-entries 512; list-max-listpack-size -2) with workload-aware self-tuning promotion/demotion, including hysteresis to avoid thrash.

proposed_issues.title: Decide string size-class header scheme (SDS-style variable headers in Rust)
proposed_issues.summary: Choose a variable-width length-prefixed string representation (analogous to sdshdr5/8/16/32/64) and the embstr<=44B inline-vs-raw split, implemented idiomatically in Rust without the legacy SDS_TYPE_5 complexity.

proposed_issues.title: Adopt intset and HyperLogLog sparse/dense encodings for wire compatibility
proposed_issues.summary: Implement an intset-equivalent for all-integer sets (sorted packed array, in-place width upgrade, 512 cap) and an HLL with P=14/16384 6-bit registers plus a sparse->dense ladder (promote past ~3000 bytes) for PFADD/PFCOUNT compatibility.

proposed_issues.title: Define cold-data compression strategy (modern codec vs Redis LZF)
proposed_issues.summary: Compare LZ4/zstd (with optional shared dictionaries) against Redis quicklist's LZF for compressing cold chunks/values, including a per-chunk compressed/recompress flag, to serve the 'smart compression' goal without hurting hot-path throughput.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal: Streams / radix-tree index in initial releases
proposed_issues.summary: Declare Redis Streams (and a bespoke rax index) out of scope for M0-M2; if pursued later, reuse a generic ordered map (B-tree or Adaptive Radix Tree) rather than a custom radix tree to limit binary size and code surface.

proposed_issues.title: Define OBJECT ENCODING compatibility mapping
proposed_issues.summary: Specify how IronCache reports OBJECT ENCODING (and DEBUG OBJECT) for Redis-wire compatibility when internal encodings differ, exposing synthetic names like 'embstr', 'int', 'listpack', 'intset', 'hashtable', 'skiplist', 'quicklist'.

proposed_issues.title: Establish a measured per-encoding memory model and benchmark harness
proposed_issues.summary: Build a harness that measures real per-key and per-encoding memory (header + container + allocator rounding) versus Redis 8.x/Valkey/Dragonfly, so the 'minimal memory' claim is backed by reproducible numbers in the README and vision EPIC.

dimensions.verify_notes: Methodology: cloned redis at the exact cited tags (7.4.0 and 8.8.0) via git, inspected source directly, and cross-checked with independent raw.githubusercontent.com fetches for object.h/server.h/object.c. IMPORTANT CONTEXT CHECK: Redis 8.8.0 is a REAL, existing release tag (verified via `git ls-remote --tags`; 8.x line goes 8.0 -> 8.2 -> 8.4 -> 8.6 -> 8.8), so the unusual version number is legitimate and all claims anchor to actual source. ALL 13 CLAIMS CONFIRMED. Two minor notes: (1) claim redis-robj-header-16-bytes-classic cites server.h#L896 but the struct actually begins at L903 in the 7.4.0 tree — the described field layout is exactly correct, so confirmed with a line-number note. (2) claim redis-dict-bucket-pointer-tagging was originally medium confidence but is strongly supported by dict.c#L129-181 — upgraded to confirmed. The 8.8.0 kvobj redesign, dictEntry-less single-key buckets via pointer tagging, embstr=44 always SDS_TYPE_8, and HLL dense=12304 / 0.81% error math all check out against primary source.

dimensions.summary:
Redis offers two persistence engines and a spectrum of durability that is weaker than most users assume. RDB is a point-in-time binary snapshot produced by fork()+copy-on-write: BGSAVE forks a child that walks the (frozen-at-fork) heap and writes a compressed RDB to a temp file, then atomically renames it. Save points are time/change thresholds; the documented default (when uncommented) is `save 3600 1 300 100 60 10000` (snapshot after 3600s if >=1 change, 300s if >=100, 60s if >=10000). The headline weakness is the COW memory-blowup: every 4KB page the parent mutates during the snapshot is duplicated, so under a fully-write-heavy workload RSS can nearly double; operators are told to keep ~50-100% headroom. fork() itself is O(heap) because the kernel copies page tables — Redis's own docs cite ~9-13 ms/GB on physical Linux, but up to 239 ms/GB on old Xen EC2, meaning fork is a multi-hundred-millisecond stall on a large heap and the single biggest source of Redis tail latency.

AOF is a write-ahead command log replayed on restart. `appendfsync` has three modes — `always` (fsync every write, slowest, ~single-write durability), `everysec` (the default; a background thread fsyncs once per second), and `no` (rely on the kernel's ~30s writeback). The honest durability story is the gap: `everysec` is widely described as "lose at most 1 second," but the source's flushAppendOnlyFile logic postpones writes for up to 2 seconds when a background fsync is still in flight on a busy disk, so the real worst case is ~2 seconds of loss. `always` fsyncs per event-loop iteration (per batch of commands), not literally per command, and still cannot survive a power loss between the write() and the fsync on some filesystems. AOF rewrite (BGREWRITEAOF, also fork-based) compacts the log; auto-triggers at `auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100` once past `auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb`.

The two most architecturally interesting modern features are (1) the hybrid RDB-preamble / multi-part AOF, and (2) diskless replication. Since 4.0, `aof-use-rdb-preamble yes` lets an AOF rewrite emit a compact RDB image as its prefix followed by an incremental command tail — fast load + ongoing durability. Redis 7.0 generalized this into multi-part AOF: instead of one file there is a directory (`appenddirname appendonlydir`) holding at most one BASE file (RDB or AOF format), one or more INCR files, and HISTORY files, all tracked by a plaintext manifest (`file <name> seq <n> type <b|i|h>`). This made AOFRW crash-safe (atomic manifest swap instead of in-place buffer copying) and decoupled the base snapshot from the live append stream.

Diskless replication (`repl-diskless-sync yes`, default since 7.0) has the fork child stream the RDB straight to replica sockets, skipping the master's disk entirely; `repl-diskless-sync-delay 5` batches multiple replicas into one fork. On the replica, `repl-diskless-load` defaults to `disabled` (spool to disk first) because the two non-default modes are dangerous: `swapdb` doubles memory by holding old+new datasets, and `on-empty-db` only diskless-loads into an empty instance. WAIT (since 3.0) and WAITAOF (since 7.2) expose tunable durability per-command, but Redis explicitly documents that WAIT "does not make Redis a strongly consistent store" — it confirms in-memory replica acks, not disk, and acked writes can still be lost on failover.

For IronCache the lessons are sharp. fork()+COW is the root cause of Redis's worst latency and memory pathologies (compounded catastrophically by Transparent Huge Pages, where a single byte write copies a whole 2MB page — hence Redis's hard recommendation to set THP to `never`). A from-scratch Rust cache should reject fork-based snapshotting outright in favor of a thread-based, bounded-memory snapshot (e.g. copy-on-write at the slab/page level under our own control, or an LSM/segment log) so snapshot memory overhead is configurable and predictable rather than "up to 2x and hope you set vm.overcommit_memory=1." We should borrow the multi-part-AOF manifest design (atomic, crash-safe, base+incremental) and the per-command durability knob (WAITAOF-style), but make the durability semantics explicit and honest in our README rather than burying the 2-second everysec caveat. Diskless replication is worth adapting (stream snapshot over the socket) but the replica-side memory-doubling trap must be designed out from day one.

Defaults verified against redis.conf at tags 7.4.0 and 8.8.0 (identical); behavior comments cross-checked against src/aof.c and the official persistence/latency/replication docs.

prior_art_claims.claim: The default RDB save points (when the save directive is uncommented) snapshot after 3600s if >=1 change, 300s if >=100 changes, 60s if >=10000 changes.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read verbatim from redis.conf at tag 8.8.0 and identical at 7.4.0.

verification.note: Verified at tag 8.8.0 (tag exists in redis/redis). Line 462: '# save 3600 1 300 100 60 10000' (commented out by default; uncommenting yields the three save points: 3600s/>=1, 300s/>=100, 60s/>=10000). The claim correctly notes 'when the save directive is uncommented'. Exact.

prior_art_claims.claim: The default AOF fsync policy is everysec (a background thread fsyncs the AOF roughly once per second).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Verbatim default in redis.conf; corroborated by persistence docs.

verification.corrected_value: appendfsync everysec
verification.note: redis.conf line 1493: 'appendfsync everysec' (active, not commented). Confirmed at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: AOF persistence is disabled by default; Redis ships with only RDB snapshotting active.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Verbatim default unchanged from 7.4.0 to 8.8.0.

verification.corrected_value: appendonly no
verification.note: redis.conf line 1434: 'appendonly no'. AOF disabled by default; RDB save points are present-but-commented, so out-of-the-box behavior is RDB-on-default-save-points if uncommented. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: With appendfsync everysec, if a background fsync is still in progress Redis postpones the write for up to 2 seconds before forcing it through, so the real worst-case data loss window is ~2 seconds, not the commonly cited 1 second.
prior_art_claims.value: up to ~2 seconds of writes lost (not 1s) on slow/busy disk
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: flushAppendOnlyFile postpone-for-2-seconds logic read in src/aof.c; this is a well-known nuance.

verification.corrected_value: up to ~2 seconds of writes lost (not 1s) on slow/busy disk
verification.note: Verified in flushAppendOnlyFile() at 8.8.0. aof.c lines 1190-1199: when a background fsync is in progress, the flush is postponed; the comment 'We were already waiting for fsync to finish, but for less than two seconds this is still ok. Postpone again.' guards 'server.mstime - server.aof_flush_postponed_start < 2000' (2000 ms). Once >=2000ms it forces the write through. So worst-case loss window is ~2s. Exact match to source.

prior_art_claims.claim: AOF rewrite auto-triggers when the AOF has grown 100% over its size at the last rewrite, but only once it exceeds a 64mb floor.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Verbatim defaults in redis.conf, unchanged 7.4.0->8.8.0.

verification.corrected_value: auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100; auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb
verification.note: redis.conf lines 1534-1535: 'auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100' and 'auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb' (both active). Confirmed at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: By default an AOF rewrite emits a compact RDB-format image as the file prefix followed by the incremental command tail (hybrid persistence).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Verbatim default in redis.conf; feature introduced in Redis 4.0.

verification.corrected_value: aof-use-rdb-preamble yes
verification.note: redis.conf line 1583: 'aof-use-rdb-preamble yes'. With multi-part AOF (7.0+) this applies to the BASE file format (RDB-format base + incremental command tail). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Since 7.0 the AOF is a directory (appenddirname appendonlydir) of one BASE file, one-or-more INCR files, and HISTORY files, tracked by a manifest with lines 'file <name> seq <n> type <b|i|h>'.
prior_art_claims.value: manifest type markers: b=base, i=incr, h=history; default dir appendonlydir
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Persistence docs + src/aof.c manifest parsing; introduced in 7.0 (PR #9788).

verification.corrected_value: manifest type markers: b=base, i=incr, h=history; default dir appendonlydir
verification.note: Persistence doc confirms multi-part AOF since 7.0.0 (base file + incr files + manifest, in appenddirname dir) but does NOT spell out the manifest line format or type markers. Anchored those specifics to source aof.c: lines 82-84 define keys 'file'/'seq'/'type'; line 202 shows format '"file" [filename] "seq" [sequence] "type" [type]'; line 206 states type is 'b' (base), 'h' (history) or 'i' (incr). redis.conf line 1467: appenddirname "appendonlydir". All elements verified.

prior_art_claims.claim: fork() copies page tables and stalls Redis proportionally to heap size: ~9-13 ms/GB on physical Linux, ~10 ms/GB on modern EC2 HVM, but up to ~239 ms/GB on old Xen-based EC2.
prior_art_claims.value: ~9-13 ms/GB physical; up to 239 ms/GB old Xen EC2
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Specific measured figures quoted verbatim from the official latency doc.

verification.corrected_value: ~9-13 ms/GB physical; up to ~239 ms/GB old Xen EC2
verification.note: Latency doc 'Fork time in different systems': physical Xeon@2.27GHz = 9 ms/GB, physical (unknown HW) = 13.1 ms/GB (so 9-13 ms/GB band is exact); old Xen EC2 = 239.3 ms/GB; new EC2 = 10 ms/GB. Confirmed. Minor: doc labels new instances '(Xen)' too in the table text but describes them as HVM in prose; the per-GB numbers all match.

prior_art_claims.claim: With Transparent Huge Pages enabled, a write to any byte after fork copies an entire 2MB huge page instead of a 4KB page, causing large latency and memory blowup during BGSAVE; Redis recommends disabling THP via 'echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled'.
prior_art_claims.value: COW unit becomes 2MB (vs 4KB); recommendation: THP = never
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Mechanism and exact command quoted from official latency doc.

verification.corrected_value: COW unit becomes 2MB (vs 4KB); recommendation: THP = never
verification.note: Recommendation confirmed verbatim: 'disable transparent huge pages' via 'echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled', and the doc describes a few thousand touched pages causing COW of 'almost the whole process memory' -> big latency and memory usage. Nuance: the literal '2MB vs 4KB' page-size figures are NOT stated on this page; they are a correct mechanistic inference (x86 THP huge page = 2MB, base page = 4KB). The qualitative blow-up and the THP=never recommendation are exact; the 2MB number is sound background, not a doc quote.

prior_art_claims.claim: During a fork-based save, every page the parent mutates is duplicated by COW, so under a write-heavy workload RSS can briefly approach 2x used_memory; operators are advised to keep ~50-100% free memory headroom.
prior_art_claims.value: worst-case ~2x RSS during BGSAVE; plan 50-100% headroom
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Mechanism is documented; the exact 50-100% headroom figure is operational guidance widely repeated rather than a hard config default.

verification.corrected_value: worst-case ~2x RSS during BGSAVE/AOF-rewrite (docs say 'up to 2 times'); '50-100% headroom' is an operator inference, not stated by Redis
verification.note: The cited source (persistence page) does NOT contain this guidance. The correct primary source is the Redis admin page, which states verbatim: 'while saving an RDB file on disk or rewriting the AOF log, Redis can use up to 2 times the memory normally used. The additional memory used is proportional to the number of memory pages modified by writes during the saving process... Make sure to size your memory accordingly.' This fully confirms the ~2x RSS mechanism. However the specific '50-100% headroom' figure is the researcher's paraphrase and is not literally stated by Redis (docs say 'up to 2x'/'size accordingly'). Corrected: right value/mechanism, but better source and headroom phrasing tempered. Original confidence was 'medium', consistent with this.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis requires vm.overcommit_memory=1 to avoid fork() failing during background save when the heap is large relative to free RAM.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Exact sysctl quoted from the admin doc.

prior_art_claims.claim: Diskless replication is enabled by default: the fork child streams the RDB directly to replica sockets without touching the master's disk; repl-diskless-sync-delay batches replicas arriving within 5 seconds into one transfer.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Verbatim defaults in redis.conf; diskless RDB transfer added in 2.8.18.

verification.corrected_value: repl-diskless-sync yes; repl-diskless-sync-delay 5
verification.note: redis.conf line 635: 'repl-diskless-sync yes'; line 647: 'repl-diskless-sync-delay 5'. Diskless replication on by default; 5s delay batches arriving replicas. Confirmed at 8.8.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: Replica-side diskless load defaults to disabled (spool received RDB to disk first); the alternatives swapdb (holds old+new datasets, ~2x memory) and on-empty-db are opt-in due to data-loss/memory risk.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Default and mode comments read verbatim from redis.conf.

verification.corrected_value: repl-diskless-load disabled (modes: disabled|swapdb|on-empty-db)
verification.note: redis.conf line 688: 'repl-diskless-load disabled'. The three documented modes (disabled, swapdb, on-empty-db) match the redis.conf comment block; swapdb holds old+new datasets (~2x memory). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: WAIT confirms in-memory replica acknowledgment, not disk persistence, and the docs explicitly state it does not make Redis a strongly consistent store; synchronously replicated writes can still be lost.
prior_art_claims.value: WAIT numreplicas timeout; in-memory ack only, not CP
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Direct quote from WAIT command docs; available since 3.0.0.

verification.corrected_value: WAIT numreplicas timeout; in-memory ack only, not CP
verification.note: Syntax 'WAIT numreplicas timeout' confirmed. Doc states verbatim: 'WAIT does not make Redis a strongly consistent store' and 'it is possible to still lose a write synchronously replicated to multiple replicas.' WAIT checks replica acknowledgment of a replication-stream offset (in-memory), not disk persistence. Confirmed; available since 3.0.

prior_art_claims.claim: WAITAOF (added 7.2) blocks until a write is fsynced to numlocal local AOFs and numreplicas replica AOFs; numlocal>0 requires appendonly yes.
prior_art_claims.value: WAITAOF numlocal numreplicas timeout; numlocal>0 needs AOF
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Available-since version and argument semantics quoted from WAITAOF docs.

verification.corrected_value: WAITAOF numlocal numreplicas timeout; numlocal>0 needs AOF
verification.note: Doc: 'Available since: Redis Open Source 7.2.0'. Syntax 'WAITAOF numlocal numreplicas timeout' confirmed. Blocks until fsynced to local AOF and/or specified number of replica AOFs. Limitations section states verbatim: 'the numlocal argument cannot be non-zero if the local Redis does not have AOF enabled' (i.e. numlocal>0 requires appendonly yes). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: RDB files are LZF-compressed and CRC64-checksummed by default; a failed BGSAVE blocks writes by default.
prior_art_claims.value: rdbcompression yes; rdbchecksum yes; stop-writes-on-bgsave-error yes
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Verbatim defaults in redis.conf.

mechanisms.name: RDB snapshot via fork()+copy-on-write (BGSAVE)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Forks a child process that inherits a COW view of the heap at fork time and serializes it to a compressed, checksummed RDB file, then atomically renames over the old one. Parent keeps serving; mutated pages are duplicated by the kernel.
mechanisms.rationale: fork()+COW is the single largest source of Redis tail latency (page-table copy is ~9-13 ms/GB, far worse on virtualized hosts) and the COW memory blowup (up to ~2x RSS, catastrophic with THP) is unpredictable and unconfigurable. IronCache should reject process fork entirely and implement snapshotting inside the process with our own bounded, page-granular COW over our slab allocator (or an LSM/segment-log design), giving a configurable memory ceiling and no page-table-copy stall.

mechanisms.name: AOF appendfsync everysec (background fsync thread)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Buffers writes and a background thread fsyncs once per second; if the prior fsync is still running, the main thread postpones the write up to 2 seconds, then forces it through.
mechanisms.rationale: The everysec model (decoupled background fsync) is a good throughput/durability tradeoff and worth keeping, but IronCache should be honest that the worst case is ~2s not 1s, expose the postpone bound as a tunable, and surface a per-write durability metric so users can see how stale the on-disk state is.

mechanisms.name: AOF appendfsync always
mechanisms.what_it_does: fsync per event-loop flush (effectively per batch of commands), giving near-per-write durability at large throughput cost.
mechanisms.rationale: Keep an 'always/strict' mode but implement it as group-commit (coalesce concurrent writers into one fsync) to avoid Redis's throughput collapse; also use fdatasync and consider io_uring batched fsync to amortize the syscall.

mechanisms.name: Multi-part AOF with manifest (base + incr + history, since 7.0)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Splits the AOF into a directory of one BASE snapshot file, one-or-more INCR command-log files, and HISTORY files, coordinated by an atomically-rewritten plaintext manifest, making AOF rewrite crash-safe.
mechanisms.rationale: This is exactly the right shape for a durable log: an atomic manifest pointer over immutable segments, base decoupled from incremental, old segments retired by manifest swap rather than in-place mutation. IronCache should adopt a near-identical segment+manifest layout (it also enables cheap point-in-time recovery and parallel load).

mechanisms.name: RDB-preamble hybrid AOF (aof-use-rdb-preamble)
mechanisms.what_it_does: An AOF rewrite writes a compact RDB image as the file prefix then appends subsequent commands, combining fast load (binary base) with ongoing command-level durability.
mechanisms.rationale: Binary base + textual/structured incremental tail is the standard log-structured recovery pattern and gives fast restart. IronCache should use a compact binary base segment plus an incremental record log, exactly this idea.

mechanisms.name: Diskless replication (repl-diskless-sync) + delayed batching
