mechanisms.what_it_does: The snapshot child streams the RDB directly to replica sockets without writing the master's disk; repl-diskless-sync-delay waits a few seconds to fan one snapshot out to multiple joining replicas.
mechanisms.rationale: Streaming the snapshot over the socket (skip master disk) is excellent for full-sync of a fresh node and worth adopting. Adapt by sourcing the stream from our in-process snapshot (no fork) and by making the multi-replica fan-out a first-class feature with backpressure rather than a fixed time delay.

mechanisms.name: repl-diskless-load swapdb / on-empty-db
mechanisms.what_it_does: Replica loads the incoming RDB straight from the socket; swapdb keeps the old dataset in RAM (doubling memory) until load completes, on-empty-db only does this when empty, disabled spools to disk first.
mechanisms.rationale: The memory-doubling swapdb trap and the abort-on-IO-error fragility are exactly the failure modes a new design should eliminate. IronCache should stream incoming snapshots into a separate arena and switch atomically with a hard, configurable memory cap and graceful abort, never silently doubling RSS.

mechanisms.name: WAIT / WAITAOF per-command durability barriers
mechanisms.what_it_does: WAIT blocks until N replicas ack a write in memory; WAITAOF blocks until the write is fsynced to N local and/or replica AOFs.
mechanisms.rationale: Per-command tunable durability (let the client choose its safety/latency point) is the right primitive and IronCache should expose an equivalent. But we should fix the honesty gap: clearly document that replica-memory-ack (WAIT) is not durability and is not CP.

mechanisms.name: stop-writes-on-bgsave-error (fail-closed on persistence failure)
mechanisms.what_it_does: By default rejects writes if the last background save failed, so users notice persistence is broken instead of silently losing data.
mechanisms.rationale: Fail-closed-on-persistence-failure is a good safety default that surfaces silent durability loss; IronCache should adopt an equivalent guard with a clear error and a metric/alert hook.

mechanisms.name: Transparent Huge Pages disablement requirement
mechanisms.what_it_does: Redis tells operators to set THP to 'never' because post-fork COW on 2MB pages copies far more memory per write than 4KB pages, spiking latency and memory.
mechanisms.rationale: Requiring users to tune a kernel sysctl is an operational footgun and contradicts IronCache's easy-single-binary goal. By not using fork at all, IronCache makes THP irrelevant to snapshotting; if we ever madvise our own arenas we control page size explicitly rather than depending on host THP config.

ironcache_implications: Do not use fork()+COW for snapshotting. Implement an in-process snapshot via our own page/slab-granular copy-on-write (or an immutable LSM/segment log), so snapshot memory overhead is a configurable ceiling rather than 'up to 2x RSS and pray', and there is no page-table-copy stall that scales with heap size.

Because we avoid fork, the entire THP-COW-blowup and vm.overcommit_memory=1 class of operational footguns disappears — a concrete win for the single-binary, zero-tuning setup goal. Document this as a positive differentiator.

Adopt the multi-part-AOF shape directly: a directory of immutable segments (one binary base + N incremental logs) coordinated by an atomically-rewritten manifest. This gives crash-safe compaction, parallel load on restart, and a natural basis for point-in-time recovery.

Use a binary RDB-preamble-style base segment plus a structured incremental record log for fast restart, with CRC checksums per segment (borrow rdbchecksum) and built-in compression on the base (consider zstd/lz4 over Redis's LZF; tie into IronCache's 'smart compression' goal).

Offer three durability tiers like appendfsync but be honest in the README: strict (group-commit fsync per write batch), interval (background fsync every N ms, with the worst-case loss window stated explicitly as up to 2x the interval if disk is slow), and os-buffered. Expose a live 'durable_offset' / 'lag-behind-fsync' metric.
Implement group-commit for the strict tier (coalesce concurrent writers into one fdatasync, batch via io_uring) to avoid Redis's throughput collapse under appendfsync always.

Provide WAITAOF-equivalent per-command durability barriers (block until fsynced locally and/or replicated), but clearly separate 'replicated to memory' from 'durable on disk' in the API and docs to avoid Redis's WAIT confusion.

Adopt diskless full-sync (stream the in-process snapshot over the socket to a joining node), and design the receive path with a separate arena + atomic switch under a hard memory cap, eliminating Redis's swapdb memory-doubling and abort-on-IO-error fragility.
Fail-closed on persistence error by default (equivalent of stop-writes-on-bgsave-error) with a structured error and metric, so silent durability loss is impossible to ignore.

Treat snapshot/compaction as a background task with explicit CPU and IO budgets (rate-limited, akin to rdb-key-save-delay) so persistence never starves the hot path on a single core — directly serving the max-throughput-per-core goal.

research_papers.title: Design and Implementation of Redis 7.0 Multi-Part AOF
research_papers.key_idea: Authoritative walkthrough by the feature's implementers of the base/incr/history segment model and the manifest that replaced in-place AOF rewrite buffer copying, removing AOFRW memory/CPU overhead and making rewrite crash-safe.
research_papers.relevance: Direct blueprint for IronCache's segment+manifest durable log layout; explains the failure modes (in-place rewrite, AOF buffer doubling) we are designing out.

research_papers.title: Don't Stack Your Log On My Log
research_papers.key_idea: Log-structured application writes (like AOF) stacked on log-structured filesystems and flash FTLs cause write amplification and unpredictable latency.
research_papers.relevance: Informs IronCache's AOF/segment fsync and compaction design on SSDs/NVMe — avoid naive append logs that multiply with the FTL's own log.

research_papers.title: The Linux implementation of a log-structured file system / RocksDB LSM background
research_papers.key_idea: LSM/segment-log designs achieve durability and compaction without process fork, using immutable on-disk runs plus an in-memory memtable and a WAL.
research_papers.relevance: Provides the alternative to RDB fork-snapshotting: an in-process, bounded-memory durable structure IronCache can use instead of fork()+COW.

research_papers.title: Redis multi-part AOF implementation PR (#9788)
research_papers.key_idea: The actual code and review thread for the 7.0 multi-part AOF: manifest parsing, atomic manifest temp-file rename, history-file cleanup, and the type-marker scheme.
research_papers.relevance: Primary source for exact on-disk format and atomicity guarantees IronCache should replicate or improve upon.

open_questions: What is IronCache's snapshot mechanism if not fork()? Decide between (a) in-process page-granular COW over our own arena, (b) a versioned/MVCC memtable that can be iterated while writes continue, or (c) a full LSM/segment-log where the snapshot is implicit. Each has different memory-overhead and latency profiles to prototype.

What is the exact worst-case memory overhead ceiling we promise during a snapshot, and how do we enforce it (back-pressure writes vs. throttle the snapshot vs. spill)? Redis's answer is 'unbounded up to ~2x'; ours must be a hard number.
For the strict durability tier, what is the achievable single-core throughput with group-commit fdatasync vs io_uring batched fsync on NVMe, and how does it compare to Redis appendfsync always?

Do we expose Redis-wire-compatible WAIT/WAITAOF semantics for compatibility, and if so how do we map our (potentially stronger) durability model onto commands whose Redis semantics are deliberately weak/non-CP?

How do we make restart load fast on a large dataset — parallel segment load across cores, mmap of the base segment, lazy/on-demand value materialization? Redis single-threaded RDB load is a known cold-start bottleneck.
What checksum/corruption-recovery policy do we adopt (Redis has aof-load-truncated yes and rdbchecksum)? Define behavior on a torn tail write vs. a corrupt base segment.
For replication full-sync, what is the backpressure and memory model on the receiving node so we never hit Redis's swapdb doubling or abort-on-IO-error, and can we make snapshot transfer resumable?
How does persistence interact with smart compression — do we persist compressed values as-is (cheap) or recompress with a stronger codec at base-segment build time (better ratio, more CPU)?

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Reject fork()+COW snapshotting; choose IronCache's in-process snapshot mechanism
proposed_issues.summary: Decide between in-process page-granular COW, MVCC/versioned memtable, or LSM/segment-log for point-in-time snapshots, with the explicit goal of a bounded, configurable memory overhead and no page-table-copy stall. Document why we reject Redis's fork-based BGSAVE.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Bound and enforce snapshot memory overhead (vs Redis's unbounded ~2x RSS COW blowup)
proposed_issues.summary: Quantify the worst-case memory overhead of each candidate snapshot mechanism under write-heavy load and define the enforcement strategy (write back-pressure vs snapshot throttle vs spill) and the hard ceiling we promise.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Segment + atomic manifest durable log (base + incremental + history)
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a Redis-7.0-multi-part-AOF-style on-disk layout: immutable binary base segment, append-only incremental record logs, and an atomically-rewritten manifest, enabling crash-safe compaction and parallel restart load.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Three durability tiers with honest, documented loss windows
proposed_issues.summary: Define strict (group-commit fsync), interval (background fsync every N ms, worst-case loss = up to 2x interval), and os-buffered tiers, plus a live durable_offset/fsync-lag metric — fixing Redis's '1 second' vs real-2-second everysec ambiguity.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Group-commit fdatasync vs io_uring batched fsync throughput on NVMe
proposed_issues.summary: Prototype-measure single-core write throughput for the strict durability tier using coalesced fdatasync and io_uring batched fsync, benchmarked against Redis appendfsync always.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: WAIT/WAITAOF-equivalent per-command durability barriers with honest semantics
proposed_issues.summary: Expose per-command durability/replication barriers, clearly distinguishing 'replicated to memory' from 'fsynced to disk', and document that replica-ack alone is not CP (the gap Redis's WAIT docs warn about).

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Diskless full-sync with bounded-memory atomic dataset switch on the receiver
proposed_issues.summary: Stream the in-process snapshot over the socket to a joining node; design the receive path with a separate arena, atomic switch, hard memory cap, and graceful IO-error abort to avoid Redis's swapdb doubling and abort-on-error fragility.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: Process fork() for persistence and any host THP / vm.overcommit tuning requirement
proposed_issues.summary: State explicitly that IronCache will not use fork()-based snapshotting and will not require operators to disable Transparent Huge Pages or set vm.overcommit_memory=1, in service of the zero-tuning single-binary goal.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Fail-closed-on-persistence-error guard with structured error and metric
proposed_issues.summary: Implement an equivalent of stop-writes-on-bgsave-error: when a snapshot/segment write or fsync fails, reject writes by default with a clear error and emit a metric/alert so silent durability loss is impossible.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Fast parallel restart load of large datasets
proposed_issues.summary: Investigate parallel segment load across cores, mmap of the base segment, and lazy value materialization to beat Redis's single-threaded RDB/AOF cold-start load time on large heaps.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Corruption and torn-write recovery policy for base and incremental segments
proposed_issues.summary: Define checksumming (per-segment CRC) and recovery behavior for a torn incremental tail vs a corrupt base segment, analogous to Redis aof-load-truncated and rdbchecksum but with explicit, testable semantics.

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: IronCache durability and persistence subsystem
proposed_issues.summary: Umbrella epic tracking the snapshot mechanism decision, segment+manifest log, durability tiers, diskless full-sync, and recovery semantics that together replace Redis RDB+AOF with a fork-free, bounded-memory, honestly-documented durability story.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Independently re-verified all 14 claims against fresh primary sources on 2026-06-13. Redis tag 8.8.0 confirmed to exist (git ls-remote redis/redis). All redis.conf-based claims (save points, appendfsync, appendonly, auto-aof-rewrite, aof-use-rdb-preamble, appenddirname, repl-diskless-sync/delay/load) verified line-by-line against the raw redis.conf at tag 8.8.0 -- 9 exact confirmations. The everysec 2-second worst-case verified directly in src/aof.c (flushAppendOnlyFile, '< 2000' ms postpone guard) -- a precise source-code match that refutes the naive '1 second' figure. Multi-part AOF manifest format/type markers (b/i/h) confirmed from aof.c (docs alone were insufficient -- they describe the directory but not the manifest line grammar). Fork latency per-GB numbers and THP recommendation confirmed verbatim from the latency optimization doc. WAIT and WAITAOF semantics/versions confirmed verbatim from command docs.

Two items needed adjustment:
1) redis-cow-rss-doubling: CORRECTED. The cited persistence-page URL does not contain the RSS-doubling/headroom guidance. The authoritative source is the admin page, which states 'Redis can use up to 2 times the memory normally used' during RDB save / AOF rewrite. The ~2x mechanism is fully confirmed, but the specific '50-100% headroom' phrasing is the researcher's inference, not a Redis-stated figure. Source corrected; original 'medium' confidence was appropriate.
2) redis-thp-cow-blowup: CONFIRMED with a caveat -- the literal '2MB vs 4KB' page sizes are not quoted in the cited doc (they are correct x86 THP background); the qualitative COW blow-up and the exact 'echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled' recommendation ARE verbatim in the doc.

Net: 12 confirmed, 1 confirmed-with-caveat (thp 2MB figure is inference), 1 corrected (cow-rss source + headroom phrasing). No refutations; no uncertains. The widely-useful correction is that the everysec data-loss window is genuinely ~2s (source-verified), and the cow-rss guidance lives on the admin page, not the persistence page.

dimensions.summary:
Redis replication is leader-follower and asynchronous. The master streams a byte-ordered command log to replicas, tagged by a (replication ID, offset) pair. On reconnect, a replica sends PSYNC <replid> <offset>; if the requested offset still lives in the master's in-memory replication backlog (a fixed-size circular buffer, compiled default 1mb, repl-backlog-ttl 3600s), the master serves a partial resync, otherwise a full resync (background RDB snapshot + buffered command stream, optionally diskless: repl-diskless-sync yes since 7.0, repl-diskless-sync-delay 5s). PSYNC2 (Redis 4.0) added a secondary replication ID (master_replid2): a promoted replica retains the old master's replid as its secondary, so the old master's other replicas and a restarted node can partial-resync across a failover instead of doing an expensive full sync. Because replication is async, acknowledged writes can be lost on failover. WAIT numreplicas timeout (since 3.0) and WAITAOF numlocal numreplicas timeout (since 7.2) let a client block until N replicas ack receipt (WAIT) or until the write is fsynced to the local/replica AOF (WAITAOF), but Redis explicitly states neither makes it a strongly consistent store — both are best-effort and writes can still be lost during failover or restart. This is the central design tension IronCache must confront: Redis chose availability/latency (async + best-effort durability) over consensus.

Redis Cluster is a shared-nothing, client-routed sharding scheme. The keyspace is statically partitioned into exactly 16384 hash slots; a key maps via HASH_SLOT = CRC16(key) & 0x3FFF using the XMODEM CRC16 (poly 0x1021, init 0x0000). Hash tags ({...}) let related keys co-locate by hashing only the substring between the first { and the first non-empty } so multi-key ops and transactions stay on one slot. Nodes form a full-mesh cluster bus on port = client_port + 10000 (default 16379) speaking a binary gossip protocol for membership, failure detection (PFAIL→FAIL escalation requires a majority of masters to agree within cluster-node-timeout, default 15000ms), and config propagation via monotonic currentEpoch/configEpoch. Clients are routed via -MOVED (permanent: update slot map) and -ASK (transient during migration: send ASKING once). Failover is a Raft-like leader election: a replica with the freshest offset requests FAILOVER_AUTH_REQUEST votes and needs a majority of masters. Defaults that matter: cluster-require-full-coverage yes (any unassigned/down slot makes the whole cluster reject writes), cluster-migration-barrier 1 (auto replica rebalancing to orphaned masters), cluster-replica-validity-factor 10, cluster-allow-reads-when-down no. Sentinel is the non-cluster HA path: 3+ Sentinels monitor a master, a quorum agrees on ODOWN (objectively down) but a majority of all Sentinels must elect a leader to actually fail over; it is eventually consistent with "last failover wins" merge semantics and an inherent acknowledged-write-loss window.

Redis Enterprise's active-active CRDB is the strongest borrowable idea for multi-region. Each region holds a full member CRDB with bidirectional async replication; commands are silently swapped for CRDT implementations so concurrent writes converge without app-side conflict logic. Strings/bitfields use last-write-wins keyed on an OS wall-clock timestamp (the only place OS time arbitrates); numeric counters (INCR/INCRBY) become conflict-free PN-counter-style accumulators (capped at 59 bits to avoid concurrent overflow); sets use an OR-Set / observed-remove (add-wins) policy where a remove can only cancel adds it has already observed. This trades RAM/metadata overhead and per-type semantics for true multi-master availability.

For IronCache's single→multi-node story, the borrowable core is: (1) offset-based async replication with a circular backlog and partial-resync-on-reconnect — cheap, simple, proven; (2) the smart-client + redirection routing model (MOVED/ASK) so the data plane stays dumb and clients cache the slot map; (3) CRC16/16384-slot hashing and hash tags for wire compatibility (Redis clients already speak this). The mechanisms to adapt or reject: 16384 is wire-compatible but a poor partition primitive — IronCache should keep CRC16 slot math for compatibility but layer a cleaner internal sharding/rebalancing engine. The big strategic decision is durability/consistency: Redis deliberately ships async + WAIT-as-best-effort and explicitly disclaims strong consistency. IronCache, if it wants a differentiator, can offer an opt-in Raft-replicated mode per shard (synchronous quorum commit) alongside the fast async default — giving users a real "no acknowledged write loss" tier that Redis OSS structurally cannot. The gossip full-mesh bus scales to ~1000 nodes (Redis's own recommendation) and is a reasonable M2 target but should be a pluggable membership layer.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis Cluster partitions the keyspace into a fixed number of hash slots
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated in the cluster spec and confirmed in src/cluster.c keyHashSlot masking with 0x3FFF (=16383)

verification.corrected_value: 16384 slots (CLUSTER_SLOTS; HASH_SLOT = CRC16(key) & 0x3FFF)
verification.note: Cluster-spec doc states keyspace split into 16384 slots and HASH_SLOT = CRC16(key) mod 16384; '14 out of 16 CRC16 output bits are used (this is why there is a modulo 16384 operation)'. mod 16384 == & 0x3FFF since 16384=2^14, so the claim's bitwise form is equivalent and correct. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Key-to-slot hashing uses the XMODEM variant of CRC16
prior_art_claims.value: CRC16/XMODEM: poly=0x1021, init=0x0000, no input/output reflection, no final XOR; CRC16('123456789')=0x31C3; 14 of 16 bits used
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: CRC parameters quoted verbatim from the cluster spec Appendix A

verification.corrected_value: CRC16/XMODEM: poly=0x1021, init=0x0000, no input/output reflection, no final XOR; CRC16('123456789')=0x31C3; 14 of 16 bits used
verification.note: Cluster-spec doc reference implementation lists Name XMODEM, Width 16, Poly 1021, Init 0000, RefIn False, RefOut False, XorOut 0000, Check 31C3. Independently corroborated: crcmod predefined xmodem and multiple CRC libraries give 0x31C3 for '123456789'. All sub-claims confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Hash tags hash only the substring between the first '{' and the first non-empty '}'
prior_art_claims.value: e.g. {user1000}.following and {user1000}.followers map to the same slot; foo{} hashes whole key; foo{bar}{zap} hashes 'bar'
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Rule and examples in spec; logic confirmed in src/cluster.c keyHashSlot/patternHashSlot

verification.corrected_value: {user1000}.following and {user1000}.followers map to the same slot; foo{} hashes whole key; foo{bar}{zap} hashes 'bar'
verification.note: All three asserted examples match the doc exactly (same-slot for {user1000}.*, foo{} hashes whole key since no chars between braces, foo{bar}{zap} hashes 'bar'). Minor wording nit: the doc's rule is 'between first { and first }' (with the precondition that >=1 chars lie between them) -- e.g. foo{{bar}}zap hashes '{bar'. The claim's phrase 'first non-empty }' is loose phrasing of that same rule; the asserted VALUE/examples are correct, so confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: The cluster gossip/bus port defaults to the client port plus 10000
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated explicitly in cluster spec

verification.corrected_value: client 6379 -> bus 16379 (overridable via cluster-port)
verification.note: Doc: 'This port will be derived by adding 10000 to the data port or it can be specified with the cluster-port config.' Example: client port 6379 with no cluster-port -> bus port 16379. Source code config.c (tag 8.0.0) confirms cluster-port exists with default 0 (meaning derive port+10000). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: cluster-node-timeout default
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read compiled default directly from src/config.c createLongLongConfig line

verification.note: Verified directly in src/config.c at tag 8.0.0, line 3224: createLongLongConfig("cluster-node-timeout", ... , 15000, INTEGER_CONFIG, ...). Default 15000 ms and cited line number both exact.

prior_art_claims.claim: cluster-require-full-coverage default (whole cluster stops accepting writes if any slot is unassigned/uncovered)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createBoolConfig default arg is 1 in src/config.c

verification.corrected_value: yes (1)
verification.note: Verified in src/config.c at tag 8.0.0, line 3090: createBoolConfig("cluster-require-full-coverage", NULL, MODIFIABLE_CONFIG, server.cluster_require_full_coverage, 1, ...). Default 1 (yes) and cited line number both exact.

prior_art_claims.claim: cluster-migration-barrier default (min replicas a master keeps before one migrates to an orphaned master)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createIntConfig default arg is 1 in src/config.c

prior_art_claims.claim: cluster-replica-validity-factor default (replica too stale to fail over if disconnected > factor*node-timeout)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createIntConfig default arg is 10 in src/config.c

prior_art_claims.claim: cluster-allow-reads-when-down default
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createBoolConfig default arg is 0 in src/config.c

prior_art_claims.claim: repl-backlog-size default (circular buffer enabling partial resync)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createLongLongConfig default arg is 1024*1024 with inline comment 'Default: 1mb'

verification.note: Verified in src/config.c at tag 8.0.0, line 3230: createLongLongConfig("repl-backlog-size", ... , 1024*1024, MEMORY_CONFIG, ...) with inline comment /* Default: 1mb */. Default and cited line number both exact.

prior_art_claims.claim: repl-backlog-ttl default (time master keeps backlog after last replica disconnects)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createTimeTConfig default arg is 60*60 with inline comment 'Default: 1 hour'

prior_art_claims.claim: replica-read-only default (replicas reject writes)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: redis.conf 8.0.0 ships 'replica-read-only yes'; docs note default since Redis 2.6

verification.corrected_value: yes
verification.note: Replication doc: 'Since Redis 2.6, replicas support a read-only mode that is enabled by default... Read-only replicas will reject all write commands.' Also corroborated in src/config.c (tag 8.0.0) line 3098: replica-read-only default 1 (server.repl_slave_ro). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: repl-diskless-sync default (master streams RDB over socket without writing to disk)
prior_art_claims.value: yes (default changed from no to yes in Redis 7.0)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: redis.conf 8.0.0 ships 'repl-diskless-sync yes'; was 'no' in 6.x

prior_art_claims.claim: repl-diskless-sync-delay default (wait window to batch multiple replicas into one diskless transfer)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createIntConfig default arg is 5 in src/config.c

prior_art_claims.claim: min-replicas-to-write default (master refuses writes below N connected good replicas)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createIntConfig default arg is 0 in src/config.c (redis.conf example shows 3 commented)

prior_art_claims.claim: min-replicas-max-lag default (seconds within which a replica must have ack'd to count as good)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createIntConfig default arg is 10 in src/config.c

prior_art_claims.claim: repl-ping-replica-period default (master PINGs replicas this often)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: createIntConfig default arg is 10 in src/config.c

prior_art_claims.claim: WAIT numreplicas timeout blocks until N replicas ack receipt but does NOT provide strong consistency
prior_art_claims.value: since 3.0.0; docs: 'it is possible to still lose a write synchronously replicated to multiple replicas'
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Version and consistency caveat quoted from official command page

verification.corrected_value: since 3.0.0; docs: 'it is possible to still lose a write synchronously replicated to multiple replicas'
verification.note: WAIT doc: 'Available since: Redis Open Source 3.0.0'. It blocks until numreplicas ack receipt (or timeout). Doc explicitly: 'WAIT does not make Redis a strongly consistent store' and 'it is possible to still lose a write synchronously replicated to multiple replicas.' All sub-claims confirmed verbatim.

prior_art_claims.claim: WAITAOF numlocal numreplicas timeout blocks until the write is fsynced to local and/or replica AOF; still not strongly consistent
prior_art_claims.value: since 7.2.0; numlocal requires local AOF enabled; cannot run on replicas; docs: 'data can still be lost during a failover or a Redis restart'
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Version, args, and caveat quoted from official command page

verification.corrected_value: since 7.2.0; numlocal requires local AOF enabled; cannot run on replicas; docs: 'data can still be lost during a failover or a Redis restart'
verification.note: WAITAOF doc: 'Available since: Redis Open Source 7.2.0'; blocks until fsynced to local and/or N replica AOFs; 'WAITAOF cannot be used on replica instances, and the numlocal argument cannot be non-zero if the local Redis does not have AOF enabled'; 'WAITAOF does not make Redis a strongly-consistent store... data can still be lost during a failover or a Redis restart.' All sub-claims confirmed verbatim.

prior_art_claims.claim: PSYNC2 (Redis 4.0) maintains a secondary replication ID so a promoted replica and old master's replicas can partial-resync across failover
prior_art_claims.value: INFO exposes master_replid (current) and master_replid2 (previous); secondary replid + offset enables partial resync after promotion
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Replication docs describe two replication IDs and the 4.0 failover partial-resync behavior

verification.corrected_value: INFO exposes master_replid (current) and master_replid2 (previous); secondary replid + offset enables partial resync after promotion
verification.note: Replication doc 'Replication ID explained' describes two replication IDs: a promoted replica sets its secondary ID to its prior main ID and remembers the offset at switch, so other replicas can partial-resync using the old master's replid; master matches both current ID and secondary ID up to a given offset. master_replid/master_replid2 are exposed via INFO replication (standard PSYNC2, Redis 4.0). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Quorum detects failure (ODOWN) but a majority of all Sentinels must elect a leader to authorize failover
prior_art_claims.value: recommend >=3 Sentinels; ODOWN needs >=quorum SDOWN reports; failover needs majority vote; eventually consistent, 'last failover wins'
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Quorum-vs-majority distinction and eventual-consistency statement quoted from Sentinel docs

verification.corrected_value: recommend >=3 Sentinels; ODOWN needs >=quorum SDOWN reports; failover needs majority vote; eventually consistent, 'last failover wins'
verification.note: Sentinel doc: 'You need at least three Sentinel instances'; SDOWN->ODOWN promoted when enough (quorum) Sentinels report; 'the quorum is only used to detect the failure. In order to actually perform a failover, one of the Sentinels need to be elected leader... This only happens with the vote of the majority of the Sentinel processes'; 'eventually consistent system where the merge function is last failover wins'. All sub-claims confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Sentinel default listening port
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated explicitly in Sentinel docs

prior_art_claims.claim: Strings/bitfields converge via last-write-wins using an OS wall-clock timestamp
prior_art_claims.value: LWW keyed on stored OS timestamp; the only place OS time arbitrates conflicts in A-A
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Quoted from A-A strings data-type doc

prior_art_claims.claim: Numeric string counters (INCR/INCRBY/DECR) are conflict-free and accumulate across regions, but are limited to 59 bits
prior_art_claims.value: counters sum all increments across members on sync; capped at 59-bit to avoid concurrent overflow
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 59-bit limit and accumulation semantics quoted from A-A strings doc

prior_art_claims.claim: Sets use an OR-Set / observed-remove policy: on concurrent add vs remove, add wins; a remove only cancels adds it has already observed
prior_art_claims.value: add-wins observed-remove; concurrent adds are unioned
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: OR-Set semantics and example quoted from A-A sets doc

prior_art_claims.claim: Cluster theoretical max is 16384 master nodes but the recommended practical maximum is far lower
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Spec states a recommended max around 1000; exact phrasing is a soft guideline not a hard limit

verification.corrected_value: recommended max ~1000 nodes
verification.note: Cluster-spec doc: upper limit 16384 master nodes, 'however, the suggested max size of nodes is on the order of ~ 1000 nodes.' Both the theoretical max (16384) and the ~1000 practical recommendation are confirmed verbatim. (Original confidence was 'medium'; the doc states it explicitly, so it is well-anchored.)

mechanisms.name: Offset-based async replication with circular backlog
mechanisms.what_it_does: Master assigns a monotonic byte offset to its command stream and keeps a fixed-size in-memory ring buffer (repl-backlog-size, default 1mb). Replicas track (replid, offset) and request only the missed range on reconnect.
mechanisms.rationale: Cheap, simple, and proven; the offset+backlog primitive is exactly what IronCache needs for its default fast async tier. IronCache should make the backlog size adaptive and possibly disk-spillable so partial resync survives longer outages than a 1mb in-RAM ring allows.

mechanisms.name: PSYNC2 secondary replication ID
mechanisms.what_it_does: A promoted replica keeps the old master's replication ID as a secondary; other replicas and a gracefully-restarted node can partial-resync against it instead of a full RDB transfer.
mechanisms.rationale: Avoids thundering-herd full syncs after every failover, which is a major operational pain in large fleets. IronCache should generalize this to a short chain of historical (epoch, offset) checkpoints rather than just one secondary ID.

mechanisms.name: WAIT / WAITAOF best-effort acknowledgement
mechanisms.what_it_does: Client blocks until N replicas ack receipt (WAIT) or fsync to AOF (WAITAOF), returning the count actually achieved; Redis explicitly disclaims strong consistency.
mechanisms.rationale: The client-driven, per-write durability knob is a good API, but 'best-effort that can still lose acknowledged writes' is a weak guarantee. IronCache should keep a WAIT-like command but back it with a real quorum-commit mode so a successful WAIT means the write survives any single-node failover.

mechanisms.name: 16384 fixed hash slots + CRC16/XMODEM + hash tags
mechanisms.what_it_does: Static partitioning of the keyspace into 16384 slots via CRC16(key)&0x3FFF, with {tag} braces to co-locate related keys on one slot.
mechanisms.rationale: Must keep CRC16 slot math and hash-tag parsing for Redis client wire compatibility (existing smart clients hardcode it). But 16384 fixed slots is a mediocre internal partition primitive; IronCache should treat slots as a compatibility surface and run a finer-grained, dynamically rebalanced internal shard map underneath.

mechanisms.name: MOVED / ASK redirection with smart clients
mechanisms.what_it_does: Server returns -MOVED (permanent, update slot map) or -ASK (transient during migration, send ASKING once) to route clients; clients cache CLUSTER SLOTS.
mechanisms.rationale: Keeps the data plane dumb and pushes routing intelligence to clients that already implement it. This is the lowest-friction path to multi-node for a Redis-compatible cache and avoids a proxy hop on the hot path.

mechanisms.name: Full-mesh gossip cluster bus (port+10000)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Every node opens N-1 TCP connections on the bus port (client+10000) and gossips membership, PFAIL/FAIL failure detection, and config epochs.
mechanisms.rationale: Full mesh is fine to ~1000 nodes (Redis's own ceiling) but O(N^2) connections and gossip overhead cap scale. IronCache should keep gossip-based membership but make it a pluggable layer (e.g. SWIM-style) so very large or cloud-managed deployments can swap it.

mechanisms.name: Epoch-based Raft-like failover election
mechanisms.what_it_does: On master FAIL, the replica with the freshest offset increments currentEpoch and requests FAILOVER_AUTH votes; needs a majority of masters; configEpoch resolves conflicting slot ownership.
mechanisms.rationale: The epoch/majority idea is sound but it is an ad-hoc consensus bolted onto an async log, which is why acknowledged writes can still be lost. IronCache should offer a real per-shard Raft group as an opt-in strongly-consistent tier, reusing the freshest-offset election heuristic for leader preference.

mechanisms.name: cluster-require-full-coverage = yes
mechanisms.what_it_does: If any of the 16384 slots is unassigned or its master is down with no failover, the entire cluster rejects writes (and reads unless allow-reads-when-down).
mechanisms.rationale: Defaulting to total unavailability on partial failure is surprising and operationally harsh. IronCache should default to serving the slots it can and returning a typed error only for affected keys (graceful partial availability), with strict mode opt-in.

mechanisms.name: Sentinel quorum-detect + majority-elect HA
mechanisms.what_it_does: 3+ external monitor processes; a quorum agrees a master is ODOWN, but a majority of all Sentinels must elect a leader to fail over; eventually consistent, 'last failover wins'.
mechanisms.rationale: A separate Sentinel fleet is operational overhead that fights IronCache's single-static-binary goal, and its eventual-consistency/last-failover-wins model still loses writes. IronCache should fold HA into the cluster membership/consensus layer of the same binary rather than ship a sidecar process.

mechanisms.name: Active-Active CRDB CRDTs (LWW strings, PN-counters, OR-Sets)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Each region is a full member DB with bidirectional async replication; commands are swapped for CRDT versions so concurrent multi-master writes converge automatically (LWW on wall-clock for strings, accumulating counters capped at 59 bits, add-wins observed-remove for sets).
mechanisms.rationale: This is the best blueprint for multi-region multi-master availability and worth borrowing conceptually, but per-type CRDTs add metadata/RAM overhead that conflicts with IronCache's minimal-memory goal, and wall-clock LWW is clock-skew-sensitive. IronCache should make CRDT multi-master an opt-in per-keyspace mode, prefer Hybrid Logical Clocks over raw OS time, and keep the default mode async non-CRDT for memory efficiency.

ironcache_implications: Ship two durability tiers in one binary: a default fast async-replication mode (offset + ring backlog + partial resync, borrowed from Redis) and an opt-in per-shard Raft quorum-commit mode that makes a successful WAIT-equivalent mean 'survives any single-node failover'. This is a concrete differentiator over Redis OSS, which structurally can only offer best-effort.

Keep CRC16/XMODEM slot math and {hash-tag} parsing exactly (crc16(key)&0x3FFF over 16384 slots) as a wire-compatibility surface so existing Redis smart clients route correctly, but decouple it from the internal physical shard map so IronCache can rebalance at finer granularity and migrate slots without the cluster-wide write freeze.
Adopt MOVED/ASK + smart-client routing as the multi-node data path (no proxy hop), and publish a CLUSTER SLOTS / CLUSTER SHARDS compatible response so off-the-shelf clients work unmodified.

Make the replication backlog adaptive and optionally disk-spillable rather than a fixed 1mb RAM ring, so partial resync survives longer outages and large fleets avoid full-resync storms; generalize PSYNC2's single secondary replid into a short ring of (epoch, offset) checkpoints.
Default to graceful partial availability (serve reachable slots, typed error per affected key) instead of Redis's cluster-require-full-coverage=yes total shutdown; expose strict mode as opt-in.
Fold HA into the same static binary's membership/consensus layer — do NOT require a separate Sentinel-style sidecar fleet; this preserves the easy single-binary setup promise.

Offer CRDT-based active-active as an opt-in per-keyspace mode (PN-counters, OR-Sets, register LWW) for multi-region, but use Hybrid Logical Clocks instead of raw OS wall-clock for LWW, and keep it off by default because per-type CRDT metadata fights the minimal-memory goal.

Use a Raft-like freshest-offset leader-preference heuristic for failover election, but back it with a real consensus group (per-shard) so configEpoch-style split-brain resolution is principled rather than ad hoc.
Treat the gossip/membership protocol as a pluggable module (Redis full-mesh is fine to ~1000 nodes; SWIM or a hub topology may be needed beyond that or in cloud-managed envs).

research_papers.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft)
research_papers.key_idea: A consensus algorithm equivalent to multi-Paxos but decomposed into leader election, log replication, and safety for understandability and practical implementation.
research_papers.relevance: The blueprint for IronCache's opt-in strongly-consistent per-shard replication tier — the thing Redis async replication + WAIT cannot provide. Redis Cluster's epoch-vote failover is a partial, ad-hoc approximation of this.

research_papers.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
research_papers.key_idea: Eventually-consistent zero-hop DHT using consistent hashing, vector clocks for causality, sloppy quorums/hinted handoff, and gossip-based membership and failure detection.
research_papers.relevance: Reference design for the always-available, gossip-membership, eventually-consistent end of IronCache's spectrum; consistent hashing is the alternative to Redis's fixed 16384 slots for internal rebalancing, and vector clocks/HLC inform CRDT causal context.

research_papers.title: Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs)
research_papers.key_idea: Formal foundation for state- and operation-based replicated data types (counters, OR-Sets, LWW registers) that converge without coordination.
research_papers.relevance: The theory behind Redis Enterprise active-active; directly informs IronCache's opt-in multi-master mode (PN-counters, observed-remove sets) and warns about the metadata cost that conflicts with the minimal-memory goal.

research_papers.title: Don't Settle for Eventual: Scalable Causal Consistency for Wide-Area Storage with COPS
research_papers.key_idea: Causal+ consistency at scale using explicit dependency tracking, offering a middle ground stronger than eventual but more available than linearizable.
research_papers.relevance: A candidate consistency model for IronCache's multi-region tier that is stronger than Redis Enterprise's wall-clock LWW yet far cheaper than full consensus; motivates Hybrid Logical Clocks over raw OS time.

open_questions: Can IronCache offer a per-shard Raft (quorum-commit) durable tier without giving up the per-core throughput crown? What is the measured write-latency/throughput delta vs async, and can batching/pipelining the Raft log hide it?

Is wire-level Redis Cluster compatibility (16384 slots, CLUSTER SLOTS/SHARDS, MOVED/ASK) worth the constraint it imposes on the internal shard map, or should IronCache expose Redis routing only as a compatibility shim over a native consistent-hashing ring?
How should slot/shard migration avoid Redis's cluster-wide write-freeze and -TRYAGAIN churn — incremental key streaming with per-key fencing, or snapshot+delta with a brief cutover?

For the CRDT multi-master mode, what is the real per-key metadata overhead (causal context / version vectors), and can it be bounded enough to keep IronCache memory-competitive, or should multi-master be a fundamentally separate engine?
Should HA membership use Redis-style full-mesh gossip, SWIM, or a managed control plane — and what is the node-count crossover where full mesh's O(N^2) connections become the bottleneck?
Should LWW use Hybrid Logical Clocks to bound clock-skew anomalies, and what HLC epsilon is acceptable for a cache where occasional staleness is tolerable?
Where does AI-driven innovation fit: e.g. learned/adaptive backlog sizing, predictive replica placement, or auto-tuning the consistency tier per keyspace based on observed access patterns?

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Default durability/consistency model (async best-effort vs opt-in quorum)
proposed_issues.summary: Decide IronCache's default replication semantics and whether to commit to an opt-in synchronous quorum-commit tier. Redis OSS ships async + best-effort WAIT and explicitly disclaims strong consistency; decide if 'no acknowledged write loss on single-node failover' is a headline differentiator.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Per-shard Raft for an opt-in strongly-consistent tier
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate Raft (ATC'14) as the consensus layer for an optional durable tier, including expected throughput/latency cost vs async and how to batch the log to preserve per-core throughput.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Offset-based async replication with adaptive, disk-spillable backlog
proposed_issues.summary: Specify a (replid, byte-offset) replication stream and a partial-resync-on-reconnect backlog that is adaptive in size and can spill to disk, generalizing Redis's fixed 1mb RAM ring and PSYNC2 secondary-replid to a checkpoint ring.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Redis-Cluster-compatible routing (16384 CRC16 slots, hash tags, MOVED/ASK)
proposed_issues.summary: Define the wire-compatibility surface: CRC16/XMODEM key->slot mapping, {hash-tag} parsing, CLUSTER SLOTS/SHARDS responses, and MOVED/ASK redirection so existing Redis smart clients route unmodified.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Internal shard map decoupled from the 16384 compatibility slots
proposed_issues.summary: Decide whether to run a finer-grained, dynamically-rebalanced internal shard map underneath the fixed 16384-slot compatibility layer, vs binding internal partitioning to Redis slots directly.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Graceful partial availability instead of cluster-require-full-coverage shutdown
proposed_issues.summary: Specify serving reachable slots and returning typed per-key errors for unavailable slots by default, with Redis's all-or-nothing strict mode as opt-in.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Online slot/shard migration without cluster-wide write freeze
proposed_issues.summary: Design incremental, fenced key migration that avoids Redis's migration-time write stalls and -TRYAGAIN churn while preserving correctness for multi-key/hash-tag operations.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Fold HA into the single binary (reject external Sentinel sidecar)
proposed_issues.summary: Decide that failure detection and failover live in IronCache's own membership/consensus layer rather than a separate Sentinel-style process, preserving the single-static-binary setup goal.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Opt-in CRDT active-active multi-master (counters, OR-Sets, HLC LWW)
proposed_issues.summary: Study Redis Enterprise CRDB and CRDT theory to scope an optional per-keyspace multi-master mode; quantify metadata/RAM overhead against the minimal-memory goal and evaluate Hybrid Logical Clocks vs OS wall-clock for LWW.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: Strong consistency / zero write loss in the default async mode
proposed_issues.summary: Explicitly state that the default fast tier, like Redis, is async and best-effort; strong durability is only the opt-in quorum tier. Prevents over-promising in the README/vision.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Membership protocol choice and node-count scaling (full-mesh gossip vs SWIM)
proposed_issues.summary: Compare Redis full-mesh gossip (practical ceiling ~1000 nodes, O(N^2) connections) against SWIM-style or hub topologies and define a pluggable membership interface.

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: Single-node to multi-node IronCache (replication, sharding, HA, multi-region)
proposed_issues.summary: Umbrella epic tracking the path from a single static binary to a clustered, optionally strongly-consistent, optionally multi-master cache, sequencing the replication, routing, HA, and CRDT decisions above.

dimensions.verify_notes:
All 13 claims verified independently against fresh primary sources at the stated version (Redis 8.0.0). Verdicts: 13 confirmed, 0 corrected, 0 refuted, 0 uncertain.

Source-code claims verified by cloning github.com/redis/redis at the exact tag 8.0.0 (src/version.h confirms REDIS_VERSION "8.0.0") and reading src/config.c directly. All three cited line numbers are exact at this tag: cluster-node-timeout=15000 @ L3224, cluster-require-full-coverage=1 @ L3090, repl-backlog-size=1024*1024 @ L3230. Bonus cross-checks from the same file: replica-read-only default 1 @ L3098, cluster-port default 0 (derive port+10000) @ L3189.

Doc claims verified via WebFetch of the cited redis.io pages; every requested quote was located verbatim (WAIT 'lose a write synchronously replicated to multiple replicas'; WAITAOF 'data can still be lost during a failover or a Redis restart' and 'cannot be used on replica instances'; Sentinel 'quorum is only used to detect the failure'/'majority of the Sentinel processes'/'last failover wins'; replication 'reject all write commands' and the secondary-replid explanation).

CRC16/XMODEM independently corroborated beyond the redis doc: crcmod predefined 'xmodem' and multiple third-party CRC libraries return 0x31C3 for input '123456789' with poly=0x1021, init=0x0000, no reflection, no final XOR.

Two minor wording observations (did not change verdicts, since the asserted VALUEs/examples are correct):
1) redis-cluster-hash-slots-16384: claim writes the formula as CRC16(key) & 0x3FFF; the doc writes mod 16384. These are mathematically identical (16384 = 2^14), so confirmed.
2) redis-cluster-hash-tag-rule: claim phrases the rule as 'first { and first non-empty }'. The doc's precise rule is 'first { and first }' with the precondition that >=1 character lies between them (illustrated by foo{{bar}}zap -> '{bar'). The phrasing is slightly loose but the three concrete examples asserted in the claim all match the doc exactly.

dimensions.summary:
"Keeping the Redis contracts" decomposes into three orthogonal surfaces that IronCache must treat separately: (1) the wire serialization (RESP2/RESP3), (2) the request/response interaction model (pipelining, inline commands, MULTI/EXEC, Pub/Sub push semantics, client-side-caching invalidation), and (3) the command surface (~240 core commands across ~20 groups, plus their exact reply types and error string conventions). The RESP3 spec (authored by Salvatore Sanfilippo, accepted, versions May 2018–Mar 2023) is small and stable: 15 base types, each identified by a single leading byte, length-prefixed for binary safety, CRLF-terminated. Critically, RESP3 is opt-in: a connection starts in RESP2 and only upgrades when the client sends HELLO 3; an unknown HELLO yields "-ERR unknown command" and NOPROTO is returned for unsupported versions. This negotiation contract is the single most load-bearing compatibility primitive — getting HELLO and per-connection protocol state right is what lets every unmodified client (redis-py, ioredis, Jedis, go-redis, StackExchange.Redis, redis-cli) connect.

The protocol itself is cheap to implement and cheap to parse. RESP is deliberately length-prefixed rather than delimiter-scanned, so a correct scalar parser is already near-optimal. Kevin Montrose's 2025 experiment building a Vector512/SWAR nearly-branchless RESP parser concluded it did NOT beat Garnet's scalar parser that special-cases common GET/SET shapes — Garnet held a 10–20% advantage across most workloads and the SIMD code was "disgustingly un-maintainable." The lesson for IronCache: the throughput bottleneck is not CRLF scanning but allocation, syscall batching, and command dispatch; invest in zero-copy slicing over the read buffer, a perfect-hash command dispatch table, and io_uring/readv batching rather than SIMD parsing theater. RESP3's streamed types ($?, *?, ~?, %? with a "." terminator) and attributes (|) are rarely used by clients and can be deferred.

The interaction model carries more semantic weight than the bytes. Pipelining is implicit (read N requests, write N replies in order) and is the highest-leverage throughput feature — it must be correct from day one. Inline commands (first byte != '*', space-split) exist mainly for telnet/redis-cli probing and a few health checks; cheap to support, low priority. MULTI/EXEC/DISCARD/WATCH give a queue-then-atomically-apply model with optimistic locking and explicitly NO rollback (runtime errors inside EXEC do not undo prior commands; EXEC returns an array, or null if a WATCHed key changed). This non-ACID semantics is a contract clients depend on — IronCache must replicate the exact behavior including dirty-CAS abort, not "improve" it. Pub/Sub (SUBSCRIBE/PSUBSCRIBE/PUBLISH plus sharded SSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH/SUNSUBSCRIBE, Redis 7.0+) and keyspace notifications (notify-keyspace-events flag string, __keyspace@db__ / __keyevent@db__ channels, off by default for CPU cost) are layered on the same push machinery.

Client-side caching is the most protocol-coupled feature and the one most worth getting right for a "most efficient cache" pitch, because it moves hot reads off the server entirely. CLIENT TRACKING ON [REDIRECT id] [BCAST] [OPTIN|OPTOUT] [NOLOOP] [PREFIX p] drives two designs: default mode keeps a server-side invalidation table (key -> client-id set, O(tracked_keys x clients) memory, evicts by sending spurious invalidations when full) versus BCAST mode (prefix subscriptions, near-zero server memory, non-overlapping prefixes). Invalidations arrive as RESP3 push messages on the same connection, or in RESP2 via a SUBSCRIBE to __redis__:invalidate on a second connection referenced by REDIRECT. The invalidation table is single-namespace across all DBs. This is a strong "borrow" — but IronCache should lead with BCAST + RESP3 push (bounded memory, single connection, race-free) and treat the per-client tracking table as the costly fallback.

Scripting splits into ephemeral EVAL/EVALSHA/SCRIPT LOAD (Lua 5.1, SHA1-keyed, lost on restart/failover, client must re-load) and Redis Functions (Redis 7.0+: FUNCTION LOAD with #!lua name=lib shebang, FCALL/FCALL_RO, register_function, persisted to AOF and replicated, no-writes flag for replicas). Both block the whole server during execution. For IronCache, an embedded Lua engine is a large surface with real semantic risk (KEYS/ARGV contract, redis.call error propagation, RESP-type conversion via redis.setresp). It is the cleanest candidate to TIER LATE or replace with a narrower native-atomic-op story, since most cache workloads never script. ACL/AUTH (Redis 6.0+: default user "on nopass ~* &* +@all", ACL SETUSER with +cmd/@cat/~keypattern/&channel/>password stored as SHA-256, requirepass legacy) and TLS are table-stakes for production but orthogonal to the cache hot path; AUTH/HELLO AUTH and a single-password mode are M1, full ACL categories M2+. Recommended tiering: Tier 0 (RESP2+RESP3 framing, HELLO, PING, AUTH, pipelining, inline, RESET, CLIENT basics) → Tier 1 (the ~80/20 data commands: strings/GET-SET-MSET-INCR/GETEX, expiry TTL/EXPIRE/PERSIST, generic DEL/EXISTS/TYPE/SCAN, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets) → Tier 2 (Pub/Sub + sharded, transactions, client-side caching) → Tier 3 (keyspace notifications, full ACL/TLS, bitmaps/HLL/geo/streams) → Tier 4 / explicit non-goal candidates (Lua scripting/Functions, modules, cluster bus).

prior_art_claims.claim: A connection starts in RESP2 mode and only upgrades to RESP3 when the client sends HELLO 3; this opt-in design keeps RESP2 clients unaffected.
prior_art_claims.value: Default per-connection protocol = RESP2; HELLO 3 required to switch to RESP3
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated directly in the canonical RESP3 specification and corroborated by the protocol-spec docs page.

verification.corrected_value: Default per-connection protocol = RESP2; HELLO 3 required to switch to RESP3
verification.note: RESP3.md spec states 'By default the connection starts in RESP2 mode' and clients send HELLO to upgrade; backward-compatible opt-in. Doc version history May 2018-Mar 2023 matches the stated version. Confirmed at the cited primary source.

prior_art_claims.claim: Each RESP type is identified by a single leading byte: + simple string, - simple error, : integer, $ bulk string, * array (RESP2); _ null, # boolean, , double, ( big number, ! bulk error, = verbatim string, % map, ~ set, | attribute, > push (RESP3).
prior_art_claims.value: 15 type markers; e.g. map=%, set=~, push=>, double=,, null=_, boolean=#, big number=(, verbatim string==, bulk error=!, attribute=|
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read from the official protocol specification; byte markers are unambiguous and stable.

verification.corrected_value: 15 type markers; map=%, set=~, push=>, double=,, null=_, boolean=#, big number=(, verbatim string==, bulk error=!, attribute=|
verification.note: All 15 markers verified verbatim against the protocol spec: RESP2 + - : $ * ; RESP3 _ # , ( ! = % ~ | >. Every byte assignment matches the claim exactly.

prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Explicit in the protocol spec; a common source of client bugs so well documented.

verification.corrected_value:
RESP2 null = $-1
 or *-1
; RESP3 null = _

prior_art_claims.claim: HELLO with an unsupported protocol version returns -NOPROTO; on servers without HELLO it returns -ERR unknown command, and clients fall back to RESP2.

verification.corrected_value: -NOPROTO sorry, this protocol version is not supported.

(Note: cited URL points to protocol-spec rather than the HELLO command page, but the protocol-spec page carries the exact text, so the claim is well anchored.)

prior_art_claims.claim: A core Redis 7.x server reports on the order of ~240-246 commands via COMMAND COUNT (excluding modules); Dragonfly cites ~240 commands as meaningful compatibility.
prior_art_claims.value: ~240-246 core commands (COMMAND COUNT, Redis 7.x, no modules)

verification.note: Cited oneuptime article states COMMAND COUNT = 246 for Redis 7.x core (vs 210 pre-7.x); a second independent search corroborates 246 before loading modules (271 after RedisJSON). The claimed '~240-246' range comfortably contains the verified 246. This is a secondary source (no canonical Redis figure published) so confidence remains medium, but the range is supported.

prior_art_claims.claim: CLIENT TRACKING ON supports REDIRECT client-id, BCAST, OPTIN, OPTOUT, NOLOOP, and PREFIX; default mode tracks per-key per-client server-side, BCAST uses non-overlapping prefix subscriptions with near-zero server memory.

prior_art_claims.claim: In RESP3 invalidation messages arrive as push messages on the same connection; in RESP2 the client subscribes to the __redis__:invalidate Pub/Sub channel on a second connection referenced via REDIRECT. FLUSHALL/FLUSHDB sends a null invalidation. The invalidation table is a single key namespace across all DBs.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis transactions queue commands at MULTI and apply them atomically at EXEC, but there is NO rollback: a runtime error on one queued command does not undo others; EXEC returns an array of per-command replies, or null if a WATCHed key was modified.
prior_art_claims.value: No rollback on runtime errors; EXEC returns array or null on WATCH abort
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Clearly documented transaction semantics; a deliberate, well-known Redis design contract.

verification.corrected_value: No rollback on runtime errors; EXEC returns array or null on WATCH abort
verification.note: Transactions doc confirms Redis does not support rollback even when a command fails during execution; EXEC returns an array of per-command replies, or null/nil if a WATCHed key was modified. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Sharded Pub/Sub (SSUBSCRIBE/SSUBSCRIBE/SUNSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH) was introduced in Redis 7.0; in Cluster mode sharded messages stay on the owning shard instead of being broadcast across the cluster bus.

Confirmed. (Note: claim text duplicates SSUBSCRIBE where it likely meant to list SSUBSCRIBE/SUNSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH; the three actual commands are correct.)

prior_art_claims.claim: Keyspace notifications are disabled by default (CPU cost) and enabled via notify-keyspace-events flags (K keyspace, E keyevent, g generic, $ string, l list, s set, h hash, z zset, x expired, e evicted, t stream, m key-miss, n new, A=all), published to __keyspace@<db>__:<key> and __keyevent@<db>__:<event> Pub/Sub channels.
