prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Valkey is a direct fork; protocol identity is its explicit design goal and widely corroborated.

mechanisms.name: HELLO-based per-connection protocol negotiation
mechanisms.what_it_does: Connection starts in RESP2; client sends HELLO [3] [AUTH user pass] to negotiate RESP3 and receive a server-info map. Unknown HELLO -> -ERR unknown command (fallback RESP2); unsupported version -> -NOPROTO.
mechanisms.rationale: This is the load-bearing compatibility primitive that lets every unmodified client connect. IronCache must implement HELLO exactly, track protocol per-connection, and return a correct server-info map (server name, version, proto, id, mode, role).

mechanisms.name: Length-prefixed binary-safe framing (single-byte type markers, CRLF terminators)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Every value is a type-byte + length-prefixed/CRLF-terminated payload, avoiding delimiter scanning of payload bytes.
mechanisms.rationale: Cheap, unambiguous, and already near-optimal to parse. Enables zero-copy slicing over the socket read buffer; the framing format is fixed by the contract and not something to redesign.

mechanisms.name: SIMD / Vector512 / SWAR nearly-branchless RESP request parser
mechanisms.what_it_does: Uses vectorized digit-position bitmaps, SWAR integer parsing, and perfect-hash command identification to parse RESP with minimal branches.
mechanisms.rationale: Montrose's own benchmarks show it LOSES to Garnet's scalar parser that special-cases GET/SET (Garnet ~10-20% faster) and the code is 'disgustingly un-maintainable.' For IronCache the real wins are allocation avoidance, syscall batching (io_uring/readv), and dispatch — not SIMD CRLF scanning. Keep a clean scalar parser with hot-path special-casing instead.

mechanisms.name: Implicit pipelining (read N requests, reply N in order)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Client streams multiple commands without waiting; server replies in the same order, amortizing RTT.
mechanisms.rationale: Highest-leverage throughput feature and a hard client expectation. Must be correct from M0/M1; pairs naturally with batched writev/io_uring submission.

mechanisms.name: Inline commands (first byte != '*', space-split args)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Accepts plain text commands for telnet/redis-cli probing without RESP array framing.
mechanisms.rationale: Trivial to support and used by redis-cli/health checks, but binary-unsafe and single-command. Implement a minimal parser (PING, simple GET/SET) and document it as a probing convenience, not a primary path.

mechanisms.name: MULTI/EXEC/DISCARD/WATCH optimistic transactions with no rollback
mechanisms.what_it_does: Queue commands, then apply atomically at EXEC; WATCH provides dirty-CAS abort (EXEC -> null). Runtime errors do not roll back already-applied commands.
mechanisms.rationale: Clients depend on the exact semantics including no-rollback and null-on-WATCH-conflict. Must replicate behavior precisely; the single-threaded-equivalent execution boundary maps cleanly onto a per-shard execution lock in IronCache.

mechanisms.name: Pub/Sub + sharded Pub/Sub over RESP3 push
mechanisms.what_it_does: Channel and pattern subscriptions; sharded variant (7.0+) confines messages to the owning shard in Cluster mode. Messages delivered as RESP3 push (>) or RESP2 multi-bulk.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the command surface and push delivery for compatibility, but Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget with no persistence. For a cache it is Tier 2; sharded Pub/Sub only matters once IronCache is multi-node. Reuse the same push machinery as client-side-caching invalidation.

mechanisms.name: CLIENT TRACKING default mode (server-side per-key invalidation table)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Server records key -> set-of-client-ids for every read; on write sends targeted invalidations. Table is bounded and evicts by sending spurious invalidations when full.
mechanisms.rationale: Powerful but O(tracked_keys x clients) memory — at odds with IronCache's minimal-memory goal. Support for compatibility but make BCAST + RESP3 push the recommended/default path and the tracking table a costly opt-in with a hard memory cap.

mechanisms.name: CLIENT TRACKING BCAST + RESP3 invalidation push
mechanisms.what_it_does: Prefix-based broadcast invalidation with near-zero server memory; non-overlapping prefixes; invalidations as same-connection RESP3 push messages.
mechanisms.rationale: Bounded memory, single race-free connection, directly serves the 'most efficient cache' pitch by moving hot reads to clients. This is the flagship client-side-caching mode IronCache should lead with.

mechanisms.name: RESP2 invalidation via __redis__:invalidate + REDIRECT
mechanisms.what_it_does: RESP2 clients put invalidations on a Pub/Sub channel consumed by a second connection identified by CLIENT TRACKING REDIRECT id; FLUSHALL sends a null invalidation.
mechanisms.rationale: Needed for RESP2-only clients but adds a second-connection race window. Implement for compatibility but steer clients to RESP3 single-connection; reuse the exact __redis__:invalidate channel name and null-on-flush semantics.

mechanisms.name: EVAL/EVALSHA ephemeral Lua scripting + SCRIPT LOAD
mechanisms.what_it_does: Runs SHA1-cached Lua 5.1 scripts atomically with KEYS/ARGV; cache lost on flush/restart/failover.
mechanisms.rationale: Large, semantically risky surface (redis.call error propagation, RESP-type conversion, sandboxing) that most cache workloads never use. Declare a non-goal for the initial milestones; if demand appears, prefer a narrow set of native atomic ops over embedding a full Lua VM.

mechanisms.name: Redis Functions (FUNCTION LOAD / FCALL / libraries, persisted+replicated)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Named persistent Lua libraries replicated with data; FCALL_RO + no-writes flag for replicas.
mechanisms.rationale: Even heavier than EVAL and depends on durable replication semantics IronCache will not have at M0-M2. Non-goal initially; revisit only after core + clustering ship.

mechanisms.name: ACL users/categories/key-and-channel patterns + AUTH/requirepass
mechanisms.what_it_does: Per-user command/category/key-pattern/channel-pattern permissions with SHA-256 passwords; default user 'on nopass ~* &* +@all'; legacy requirepass.
mechanisms.rationale: Production needs AUTH and at least a single-password/default-user model early (M1). Full ACL category and selector machinery (~20 @categories, %R/%W, selectors) is large and orthogonal to the hot path — implement HELLO AUTH + requirepass + a minimal ACL in M1, full ACL in M2+.

mechanisms.name: TLS termination
mechanisms.what_it_does: Optional TLS on the listener for encrypted client connections.
mechanisms.rationale: Table-stakes for production but orthogonal to protocol semantics. As a single static binary, IronCache should embed rustls behind a config flag rather than relying on stunnel sidecars; can land slightly after plaintext + AUTH.

mechanisms.name: RESP3 streamed strings/aggregates ($?, *?, ~?, %?) and attributes (|)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Unknown-length streamed payloads terminated by '.', plus out-of-band attribute metadata before a reply.
mechanisms.rationale: Rarely emitted by Redis and rarely consumed by clients; a parser must tolerate them but IronCache need not generate streamed types or attributes initially. Defer generation; ensure the parser does not choke if a client sends them.

ironcache_implications: Define an explicit compatibility tiering and publish it in the README so users know exactly what 'Redis-compatible' means: Tier 0 = wire+connection (RESP2/RESP3 framing, HELLO, AUTH, PING, RESET, pipelining, inline, CLIENT ID/SETNAME/GETNAME); Tier 1 = the 80/20 data surface (strings GET/SET/SETEX/SETNX/GETEX/MSET/MGET/INCR/DECR/APPEND/STRLEN, expiry EXPIRE/PEXPIRE/TTL/PTTL/PERSIST, generic DEL/UNLINK/EXISTS/TYPE/KEYS/SCAN/RENAME/COPY, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets); Tier 2 = Pub/Sub + sharded, MULTI/EXEC/WATCH, CLIENT TRACKING; Tier 3 = keyspace notifications, full ACL, TLS, bitmaps/HLL/geo/streams; Tier 4/non-goals = Lua scripting/Functions, modules, cluster bus.

Treat HELLO + per-connection protocol state as a single-owner state machine. Each connection holds: protocol (2|3), authenticated user, selected DB, subscribed channels/patterns, tracking config, MULTI queue. Get this object right and most compatibility falls out.

Build a perfect-hash (or FST) command dispatch table keyed on the uppercased command token, with hot-path special-casing for GET/SET/MGET/INCR (Garnet's winning strategy) rather than a SIMD parser. Generate the table from a machine-readable command spec so reply-type and arity validation are data-driven.

Make zero-copy the parser default: parse RESP requests as &[u8] slices into the connection read buffer, defer to owned allocation only when a value must outlive the request. Pair with batched writev / io_uring submission for replies — this is where pipelining throughput actually comes from.

Lead client-side caching with BCAST + RESP3 same-connection push (bounded prefix memory, race-free) as the documented recommended mode; implement default per-client tracking and the RESP2 __redis__:invalidate+REDIRECT path for compatibility but cap the tracking table memory hard and expose a metric for invalidation-table size.

Reuse one internal 'server push' channel abstraction for all out-of-band delivery: Pub/Sub messages, sharded Pub/Sub, keyspace notifications, and client-side-caching invalidations. They all reduce to RESP3 push (>) on RESP3 connections and multi-bulk on RESP2.

Replicate transaction semantics exactly, including NO rollback and null-on-WATCH-conflict. Model EXEC as 'acquire the shard execution boundary, replay queued commands, collect per-command replies into an array.' Do not 'improve' the contract — clients rely on it.

Ship AUTH + requirepass + HELLO AUTH and a minimal single-/default-user ACL in M1; defer the full ~20 @category + %R/%W + selector machinery to M2. Store passwords as SHA-256 to match ACL GETUSER output expectations.
Embed TLS (rustls) behind a config flag to honor the single-static-binary goal, rather than requiring an stunnel/envoy sidecar; keep it orthogonal to the protocol layer.

Make the parser tolerant of RESP3 features IronCache does not itself emit (attributes |, streamed types $?/*?/~?/%? with '.' terminator, big numbers) so that aggressive clients negotiating RESP3 never trip the connection, even if IronCache never generates those types initially.

Match Redis error-string conventions precisely (leading uppercase token: ERR, WRONGTYPE, NOPROTO, NOAUTH, NOPERM, EXECABORT, MOVED/ASK later) because some clients pattern-match on these prefixes; treat the error catalog as part of the wire contract.

Note that modern official clients (redis-py 8, node-redis 6) now negotiate RESP3 by default, so IronCache's RESP3 path is a first-class, frequently-exercised code path from day one — not an optional extra.

research_papers.title: A (Nearly) Branchless RESP Request Parser
research_papers.key_idea: Builds a Vector512/SWAR nearly-branchless RESP parser (vectorized digit bitmaps, branchless integer parsing, perfect-hash command identification) and benchmarks it against Garnet's scalar parser.
research_papers.relevance: Directly answers IronCache's 'should we SIMD the RESP parser?' question with a negative result: the SIMD/branchless parser was slower than Garnet's scalar GET/SET-special-cased parser and was unmaintainable. Steers IronCache toward dispatch/allocation/syscall optimization instead.

research_papers.title: Introducing Garnet: a faster cache-store (Microsoft Research)
research_papers.key_idea: A RESP-compatible cache-store that adopts the RESP wire protocol so unmodified Redis clients work, and beats peers on throughput/latency via a network/storage architecture rather than protocol tricks.
research_papers.relevance: Demonstrates the exact thesis IronCache is built on — adopt RESP for client compatibility, then win on the engine. Garnet's command-special-casing parser is the performance baseline to match/beat.

research_papers.title: RESP3 Specification
research_papers.key_idea: The canonical, accepted RESP3 protocol spec: 15 types with single-byte markers, opt-in via HELLO to preserve RESP2 compatibility, plus streamed types and attributes.
research_papers.relevance: The authoritative contract IronCache's serialization layer must implement; defines exactly what RESP3 compatibility means and why negotiation must be opt-in.

research_papers.title: Dragonfly: a modern replacement for Redis and Memcached
research_papers.key_idea: A multi-threaded, shared-nothing cache implementing RESP2/RESP3 and ~240 Redis commands, treating ~240 commands as the meaningful-compatibility threshold.
research_papers.relevance: Validates IronCache's ~240-command 80/20 target and the RESP2+RESP3 dual-protocol requirement as the bar for 'drop-in Redis replacement.'

open_questions: What exactly is IronCache's RESP3 emission policy: should it emit native RESP3 aggregate types (map/set/double) for commands like HGETALL/CONFIG GET/SMEMBERS when proto=3, or always emit RESP2-shaped replies and only differ on null? (Redis differs per-command; emitting RESP2 shapes under RESP3 risks subtle client breakage.)

Should the tracking table for default-mode client-side caching live per-shard or be a global structure, and what is its hard memory cap and eviction policy (spurious-invalidation eviction like Redis, or LRU) given IronCache's minimal-memory goal?

Is multi-DB / SELECT supported, and if so does client-side-caching invalidation use Redis's single-cross-DB-namespace behavior or a per-DB namespace? This affects both the tracking design and SELECT semantics.

What is the precise scope of Lua: confirm scripting/Functions as an explicit non-goal for M0-M2, or carve out a tiny set of server-side atomic primitives (e.g., compare-and-set, atomic counters with TTL) that cover the common scripting use cases without a Lua VM?
How faithfully must error strings match Redis (exact wording vs just the leading uppercase token)? Need a test corpus of clients that pattern-match on error text to set the bar.

For multi-node IronCache, does sharded Pub/Sub (SSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH) and MOVED/ASK redirection get implemented, and does that pull in the cluster-bus protocol — or does IronCache use a different topology that still presents the Redis Cluster client contract?

Which CLIENT subcommands are in Tier 0 (ID, SETNAME, GETNAME, NO-EVICT, NO-TOUCH, INFO, LIST, KILL, UNPAUSE) versus deferred, given that several clients issue CLIENT SETINFO/SETNAME automatically on connect?

proposed_issues.title: EPIC: RESP wire protocol and command-surface compatibility
proposed_issues.summary: Umbrella issue tracking RESP2/RESP3 framing, HELLO negotiation, the request/response model, and the tiered command surface that defines 'Redis-compatible' for IronCache.

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: Define the IronCache compatibility tiering (Tier 0-4) and publish it
proposed_issues.summary: Ratify the explicit tier list (wire/connection -> 80/20 data commands -> Pub/Sub+txn+CSC -> notifications/ACL/TLS -> scripting/non-goals) and document it in the README and vision EPIC so 'compatibility' has a precise, testable meaning.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: RESP2/RESP3 parser and serializer with HELLO per-connection state machine
proposed_issues.summary: Specify the zero-copy RESP parser, the 15 RESP3 type encoders, HELLO negotiation (RESP2 default, NOPROTO/unknown-command fallback), and the per-connection state object (proto, user, db, subscriptions, tracking, MULTI queue).

proposed_issues.title: DECISION: RESP3 reply-shaping policy (native aggregate types vs RESP2-shaped under proto=3)
proposed_issues.summary: Decide, per command group, whether IronCache emits native RESP3 maps/sets/doubles or RESP2-shaped replies when proto=3, matching Redis's per-command behavior to avoid client breakage.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: RESP parser performance strategy (scalar+special-case vs SIMD)
proposed_issues.summary: Validate, via microbenchmark, that a scalar parser with GET/SET hot-path special-casing plus perfect-hash dispatch beats a SIMD/branchless parser (per Montrose/Garnet), and pin the dispatch and allocation strategy.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Pipelining and batched I/O (readv/io_uring) for the connection loop
proposed_issues.summary: Specify in-order multi-request parsing and batched reply flushing to maximize pipelined throughput, including backpressure and max-inflight limits.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: MULTI/EXEC/DISCARD/WATCH with optimistic locking and no rollback
proposed_issues.summary: Define the queue-then-atomically-apply execution boundary, dirty-CAS WATCH abort (EXEC->null), and exact no-rollback runtime-error semantics matching Redis.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Unified server-push channel for Pub/Sub, sharded Pub/Sub, keyspace notifications, and CSC invalidation
proposed_issues.summary: One internal push abstraction that renders as RESP3 push (>) or RESP2 multi-bulk, serving SUBSCRIBE/PSUBSCRIBE/SSUBSCRIBE, __keyspace/__keyevent notifications, and client-side-caching invalidations.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: CLIENT TRACKING (BCAST+RESP3-push default, per-client table fallback, RESP2 REDIRECT)
proposed_issues.summary: Implement BCAST + RESP3 same-connection invalidation as the recommended mode, plus the default per-client tracking table (hard memory cap, eviction policy) and the RESP2 __redis__:invalidate + REDIRECT path.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: AUTH, requirepass, and minimal ACL (default user, single-password)
proposed_issues.summary: Implement HELLO AUTH, AUTH user pass, legacy requirepass, SHA-256 password storage, and a minimal default-user model; defer full @category/%R%W/selector ACL to a later issue.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Full ACL category/selector engine and aclfile load/save
proposed_issues.summary: Add the ~20 ACL command categories, key %R/%W patterns, channel & patterns, selectors, ACL SETUSER/GETUSER/LIST/CAT/GENPASS, and aclfile persistence.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Embedded TLS (rustls) listener behind a config flag
proposed_issues.summary: Add optional in-process TLS termination consistent with the single-static-binary goal (no stunnel sidecar), including cert/key config and TLS-only listener mode.

proposed_issues.title: NON-GOAL: Lua scripting (EVAL/EVALSHA) and Redis Functions for M0-M2
proposed_issues.summary: Declare embedded Lua scripting and Functions out of scope for the initial milestones due to surface/semantic risk; document the decision and the trigger conditions for revisiting.

proposed_issues.title: RESEARCH: Carve a minimal native atomic-op set to cover common scripting use cases without a Lua VM
proposed_issues.summary: Survey the most common EVAL use cases (atomic CAS, rate limiters, atomic counters with TTL) and propose narrow native commands that satisfy them without embedding Lua.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Machine-readable command spec + conformance test harness against redis-cli/redis-py/ioredis
proposed_issues.summary: Generate the dispatch table and arity/reply-type validation from a command spec file, and build a conformance suite that runs unmodified Redis clients (and the Redis test framework) against IronCache.

proposed_issues.title: DESIGN: Redis-compatible error-string catalog (ERR/WRONGTYPE/NOPROTO/NOAUTH/NOPERM/EXECABORT)
proposed_issues.summary: Define the canonical error prefixes and messages as part of the wire contract, since some clients pattern-match on error text.

proposed_issues.title: TASK: Tier 0 CLIENT subcommands (ID/SETNAME/GETNAME/SETINFO/INFO/LIST/KILL/NO-EVICT/NO-TOUCH)
proposed_issues.summary: Implement the CLIENT subcommands that mainstream clients issue automatically on connect (notably SETNAME/SETINFO/ID) so handshakes do not error.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Re-verified all 14 claims against fresh primary sources (Redis official docs at redis.io/docs/latest, the redis/redis-specifications RESP3.md spec on GitHub, and the redis-py / node-redis GitHub release notes), plus independent corroboration for the two medium-confidence claims.

Outcome: 14 confirmed, 0 corrected, 0 refuted, 0 uncertain.

Anchoring quality:
- Strong primary-source confirmation (verbatim text match) for the RESP protocol spec claims (type prefixes, null encodings, 512 MB bulk limit, NOPROTO error, RESP2/RESP3 opt-in), client-side caching, transactions, sharded Pub/Sub, keyspace notifications, and ACL claims.
- redis-core-command-count: only a secondary source (oneuptime blog) gives a hard number (246 for Redis 7.x), corroborated by a second search (246 core, 271 with RedisJSON). No canonical Redis-published figure exists, so this remains the weakest link, but the claimed '~240-246' range contains the verified value -> confirmed at medium confidence.
- client-default-resp3-redis8: upgraded from the original 'medium' because both redis-py 8.0.0 and node-redis 6.0 release notes independently confirm RESP3-by-default, and the server-side RESP2 default is corroborated by the RESP3 spec.

Minor framing nits worth flagging (none rise to a correction):
1. keyspace 'A' flag is documented as an alias for g$lshzxet that EXCLUDES m (key-miss) and n (new); the claim's gloss 'A=all' is slightly loose but its per-flag definitions are all accurate.
2. hello-noproto-error cites the protocol-spec URL rather than the HELLO command reference page, but the protocol-spec page does carry the exact -NOPROTO text, so the claim is properly anchored.
3. sharded-pubsub claim text appears to list 'SSUBSCRIBE' twice (likely a typo for SUNSUBSCRIBE); the three real commands SSUBSCRIBE/SUNSUBSCRIBE/SPUBLISH are correct per the doc.

Two redis.io fetches initially hit a stale internal redirect and returned model-general-knowledge instead of page content; I re-fetched the correct URLs (develop/pubsub/ and develop/pubsub/keyspace-notifications/) and confirmed against actual page content before recording verdicts.

dimensions.summary:
Valkey is the BSD-3-Clause, Linux Foundation-governed fork of Redis, created in March 2024 after Redis Ltd. relicensed Redis 7.4 to dual SSPLv1/RSALv2. Its license (verified directly in the repo COPYING file at tag 8.1.8: SPDX BSD-3-Clause) and neutral governance are the strategic reason it matters for IronCache: it is the permissively-licensed, AWS/Google/Oracle-backed continuation of the Redis wire protocol and RESP semantics that IronCache aims to be compatible with. We should treat Valkey (not relicensed Redis) as the canonical RESP-compatibility reference and conformance oracle. Note that as of 2026-06-13 the line has advanced well past the prompt's 8.0/8.1 framing: 9.0.0 GA shipped 2025-10-21, 9.1.0 on 2025-05-19 (sic — actually 2026; latest tags pulled live: 9.1.0, 8.1.8, 9.0.4), so 8.0/8.1 are now the historical baseline of the threading/memory work, with 9.x extending it (zero-copy responses, MPTCP, AVX-512, atomic slot migration, cluster multi-DB).

Threading model: Valkey 8.0's "asynchronous I/O threading" (AWS-contributed) keeps command EXECUTION single-threaded on the main thread but offloads the full connection lifecycle — socket read, RESP protocol PARSING, reply formatting, socket write, epoll polling, and even memory deallocation — to I/O threads via per-thread single-producer/single-consumer atomic ring-buffer job queues (verified in src/io_threads.c at 8.1.8: cache-line-aligned _Atomic head/tail IOJobQueue). This is strictly more aggressive than Redis's io-threads, which historically threaded only the read/write syscalls. The headline number is ~1.2M QPS on AWS r7g vs ~380K previously (~3.16x), with TLS workloads benefiting most. Crucially, io-threads defaults to OFF (the `io-threads 4` line is commented in valkey.conf; setting 1 = main-thread-only), and the old `io-threads-do-reads` knob is deprecated/no-op. The execution path stays single-threaded, so this scales I/O, not command throughput — the well-understood ceiling that Dragonfly critiques and that IronCache must decide whether to accept or break.

Memory efficiency is Valkey's most directly borrowable work. 8.0 delivered ~20% RSS reduction vs 7.2 (benchmark: 693.64MB→550.56MB at ~6.3M keys) via two mechanisms: (1) per-slot dictionaries / the kvstore refactor — splitting the single global hashtable into 16,384 per-slot dictionaries eliminated the two cluster slot-tracking linked-list pointers, saving 16 bytes/entry; and (2) embedding the key's SDS directly into the dictionary entry instead of a separate pointer+allocation, saving ~8 bytes/entry and improving cache locality. 8.1 went further by replacing the chaining `dict` entirely with a new open-addressing `hashtable` (designed by Viktor Söderqvist, Swiss-tables-inspired, bucket-chaining idea from Madelyn Olson), giving a ~20 byte/entry reduction for keys without TTL and ~30 bytes with TTL, plus ~47% lower fork copy-on-write. Verified in src/hashtable.c@8.1.8: 64-bit layout uses cache-line-sized buckets of 7 entries (ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET 7) carrying a presence bitmask + 7 one-byte stored hashes + 7 entry pointers, with soft max fill ~91.43%. This is essentially the design IronCache should start from.

Replication and transport: dual-channel replication (8.0, #60) streams the RDB and the replica backlog on two parallel connections during full sync, cutting sync time up to ~50% under heavy reads and moving Client-Output-Buffer tracking to the replica to relieve primary memory pressure — but it defaults OFF (`dual-channel-replication-enabled no` at 8.1.8). Valkey Over RDMA (8.0, #477) is an experimental, Linux-only connection module claiming up to +275% throughput; it remains explicitly "may be removed in any minor/major version" and does not support replication (TCP/TLS still required). For IronCache's single-binary, single-node-first ethos these are M2+/optional concerns, but dual-channel's COB-on-replica design and RDMA's zero-copy framing are worth studying.

Bottom line for IronCache: borrow Valkey's memory layout aggressively (open-addressing cache-line buckets, embedded keys, slot-partitioned tables, AVX SIMD for bitops/HLL), study its I/O-thread job-queue design as a proven RESP offload pattern, but treat its single-threaded execution core as the bar to BEAT — IronCache's "max throughput per core" thesis should evaluate sharded-by-key multi-threaded execution (Dragonfly/shared-nothing style) rather than inheriting Valkey's main-thread bottleneck. Valkey should also be wired in as a differential-fuzzing/conformance oracle for RESP2/RESP3 and as the head-to-head benchmark baseline.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey is licensed under BSD-3-Clause (verified in repo COPYING).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from COPYING at tag 8.1.8 via gh api raw.

verification.corrected_value: BSD-3-Clause (SPDX-License-Identifier: BSD-3-Clause)
verification.note: Verified by checking out tag 8.1.8 in a fresh clone: COPYING line 3 is exactly 'SPDX-License-Identifier: BSD-3-Clause', followed by 'BSD 3-Clause License, Copyright (c) 2024-present, Valkey contributors' (License 1) plus a second BSD-3-Clause for Redis Ltd. 2006-2020. WebFetch of the cited URL agrees. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey is the Linux Foundation fork forked from Redis after Redis Ltd. relicensed to SSPLv1/RSALv2 in March 2024.
prior_art_claims.value: fork created March 2024; governed by the Linux Foundation
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: LF press release; widely corroborated.

verification.corrected_value: Valkey forked Redis 7.2.4 on 2024-03-28 as a Linux Foundation project, after Redis Ltd. relicensed the Redis core to dual SSPLv1/RSALv2 on 2024-03-20 (away from BSD). Governed by the Linux Foundation. Better source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Valkey
verification.note: The stated VALUE (fork created March 2024, governed by Linux Foundation, prompted by the SSPLv1/RSALv2 relicense) is CORRECT and independently verified (Wikipedia: forked Redis 7.2.4 on 2024-03-28; relicense announced 2024-03-20; 'a project of the Linux Foundation'). However the CITED SOURCE is wrong: linuxfoundation.org/press/valkey-8-0 is the Valkey 8.0 release announcement and, per WebFetch, contains NO mention of the fork, March 2024, or the SSPL/RSAL relicense (it only confirms LF governance). Corrected: substituting a source that actually supports the fork-origin facts. Minor precision: the relicense was to SSPLv1 + RSALv2 (the prompt's 'SSPLv1/RSALv2' is accurate).

prior_art_claims.claim: Asynchronous I/O threading raised single-instance throughput to ~1.2M QPS on AWS r7g vs ~380K prior, ~3.16x.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Official Valkey 8.0 blog; reproduced by Dragonfly's independent c7g benchmark (1.19M RPS).

verification.corrected_value: 1.2M QPS (r7g) up from 380K QPS; ~3.16x
verification.note: WebFetch of the cited blog quotes verbatim: 'up to 1.2 million Queries Per Second (QPS) on AWS's r7g platform, compared to the previous limit of 380K QPS.' 1.2M/380K = 3.16x. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: io-threads defaults to OFF: the `io-threads 4` directive is commented out in valkey.conf, and io-threads 1 means main-thread-only.
prior_art_claims.value: default = single-threaded (io-threads line commented; 1 = main thread)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from valkey.conf at 8.1.8.

verification.corrected_value: default = single-threaded (io-threads line commented; 1 = main thread)
verification.note: Verified in checked-out 8.1.8 valkey.conf: line 1432 '# io-threads 4' is commented out; lines 1422-1423 'By default threading is disabled'; line 1434 'Setting io-threads to 1 will just use the main thread as usual.' Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: The legacy `io-threads-do-reads` config is deprecated and has no effect in the new threading model.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Stated verbatim in valkey.conf NOTE at 8.1.8.

prior_art_claims.claim: I/O threads receive work via per-thread single-producer/single-consumer ring-buffer job queues with cache-line-aligned atomic head/tail indices; main thread falls back to doing the job itself if the queue is full.
prior_art_claims.value: SPSC atomic ring buffer per IO thread (IOJobQueue, CACHE_LINE_SIZE-aligned head/tail)
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read the IOJobQueue struct and push/isFull logic directly from src/io_threads.c@8.1.8.

verification.corrected_value: SPSC atomic ring buffer per IO thread (IOJobQueue, CACHE_LINE_SIZE-aligned head/tail)
verification.note: Verified directly in src/io_threads.c at 8.1.8: 'typedef struct IOJobQueue { iojob *ring_buffer; size_t size; _Atomic size_t head __attribute__((aligned(CACHE_LINE_SIZE))); _Atomic size_t tail __attribute__((aligned(CACHE_LINE_SIZE))); }' with 'IOJobQueue io_jobs[IO_THREADS_MAX_NUM]' (per-thread). Head=producer (main thread), tail=consumer (IO thread) -> SPSC. Comment at line 48 confirms fallback: 'In the worst case we will just assume wrongly the buffer is full and the main thread will do the job by itself.' All elements confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey 8.0 I/O threads handle socket reads, RESP protocol parsing, reply writes, epoll polling, and memory deallocation — keeping only command execution on the main thread; broader offload than Redis io-threads.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Valkey 8.0 blog plus valkey.conf comment ('threading of reads and protocol parsing'); corroborated by Dragonfly comparison.

verification.corrected_value: offloads read+parse+write+poll+free; execution stays single-threaded
verification.note: The cited blog (valkey-8-0-0-rc1) only describes the offload generically, so I verified the specifics in source. src/io_threads.c contains: ioThreadReadQueryFromClient via trySendReadToIOThreads (socket reads + RESP parse), ioThreadWriteToClient via trySendWriteToIOThreads (reply writes), IOThreadPoll calling aePoll (epoll polling), and tryOffloadFreeArgvToIOThreads / tryOffloadFreeObjToIOThreads (memory deallocation). Command execution remains on the main thread. The 'broader than Redis io-threads' comparison is supported by the Dragonfly comparison and Redis's own description (Redis threads socket I/O only). Confirmed; note I anchored to code rather than the cited blog.

prior_art_claims.claim: When I/O threads parse multiple commands ahead, Valkey batch-prefetches their dictionary entries; default prefetch-batch-max-size is 16 (0 disables).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from valkey.conf@8.1.8.

verification.corrected_value: prefetch-batch-max-size 16 (default)
verification.note: Verified in 8.1.8 valkey.conf lines 1443-1449: 'The default batch size is 16, which can be modified using the prefetch-batch-max-size config' and 'When the config is set to 0, prefetching is disabled', with commented directive '# prefetch-batch-max-size 16'. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey 8.0 reduces memory ~20% vs 7.2 (benchmark 693.64MB->550.56MB at ~6.3M keys).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Official Valkey memory-efficiency blog with stated dataset and numbers.

verification.corrected_value: ~20.63% RSS reduction (693.64MB -> 550.56MB, ~6.3M keys)
verification.note: WebFetch of the cited blog confirms: 7.2 baseline 693.64 MB, 8.0 optimized 550.56 MB for ~6.3 million keys, overall ~20.63% reduction. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Splitting the global dictionary into 16,384 per-slot dictionaries (kvstore refactor) removed the two cluster slot-tracking pointers, saving 16 bytes per key-value entry.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Valkey memory-efficiency blog; corroborated by 8.0.0 release notes kvstore refactor (#358).

verification.corrected_value: 16,384 per-slot dicts; -16 bytes/entry
verification.note: Cited blog confirms 16,384 per-slot dictionaries (kvstore refactor) removing the slot-prev/slot-next pointers for '16 bytes per dictionary entry' (693.64MB -> 598.77MB, 13.68% of the savings). Cross-checked in 8.0.0 00-RELEASENOTES: 'Refactor the per-slot dict-array db.c into a new kvstore data structure' and 'Replace cluster metadata with slot specific dictionaries to reduce memory'. Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Embedding the key's SDS directly inside the dictionary entry (instead of a separate pointer+allocation) saves ~8 bytes per key and improves cache locality.
prior_art_claims.value: -8 bytes/key; embedded key in dict entry
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: 8.0.0 release notes 'Embed key directly in main dictionary entry'; quantified in memory blog.

verification.corrected_value: -8 bytes/key; embedded key in dict entry
verification.note: Verified in 8.0.0 00-RELEASENOTES line 188: 'Embed key directly in main dictionary entry for improved memory efficiency.' The valkey-memory-efficiency-8-0 blog quantifies it: 'saves 8 bytes additional memory overhead per key' while improving cache locality (598.77MB -> 550.56MB). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey 8.1 replaces the chaining dict with a new open-addressing hashtable saving ~20 bytes/entry (no-TTL) and up to ~30 bytes/entry (with TTL), and cutting fork COW overhead ~47%.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Valkey 8.1 GA blog; new hashtable.c present in repo.

verification.corrected_value: -20 bytes/entry (no TTL), -30 bytes/entry (TTL); -47% fork COW
verification.note: WebFetch of cited blog confirms verbatim: 'roughly 20 byte reduction per key-value pair for keys without a TTL', 'up to a 30 byte reduction for key-value pairs with a TTL', and fork COW overhead 'reduced by up to 47%'. All three numbers confirmed. (The 'open-addressing/Swiss-tables' framing is not in this GA blog but is verified separately in hashtable.c -- see valkey-hashtable-bucket-layout.)

prior_art_claims.claim: The new hashtable uses cache-line-sized buckets holding 7 entries (64-bit) with a presence bitmask + 7 stored hash bytes + 7 entry pointers; Swiss-tables-inspired open addressing with bucket chaining; soft max fill ~91.43%.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read bucket layout, ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET, BUCKET_FACTOR/DIVISOR, and design credits directly from src/hashtable.c@8.1.8.

verification.corrected_value: ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET=7 (64-bit), 12 (32-bit); max fill 91.43%
verification.note: Verified in src/hashtable.c at 8.1.8: '#define ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET 7' (64-bit) and '#define ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET 12' (32-bit); line 172 'When resizing, we get a fill of at most 91.43% (32 / 5 / 7)'; line 41 'The cache-line sized bucket is inspired by ideas used in Swiss tables'; bucket struct has 'BUCKET_BITS_TYPE presence : ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET' (presence bitmask), 'uint8_t hashes[ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET]' (7 stored hash bytes), 'void *entries[ENTRIES_PER_BUCKET]' (7 pointers); 'bucket size is one cache line' (64 bytes). Open addressing with bucket chaining (the b->chained field). All elements confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dual-channel replication is disabled by default (dual-channel-replication-enabled no).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Read directly from valkey.conf@8.1.8.

verification.corrected_value: dual-channel-replication-enabled no (default)
verification.note: Verified in 8.1.8 valkey.conf line 787: 'dual-channel-replication-enabled no' (active, uncommented default). Confirmed.

prior_art_claims.claim: Dual-channel replication transfers RDB and replica backlog in parallel during full sync, cutting sync time up to ~50% under heavy reads and moving COB tracking to the replica.
prior_art_claims.value: up to 50% faster full sync; COB tracked on replica
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Vendor blog number; not independently reproduced here.

verification.corrected_value: up to 50% faster full sync; COB tracked on replica
verification.note: WebFetch of cited blog confirms: 'dual-channel replication scheme, allowing the RDB and the replica backlog to be transferred simultaneously'; 'In scenarios with heavy read commands, the sync time can be cut by up to 50%'; 'The replica now manages the Client Output Buffer (COB) tracking'. All elements confirmed (upgrading from the original 'medium' confidence).

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey Over RDMA is an experimental, Linux-only connection-module transport claiming up to +275% throughput; it does not support replication (TCP/TLS still required) and may be removed in any minor/major release.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Official Valkey RDMA docs explicitly mark experimental and list limitations; 8.0 notes #477.

prior_art_claims.claim: Redis 8.0's reworked io-threads model claims up to 112% throughput improvement with io-threads=8 on multi-core Intel; it threads socket I/O while keeping execution single-threaded (narrower than Valkey).
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Third-party (Dragonfly) restating Redis's claim; useful as comparison anchor, not a Redis primary source.

verification.corrected_value: up to 112% with io-threads=8 (Intel); range 37%-112%
verification.note: Cited Dragonfly comparison quotes 'When the io-threads parameter is set to 8 on a multi-core Intel CPU, we've measured up to 112% improvement in throughput.' Independently confirmed against Redis's own wording: improvements 'range from 37% to 112%' depending on command, io-threads=8, multi-core Intel. Redis threads socket I/O while keeping command execution single-threaded (confirmed), which is narrower than Valkey's offload (Valkey also offloads parsing/poll/free per io_threads.c). Confirmed; the better primary source is Redis's own GA blog rather than the Dragonfly secondary source.

prior_art_claims.claim: As of 2026-06-13 the live release line is past 8.0/8.1: 9.0.0 GA shipped 2025-10-21, with 9.1.0, 9.0.4, and 8.1.8 as recent tags.
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Pulled release tags+dates live via gh api releases.

verification.note: Verified against git tags in a fresh clone: tag 9.0.0 dated 2025-10-21 (-0700); 9.1.0 dated 2026-05-19; 9.0.4 dated 2026-05-05; 8.1.8 dated 2026-06-02. 'git tag | sort -V' shows 9.1.0 as the highest release (no tag beyond it), with 9.0.4 and 8.1.8 the recent per-line tags. As of 2026-06-13 the release line is past 8.0/8.1. Confirmed. (Some secondary articles cite earlier planned Sept 2025 GA dates, but the actual GA tag is 2025-10-21.)

prior_art_claims.claim: Valkey 9.0 adds atomic slot migration, hash-field expiration, cluster multi-DB, zero-copy responses, MPTCP, and AVX-512; a 9.0 cluster reportedly sustains 1B+ requests/sec and ~40% more single-node throughput than prior.
prior_art_claims.value: 1B+ req/s (cluster); ~40% more throughput; AVX-512 + MPTCP + zero-copy
prior_art_claims.confidence_reason: Vendor blog/press; cluster aggregate number is marketing-flavored, not per-node.

verification.corrected_value: 1B+ req/s (cluster, 2,000 nodes); ~40% more throughput (pipelining prefetch); AVX-512 + MPTCP + zero-copy
verification.note: WebFetch of cited blog confirms: atomic slot migration, hash-field expiration (HEXPIRE/HEXPIREAT/HTTL), cluster multi-DB ('full support for numbered databases in cluster mode'), zero-copy responses ('Large requests avoid internal memory copying, yielding up to 20% higher throughput'), MPTCP ('Multipath TCP... reduce latency by 25%'), '1 billion requests per second' at 2,000 nodes, and 'up to 40% higher throughput' from memory prefetching when pipelining. AVX-512 is NOT in this blog but IS confirmed in the 9.0.0-rc1 GitHub release notes ('Optimize string-to-integer performance using AVX512', ~19% gain per Phoronix). Every listed feature is verified; original 'medium' confidence upgraded to confirmed.

mechanisms.name: Asynchronous I/O threading (full connection-lifecycle offload)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Offloads socket read, RESP parse, reply format, socket write, epoll poll, and memory free to I/O threads via per-thread SPSC atomic ring-buffer job queues, while command execution stays on the single main thread. ~1.2M QPS on r7g.
mechanisms.rationale: Borrow the proven job-queue/offload pattern and the batched-prefetch idea, but do NOT inherit the single-threaded execution core. IronCache's 'max throughput per core' goal argues for sharded-by-key parallel execution (shared-nothing) so adding cores scales command throughput, not just I/O. Use Valkey's model as the conservative baseline and the thing to beat.

mechanisms.name: Open-addressing cache-line hashtable (Swiss-tables-inspired)
mechanisms.what_it_does: Replaces chaining dict with 64-byte buckets of 7 entries: 1-byte presence bitmask, 7 one-byte secondary hashes for SIMD/branch-light probing, 7 entry pointers; bucket-chained on overflow; incremental rehash via two tables; soft max fill ~91.43%. Saves ~20-30 bytes/entry.
mechanisms.rationale: This is close to ideal for a memory-efficient cache: high cache locality, low per-entry overhead, SIMD-friendly probing. IronCache should start from this design (or a Rust hashbrown/SwissTable variant) rather than a chaining map. The stored-hash-byte trick and presence bitmask are directly applicable.

mechanisms.name: Embedded key in dictionary entry
mechanisms.what_it_does: Stores the key's SDS bytes inline in the hash-table entry instead of a separate pointer + heap allocation, removing one pointer and one malloc header per key (~8 bytes) and improving locality.
mechanisms.rationale: Eliminating per-key indirection and allocator overhead is exactly the kind of memory win IronCache wants. In Rust this maps to a single allocation holding key bytes + value handle inline (inline small keys, e.g. <=23 bytes, like SSO).

mechanisms.name: Per-slot dictionaries / kvstore partitioning
mechanisms.what_it_does: Splits the single global dict into 16,384 per-cluster-slot dictionaries, eliminating two slot-tracking linked-list pointers per entry (-16 bytes) and enabling per-slot stats and finer rehashing.
mechanisms.rationale: The memory win (no slot pointers) is real, but 16,384 fixed slots is a Redis-cluster artifact. IronCache should partition the keyspace for its OWN purposes — per-execution-shard tables (to enable parallel command execution) — and let those shards double as slot/migration units, rather than hardcoding 16,384.

mechanisms.name: Batched dictionary prefetch for pipelined commands
mechanisms.what_it_does: When I/O threads have parsed N commands ahead, the engine software-prefetches the hash-table entries for the next batch (prefetch-batch-max-size default 16) before executing, hiding memory-access latency; reported ~3.5x faster key ops / ~10% pipeline throughput.
mechanisms.rationale: Memory-latency hiding via lookahead prefetch is high-value for a cache and orthogonal to the threading decision. IronCache should design its command pipeline to expose a lookahead window and issue __builtin_prefetch-equivalents on the bucket addresses.

mechanisms.name: Dual-channel replication
mechanisms.what_it_does: During full sync, streams the RDB snapshot and the live replica backlog over two parallel connections, with the replica owning Client-Output-Buffer accounting; cuts sync time up to ~50% and reduces primary memory pressure. Default off.
mechanisms.rationale: Replication is M2+ for a single-node-first project, but the COB-on-replica idea (don't buffer the backlog on the busy primary) is a clean design and worth adopting when IronCache grows to multi-node. The two-connection complexity should be opt-in, mirroring Valkey defaulting it off.

mechanisms.name: Valkey Over RDMA transport (experimental)
mechanisms.what_it_does: A Linux-only connection-module transport over RDMA verbs (librdmacm/libibverbs) claiming up to +275% throughput; incompatible with replication; explicitly may be removed in any release.
mechanisms.rationale: RDMA conflicts with IronCache's single-static-binary, easy-setup, broad-portability ethos (requires RDMA-capable NICs + kernel libs, Linux-only, still experimental upstream). Note it as a far-future optional feature gate, not a core direction.

mechanisms.name: AVX2/AVX-512 SIMD for bitops and HyperLogLog
mechanisms.what_it_does: Vectorizes PFCOUNT/PFMERGE (reported ~12x via AVX2) and BITCOUNT (up to +514%), with AVX-512 added in 9.0.
mechanisms.rationale: SIMD acceleration of bitmap/HLL/aggregation paths is a pure efficiency win aligned with IronCache's throughput-per-core goal; Rust gives us std::arch intrinsics and portable SIMD. Worth a dedicated design issue for the data-structure command paths.

mechanisms.name: Differential testing vs Valkey as RESP oracle
mechanisms.what_it_does: Valkey is the permissively-licensed reference implementation of the RESP2/RESP3 protocol and Redis command semantics.
mechanisms.rationale: Because it is BSD-3 (not relicensed Redis), IronCache can legally run Valkey as a side-by-side conformance/differential-fuzzing oracle and as the head-to-head benchmark baseline without SSPL contamination. This should be a standing part of the test harness.

ironcache_implications: Adopt an open-addressing, cache-line-bucketed hash table as IronCache's core index from day one (Rust hashbrown/SwissTable-style): 64-byte buckets, stored secondary-hash bytes for SIMD probing, presence bitmask, incremental two-table rehash. This directly captures Valkey 8.1's ~20-30 bytes/entry savings without inheriting C's dict baggage.

Store small keys inline in the entry (SSO-style, e.g. keys <= ~23 bytes inline) to replicate Valkey's embedded-key win, eliminating a pointer indirection and an allocator header per key; benchmark the inline threshold against real key-size distributions.

Make the threading decision explicit and contrarian: Valkey proves I/O offload alone hits a single-threaded execution ceiling. IronCache should prototype shared-nothing, key-sharded parallel command execution (one shard = one thread = one partition of the index) so 'throughput per core' scales with cores for the common single-key GET/SET path, falling back to Valkey-style I/O-only threading only if sharding proves too costly for multi-key/transaction semantics.

Reuse Valkey's I/O-thread job-queue pattern (per-thread SPSC atomic ring buffer, main-thread fallback when full) as the connection-handling layer regardless of the execution model; it is a clean, lock-light design that maps well to Rust (crossbeam ArrayQueue / a hand-rolled SPSC).

Build a lookahead command pipeline that prefetches index buckets for the next batch (Valkey default 16) using prefetch intrinsics — a memory-latency win orthogonal to threading and high-impact for pipelined workloads.

Keep advanced replication/transport OFF by default and out of the core binary's hot path, exactly as Valkey defaults dual-channel-replication and io-threads off; favor a simple single-node story first (matches IronCache's easy-setup goal).

Reject RDMA as a core transport (Linux-only, special NICs, still experimental upstream) — it violates the single-binary/portable/easy-setup thesis. Track it only as a far-future optional cargo feature flag.

Vectorize bitops/HLL/aggregation command paths with portable SIMD (std::arch AVX2/AVX-512 with runtime feature detection) — Valkey's 12x PFCOUNT and +514% BITCOUNT show the upside and these are self-contained.

Wire Valkey (BSD-3, license-safe) into CI as a RESP2/RESP3 differential-testing oracle and as the head-to-head benchmark baseline (target: beat Valkey 9.x QPS-per-core and bytes-per-key on identical hardware). Pin to specific Valkey tags for reproducibility.

Track the live Valkey roadmap (9.0 atomic slot migration, cluster multi-DB, zero-copy responses, MPTCP) as the moving compatibility/perf target — IronCache's single-to-multi-node story should study atomic slot migration as the modern alternative to Redis's key-by-key cluster resharding.

research_papers.title: Swiss Tables / flat_hash_map design (Abseil)
research_papers.key_idea: Open-addressing hash table with a control byte per slot (7-bit hash + presence) probed with SSE/SIMD to test a group of slots per instruction, yielding high load factors and cache locality.
research_papers.relevance: Valkey 8.1's new hashtable explicitly credits Swiss-tables for its cache-line bucket design; this is the canonical reference IronCache should base its core index on (via Rust's hashbrown, which is a SwissTable port).

research_papers.title: Scaling Memcache at Facebook
research_papers.key_idea: Engineering a high-throughput distributed cache: lease mechanisms, request coalescing, regional pools, and handling thundering herds at scale.
research_papers.relevance: Provides the systems context for what a cache must do beyond single-node speed; informs IronCache's eventual single-to-multi-node design and contrasts with Valkey/Redis's cluster model.

research_papers.title: MICA: A Holistic Approach to Fast In-Memory Key-Value Storage
research_papers.key_idea: Shared-nothing partitioning of the keyspace across cores with each core owning a partition, plus lossy/lossless index structures, to achieve near-linear multicore scaling for KV operations.
research_papers.relevance: Directly supports IronCache's contrarian thesis: partition-per-core execution beats Valkey's single-threaded-execution + I/O-threads model for throughput-per-core.

research_papers.title: Memshare / log-structured memory & memory-efficient KV (and RAMCloud log-structured memory)
research_papers.key_idea: Log-structured memory management and adaptive memory partitioning to minimize fragmentation and maximize hit rate per byte in in-memory caches.
research_papers.relevance: Valkey leans on jemalloc + active defrag; IronCache's 'minimal memory' goal should evaluate log-structured/slab approaches as an alternative to allocator-dependent layouts.

research_papers.title: Dragonfly: a modern replacement for Redis and Memcached (VLL / shared-nothing threading)
research_papers.key_idea: A shared-nothing, thread-per-core architecture (built on a userspace fiber/io_uring runtime) that shards the keyspace so command execution itself scales across cores, unlike Redis/Valkey's single execution thread.
research_papers.relevance: The concrete counter-design to Valkey's threading model and the architecture IronCache should benchmark its own multicore-execution prototype against.

open_questions: Can IronCache's RESP/transaction/MULTI-EXEC and Lua/scripting semantics be preserved under a shared-nothing key-sharded execution model, or do multi-key/cross-slot commands force a Valkey-style single-execution-thread fallback for those paths?

What is the real bytes-per-entry IronCache can achieve in Rust vs Valkey 8.1's hashtable once you account for the value handle, TTL, LRU/LFU metadata, and Rust's allocator — is the ~20-30 byte/entry parity beatable?

Does io_uring (Linux) give IronCache a materially better I/O path than Valkey's epoll + I/O-thread job queues, and can it be made portable/optional without breaking the single-binary story on macOS/BSD?
Should IronCache target Valkey 9.x's atomic slot migration semantics for its eventual cluster mode, or design a different resharding primitive better suited to a from-scratch engine?
How much of Valkey's claimed throughput is TLS-dominated (the offload helps TLS most) — what is the apples-to-apples plaintext QPS-per-core baseline IronCache must beat?
What is the right keyspace-partition count for IronCache (Valkey's 16,384 is a cluster artifact) given that the same partitions must serve as both execution shards and migration units?

proposed_issues.title: Decide IronCache threading model: shared-nothing key-sharded execution vs Valkey-style I/O-only offload
proposed_issues.summary: Choose whether command execution is parallelized by sharding the keyspace per core (Dragonfly/MICA style) or kept single-threaded with I/O offload (Valkey style). This is the load-bearing decision for the 'max throughput per core' goal; document the multi-key/transaction tradeoffs.

proposed_issues.title: Design IronCache core index as an open-addressing cache-line hashtable (SwissTable/hashbrown)
proposed_issues.summary: Spec a 64-byte-bucket open-addressing table with stored secondary-hash bytes, presence bitmask, SIMD group probing, and incremental two-table rehash, mirroring Valkey 8.1's hashtable to capture its ~20-30 bytes/entry savings.

proposed_issues.title: Embed small keys inline in index entries (SSO-style) to eliminate per-key indirection
proposed_issues.summary: Replicate Valkey 8.0's embedded-key win: store keys below an inline threshold directly in the entry to remove a pointer and allocator header per key; determine the threshold from key-size distributions.

proposed_issues.title: Benchmark harness: Valkey 9.x as RESP differential-test oracle and head-to-head baseline
proposed_issues.summary: Stand up a CI harness that runs IronCache against pinned Valkey tags for RESP2/RESP3 conformance (differential fuzzing) and side-by-side QPS-per-core and bytes-per-key benchmarks on identical hardware.

proposed_issues.title: Research: lookahead command pipeline with bucket prefetching
proposed_issues.summary: Evaluate Valkey's batched index prefetch (default lookahead 16) in a Rust context: expose a pipeline lookahead window and prefetch index buckets for the next batch to hide memory latency; quantify the win.

proposed_issues.title: Non-goal: RDMA as a core transport
proposed_issues.summary: Declare RDMA out of scope for the core single binary (Linux-only, special NICs, experimental upstream), preserving the single-static-binary/easy-setup thesis; revisit only as an optional far-future feature flag.

proposed_issues.title: Design: connection I/O layer using per-core SPSC job queues (Valkey io_threads pattern) and/or io_uring
proposed_issues.summary: Spec the socket-read/parse/write offload layer: adopt Valkey's lock-light per-thread SPSC ring-buffer job queue with main-thread fallback, and evaluate io_uring as an optional faster path without breaking portability.

proposed_issues.title: Research: SIMD acceleration for bitops, HyperLogLog, and aggregation command paths
proposed_issues.summary: Scope portable-SIMD (std::arch AVX2/AVX-512 + runtime feature detection) implementations of BITCOUNT/PFCOUNT/PFMERGE-equivalents, targeting Valkey's reported 12x HLL and +514% BITCOUNT gains.

proposed_issues.title: Decide keyspace partition count and dual-purpose shard/migration unit
proposed_issues.summary: Pick IronCache's partition count (Valkey's 16,384 is a Redis-cluster artifact) so partitions serve as both per-core execution shards and future migration units; study Valkey 9.0 atomic slot migration as the resharding model.

proposed_issues.title: Meta: pin Valkey as the canonical RESP-compatibility reference (not relicensed Redis)
proposed_issues.summary: Record the decision to treat BSD-3 Valkey as IronCache's license-safe compatibility reference and oracle, avoiding any SSPL/RSAL-licensed Redis code or test artifacts; pin specific Valkey versions in docs.

dimensions.verify_notes:
Method: fresh shallow clone of valkey-io/valkey with all relevant tags fetched (8.0.0, 8.1.0, 8.1.8, 9.0.0, 9.0.4, 9.1.0), checking out each tag and reading the actual source (valkey.conf, src/io_threads.c, src/hashtable.c, COPYING, 00-RELEASENOTES) for every code-default claim, plus WebFetch of each cited URL and independent cross-checks (Wikipedia, Redis GA blog, Phoronix, 9.0.0-rc1 release notes, web search). All 16 claims land confirmed except one source correction.

Two items worth flagging to the record owner:
1. [valkey-fork-origin] CORRECTED on SOURCE only — the facts (March 2024 fork, SSPLv1/RSALv2 relicense, Linux Foundation governance) are all correct and independently verified, but the cited linuxfoundation.org/press/valkey-8-0 URL does NOT support them (it is the 8.0 release announcement and never mentions the fork or relicense). Additional precision available: Valkey forked Redis 7.2.4 specifically, the fork was announced 2024-03-28, and the Redis relicense was 2024-03-20.
2. [valkey-9-billion-rps] every listed feature is real, but AVX-512 is documented in the 9.0.0-rc1 GitHub release notes, NOT in the introducing-valkey-9 blog cited; if precision matters, add the rc1 release-notes URL alongside the blog. The ~40% figure specifically refers to memory prefetching when pipelining (not a generic single-node number), and the 1B+ req/s is tied to a 2,000-node cluster.

Also note (no verdict change): the Swiss-tables/open-addressing characterization in [valkey-hashtable-bucket-layout] is not in any GA blog but is explicitly stated in src/hashtable.c (line 41 'inspired by ideas used in Swiss tables'), which is the right primary source and was used.
